titleStmt.title: Commentary to John of Ephesus Ecclesiastical History

editionStmt.p: This new digital commentary to John of Ephesus’s Ecclesiastical History, Part 3 has been created within the context of the DFG Project "Commentary on John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History." The current version should be considered preliminary, as it will be updated throughout the duration of the project. The authorship of each entry in the commentary has been indicated, and the authorship of individual comments should be indicated in citations of this work. Silvio Roggo is the main author of the commentary to Book 1 and was responsible for the overall consistency of the commentary. The remaining authors (Nikolai Kiel, Philip Michael Forness, and Hartmut Leppin) are listed in order of the number of entries of which they were the sole author or co-author. All authors have contributed to writing, checking, and correcting entries. Contributors of individual entries to the commentary have been indicated and should be cited as the authors of these entries. The student assistants (Katharina Koch, Marie Skubowius, and Eliano Veronesi) have tagged persons and places; this has been checked for accuracy by Silvio Roggo.

availability.licence: Available under a Creative Commons Attribution ShareAlike 4.0 International License

cRefPattern.p: For references to the Peshitta Old and New Testaments, PES is used as the abbreviation.
Noted are references to a biblical book, chapter, verse, sc. Peshitta:Gn:1:1; also possible: Peshitta:Gn:1:1-3 for a sequence of verses or LXX:Gn:1:1.3 for two verses not following each other.

The following abbreviations are used: Gn, Ex, Lv, Num, Dt, Jos, Judg, Rt, 1Sa, 2Sa, 1Ko, 2Ko, 1Chr, 2Chr, 3Esr, Esr, Est, Jdt, Tob, 1Mak, 2Mak, 3Mak, 4Mak, Ps, Oden, Prov, Eccl, Song, Job, Wis, Sir, PsSal, Hos, Am, Mi, Joel, Ob, Jon, Nah, Hab, Zeph, Hag, Sach, Mal, Is, Jr, Bar, Lam, EpistJer, Hes, Sus, Dn, Bel; Mt, Mk, Lk, Jn, Act, Rom, 1Cor, 2Cor, Gal, Eph, Phil, Col, 1Th, 2Th, 1Tim, 2Tim, Tt, Phm, Heb, Jak, 1P, 2P, 1Jn, 2Jn, 3Jn, Jud, Rev.
Noted are references to a biblical book and chapter, sc. Peshitta:Gn:1; also possible: Peshitta:Gn:1-3 for a sequence of chapters or Peshitta:Gn:1.3 for two chapters not following each other.
Noted are references to a biblical book, sc. Peshitta:Gn.

interpretation.p: This file is encoded according to the guidelines of the Patristic Text Archive (PTA). See https://pta.bbaw.de/project/encoding-guidelines and https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3737666.
Biblical quotations are annotated and referenced. References follow the scheme declared above.

Persons are annotated and referenced with IDs according to the TIPNR - Tyndale Individualised Proper Names with all References list for biblical persons and IDs of PTA persons index or of Syriaca.org for all other persons.
Groups of persons and organisations are not annotated.
Places are annotated and referenced with Pleiades Gazetteer or Syriaca.org IDs.
Words are not tagged and not analyzed.

category.catDesc: Critical edition in which the text-critical variants have been encoded.

Critical edition in which the text-critical variants have not been encoded, e.g. because it is a retro-digitisation of a printed critical edition and for copyright reasons the apparatus cannot be included in the retro-digitisation.
Edition which notes text-critical variants, but does not, for example, comprehensively include the manuscripts.
Pre-modern editions (e.g. editions in the Patrologia Graeca or Latina)

div.p: This new digital commentary to John of Ephesus’s Ecclesiastical History, Part 3 has been created within the context of the DFG Project "Commentary on John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History." The current version should be considered preliminary, as it will be updated throughout the duration of the project. The authorship of each entry in the commentary has been indicated, and the authorship of individual comments should be indicated in citations of this work. Silvio Roggo is the main author of the commentary to Book 1 and was responsible for the overall consistency of the commentary. The remaining authors (Nikolai Kiel, Philip Michael Forness, and Hartmut Leppin) are listed in order of the number of entries of which they were the sole author or co-author. All authors have contributed to writing, checking, and correcting entries. Contributors of individual entries to the commentary have been indicated and should be cited as the authors of these entries. The student assistants (Katharina Koch, Marie Skubowius, and Eliano Veronesi) have tagged persons and places; this has been checked for accuracy by Silvio Roggo.

Recommended Citation of Commentary: Silvio Roggo, Nikolai Kiel, Philip Michael Forness, and Hartmut Leppin, Commentary to John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History, Part 3, Patristic Text Archive (Berlin: Berlin-Brandenburgische Akademie der Wissenschaften, 2025), https://pta.bbaw.de/text/urn:cts:pta:pta0200.pta001.pta-com1.

Recommend Citation of an Individual Entry: Silvio Roggo, “EH 3.1.3.b,” in Silvio Roggo, Nikolai Kiel, Philip Michael Forness, and Hartmut Leppin, Commentary to John of Ephesus's Ecclesiastical History, Part 3, Patristic Text Archive (Berlin: Berlin-Brandenburgische Akademie der Wissenschaften, 2025), https://pta.bbaw.de/text/urn:cts:pta:pta0200.pta001.pta-com1:EH3.1.3.commentary.2.

div.head: EH 3.0.0.a (PMF)
div.p: ܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ ܕܩܕܝܫܐ ܡܪܝ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܕܐܦܣܘܣ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ ܦܠܓܘܬܐ ܕܬܠܬ “The Ecclesiastical History of Holy Mar John, the Bishop of the City of Ephesus: Part 3“: The title we have assigned to the EH does not go back to John of Ephesus but is based primarily on the paratexts. This brief exposition summarizes the evidence for how John himself as well as later editors and scribes referred to the work.

The title given by John of Ephesus to the EH is not known. In the first extant chapter of the EH, John refers to the “stories of the church”: ܬܫ̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ (3.1.3.m) and ܬܫܥ̈ܝܬܐ ܥܕܬܢ̈ܝܬܐ (3.1.3.p). The same phrase is used to designate the EH of Eusebius of Caesarea in a manuscript dated to 462 (Wright and McLean 1898, 3.1). It is conceivable that John of Ephesus referred to his work in the same way. This same chapter of John’s EH also offers an indication of how John described the divisions of his work, referring to the ܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܦܠܓܘ̈ܢ “two parts” and the ܣ̣ܦܪ̈ܐ ܬܪ̈ܥܣܪ “twelve volumes” (3.1.3.m). These terms all recur in the discussion of the title of the work below.

Though John’s own designation for the work remains uncertain, it seems possible to narrow in on a hypothetical title for the work at the time when it reached its current form, that is, in the late sixth or in the seventh century. When extant, the beginning and end of each of the six books of the EH contain initial and final titles, which should be regarded as paratexts. In the following, we will first list the final titles and then turn to the more informative initial titles that precede and that follow the tables of contents.

The final titles have been preserved for Books 1–2 and 4–5 and read as follows: ܫܠ̣ܡ ܣ̣ܦܪܐ ܩܕܡܝܐ ܕܬܫ̣̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “Ended is the first volume of the stories of the church”; ܫܠ̣ܡ ܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܢ ܕܬܫ̣̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܐ̣ܩܠܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ “Ended is the second volume of the stories of the Ecclesiastical History; ܫܠܡ ܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܒܥܐ܆ ܕܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “Ended is the fourth volume of the stories of the church”; ܫܠ̣ܡ ܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܚܡܫܐ܆ ܕܬܫ̣̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ
“Ended is the fifth volume of the stories of the church”. The final titles for Books 1 and 4 parallel each other and include the same phrase found in John’s own description of his work discussed above: ܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “stories of the church.” The final title for Book 2 introduces the Greek loan word ܐܩܠܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ ἐκκλησιατική “Ecclesiastical History,” which occurs elsewhere.

Books 2–3 and 5–6 begin with tables of contents which have titles at the beginning and end. The titles at the beginning of the tables of contents are as follows: ܬܘܒ ܣ̣ܦܪܐ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܢ ܕܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܐܩ̣ܠܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ “Next, the second volume of the stories of the Ecclesiastical History”; ܪ̈ܝܫܐ ܕܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܬܠܬܐ ܕܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “The chapters of the third volume of the stories of the church”; ܪ̈ܝܫܐ ܕܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܚܡܫܐ܆ ܕܬܫ̈ܥܝ̣ܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “The chapters of the fifth volume of the stories of the church”; ܪ̈ܝܫܐ ܕܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܫܬܐ܆ ܕܬܫ̣̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܠ ܩܪ̈ܒܐ “The chapters of the sixth volume of the stories about the wars”. Two remarks are warranted here. First, the Greek loan word ܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ “Ecclesiastical History” surfaces in the title for Book 2, just as in its final title. Second, the content of Book 6 is described as ܬܫ̣̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܠ ܩܪ̈ܒܐ “the stories about the wars” in contrast to ܬܫ̈ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “the stories of the church” found in the titles for Books 3 and 5. While the titles are uniform in their use of the word ܣܦܪܐ “volume” to designate each book, there appears to be sensitivity to the books’ differing contents.

The tables of contents for Books 3 and 5–6, but not for Book 2, contain an additional title directly following the table of contents. As we have already seen with the other initial titles, these titles also clearly distinguish between the contents of Books 3 and 5, on the one hand, and Book 6, on the other: ܣ̣ܦܪܐ ܕܬܠܬܐ ܕܦܠܓܘܬܐ ܕܬܠܬ ܕܐܩ̣ܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ܆ ܕܥܠ ܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ̣ ܘܫܪܟܐ “The third volume of the third part of the Ecclesiastical History: On the stories of the church and other matters”; ܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܕܚܡܫܐ ܕܦܠܓܘܬܐ ܕܬܠܬ ܕܐܩܠ̣ܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ܆ ܕܥܠ ܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ. ܘܫܪܟܐ “The fifth volume of the third part of the Ecclesiastical History: On the stories of the church and other matters”; and ܣ̣ܦܪܐ ܕܫܬܐ܆ ܕܦ̇ܠܓܘܬܐ ܕܬܠܬ ܕܐܩ̣ܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ܆ ܕܥܠ ܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܩܪ̈ܒܐ ܕܒܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܡܬ̇ܪ̈ܫܡܢ “The sixth volume of the third part of the Ecclesiastical History: On the stories of the wars recorded in brief”.

It seems likely to us that the shared phrase ܦܠܓܘܬܐ ܕܬܠܬ ܕܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ “the third part of the Ecclesiastical History” in the titles to Books 3 and 5–6 was a manner of referring to the work as a whole. It features the same word (ܦܠܓܘܬܐ “part”) used by John to indicate the earlier two divisions. It also contains the Greek loan word ܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ “Ecclesiastical History.” The same word designates an excerpt of Part 2 of the EH (Chabot 1933a, 403.11), and it is also the term John’s younger contemporary Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 1.1 Greatrex 2011, 78 Brooks 1921, 5.18–19 uses to refer to the EH of Eusebius as well as those of Socrates and Theodoret. While we remain unsure whether John would have used ܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ to describe the EH, this was a way of designating the text in the century following its creation.

The title found at the beginning of our edition is based on the the running title that appears at the end of one quire and the beginning of the next quire throughout the manuscript: ܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ ܕܩܕܝܫܐ ܡܪܝ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܕܐܦܣܘܣ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ “The Ecclesiastical History of Holy Mar John, the Bishop of the City of Ephesus” (fol. 3v–4r, 23v–24r, 33v–34r, 43v–44r, 53v–54r, 63v–64r, 73v–74v, 83v–84r, 93v–94r, 99v–100r, 109v–110r, 119v–120r, 129v–130r, 139v–140r, 149v–150r, 159v). This title cannot be dated any earlier than the manuscript in which it occurs, and the designation of the author as “Holy Mar John” makes clear that the title cannot be attributed to John of Ephesus himself. To the running title, we have added ܦܠܓܘܬܐ ܕܬܠܬ “Part 3” as an aid to readers and on the basis of the appearance of this word in the titles for the books of EH 3.

div.head: EH 3.1.0.a (PMF)
div.p: ܣ̣ܦܪܐ ܩܕܡܝܐ “Volume 1”: The initial title for Book 1 is not extant, and we have supplied a short version of the title based on the title found at the end of the book (see 3.1.42). In general, short titles for books and chapters have been supplied when they are not extant to facilitate reading. For a discussion of the size of the lacuna at the beginning of the EH, see 3.1.3.a.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: The first two chapters of the first book of EH 3 are lost, as is the start of the third chapter. John of Ephesus’s account, as it has been preserved, therefore begins in medias res with a bleak description in eschatological language of the hardship his religious community, the Severans (anti-Chalcedonians in the tradition of Severus of Antioch), has to endure because of the cruelty of the Chalcedonian suppressors. This is the main topic of EH 3.1.3–3.1.30. By employing biblical quotations, John presents his community (and himself) as standing in the direct tradition of Jesus’s disciples; he parallels the suffering of the Severans to that of the Apostles. In the latter half of the chapter, John explains the structure of the EH, subdivided in parts, books, and chapters. Furthermore, he states that it was the experience of the sudden violence directed against the Severans, after a prolonged period of peaceful cohabitation with the Chalcedonians, which prompted him to add a third part to his EH, after the two earlier parts in which he treated the ecclesiastical events up to 570.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.a (PMF) 3.3
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܬܠܬܐ “Chapter 3”: A brief codicological examination of the primary manuscript (London, British Library, Add. 14640) that transmits EH 3 can provide helpful hints about the size of the lacuna at the beginning of Book 1. All fully extant quires in the manuscript take the form of quinions consisting of five bifolia, which is equal to ten folios, twenty folio sides, or forty columns of text (Brooks 1935, i). The transition from one quire to another is almost always indicated by a running title written in the upper margin across the opening from the last folio of one quire to the first folio of the following quire. Thus, we find the running title on the opening of folios 3v and 4r as follows: ܀.ܐܩܠܝܣܝܣܛܝܩܐ ܕܩܕܝܫܐ ܡܪܝ ܝܘܚܢܢ.܀ “The Ecclesiastical History of holy Mar John” (3v), followed by ܀.ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܕܐܦܣܘܣ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ.܀ “the bishop of the city of Ephesus” (4r). The next such opening appears on folios 13v and 14r, that is, after ten folios. Given the script of the superscript titles, it seems likely that they go back to the original production of the codex, being added either by the copyist or perhaps its owners, as known from Syriac manuscript culture (Borbone, Briquel-Chatonnet, and Balicka-Witakowska 2015, 256). Here they help demarcate the first three folios of the extant manuscript as part of the same quire. It also establishes that folio 3 was the final folio of the first quire, as the text is continuous from folio 3v to folio 4r.

The determination of the place of folios 1 to 2 in the original manuscript proves more complex, and we have arrived at a different conclusion than Brooks. In his brief codicological description of the manuscript, Brooks (1935, ii) notes the inclusion of numerals that number the bifolia within individual quires of the manuscript. Thus, in the second two quires, we find Syriac letters used as numerals on the verso side of the first folio of the quire (ālap̄ = 1 on fol. 4v, 14v), and the recto side of the following four folios (bēṯ = 2 on fol. 5r, 15r; gāmal = 3 on fol. 6r, 16r; dālaṯ = 4 on fol. 7r, 17r; hē = 5 on fol. 8r, 18r). On this basis, Brooks (1935, ii) interprets the gāmal = 3 which is found on folio 2r as indicating the third folio of the first quire. This would imply that one folio is missing at the beginning of the quire. Yet, these numbers need not be original, and indeed another explanation seems more likely.

The original place of folios 1 and 2 in the first quire can rather be determined by two factors. First, the text from folio 1v to folio 2r is continuous, meaning the two folios must have appeared next to one another in the original manuscript. Second, both folio 1v and folio 2r have a lighter color, indicating that they are the flesh side of the manuscript, while folio 1r and folio 2v have a more yellowish tint indicating they are the hair side. As Syriac manuscripts do not follow Gregory’s Rule (Borbone, Briquel-Chatonnet, and Balicka-Witakowska 2015, 256), having the flesh side of one folio face the other with a continuous text would only make sense for the central bifolium. Thus, folios 1 and 2 formed the center of the original first quire.

As noted just above, all fully extant quires in the manuscript take the form of quinions, which is the standard quire size in Syriac manuscripts (Briquel-Chatonnet 1998, 155–160). Assuming this is the case here, the original quire construction would be as follows: 1–4 (lost), 5–6 (extant: fol. 1–2), 7–9 (lost), 10 (extant: fol. 3). This means that four full folios are missing at the beginning of Book 1 of EH 3. The recto side of the first folio would have been left blank as was customary for Syriac manuscripts (Borbone, Briquel-Chatonnet, and Balicka-Witakowska 2015, 256). Each of the books of EH 3 for which the beginning is extant begins with a table of contents, and we can assume that this would have been the same for Book 1. This book contains forty-two chapters, and we can estimate the required folio sides needed for the table of contents based on a comparison to Book 2. The table of contents for Book 2, which has fifty-two chapters, occupies almost five columns or two and a half folio sides (fol. 24v–25v). For book one, we can estimate that the table of contents would have covered four columns or two full folio sides (verso of 1 and recto of 2). This leaves ten columns or five folio sides for the first two chapters and the beginning of the third chapter of Book 1 (from the verso of 2 to the verso of 4). As chapter three already occupies just over four columns (fol. 1r–2r), one could assume that it was no more than one or two columns more. This would leave four to four and a half columns for each of the first two chapters. This represents an above average length for chapters in Book 1. For a discussion of the remaining part of the original first quire, see 3.1.5.i. We are grateful to Françoise Briquel-Chatonnet and Grigory Kessel for their advice regarding the codicological reconstruction of the original first quire.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.b (SR) 3.3
div.p: ܪܘܓܙܐ] ܕܐܠܗܐ ܐܬܬ̣ܥܝܪ] “the anger of God has been awakened”: The first preserved word of the third part of the EH is ܕܐܠܗܐ d-’alāhā “of God.” Schönfelder (1862, 3) convincingly conjectured ܪܘܓܙܐ rugzā “the anger” as the antecedent of this genitive. This matches with the eschatological tone of this chapter; moreover, this phrase is attested in 6th-century Syriac literature, for instance in the Cathedral Homily 68 of Severus of Antioch (Brière 1907, 381.12) and in the Life of Severus attributed to John of Beth Aphthonia (Kugener 1904, 245.5). John of Ephesus himself combined ܪܘܓܙܐ rugzā “the anger” with ܐܬܬ̣ܥܝܪ ʾettʿir “to be awakened,” in EH 3.1.5, and with ܐܥܝܪ ʾaʿir “to awake,” the active form of the same verb, in EH 3.1.38 and 3.2.26.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.c (SR) 3.4
div.p: ܣܕ̈ܩܐ ܘܚ̣ܪ̈ܝܢܐ [‍‌…] ܘܦ̇ܠܓܘ̈ܬܐ “schisms, quarrels, divisions”: These three terms can all be used interchangeably to denote schisms in the church. John very frequently uses a hendiadys or, as in this case, a hendiatris as a figure of speech to convey emphasis (van Ginkel 1995, 99–100 n.40). Another example is ܚܒ̈ܘ̣ܫܝܐ ܘܐܣ̣ܘܪ̈ܐ “imprisonments and bonds” in the following line; these figures of speech will not usually be noted in the commentary.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.d (SR) 3.5
div.p: ܪ̈ܕܘܦܝܐ [‍‌…] ܘܚܒ̈ܘ̣ܫܝܐ ܘܐܣ̣ܘܪ̈ܐ “persecutions […] imprisonments and bonds”: John here refers to the violence directed against his co-religionists, the anti-Chalcedonians (more specifically, the Severans). This violence increased in intensity in 571 after a period of relative peace Wood 2010, 171–172 van Ginkel 1995, 34–35 and 170 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 508–511 Meyendorff 1989, 262–263 Averil Cameron 1976a, 64–65; Frend 1972, 321–323. John himself states that he was also repeatedly imprisoned and even forced to enter into Chalcedonian communion for a short period (EH 3.1.24). This experience was the main reason for why he decided to add a third part, dealing with these recent events, to the EH. The violence directed against the Severans is the core theme of EH 3, Books 1 and 2, and John’s account is clearly apologetic in nature, as he had to defend himself for his short-lived communion with the Chalcedonians, which he may in fact have entered without much pressure being applied (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f and Roggo (2022, 103–107)). John may therefore have been prompted to emphasize the horrors of his suffering by equating the hardship of the Severans to the turmoil and violence that will precede the Last Judgment, and by employing eschatological language in the introductory chapters 3.1.3 and 3.1.4 (see 3.1.3.y). More detailed information about the start of the anti-Severan violence is given at the end of chapter 3.1.4 and in 3.1.5.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.e (SR; HL) 3.7
div.p: ܚܒ̈ܠ̣ܐ ܕܚܪܬܐ “the final birth pangs”: Late antique Christians expected the Last Judgment to be preceded by various calamities and a period of warfare, disorder, and the breakdown of social conventions, in accordance with Jesus’s prediction recorded in Matthew 24:4–51 Meier 2025; Shoemaker 2018; Amirav, Grypeou, and Stroumsa 2017. In line with this expectation,John of Ephesus evokes eschatological imagery by identifying the divisions and violence which he and his fellow clergy suffer as signs of the approaching end of the world and the Second Coming (van Ginkel 2007, 206–208).

However, in ancient ecclesiastical historiography, eschatological and apocalyptic motifs did not play a major role. One exception was Philostorgius (first half of the 5th century), who wrote in Greek (Bleckmann 2008). As a Eunomian, he belonged, like John, to a suppressed group of Christians. Yet no direct relationship between these two authors can be established, nor between John and his contemporary Gregory of Tours, in whose universal history eschatological passages are of great importance (Palmer 2014, 68–78).

The few eschatological passages in the EH present us with a dilemma because on the one hand, John emphasizes that he wants to record the events of his time in writing for those who will come after him, but on the other hand, he doubts that the world will last much longer (see 3.1.3.y). It is therefore disputed among scholars whether John used eschatological passages primarily as a rhetorical tool to dramatize his account and to emphasize the quality and the significance of the Severans’ suffering (van Ginkel 1995, 199–200), or whether eschatology belonged to the core of John’s historical thinking Leppin 2019; Bruns 2006. In favor of the latter position is the fact that John repeatedly referred to eschatological thought in the LES; see, for instance, LES 8; 19 (Brooks 1923, 125–126 and 268); 27; 41 (Brooks 1924, 554–555 and 653); 52 (Brooks 1926, 171).

However, in EH 3, eschatological motifs appear only very occasionally with the exception of this chapter: In EH 3.4.53, John relates the propagation of the Gospel to Nubia to Matthew 24:14 and in this respect gives it an eschatological perspective; in EH 3.6.1, John quotes Matthew 24:33 . It is noticeable that unlike other apocalypses, John never refers to the Book of Daniel; the Book of Revelation was not part of the Syriac canon anyway.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.f (SR) 3.8–9
div.p: ܬܘܒܘ̣ ܩܪܒ̣ܬ݀ ܠܗ̇ ܥܠܝܟܘܢ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ ܕܫܡܝܐ “Repent! The kingdom of heaven has drawn near to you”: John quotes a biblical passage that occurs twice in almost the same words in Matthew 3:2 and 4:17, respectively. However, since John says that he quotes the words preached by Jesus (he calls him in 3.1.3.e “our Savior,” ܦܪܘܩܢ pāroqan), this must refer to Matthew 4:17 , describing the start of Jesus’s preaching at the Lake of Galilee. Matthew 3:2 does not quote Jesus, but John the Baptist. John’s source clearly was a Gospel very close to or identical with the Peshitta. A comparison of John’s quotation to the Peshitta (Kiraz 2004, 1:41) shows that he only omitted the causal particle ܓܝܪ gēr and added ܥܠܝܟܘܢ ʿlaykun “to you” instead. The addition of this preposition further strengthens the eschatological image John created by emphasizing that everyone, including his readership, is about to face the consequences of their actions at the Last Judgment.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.g (SR) 3.10–11
div.p: ܕܡܐ ܠܡ ܕܚ̣ܙܝܬܘܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܕܗ̈ܘ̣ܝܢ܆ ܕܥܘ̣ ܕܡ̣ܛܬ ܠܗ̇ ܠܬܪܥܐ “When you have seen all these things coming to pass, know that it has arrived at the door”: Another quotation with eschatological connotations taken from Matthew 24:33 , the chapter containing Jesus’s teaching about the end times on the Mount of Olives. The only difference to the Peshitta is John’s insertion of the participle ܕܗ̈ܘ̣ܝܢ d-hāwēn (Kiraz 2004, 1:375). Bunce (2023, 69) has argued that John used the insertion of this participle to refer to his own times and that he wanted to place the current hardship of the Severans within the biblical eschatology by stressing that the signs of the end are visible now, in his time, and to his readership. This interpretation of the participle indeed sits well with John’s rhetorical intentions in this chapter. Note that John quotes Matthew 24:33 for a second time, again inserting ܕܗ̈ܘ̣ܝܢ, in the introduction to Book 6 (3.6.1), which is eschatologically equally loaded (van Ginkel 2007, 207–208).

div.head: EH 3.1.3.h (SR) 3.13–14
div.p: ܠܗ̇ܝ ܓܘܕܐ ܪܒܬܐ ܕܫܠܝ̣ܚܘܬܐ “the great company of the apostles”: The phrase ܓܘܕܐ ܕܫܠܝ̣ܚܘܬܐ “company of the apostles” was repeatedly used in Syriac poetry in the 5th and 6th centuries to refer to the apostles, for instance by Jacob of Serugh in several of his homilies (Bedjan and Brock 2006, 5:678.1 and 6:211.16). John’s use of the adjective ܪܒܬܐ“great” suggests that he was not only thinking of the twelve apostles whom Jesus sent out in Matthew 10:5–42 (partly quoted by John immediately afterwards in 3.1.3.i), but of the larger group of the 70 or 72 disciples whom Jesus dispatched to do missionary work according to Luke 10:1–16 .

div.head: EH 3.1.3.i (SR; HL) 3.16–26
div.p: ܐܙܕ̣ܗܪ̈ܘ [‍‌…] ܡܛܠ ܫܡܝ “Beware […] for my name’s sake”: This quotation of Matthew 10:17–22 corresponds neatly to the Peshitta. John employs this passage to illustrate the injustice and the breakdown of society expected for the end times which he also observes in his own time. Interestingly, however, in its biblical context, this passage has no connection to eschatology: in Matthew 10:5–42 , Jesus dispatches his disciples and predicts to them the difficulty and enmity they are going to face when doing their missionary work. But John constructs a parallel between the hardship suffered by Jesus’s disciples on the one hand, and by himself and his Severan co-religionists on the other (Bunce 2023, 68–69). Matthew’s account about the fate of the disciples anticipates John’s own experience which he describes in EH 3.1.3–3.1.30: the Severans were arrested in their churches and monasteries, subjected to irregular court proceedings, and imprisoned on the orders of the emperor and the patriarch, with whom they had previously enjoyed peaceful and even amiable relations.

By linking Matthew 10 closely to Matthew 24 , where Jesus foretells the end of the age, John presumably wants to highlight the permanence of the suffering of God’s people, which is promised by God and thus confirms his power in the powerlessness of the believers. This motif continues in the following chapter with the allusions to prophets of the Old Testament (see 3.1.4.b and 3.1.4.e). It may be significant that John chose to omit the latter half of verse 22 (“but he that endures to the end shall be saved”) from this quotation, thereby removing “any silver lining from his bleak narrative of earthly persecution” (Bunce 2023, 69) (see 3.1.3.k).

div.head: EH 3.1.3.j (SR) 3.26–28
div.p: ܗܠܝܢ ܕܗܐ ܚ̇ܙܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܓܠܝ̣ܐܝܬ܆ ܕܐܦ ܗܫܐ ܥܠ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܒܢ̈ܝ ܥܕܬܐ ܒܙܒܢܐ ܗܢܐ ܡ̇ܛܝ ܘܫ̣ܠܡ܆ “these are the things that we now see clearly, which have come even now in this time to all the members of the church and have been fulfilled”: John here links back to his quotation of Matthew 10:17–22 with its vivid imagery of inner-familial violence. He highlights that in his view, the same unjustified and deplorable cruelty is also happening in his own time in the church, with some of its members, John and his Severan co-religionists, suffering from the religious violence, and others, the Chalcedonians, causing this suffering. This passage shows exemplarily that John still perceived opponents and supporters of Chalcedon as part of the same church and that he did not yet consider the ongoing process of alienation as irreversible; see 3.1.37.c Leppin 2019; Francisco 2011a, 119 van Ginkel 2005, 43–44; 1995, 114–115; 126; 147; 166–167.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.k (PMF; SR) 3.28–4.1
div.p:
ܠܘ ܡܢ ܒ̇ܪ̈ܝܐ ܘܚ̈ܢ̇ܦܐ ܣ̇ܒܠܝܢܢ “we are enduring […] not from outsiders and pagans”: The language of endurance picks up on the omitted phrase from Matthew 10:22
“but he that endures to the end shall be saved,” as discussed in 3.1.3.i. The Syriac word here for “endure,” ܣܒܠ sḇal, differs from that in the Peshitta, ܣܝܒܪ saybar. But the two are used synonymously in Syriac literature. While John seems to replace the biblical phrase with references to sufferings in his own time, the notion that suffering can make one worthy of salvation is not explicitly expressed in the EH.

Foreign powers, such as the Persian Sasanian Empire, and pre-Constantinian pagan emperors (e.g., Decius, Diocletian, and Galerius) were, as archetypical suppressors of the Christians, remembered in late antiquity for their cruelty C. Moss 2013; Lane Fox 2006. However, John emphasizes here that his own experience of the persecution of the Severans, which he describes in the EH, is even worse than those past instances since it is committed by fellow Christians, the Chalcedonians. He repeatedly draws parallels between the suppressors of his time and the pagan rulers of old, for instance in EH 3.1.37.f; 3.2.26; 3.3.12, and he highlights in EH 3.2.18–3.2.20 that a foreign ruler, the Persian king Khosrow I (531–579), was still hostile to Christianity and embarked on a violent persecution (but in another context, EH 3.6.20, Khosrow is depicted as a tolerant ruler).

div.head: EH 3.1.3.l (PMF) 4.4
div.p: ܘܕܠܐ ܪ̈ܚ̣ܡܝܢ […] ܚ̇ܪܡܐܝܬ “without mercy, in a cruel […] manner”: These two expressions, separated by only one word in the Syriac text, feature alliteration: wa-ḏlā raḥmin […] ḥarmāʾiṯ. Sixth-century Syriac poets would sometimes pair them in order to express contrast between the virtue of mercy and the vice of cruelty, as one only has to swap one root letter from “mercy” to “cruelty”: rḥm to ḥrm. See, for example, Narsai of Nisibis, Homily 75 on Solomon (Homilies of Mar Narsai 1970, 2.805.5–8).

div.head: EH 3.1.3.m (SR; PMF) 4.5–9
div.p: ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ ܩܕܡܝܬܐ ܕܒܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܦܠܓܘ̈ܢ [‍‌…] ܣܝܟ̣ܢܢ [‍‌…] ܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ [‍‌…] ܒܣ̣ܦܪ̈ܐ ܬܪ̈ܥܣܪ “in the original composition, we previously, in two parts, […] told the stories of the church […] within twelve volumes”: Before starting with the third part of the EH, in which he dealt with the events of the 570s and 580s, John had written a historiographical work in two parts in the late 560s. John therefore authored an EH with three parts in total, yet the first two parts have not been preserved directly. The first part is almost completely lost, whereas the contents of the second part can be reconstructed to some extent from the Zuqnin Chronicle and the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian, which both used it as a source. John indicates in the present passage that the first two parts consisted of twelve books in total. Given that the third part of the EH is formed of six books, this piece of information can serve as an indication that also the first two parts each comprised six books. The most detailed discussion to date with regard to the scope of the first two parts of the EH and to the sources John used is van Ginkel (1995, 46–68); see also Destephen (2008, 518). Interestingly, Pseudo-Zachariah, a contemporary of John, also completed an EH in Syriac in twelve books in the late 560s (Debié 2015, 529–534). Similar to John, he also indicated at the start that his work would consist of different ܫܪ̈ܒܐ šarbē “accounts” subdivided in ܪ̈ܝܫܐ rišē “chapters”; Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 1.1 (Brooks 1919, 6).

ܡܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ maḵtḇānuṯā translates broadly as “composition” or “description,” sometimes also in the sense of “historiographic work” (Debié 2015, 517 and 522). Some other late antique Syriac historiographers used this term in the titles of their works, for instance Pseudo-Joshua the Stylite ܶin the early 6th century (ܡܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ ܕܬܫܥܝܬܐ ܕܙܒܢܐ ܕܐܘܠܨܢܐ “a historical narrative of the period of distress”) (Trombley and Watt 2001, 1). Moreover, it was also employed in the Syriac translation of Eusebius’s EH (EH 1.1) to refer to the writing of an ecclesiastical history in general (Wright and McLean 1898, 6). For an overview of the different terms used by Syriac chronographers and historians for their works, see Witakowski (1987, 162–169), with a discussion of maḵtḇānuṯā on page 168. Witakowski together with Wolfe (2023, 411–412) and Debié (2015, 40) points out that the term ܡܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ ܕܙܒܢܐ maḵtḇānuṯā ḏ-zaḇnā eventually came to acquire a meaning identical to the Greek χρονογραφία, “chronicle.” However, this new meaning is only attested after the early 8th century (its first occurrence is in the Chronicle of Jacob of Edessa) and the term, when employed in John’s EH, does not yet have the same precision as it would later.

A brief note on the rejection of an emendation of this passage found in Brooks’s edition is warranted. Brooks (1935, 4.6) suggests inserting the phrase “in [which]” (ܒܗ̇) near the beginning of the clause, so that it would read when translated literally: “after the original composition in which in two parts we previously told” (ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ ܩܕܡܝܬܐ ܕܒܗ̇ ܒܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܦܠܓܘ̈ܢ܇ ܡܢ ܩܕܝܡ ܣܝܟ̣ܢܢ). The feminine singular pronoun in the phrase “in [which]” (ܒܗ̇) refers back to “composition,” and the Syriac particle ܕ d marks the beginning of a relative clause. Brooks’s emendation corresponds better to the standards of Syriac grammar than what is found in the manuscript (Skaf 2020, 162–167). Yet the diverse use of the particle ܕ d in the EH and the non-standard features of the text speak against the emendation. It does not seem unreasonable that the earliest recoverable text of the EH included a relative clause where the resumptive pronoun was omitted (cf. Skaf 2020, 173–174).

div.head: EH 3.1.3.n (SR) 4.9
div.p: ܕܡܬ̇ܝܕܥܝܢ ܒܟܠܗܘܢ ܪ̈ܝܫܐ ܡܫ̣̈ܚܠܦܐ “in which all the different chapters are indicated”: The twelve books of the EH were subdivided in ܪ̈ܝܫܐ rišē “chapters,” each chapter containing one ܬܫܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ tašʿiṯā d-ʿē[d]tā “a story of the church,” as John calls them in line 4.8 (van Ginkel 1995, 195) From this description, it appears thus that the lost first two parts of the EH were structured in the same way as the third part.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.o (SR) 4.10
div.p: ܡܢ ܙܒ̈ܢܘܗܝ ܕܝܘܠܝܘܣ ܩܣܪ “from the time of Julius Caesar”: It is somewhat surprising that an ecclesiastical history should have had as its starting point a person who lived before the birth of Jesus. For instance, Eusebius, the model for all late antique ecclesiastical historians, starts his historiographical part with Herod and Augustus, the rulers at the time of the Nativity. As the earliest preserved fragment of John’s EH deals with the time of Constantine (contained in Michael the Syrian’s Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 1:239; 4:121; see van Ginkel (1995, 49)), it is impossible to ascertain how detailed his treatment of the first centuries actually was. Perhaps, John’s first part was little more than a collection of excerpts from Eusebius’s EH (van Ginkel 1995, 49), possibly together with some other documents and dossiers (van Ginkel 1995, 67 n.135). This might then have included a list of the Roman emperors, and Caesar was often seen as the first autokrator in Antiquity (e.g., Clement of Alexandria, Strom. 1.21.144 (Stählin 1906, 89) (note however that Clement gives two emperor lists in this passage, one starting with Caesar and the other with Augustus); Malalas, Chronicle 9.2 (Thurn 2000, 161–162); Evagrius, EH 3.41 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 143.5–6); Chronicon Paschale, 183rd Olympiad (Dindorf 1832, 353)); on this topic, see Schropp (2024).

D’yakonov (1908, 178) hypothesized that John might have taken this list from a source from the region of Antioch since Caesar’s victory over Pompey formed the starting point of the Antiochene era. However, there is no compelling necessity for this, as Caesar was viewed as the first emperor also in the non-Antiochene tradition.

Roberto (2011, 120–123 and 130–131) notes how important the (re-)foundation of the Roman monarchy under Caesar, as it was perceived in late antiquity, might have been from a Christian point of view. Roberto shows that according to the influential Christian chronicler and apologist Julius Africanus (fl. early 3rd century), the incarnation of Christ took place after Rome had come to dominate the world under Augustus, and, importantly, this world dominion could only be achieved when Rome was ruled by one man. In this line of thought, there is thus a connection between Caesar’s establishment of autocracy and the incarnation. It cannot be excluded that John or his sources might have been influenced by these ideas and therefore chose Caesar as starting point.

Be that as it may, there is no reason for supposing that John included an extensive treatment of events of the 1st century BCE in the EH, and his mention of Caesar need not mean more than a brief reference to him as Augustus’s predecessor and founder of the monarchy.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.p (SR) 4.11–12
div.p: ܗܝ̇ܕܝܢ ܒܬܪ ܒܬܪ ܟ̇ܢ̣ܫܢ ܘܣ̣ܕܪܢ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ ܠܬܫ̣ܥ̈ܝܬܐ ܥܕ̈ܬܢܝܬܐ “then sequentially assembled and set in order the stories of the church”: John indicates here that he proceeded in the first two parts of the EH systematically through the history of the church and charted its growth and development from the very beginnings. The ܬܫ̣ܥ̈ܝܬܐ ܥܕ̈ܬܢܝܬܐ “stories of the church,” refer to the single chapters (ܪ̈ܝܫܐ) of the EH, with every chapter containing one “story,” in the most cases probably extracts or summaries of his sources (van Ginkel 1995, 195). John used various different sources, yet in all likelihood, his main ecclesiastical sources for the period up to Anastasius (r. 491–518) were Eusebius and a version of Theodore the Lector (van Ginkel 1995, 46–68).

div.head: EH 3.1.3.q (SR) 4.12–14
div.p: ܥܕܡܐ ܠܫܢܬ ܫ̣ܬ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܗ ܕܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ ܗܢܐ ܐܚܪܝܐ ܒܪ ܚܬܗ ܕܝܘܣܛܝܢܝܢܐ “until the sixth year of the reign of Justin II, the son of Justinian’s sister”: Justin II (r. 565–578; PLRE 3:754–756 (Martindale 1992); on his reign, see Sarris (2011, 177–180 and 227–232); Averil Cameron (1976a)) acceded to the imperial throne on 14 November 565, after the death of his uncle Justinian (r. 527–565; PLRE 2:645–648 (Martindale 1980)). Thus, Justin’s sixth year lasted from 14 November 570 to 13 November 571. Justin was the son of Vigilantia, the only known sibling of Justinian; she was still alive when her son became emperor (PLRE2:1165 and 1315; 3:1376 Martindale 1992; 1980). In this passage, John indicates 570/571 as the end point of the second part of the EH. The main theme of the third part is the violence directed against the Severans, and according to EH 3.1.5, this started on 21 March 571, during Justin’s sixth year. This experience thus served as a caesura that prompted John to abandon the continuation of the second part and begin a new one. The last element known to have been treated in the second part are the discussions between Severans and adherents of John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine (the so-called “tritheists”) in 569/570 (these are alluded to in EH 3.5.3; John’s lost account is paraphrased in Michael the Syrian’s Chronicle 9.30 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:258–260; 4:317–318 and 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:293–294; 4:337–338; see Van Roey (1982, 487–489); Honigmann (1951, 185–186). It appears thus indeed very likely that John stopped updating the second part in 570/571.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.r (SR) 4.14–15
div.p: ܐܦ ܥܠ ܚܦ̣ܝܛܘܬܗ ܘܝܨ̇ܝܦܘܬܗ ܕܝܠܗ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ ܕܡܛܠ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “as for the same emperor Justin’s diligence and concern for the unity of the church”: In the first half of his reign, 565–571, Justin II continued the reconciliatory religious policy of Justinian towards the Severans and attempted to overcome the divide between supporters and opponents of Chalcedon in a series of negotiations with the Severan leaders and bishops. In 567, representatives of the two parties met in Callinicum (modern Raqqa in Syria) and a compromise seemed possible, had it not been for intransigent miaphysite monastics who forced the bishops to withdraw their assent to the edict of union. The best source for the religious talks of this period is Michael the Syrian’s Chronicle 10.1–2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:283–290; 4:331–336, who used John of Ephesus’s lost second part as source Booth forthcoming; Roggo 2022, 91–100 Wood 2010, 170–172 van Ginkel 2005, 42; 1995, 179–180 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 503–511 Meyendorff 1989, 258–262 Van Roey 1985, 156–160 Allen 1981, 22–25 Cameron 1976; Frend 1972, 317–323.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.s (SR) 4.17–18
div.p: ܣ̇ܗܕܝܢܢ ܠܗ̇ ܠܗܕܐ ܐܟܝܦܐܝܬ ܘܚܦܝ̣ܛܐܝܬ܇ ܟܕ ܢܩ̇ܦܝܢܢ ܠܫܪܪܐ ܒܫܪ̈ܒܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܩܕܝܡ ܐ̇ܘ̣ܕܥܢ “we also carefully and diligently testified to this while clinging to the truth in the accounts that we previously related”: John himself participated in the negotiations between Severans and Chalcedonians in the first years of Justin II. After the death of the Severan leader Theodosius of Alexandria in Constantinople in June 566, John assumed a leading role among his co-religionists in the capital; see Michael the Syrian’s Chronicle 9.30 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:257; 4:317; 9.32 Chabot 1899–1910, 2.268; 4.323; 10.13 Chabot 1899–1910, 2.324; 4.356 Debié 2015, 137–138 Destephen 2008, 508–509 van Ginkel 2005, 41–42; 1995, 32–34. We may therefore safely assume that John was well informed about Justin’s religious policy; he then included his view on the events in the second part of the EH, as indicated in 3.1.3.r.

The emphasis on truthfulness is a recurring theme in ancient historiography; John insists in EH 3.1.30; 3.2.18; 3.3.22; 3.4.13; 3.4.46 that he has always been a both accurate and impartial reporter of facts. This claim should evidently not be taken at face value.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.t (SR) 4.19–22
div.p: ܙܩ̣ܦ ܟܝ̣ܡܘܢܐ ܩܫ̣ܝܐ [‍‌…] ܥܠ ܐܠܦ̣ܐ ܚܠܝ̣ܡܬܐ ܕܟܠܗ̇ ܥܕܬܐ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܨܝ ܫܘܒܚܐ “a harsh storm has […] swelled up […] against the firm ship of the whole church of the orthodox”: The metaphor of the church as a ship weathering the raging seas is a very old image and has connections to the account in Matthew 8:23–27 and Matthew 14:24–33, describing how Jesus twice calmed storms when he was sailing on the Lake of Galilee. The metaphor was employed by various church fathers (starting around 200 CE with Tertullian, De baptismo 12.7 (Evans 1964, 28)) and hence presumably well known to John; for its use in the ancient church, including a treatment of its sources in Greek philosophy, see Rahner (1964, 272–319); cf. also Heindl (2015, 85–90); Minear (2004, 33).

John’s use of the adjective ܚܠܝ̣ܡܬܐ ḥlimtā “safe” to describe the ship and therefore also the Severan community indicates that it remained unharmed despite the attacks by the Chalcedonians. This is in line with John’s assertion in EH 3.1.29 and 3.2.1 that all the imprisoned bishops ultimately resisted the attempts to bring them into communion.

ܬܪ̈ܝܨܝ ܫܘܒܚܐ triṣay šuḇḥā, lit. “the upright in glory” is an often-used term for “the orthodox” (hence, in John’s view, the Severan anti-Chalcedonians) and very occasionally employed in EH 3 as a synonym for the Greek loanword ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ (ὀρθόδοξοι); it also occurs in 3.1.5.e and 3.2.25.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.u (PMF; SR) 4.23–24
div.p: ܕܠܘܬ ܪܘܫܡ̣ܐ ܕܥܘܗ̈ܕܢܐ܇ ܐܦܢ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܕܡܢܗܝܢ ܢܝ̇ܬܐ “to commit the records, even if only a few, to writing”: The collocation ܠܘܬ ܪܘܫܡ̣ܐ ܕܥܘܗ̈ܕܢܐ ܢܝ̇ܬܐ (“to commit the records to writing”) appears to be typically Johannine. To this point, we have not been able to find other authors who use the same phrase. For further examples in John’s writings, see LES 43 and 47 (Brooks 1924, 458.45 and 479.10).

John wrote the chapters until EH 3.1.30, describing the outbreak of the persecution in 571, when the suppression was still ongoing, before patriarch John Scholasticus’s death in 577 (on him, see 3.1.5.d), the instigator of the persecution in John’s eyes. This first part of the EH may even have been written within a few years after the start of the anti-Chalcedonian hardship Roggo 2022, 103–104 van Ginkel 1995, 73; see 3.2.2.d.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.v (SR) 4.24–27
div.p: ܐܦܢ ܗ̣ܘ ܙܒܢܐ ܡܢ ܟܠ ܓ̇ܒ̈ܝܢ ܬܟ̇ܣ ܠܢ ܘܡ̇ܥܟܪ ܒܣܓ̈ܝܐܬܐ܇ ܘܟ̇ܠܐ ܕܐܝܟ ܕܡ̣ܨܝܐ̣ ܐܦܢ ܒܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܢܬ̇ܚܫܚ ܒܬ̇ܘܢܝܗܝܢ ܕܬܫ̈ܥ̣ܝܬܐ ܥܕ̈ܬܢܝܬܐ܇ ܕܐܦ ܗܫܐ ܬܢ̣ܝܢܘܬ ܡܣܬ̇ܥܪ̈ܢ “though time on all sides would hold us back and impede us from great length and prevent us from relating insofar as possible even briefly the narrative of the ecclesiastical stories, which even now are taking place once again”: The same motif as in 3.1.3.u; John insists that he does not mention everything he could. He gives a lack of time as the reason for why he has to keep his account short; this links back to the eschatological tone he employed at the start of this chapter: time is running out and the end of the world is near; see also 3.1.3.y.

The sense of ܬܢ̣ܝܢܘܬ “once again” is somewhat obscure. Brooks (1936, 2 n.3) suggested that it might refer to a second outbreak of the religious violence directed against the Severans, yet this fails to convince. It probably means that John is now treating ecclesiastical events again, in a new work, namely the third part of the EH, after ܡܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ ܩܕܡܝܬܐ “the original composition,” as he referred to the first two parts of the EH above in 3.1.3.m.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.x (SR) 4.29
div.p: ܒܡ̇ܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ ܗܕܐ ܬܠܝܬܝܬܐ “through this third composition”: The third part of the EH, after his previous work that was divided in two ܦܠܓܘ̈ܢ “parts,” as John put it in 3.1.3.m.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.y (SR; NK) 4.30–5.4
div.p: ܐܦܢ ܠܐ ܛܥ̣ܬܢܝ ܕܙܒ̈ܢܝ ܥܠܡܐ ܐܘܦ̣ܝܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܡܢ ܟܕܘ ܘܛܠܩ̣ܘ [‍‌…] ܐܦܢ ܒܗ ܒܙܒܢܐ ܙܥܘܪܐ܇ ܕܫ̇ܪܟ ܠܗ ܠܥܠܡܐ ܗܢܐ ܡܕܘܕܐ ܗܕܐ ܗܘ̣ܬ “even if it has not escaped me that the times of the world have already been drawing to a close and passing away. Even if this should only happen in the short time that remains for this turbulent world”: John again employs eschatological language and resumes the image created at the start of this chapter that the end of the world and the Last Judgment are near (see 3.1.3.d–3.1.3.i). However, John nevertheless saw fit to write a very detailed and lengthy (despite his repeated claims to the opposite) account of the ecclesiastical events in which he was involved in the 570s and 580s. It is thus not clear whether he really assumed that the end was close. He may also have wanted to achieve a strong rhetorical effect on his readership and, by describing in eschatological terms the hardship he had to endure, induce them to lend credence to his apology and thus to forgive him his short-term lapse into Chalcedonian communion (EH 3.1.24–3.1.25) (see 3.1.3.e).

The last two letters of the verb ܛܥ̣ܬ are illegible in the manuscript. While Brooks (1935, 4.30) only added a yuḏ (ܛܥ̣ܬܝ̣), Cureton (1853, 3.15) suggested the addition of two letters (ܛܥ̣ܬܢܝ) . Indeed, the letter nun can still be discerned in the manuscript, so Cureton’s reconstruction can be accepted. In the case of two other words, the last letters have disappeared and need to be reconstructed: In the verb ܫ̇ܪܟ, the last letter is no longer legible, and similarly, in the word ܡܕܘܕܐ, the last two letters cannot be read. While Cureton (1853, 3.18–19) left these two gaps unresolved, Brooks (1935, 5.4) convincingly reconstructed the missing letters.

div.head: EH 3.1.3.z (NK) 5.5–6
div.p: ܒܝܕ ܥܘܗ̈ܕܢܐ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܐ ܪܫܡ̣ܢ ܚܢܢ܇ ܐܝܟ ܕܠܐܝܕܥܬܐ ܕܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܒܬܪܟܢ “we have committed them to writing in brief records for the knowledge of posterity”: Brooks (1935, 5.5) added the particle ܕ to the verb ܪܫܡ̣ܢ “we have committed” in order to mark the beginning of a relative clause, which would result in a reading of “in the brief records which we have committed to writing.” Our understanding of the text does not require a relative here, as it is assumed that John is referring to the ecclesiastical histories mentioned above and has simply not included an object pronoun here. Thus, we have not followed Brooks’s emendation.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter supplements the preceding chapter 3.1.3, where John rhetorically likens the plight of the Severans to that of Jesus’s disciples, thereby repeatedly quoting the New Testament. Here, however, the focus lies on the Old Testament. John constructs a parallel between the violence directed against the Severan clerics in his own time and the affliction of the Jews after the capture of Jerusalem by the Babylonians and the end of Jewish independence in the 6th century BCE. He introduces the Book of Lamentations and quotes the Book of Isaiah twice, highlighting the wrath of God as the reason for why their religious suppression has started. This complements his reasoning in EH 3.1.3, where he explains the hardship of the Severans as a sign of the approaching end of times.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chron 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.6–16; 4:336.c.15–27: summary of parts of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.4.b

div.head: EH 3.1.4.a (PMF) 5.7
div.p: ܡܢ ܟܬܒ̈ܝܗܘܢ ܕܢܒ̈ܝܐ “from the books of the prophets”: The word “from” (ܡܢ) is regularly used in manuscripts and florilegia to indicate that selections or extracts of a particular work follow. See, for example, the titles for the first few works found in a Syriac florilegium written sometime before 562 (Rucker 1933, 2–3 (no. 1–2, 4–5)). The chapter title thus gives the impression that a string of biblical quotations are going to follow. In line with this, the chapter itself refers to Jeremiah and includes two quotations from Isaiah.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.b (SR) 5.10–11
div.p: ܩ̈ܠܐ ܚ̈ܢ̣ܝܓܐ ܕܐܘ̈ܠܝܬܗ ܕܐܪܡܝܐ ܢܒܝܐ ܚ̇ܫܝܫܐ “the groans of the suffering prophet Jeremiah’s Lamentations”: ܐܘ̈ܠܝܬܐ ܕܐܪܡܝܐ “the lamentations of Jeremiah” is the Syriac title of the biblical Book of Lamentations, the authorship of which was traditionally attributed to Jeremiah. The modern scholarly consensus however holds that Jeremiah is very unlikely to be the author of Lamentations and that its five poems may have been written by different authors. Its main topic is mourning because of the destruction of Jerusalem by the Babylonians in 587/586 BCE Berlin 2004; Hillers 1992.

John’s introduction of Lamentations in this passage is due to his interest to present the fate of the suffering Severan community as similar to that of the exiled Jews: having been deprived of the peaceful tranquility he and his co-religionists had enjoyed so far in Constantinople, they are reduced to lamenting and repenting their sins, just as in the passages of the Old Testament which bewail the fall of Jerusalem and the destruction of the temple.

The start of this chapter with the lamentations attributed to Jeremiah is referred to in the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.6–16; 4:336.c.15–27. Michael used the third part of the EH extensively as a source, and he is also an important witness for the content of its lost chapters (van Ginkel 1995, 79–81 and 237–240). However, the passage in question here demonstrates that we need to be cautious when using Michael to close gaps in the EH: Michael claims to be quoting the start of the EH, but what then follows is not taken from the very beginning, but from this chapter. Nor is it, despite the phrasing ܝܘܚܢܢ ܕܐܣܝܐ ܐܡܪ “John of Asia says,” a real quotation of the EH. It is rather a summary of John’s statement that the fall of Jerusalem in the 6th century BCE and the violence directed against the Severans in the time of Justin II are equally worthy of lamentation. Michael excerpted the EH and greatly reduced it in size, thereby omitting material he was not interested in, simplifying John’s complex sentences, and sometimes rearranging the order of chapters. Thus, Michael was by no means a simple copyist or excerpter, and this explains why some phrases in his chronicle are copied practically verbatim from the EH, while other chapters appear to have been shortened to such a degree that their original content has become distorted Weltecke 2021, 134–136 van Ginkel 1998; 1995, 79–81.

Conspicuously, Michael introduces Justin in this passage as ܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ ܕܬܠܬܐ “Justin the Third.” The reason for this is that Michael appears not to have distinguished between the names Justin and Justinian and to have treated them as two forms of the same name. This is particularly evident at the very start of Book 10 of his Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 2:282.7–8; 4:331.b.15–17, where Michael mentions both Justinian and Justin II in the same sentence: “Justinian (ܝܘܣܛܝܢܝܢܘܣ) having died, his sister’s son reigned, whose name was also Justin (ܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ).” Consequently, Justin II was, for Michael, the third one with this name after Justin I and Justinian.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.d (SR) 5.14–16
div.p: ܥܠ ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕ[…] ܓ̣ܕܫ ܒܙܒܢܐ ܗܢܐ ܒܥܕܬܗ ܕܐܠܗܐ “regarding those things which have happened at this time in the church of God”: A reference to the hardship and the repeated imprisonment of the Severan clerics which started in Justin’s sixth year; see 3.1.4.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.e (SR; PMF) 5.16–20
div.p: ܐܝܟܢܐ ܬܘܒ ܕܐܦ ܡ̇ܫܒܚ̣ ܒ̣ܢܒ̈ܝܐ ܐܫܥܝܐ܇ ܘܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ ܢܩ̣ܥܐ ܗܘܐ ܒܓܓܪܬܗ ܘܠܐ ܢܚ̣ܘܣ܇ ܘܢܪ̣ܝܡ ܩܠܗ ܐܝܟ ܫܝ̣ܦܘܪܐ܇ ܟܕ ܚ̇ܙܐ ܡ̇ܪܕܘܬܐ ܗܕܐ ܩܫ̣ܝܬܐ ܕܐܕܪܟ̣ܬ̇ܗ̇ ܠܥܕܬܗ ܕܐܠܗܐ. ܟܕ ܐܦ ܬܘܒ ܢܘ̇ܣܦ ܗܘܐ ܘܢܬ̣ܢܐ “so also would Isaiah, illustrious among the prophets, shout at the top of his lungs without holding back and raise his voice like a trumpet upon seeing the harsh chastisement that has come upon the church of God. He would also add and recount”: ܐܫܥܝܐ “Isaiah” (fl. late 8th century BCE) was one of the major prophets of the Old Testament, traditionally assumed to be the author of the biblical Book of Isaiah (Blenkinsopp 2000, 83–92). The Book of Isaiah was one of the most influential texts of the Old Testament for early Christianity because it was understood to contain prophecies about the coming of the Messiah, identified as Jesus. Its popularity was such that it came to be called “the Fifth Gospel” (Sawyer 1996) and it is hence not at all surprising that John quotes two passages from it in 3.1.4.g. and 3.1.4.h. Other examples of 6th-century Syriac historiography where Isaiah is quoted or alluded to are the Chronicle of Pseudo-Joshua the Stylite (Wolfe 2023, 415–421) and the EH of Pseudo-Zachariah (for instance 12.7j Greatrex 2011, 446 Brooks 1921, 214). For the use of the Bible in Pseudo-Zachariah, see (Forness forthcoming).

As explained in 3.1.4.b, John likens the hardship of the Severans of his own time to that of the people of Judah in the 8th–6th centuries BCE oppressed by the Assyrians and Babylonians. Here, he says explicitly that the passages from the Book of Isaiah he is about to quote might refer just as well to his own time and experience as to their original context, as there is, so he claims, no discernible difference between God’s punishment of the Israelites and that of the anti-Chalcedonians.

ܡ̇ܪܕܘܬܐ “chastisement”: This word shares the same root (ܪܕܐ r-d-ʾ) as ܠܡܬܪܕܝܘ “to be chastised” in Isaiah 1:5
(Brock 1987, 1), which is the final word in Isaiah before the quoted portion of Isaiah 1:5–6 that follows in the EH. The word ܡ̇ܪܕܘܬܐ “chastisement” in the EH seems to anticipate the quotation by drawing on the language of the surrounding passage.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.g (SR; NK) 5.20–24
div.p: ܟܠ ܠܡ ܪܝܫ̣ ܠܟܐ̣ܒܐ […] ܘܐܦ ܠܐ ܠܡ̇ܪܟܟܘ ܒܡܫ̣ܚܐ “the whole head in pain […], nor soothed with oil”: This quotation of Isaiah 1:5–6 corresponds neatly to the Peshitta (Brock 1987, 1). The opening chapter of the Book of Isaiah describes Israel as a rebellious nation that is disobedient to God and has therefore been severely punished; the people of Israel are compared to someone who has been beaten up and is covered with wounds and injuries (Blenkinsopp 2000, 183). By choosing this and the following passage, John of Ephesus implies that the Severans have also been sinful and are now receiving the blows they deserve under the form of persecution by the Chalcedonians.

The manuscript contains a marginal correction from ܠܡ̇ܪܟܘ to ܠܡ̇ܪܟܟܘ (here translated as “soothed”) made by a later scribe. He explicitly added the imperative ܚ̣ܙܝ (“see”) together with the corrected spelling ܠܡ̇ܪܟܟܘ.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.h (SR) 5.25–27
div.p:
ܐܠ̣ܬ݀ ܘܝܬܒ̣ܬ݀ ܒܐܒ̣ܠܐ ܐܪܥܐ […] ܘܐܪܥܐ ܐܬܕܡ̣ܝܬ݀ ܠܥܡ̇ܘܪ̈ܝܗ̇ “the earth lamented, it dwelt in mourning […] the earth has become like its inhabitants”: This is an exact quotation of Isaiah 24:4–5
(Brock 1987, 39). Chapter 24 of the Book of Isaiah contains a curse of God on the earth and its inhabitants as a result of their continued disobedience, followed by a description of how mankind will be severely affected by this and reduced to lamentation (Blenkinsopp 2000, 350–352).

div.head: EH 3.1.4.i (SR) 5.28–6.1
div.p: ܥܠ ܪܘܓܙܐ ܪܒܐ ܗܢܐ ܕܡܢ ܥ̣ܠܬ݀ ܚܛ̈ܗܝܢ܇ ܡܢ ܓܘ ܫܠ̣ܝܐ ܥܠ ܥܕܬܗ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪ܇ “regarding the great anger which on account of our sins has been sent upon the church of God all of a sudden”: Again, John presents the religious violence under Justin II, which he is about to describe, as a result of divine punishment. Both in this passage and below in 3.1.4.k, John highlights that the Severans enjoyed peace when the measures directed against them suddenly started. Indeed, in the first years of his reign, Justin attempted to restore the union between defenders and opponents of Chalcedon mainly peacefully through negotiations; see the literature in 3.1.3.r.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.j (SR) 6.3
div.p: ܟܠܗ ܥܡܐ ܕܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ “all the faithful people”: The ܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ mhaymānē “the faithful” are John’s co-religionists, the Severan anti-Chalcedonians. On John’s ways to refer to his own religious group, see 3.1.3.t.

div.head: EH 3.1.4.k (SR) 6.4–7
div.p: ܒܫܠ̣ܡܐ ܘܒܫܠ̣ܝܐ ܫ̣ܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܘܡܬ̇ܒܣܡܝܢ܇ ܗܐ ܡܢ ܙܒ̈ܢܐ ܡ̈ܬܝܚܐ ܒܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ ܘܒܟܠܗܘܢ ܚ̇ܕܪ̈ܝܗ̇܇ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܫܢܬܐ ܫܬܝܬܝܬܐ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܗ ܕܙܟ̇ܝܐ ܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ܇ “who had been living in and enjoying peace and tranquility for an extended period of time in the imperial city and in all its suburbs until the sixth year of the reign of the victorious Justin”: John repeats his assertion of 3.1.4.i that the Severans had been enjoying peace when the religious violence erupted in Justin’s sixth year (14 November 570–13 November 571). He gives more information about this period of religious peace in 3.1.5.a. However, this peace seems to have been limited mainly to the region of Constantinople (in the EH almost always called ܡܕܝܢܬ ܡܠܟܘܬܐ mḏinat malkuṯā “the imperial city”), whereas those opposed to Chalcedon may have been subject to occasional pressure in other regions, especially in the eastern provinces, already in the first six years of Justin’s reign (Averil Cameron 1976a, 64). John’s focus throughout the EH lies, apart from Book 6 and a few other, shorter exceptions, on the region of Constantinople Leppin 2019, 122–124 van Ginkel 1995, 175, as is already made clear in this passage: John is about to narrate the hardship of those Severans, including himself, who had so far been allowed to live at peace in the capital.

As for the ܚ̇ܕܪ̈ܐ “suburbs” of Constantinople mentioned in this passage, there is no exact definition of how far they reached. In the mid-6th century, Constantinople was a huge town, with over 300,000, and perhaps as many as 500,000, inhabitants and even more in the surroundings Croke 2005, 67 Dagron 1974, 525. John possibly refers here to the quite densely populated surroundings of the capital, for instance to places such as Hebdomon and Rhegion (located about 11 and 20 km to the west of the city center on the Via Egnatia, the main road to the west). However, it is also imaginable that John understood as suburbs those parts of Constantinople itself that were located outside the city walls, namely the regions XIII (Sycae on the other side of the Golden Horn, where anti-Chalcedonian monasteries were located; see 3.1.10.b) and XIV (unknown location, see the discussion in Matthews (2012, 110–112)). An argument in favor of the latter hypothesis is that the urban prefect of Constantinople is mentioned as the main executor of the seizure of the anti-Chalcedonian monasteries in EH 3.1.10, and the jurisdiction of this official was limited to the city itself (Dagron 1974, 229–230).

By calling Justin II ܙܟ̇ܝܐ (“victorious”), John of Ephesus took over an element of the official imperial title. For instance, in Justin’s edict on faith of 571, preserved in Evagrius, EH 5.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 197.32), the emperor is, next to other honorific titles, also called νικητής (“victorious”) (on the edict, see EH 3.1.19.g). Consequently, John’s use of this epithet follows the standard rules of his time and does not mean that he considered Justin to be particularly victorious (Arcuri 2008, 600–602).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: After lamenting the suppression of the Severans in the two preceding chapters by employing eschatological language and by explaining it with the wrath of God, John begins in this chapter to describe the start of the hardship in more detail. He highlights the suddenness of the measures directed against them after a prolonged period of peace. The Severans, so he asserts, had enjoyed religious freedom for more than 40 years in Constantinople, until patriarch John III Scholasticus convinced the emperor Justin II to suppress them, a point repeated in EH 3.1.11. Starting on 21 March 571, the Severan churches and monasteries were closed and defiled, the bishops and priests imprisoned. The end of the chapter is lost due to a lacuna in the sole manuscript, but it may have been excerpted and its content thus preserved in the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian. If this is correct, then John probably ended the chapter by listing the different types of confinement where the Severan clerics were held.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.16–26; 4:336.c.27–41: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.5.a

div.head: EH 3.1.5.a (SR) 6.14–16
div.p: ܟܕ ܓܝܪ ܙܒ̈ܢܐ ܡ̣̈ܬܝܚܐ ܕܝܬܝܪ ܡܢ ܐܪ̈ܒܥܝܢ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ܆ […] ܒܡܕܝܢܬܐ ܗܕܐ ܪܒܬܐ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ “For an extended period of time of more than forty years […] in this great imperial city”: John resumes the statement he made in EH 3.1.4.k that he and other Severan clerical leaders had been enjoying peace and the freedom to practice their cult in Constantinople and its surroundings before they were suddenly targeted. The “more than forty years” of religious peace John introduces in this passage (thus covering the period from c. 530–571) cannot refer to the Empire as a whole. It is true that there is much to suggest that parts of the reign of Justinian and the first years of Justin II were quite peaceful for the anti-Chalcedonians in the tradition of Severus of Antioch and Theodosius of Alexandria since both emperors tried to overcome the looming inner-ecclesiastical divide mainly by means of negotiations and theological discussions Roggo 2022, 81–100 Hasse-Ungeheuer 2016, 40–49 Leppin 2011, 181–190 Wood 2010, 168–172 Menze 2008b, 58–66 and 187–190 Greatrex 2007, 289–290 van Rompay 2005; Uthemann 1999; van Ginkel 1995, 177–180 Meyendorff 1989, 223–246 and 258–262 Allen 1981, 22–25 Averil Cameron 1976a; Frend 1972, 261–276 and 317–323. However, there was at least one instance of severe persecution in the eastern provinces of the Empire after the failure of the religious talks with the miaphysite champions Severus of Antioch and Theodosius of Alexandria in 536, organized by patriarch Ephrem of Antioch and bishop Abraham of Amida Menze 2008b, 110–111 and 231–245 Greatrex 2007, 290–292 van Ginkel 2005, 39. Furthermore, Justinian forcefully installed Chalcedonian patriarchs in Alexandria after 536, and religious violence flared up on a local level in Egypt throughout the reigns of Justinian and Justin II (Booth 2017, 154–160).

All of this demonstrates that a period of peace which, as John claims, lasted for over forty years (the same number is given in the short summary of this chapter preserved in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.16–26; 4:336.c.27–41), can, if at all, only be assumed for the region of Constantinople. John’s strong focus on the imperial capital in the EH has already been noted Leppin 2019, 122–124 van Ginkel 1995, 175. From the early 530s onwards, Justinian and Theodora assembled anti-Chalcedonian monks and clerics in the capital, eventually including important leaders such as the bishops Theodosius of Alexandria and John of Ephesus himself. The majority of them were not allowed to leave the city, but they lived in their own monastic communities and discussed their faith with the emperor on several occasions, as is stated by John himself in LES 47 (Brooks 1924, 474–482) and in his accounts preserved in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.21 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:192-193; 4:278 and in the Zuqnin Chronicle Chabot 1933a, 128–129 and 136–141 Witakowski 1996, 115–116 and 121–125; see Hasse-Ungeheuer 2016, 261–296 Leppin 2011, 290–292 Menze 2008b, 217–224 and 267–269 Hatlie 2007, 142–145 Croke 2006, 31–46 van Ginkel 2005, 39; 1995, 177–178.

In all likelihood, John is referring to this peaceful cohabitation in Constantinople and to his own experience when he speaks of an extended period of quiet starting in c. 530: the anti-miaphysite measures that had been enforced in the east during the reign of Justin I were temporarily relented in the early years of Justinian and, according to LES 35 (Brooks 1924, 416–418), John and his monastic community were able to return to their monastery in Amida after an exile of several years Destephen 2008, 497 van Ginkel 2005, 38–39; 1995, 28. John was well informed about the religious violence in the east in the 520s and 530s, thus at least partly during this period of “religious peace,” and had already written a short report on it as early as 540 van Ginkel 2005, 38; 1995, 87–88 D’yakonov 1908, 167–168. This piece is lost, but John probably used it as a source for the second part of his EH; elements of it are preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle Chabot 1933a, 21–44 Witakowski 1996, 22–41 and in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.14–15 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:173-178; 4:267-270.

div.head: EH 3.1.5.b (HL) 6.17
div.p: ܦܪܗܣܝܐ “freedom of assembly”: A loanword from the Greek παρρησία that is attested in the earliest Syriac literary sources (Butts 2016a, 82). This term has two basic meanings in the EH, where it is frequently employed, and they seem to converge in this passage: (1) the courage to speak and (2) the right of a particular confession to gather publicly (Leppin 2022, 53–54 and 139–143).

div.head: EH 3.1.5.c (HL) 6.20
div.p: ܟܠܗܘܢ ܪ̈ܐܙܐ ܘܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܐ ܥܕܬ̈ܢܝܐ “all the ecclesiastical mysteries and liturgies”: The pairing of the Syriac words ܪ̈ܐܙܐ “mysteries” and ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܐ “liturgies” seems to be quite rare, and we did not find any parallels. The latter term, ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܐ, regularly appears with ܩ̈ܢܘܢܐ, meaning “ordinances and canons”; see EH 3.1.18.k; 3.2.32; 3.4.16; 3.5.2. For the importance of this pairing for John of Ephesus, see 3.1.18.k. ܪܐܙܐ, on the other hand, means “secret / sacrament / Eucharist” and thus refers to the part of the liturgical service which only the faithful could attend cf. Alencherry 2006; Luke 1983. This makes the unusual expression all the more remarkable. It seems possible that John of Ephesus is deliberately creating a contrast to parrhesia (freedom of assembly) mentioned in 3.1.5.b. John may have wanted to emphasize that despite the open worship that was allowed, the mystery of the Eucharist and, implicitly, the regular order of his church were preserved.

div.head: EH 3.1.5.d (SR) 6.20–22
div.p: ܒܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܩ̈ܕܝܫܐ ܕܨܘܡܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܒܥܝܢ܆ ܒܫܒܬܐ ܕܢܓ̇ܗ ܚܕ ܒܫܒܐ ܕܐܘ̈ܫܥܢܐ܆ “during the holy days of the fast of forty days, on Saturday when the Sunday of Hosannas was beginning to dawn”: John provides an exact date for when the hardship of the Severan clerics started. Together with his earlier chronological indication in EH 3.1.4.k that the violence erupted in Justin’s sixth year (14 November 570–13 November 571), this allows for an exact establishment of the day in question: the Easter tables show that Easter Sunday was celebrated on 29 March in 571 (Grotefend 2007, 215). Consequently, the Saturday before the Sunday of Hosannas (usually called Palm Sunday, the Sunday before Easter), fell on 21 March. According to John, the measures directed against himself and his co-religionists began thus on 21 March 571; some scholars erroneously indicated Palm Sunday itself, 22 March, as the starting date Maraval 1998, 466 Van Roey 1982, 491 Honigmann 1951, 199 n.2, but John states very clearly that it was on the day before Palm Sunday.

In this passage, John clearly took the Matthew 28:1 as an example, as was already noted by Schönfelder (1862, 6): exactly the same phrase ܒܫܒܬܐ ܕܢܓ̇ܗ ܚܕ ܒܫܒܐ “on Saturday when Sunday was beginning to dawn” is used in the story of the Gospel narrating the visit of Mary and Mary Magdalene to Jesus’s tomb, one week after Palm Sunday, which they were going to find empty (Kiraz 2004, 1:449). John’s evident interest to align the fate of the Severans with the suffering of Jesus does not necessarily mean that his indication is wrong. Yet we should not put too much weight on the accuracy of the exact date given here. Bunce (2023) has demonstrated that parts of Book 1 of EH 3 are modeled according to Scripture and are thus probably rather a reflection of John’s eagerness to draw a parallel between the hardship of his own community and the tribulations undergone by the early Christians, as described for instance in the Acts of the Apostles. He has not considered the passage in question here, but argued convincingly that John’s description of the single elements of the religious violence and the order in which they are presented in Book 1 of the EH may in part be fictional (see also 3.1.9.b; 3.1.16.d; 3.2.1.b). On the reliability of the date given here, see also 3.1.18.n; 3.1.23.a; 3.2.7.g.

div.head: EH 3.1.5.e (SR) 6.22–25
div.p: ܡܢ ܚܦܝ̣ܛܘܬܗ ܘܥ̇ܙܝܙܘܬ ܒܝܫ̣ܘܬܗ ܕܗ̇ܘ ܕܐ̇ܚ̣ܝܕ ܗܘܐ ܥܕܬܐ ܕܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ܆ ܗ̇ܢܘ ܕܝܢ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܡܢ ܣ̣ܪܡܝܢ ܩܪܝܬܐ ܕܣܘܪܝܐ: ܘܡܐ̣ܟܠ ܩܪ̈ܨܘܗܝ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ ܕܥܠ ܟܠܗ ܓܒܐ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܨܝ ܫܘܒܚܐ “at the insistence and wicked vehemence of the one who was in charge of the church in the imperial city, namely, John from the village of Sirmin in Syria, who repeatedly slandered the whole side of the orthodox”: Throughout Book 1 and 2 of EH 3, John of Ephesus holds the patriarch of Constantinople, John III Scholasticus (sed. 565–577), mainly responsible for the oppression of the Severans, thus taking the blame from the emperor Justin II. According to John’s portrayal, Justin appears to have embarked on a relentless persecution in March 571 only because the patriarch persuaded and incited him to do so. The reason for this attribution of blame is that John wrote Books 1 and 2 largely before 578, when Justin was still alive. Only in Book 3, written after Justin’s death, does John ascribe more responsibility to the late emperor (van Ginkel 1995, 107–108). To write critically about the ruling emperor could prove very dangerous throughout Antiquity, and Justin II was no exception to this rule. EH 3.2.2 provides a fine example of this; one of the imprisoned Severan bishops, Paul of Antioch, was found writing a piece in which he criticized Justin’s ecclesiastical policy and came into grave danger. The presentation of patriarch John Scholasticus as the archvillain in the first two books of the EH should thus be taken with a grain of salt. Yet this is not to say that he was innocent. Roggo (2022, 106–113) has argued that John Scholasticus indeed played a crucial role; he was forced to give in to the demands of unrelenting Chalcedonians opposed to Justin’s edict on faith (see EH 3.1.19) and therefore decided to reordain the Severan clerics who had entered into Chalcedonian communion (see EH 3.1.12–3.1.18). This, he contends, ultimately led to their withdrawal from the union and Justin’s order to imprison them.

John Scholasticus was, in the first years of his patriarchate, involved in the attempts to find a definition of faith acceptable to both defenders and opponents of Chalcedon. It was thus by no means the case that he was an irreconcilable enemy of the anti-Chalcedonians, even though this is how John depicted him in the third part of the EH. On the contrary, the short description of one of his theological treatises (none of which have been preserved) in Chapter 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 400) of the Chronicle of John of Nikiu suggests that the patriarch professed a neo-Chalcedonian Christology similar to that outlined in Justin’s edicts on faith in 567 and 571 (Roggo 2022, 92 and 96). Yet the memory of the more irenic first years of his patriarchate was eclipsed by the religious violence starting in 571: when his name occurs for the first time in the manuscript of the EH, on fol. 2v of BL Add. 14640 (Brooks 1935, 6.24), there is found in the margin written by a different hand ܘܝ ܠܟ wāy lāḵ, “woe to you.” Moreover, he is, without further details, praised as a persecutor of the miaphysites in the dyophysite Chronicle of Seert 34 (Scher 1911, 190).

John Scholasticus hailed from Sirmin (also called “Seremis,” Σηρήμις / Σερέμις), a settlement in north-western Syria belonging to the territory of the city of Antioch Todt and Vest 2014, 1722–1725 Honigmann 1939, 617. His place of origin is attested not only by John of Ephesus, but also in Evagrius, EH 4.38 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 189.33), and in the Life of Symeon the Younger 202.3 (van den Ven 1962, 176). On John Scholasticus, see Roggo (2022); Roggo (2019, 441–446); PLRE 3:671–672 (Martindale 1992); Allen (1980, 9–10); van den Ven (1965).

The expression ܬܪ̈ܝܨܝ ܫܘܒܚܐ triṣay šuḇḥā, lit. “the upright in glory” refers to “the orthodox” (John’s co-religionists, the Severan anti-Chalcedonians). It only occurs three times in EH 3, apart from this passage in 3.1.3.t and 3.2.25.

div.head: EH 3.1.5.h (SR) 6.27–7.3
div.p: ܦܩ̣ܕ ܕܢܬ̇ܬܚܕܘܢ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܒܝܬ ܟܢܘ̣̈ܫܝܐ ܕܟܠܗܘܢ ܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ […] ܕܠܐ ܬܘܒ ܢܟ̣ܢܫܘܢ “he ordered all the gathering places of all the faithful to be seized […] so that they might not gather together again”: This passage introduces and summarizes some elements of the violence the Severans, here as often in the EH called ܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ, “the faithful,” had to endure. The closure of the places of worship and monasteries is further treated in 3.1.10, 3.1.11, and 3.1.36; the repeated incarceration of priests and bishops (often referred to as ܪ̈ܝܫܝ ܟܗ̈ܢܐ, lit. “the heads of the priests”) is narrated in 3.1.17, 3.1.25, and 3.1.29. Note that Justin’s command to suppress the Severans is also listed in Dölger and Müller (2009, 9.18), yet only the summary of this chapter of the EH in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.16–26; 4:336.c.27–41 is indicated as a source.

div.head: EH 3.1.5.i (SR; PMF) 7.5–6
div.p: ܡܫܝܢܐܝܬ ܘܒܗܝܠܐܝܬ ܒܟܘܠܗ̇ “harmoniously and quietly in the whole”: Folio 2v ends at this point. As the next few folios of the sole manuscript of the EH are missing, chapter 3.1.5 breaks off in the middle of a sentence. Brooks (1935, 7.6) conjectured ܡܕܝܢܬܐ ܫ̇ܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ܇ as the words that were probably following, resulting in “[they were living] harmoniously and quietly in the whole [city].” This conjecture is indeed likely since the incomplete final sentence of the folio mainly seems to recapitulate what John stated at the beginning of the chapter, namely that the Severans had been living in peace in Constantinople.

We cannot determine how much of the end of 3.1.5 is lost due to this lacuna in the manuscript. Brooks (1935, 7.6–15) added a passage which he took from Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 (Chabot 1899–1910, 4:336.c.41–337.a.15) and which directly follows Michael’s summary of the extant part of 3.1.5. The ascription of this passage to 3.1.5 is indeed possible and also suggested by Michael’s punctuation: Often – though not always – Michael marks the end of material he has taken from a particular chapter of the EH with the sign ܀. The absence of this mark at 4:336.c.41 may suggest that the summary of 3.1.5 was not yet ended at this point and that the passage in Michael until 4:337.a.15 (where the sign ܀ occurs) still belongs to 3.1.5. However, this is not completely certain and this passage might also stem from the lost chapters. For this reason, we discuss it in the commentary to 3.1.5-8, where we treat the whole passage from Michael (Chabot 1899–1910, 4:336.c.41–337.a.38) which can be attributed to the parts of the EH lost because of this lacuna.

The size of the lacuna can be estimated in light of the codicological reconstruction of the first quire. As noted above in 3.1.3.a, folios 1 and 2 likely formed the middle bifolium of the first quire, while folio 3 was the final folio of the same quire. Assuming the quire was a quinion with ten folios, this would lead to a reconstruction of the second half of the quire as follows: 6 (extant: fol. 2), 7–9 (lost), and 10 (extant: fol. 3). On this calculation, the three folios are missing for the end of chapter 5, chapters 6 to 8, and the beginning of chapter 9. Although it is difficult to know how much of chapters 5 and 9 has been lost, we arrive at somewhat less than two folio sides or four columns on average for chapters 6 to 8. We are grateful to Françoise Briquel-Chatonnet and Grigory Kessel for their advice regarding the codicological reconstruction of the original first quire.

div.head: Introduction (SR)
div.p: A lacuna in BL Add. 16640, the primary manuscript of the EH, has caused the loss of the end of chapter 3.1.5, of the whole of chapters 3.1.6–3.1.8, and of the start of chapter 3.1.9. However, Michael the Syrian still had access to those lost chapters and summarized them in his Chronicle (Book 10, Chapter 3), as is evident from the position of this summary between material he drew from the preserved parts of EH 3.1.5 and 3.1.9. The latter chapter treats the fate of the Severan priest Stephen; therefore, the passage of Michael dealing with Stephen Chabot 1899–1910, 2:293.b.34–294.b.22; 4:337.a.38–338.b.13 is discussed together with the preserved part of 3.1.9.

As for the remaining material which is extant solely in Michael’s Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.26–293.b.33; 4:336.c.41–337.a.38, it is impossible to determine from which one of the chapters 3.1.5–3.1.8 the single elements originally stemmed (although Brooks (1935, 7) assumed that he could identify which parts formed the end of 3.1.5, but see 3.1.5.i; hence their discussion here en bloc. The main content of these chapters seems to have been a summarizing introduction into the various maltreatments which the Severans suffered and which John treats in more detail in later chapters. The hypothesis that the lost chapters were, first and foremost, introductory in their nature is supported by the story of the priest Stephen in EH 3.1.9, whom John appears to have modeled as the protomartyr of the persecution (Bunce 2023, 48–49 and 71). But see also 3.1.12.a, suggesting that there might have been some treatment in these lost chapters of how Severan priests were forced to enter into communion with John Scholasticus.
