Michael’s excerpt drawn from the lost chapters lists the different types of prison into which the Severans were thrown and it has preserved John’s complaint both about the fact that the bishops were subjected to reordination and about their maltreatment in prison, including their prolonged isolation and their insufficient provision with enjoyable drinks.

There is one further passage in Michael’s Chronicle which appears to contain material from these lost chapters. This passage, not included in Brooks (1935, 7–8) to fill the lacuna in the EH, is at the beginning of 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:295.a.9–296.a.8; 4:338.a.28–339.b.9. It starts, like EH 3.1.5, with the statement that the Severans had lived in peace in Constantinople for forty years and introduces John Scholasticus as responsible for their sudden oppression. The start of this passage is thus a mere repetition of what Michael already said in 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292.b.16–26; 4:336.c.27–41, where he summarized EH 3.1.5. However, what follows next is not preserved in any other source, hence its possible origin in John’s lost chapters 3.1.6–3.1.8. Michael provides a reason for why John Scholasticus started acting against the Severans: apparently, he was upset when he heard that a delegation of Severan bishops, including Paul of Antioch and John of Ephesus himself, was about to depart for Rome to find a formula of faith on which both Severans and Chalcedonians could agree. Angry at not being part of this mission, he and his advisors managed to instill fear in the emperor Justin II lest the Severans depose the Roman bishop John III, sed. 561–574). The voyage having been canceled on imperial orders, the bishops were handed over to John Scholasticus. After this passage, Michael’s Chronicle 10.5 continues with a summary of EH 3.1.11.

While it is thus the case that this story about the Severan bishops’ supposed journey to Rome is found between material drawn from EH 3.1.5 and 3.1.11, it remains questionable whether John was the source of this. Given the uncompromisingly pro-Chalcedonian stance of the Roman church (see 3.1.20.f and 3.1.24.n), it appears highly unlikely that the Severan leaders could have realistically hoped to find a viable solution in Rome Sotinel 2005; 1998. After all, such a solution also would have needed to be acceptable to the monastic hardliners in the east who had caused the rejection of Justin’s only moderately Chalcedonian edict on faith just a few years earlier in 567 (see 3.1.3.r). Furthermore, it seems outrightly anachronistic that Justin would have been afraid of the Severans’ deposing the Roman bishop. Their sentence would have had no force whatsoever since no one of them was officially recognized as bishop by the imperial Chalcedonian church. John Scholasticus, directly involved in the religious discussions with the emperor, was undoubtedly aware of all these problems. This allows the conclusion that EH 3.1.6–3.1.8 cannot have been the source of this passage. Already Destephen (2008, 512) briefly expressed doubts about its authenticity, yet did not discuss it in more detail. Michael may have felt dissatisfaction about the absence in the EH of any explanation for why John Scholasticus suddenly turned to an oppressive policy, hence the inclusion of this fictional account.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.a (SR) 7.7–10
div.p: ܟܝ̣ܡܘܢܐ ܥ̇ܙܝܙܐ […] ܥܠ ܐܠܦܐ ܡܫܝܢܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ ܩܕܝܫܬܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ “a vehement storm […] against the calm ship of the holy church of the orthodox”: This passage bears a likeness to 3.1.3.t, where the church is also compared to a ship that is suddenly shaken by the tempest of persecution. Given that Michael did not use any excerpts from EH 3.1.3 elsewhere in his Chronicle, it might well be that he integrated this image from EH 3.1.3 in this place, after summarizing 3.1.5. Van Ginkel’s overview of the excerpts of John found in Michael shows that he frequently changed the order of John’s chapters (van Ginkel 1995, 237–240). However, it remains equally possible that John simply used this metaphor for a second time at the end of 3.1.5 (as assumed by Brooks (1935, 7); see 3.1.5.i or in one of the lost chapters 3.1.6–3.1.8 and that Michael copied it from there.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.b (SR) 7.10–11
div.p: ܘܒܒܝܬ ܚܒܘ̣ܫ̈ܝܐ ܡܫܚ̈ܠܦܐ ܐܬܪܡܝܘ. ܐܝܬ ܕܒܒܝܬ ܐܣܝ̣ܪ̈ܐ ܥܡ ܩܛܘ̈ܠܐ “and they were cast into different places of confinement. Some were in prison with murderers”: This is the start of a list of various places of confinement (until 3.1.5-8.g) where the Severan clerics were held. This list partly anticipates and summarizes information provided in later chapters of the EH. However, the first prison type indicated, ܒܒܝܬ ܐܣܝ̣ܪ̈ܐ ܥܡ ܩܛܘ̈ܠܐ, “in prison with murderers,” is not mentioned elsewhere in the EH. In Book 1 and 2 of EH 3, John repeatedly introduces Severans who were thrown into ܒܝܬ ܐܣܝ̣ܪ̈ܐ, “prison,” where they were maltreated and suffered in various manners, yet he never adds, as Michael’s excerpt does here, that they were forced to share their place with murderers. However, in 3.1.29.h, John claims that he and his peers were treated in prison as if they were murderers; possibly, this comparison lies at the root of Michael’s assertion here.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.c (SR) 7.12
div.p: ܘܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܕܒܦܪܛܘܪ̈ܝܐ “others in praetoria”: Similarly, praetoria, the next element on the list, are never mentioned in the EH in the context of violence directed against Severans. When discussing the series of anti-pagan public riots and trials that took place in Constantinople in the early 580s (EH 3.3.31), John states that both the praetorium (the official residence) of the urban prefect and that of the praetorian prefect were equipped with prison cells (on these two offices, see (Jones 1964); on the praetorium of the urban prefect, located on the Mese just to the east of the Forum of Constantine, see Berger 1988, 738–740 Janin 1964, 165–169; numerous examples of its use as prison, especially in the iconoclast period, are given in (Papavarnavas 2021, 11 and 40)). In the passage in question here, ܦܪܛܘܪܝܐ preṭoria has been transmitted as a singular, without syāmē plural points, in the sole manuscript containing Michael’s Chronicle. If this singular were correct, we would not know which one of the praetoria in Constantinople John was referring to. However, there are good reasons for assuming that ܦܪܛܘܪܝܐ should be read as a plural: when praetorium is used in the singular in Syriac, this Latin loanword is usually rendered as ܦܪܛܘܪܝܢ preṭoriān (via the hellenized form πραιτώριον), also by John in EH 3.3.31 (Butts 2016b, 140). Yet for the plural, John appears to have used the form ܦܪ̈ܛܘܪܝܐ preṭoria (corresponding to the Greek plural πραιτώρια), for instance in EH 3.1.11. Consequently, the word should probably be read as a plural also here in this passage (as tacitly already done by Brooks (1936, 4.9)), and there are simply the syāmē points missing in the manuscript of Michael’s Chronicle.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.d (SR) 7.12
div.p: ܘܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܒܕܝܩܘܢܝܩܘܢ “others in a dekanikon”: This is the third category on the list of prisons. Our translation departs from those of Brooks and Chabot who both read it as diakonikon (διακονικόν), thus referring to a vestry or a sacristy, in late antique Constantinople usually a separate building adjacent to a church (Mathews 1971, 105–107 and 144–145). Moreover, Brooks (1936, 4 n.4) suggested reading it as a plural. Yet their assumption is, in the context of imprisonment, less than convincing since these structures were in constant liturgical use and therefore rather unsuitable to serve as a prison. It is more likely to understand it as dekanikon (δεκανικόν), that is, a guardhouse used by the guards or attendants (δεκανοί) of a bishop (on whom see also 3.1.23.g). Different sources attest to the existence of such establishments under episcopal authority. For instance, a letter included in the Acts of the council of Ephesus in 431 mentions that patriarch Nestorius detained clerics who took issue with his Christological stance in his dekanikon Schwartz 1960c, 8 cf. Price and Graumann 2022, 191–192; moreover, Justinian’s Novel 79.3 specifies that bishops had dekanika which served as prisons (Schöll and Kroll 1895, 389). Importantly, John of Ephesus himself talks of the detention of Severan clerics in a ܒܝܬ ܕܝܩܢ̣ܝܩܘܢ in 3.2.13.d; his way of spelling (the absence of waw in the middle of the word) suggests reading this as dekanikon rather than diakonikon. Yet Michael the Syrian, unfamiliar with this term, might have wrongly understood it as diakonikon, hence his slightly different spelling in this passage, including a waw in the middle.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.e (SR) 7.13
div.p: ܘܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܬܘܒ̣ ܒܚܒ̣ܘܫ̈ܬܐ ܕܬܚܝ̣ܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ “still others in prison cells under the episcopal palace”: ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ (ἐπισκοπεῖον) “the episcopal palace” is the standard term John employs to refer to the patriarchal residence in Constantinople. It was located directly adjacent to the south-western corner of Hagia Sophia Dark and Kostenec 2019, 46–59; 2014; see 3.1.23.f. John repeatedly mentions the confinement of resisting Severans there, for instance in EH 3.1.15; 3.1.17; 3.1.31; 3.2.8; 3.2.14.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.f (SR) 7.14
div.p: ܘܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܒܐܟܣܘܪ̈ܝܐܣ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܕܥ̈ܝܬܐ “others in other places of banishment within the churches”: The next element on the list introduces yet another, albeit unspecified, category of ecclesiastical prison, after the dekanikon and the patriarchal residence. The term ܐܟܣܘܪܝܐܣ (ἐξορία) “banishment” (Lampe 1961, 500) does in the EH not necessarily imply punishment by deportation to a spatially remote location; it suffices that the victim is separated from his usual everyday surroundings. John thus also uses it generally to denote imprisonment or a monastic confinement which need not be in a different city. For instance, he describes in EH 3.2.2 the relegation of Paul of Antioch to the monastery of Abraham in Constantinople as an example of such a ܐܟܣܘܪܝܐܣ “banishment.” On the use of banishment and exile in John’s works, see Shepardson (2019).

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.g (SR) 7.15
div.p: ܘܣܓܝ̈ܐܐ ܥ̣ܪܩܘ ܘܐܙܕܪܩܘ ܒܟܠ ܐܬܪ̈ܘܢ܀ “many fled and were scattered about in all manner of places”: This statement ends Michael’s excerpt on the different prison types; see 3.1.29.f for another, similar list of places of detention, including monasteries, islands, and hospices. In the EH, there are several instances of Severan clerics escaping from their suppressors; these are discussed in the commentary as they are encountered. Examples include the escape of Paul of Antioch from Constantinople noted in EH 3.2.8 and the repeated prison breaks of the presbyter Andreas in EH 3.2.14.

As stated in 3.1.5.i, Michael’s summary of EH 3.1.5 might have ended at this point (Chabot 1899–1910, 4:337.a.15) because the punctuation mark ܀ often indicates chapter breaks in Michael. This was assumed by Brooks (1935, 7.15), yet, due to the lacuna in the sole manuscript of the EH with this chapter, it is impossible to decide with certainty where EH 3.1.5 ended. If Brooks’s hypothesis is correct, then the following passage until the next occurrence of the sign ܀ at 4:337.a.38 stems from one or more of the completely lost chapters EH 3.1.6–3.1.8.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.h (SR) 7.17–18
div.p: ܘܗ̇ܘ ܕܝܬܝܪ ܩܫܐ ܕܠܪ̈ܝܫܐ ܟܗ̈ܢܐ ܢܟܦ̈ܐ ܡܣܪܚܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܬܢܝ̣ܢܘܬ “what is more grievous is that they ordained the devout bishops once again”: In EH 3.1.12–3.1.18, John laments the, in his eyes, uncanonical sudden demand of patriarch John Scholasticus for a reordination to the priesthood of some Severan clerics who had accepted Chalcedonian communion (see 3.1.12.f). It is possible that John already included a prolepsis to this in the lost chapters 3.1.6–3.1.8. Alternatively, Michael might have wanted to continue the summary of the hardship the Severans had to tolerate in the different prisons by adding also other elements of mistreatment that are mentioned later in the EH.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.i (SR) 7.18–19
div.p: ܘܐܦ ܠܝܒܠܘܢ ܬܒ̇ܥܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܕܢܬ̣ܠܘܢ ܠܗܘܢ “and demanded that they produce a libellus”: A libellus (hellenized λίβελλος) is a small book, a term often used for written statements of orthodoxy (Lampe 1961, 801–802) and taken over as a loanword into Syriac (Butts 2016b, 158). However, in the EH, there is only one single case when a Severan bishop, blind Paul of Aphrodisias, was cheated into signing such a document to publicly demonstrate his adherence to the contentious faith of Chalcedon (EH 3.2.43. It is thus very likely that John treated the case of Paul of Aphrodisias at least briefly in the lost chapters EH 3.1.5-8, as is also suggested by the statement in EH 3.1.14.b (where Paul is introduced for the first time in the EH as it has been preserved) that there had already been a previous mention of Paul.

Interestingly, John never mentions any demand for the composition of a libellus in the case of the other Severan bishops who eventually gave in to the pressure of Justin II and John Scholasticus (EH 3.2.2; 3.2.3; 3.2.4), but he does so with regard to the, in John’s eyes, heretical bishop Conon of Tarsus (see 3.1.31.d). However, it seems probable that all the anti-Chalcedonian bishops would have needed to profess their adherence to the faith of the imperial church in writing. John’s interest in protecting himself and his peers, especially Paul of Antioch, may provide an explanation for why these libelli do not figure more prominently in the EH. This interest, noted by Roggo (2022, 106–107; 127–128; 162), may have prompted him to omit some elements which would have cast a poor light on himself and on his peers, including their probable composition of such a libellus.

The fact that Michael generalized, in the passage in question here, the rare occurrence of libelli in the EH to something that was required of all the Severan clerics demonstrates again that he was anything but an exact copyist and had no qualms about interpreting his sources quite liberally; see 3.1.4.b.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.j (SR) 7.21–23
div.p: ܠܐ ܫܒܩܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܐܢܫ ܡܢ ܝܕܘܥ̈ܝܗܘܢ ܕܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܦܐ ܕܚܒܝ̣ܫܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܕܢ̣ܥܘܠ ܠܘܬܗܘܢ܇ ܐ̇ܘ ܣܟ ܢܡܠܠ ܥܡܗܘܢ “they did not allow anyone the imprisoned bishops knew to come to them or to speak with them at all”: Complaints about the harsh conditions of imprisonment, including the prevention of social interaction with friends and collaborators, as in this generalizing passage, sometimes occur in the EH. Examples include EH 3.1.29.g and 3.2.5, where John describes his own desolation when he was kept in isolation in the hospice of Eubulus.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.k (SR) 7.24–8.2
div.p: ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܒܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܚܒܝ̣ܫܝܢ ܗܘܘ܇ […] ܝܬܝܪ ܐܘ̈ܠܨܢܐ ܡܣܒܠܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗܘܢ܇ “they made those who were confined in monasteries […] endure more tribulations”: Various Constantinopolitan monasteries are the most common places of detention for resisting Severans mentioned in the EH (see, for instance, 3.1.15; 3.1.17; 3.1.31; 3.2.2; 3.2.12; 3.2.13; 3.2.14; 3.2.38; 3.3.20. It is all the more noticeable that monasteries are not included in the list of different prison types above in lines 3.1.5-8.b–3.1.5-8.g; we may hypothesize that Michael perhaps simply omitted them by mistake. In late antiquity, it was a common practice to detain clerics in monasteries. At times, recalcitrant bishops and priests would be sent to such monasteries where the inhabitants held doctrinally different views from them so as to add to their discomfort and to the pressure exerted on them. On the use of monasteries for detaining ecclesiastical figures, see Hillner (2015, 281–341); a list of individual cases, including those in EH and LES, appears on pages 380–386 of her book.

div.head: EH 3.1.5-8.l (SR) 8.3–6
div.p:
ܟܕ ܫܝܓ̈ܬܐ ܕܠܓ̈ܝܗܘܢ ܫ̇ܕܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܘܚܠܦ ܚܡܪܐ ܚܠܐ ܡܕܡ܇ ܝܗ̇ܒܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܕܢܫܬܘܢ܀ “casting the rinsing liquid from their basins at them, giving them instead of wine some vinegar to drink […]”: This is an allusion to Luke 23:36
where the soldiers give vinegar to Jesus during the crucifixion. The Peshitta version of the Gospel uses the same word (ܚܠܐ) for “vinegar” as the EH (Kiraz 2004, 3:483). The provision of the imprisoned Severan clerics with unorthodox drinks is not mentioned elsewhere in the EH. Closest to it comes John’s narration in 3.2.6: When imprisoned in the hospice of Eubulus, John was visited by the apparition of an angel or a saint. Noticing that John was thirsty, his visitor provided him with a strengthening and miraculously shining drink.

Note that when listing the different tortures the imprisoned Severans had to suffer in the 570s, Hillner (2015, 338) also states that they “were tortured with lances,” with the non-extant chapter EH 3.1.8 indicated as the source of this. She appears to have misunderstood Brooks’s (1936, 4.21–22) Latin translation purgamenta lancium suarum eis proiciunt, which we have rendered as “casting the rinsing liquid from their basins at them”; the same mistake happened to Hatlie (2007, 201 n.74).

Although some of the maltreatments listed in Michael’s excerpt drawn from EH 3.1.5-8 are not found in the preserved parts of the EH, it remains possible that John listed these afflictions in the lost chapters in a summarizing manner without later treating them again in more detail. However, we cannot exclude that Michael in this part of his Chronicle also added material from other sources or adapted and generalized elements that he found elsewhere in the EH.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: The start of this chapter has not been preserved due to the lacuna which also caused the (partial) loss of the chapters 3.1.5-8. However, its extensive summary in the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian indicates that its protagonist was an otherwise unknown priest called Stephen. John introduces him as the first martyr of the persecution instigated by John Scholasticus and Justin II. The chapter outlines the various stages of his plight in the hands of the Chalcedonians: after an initial imprisonment, Stephen was brought in front of a prefect, probably the urban prefect of Constantinople, who then handed him over to patriarch John Scholasticus. The patriarch sent Stephen to a prison in Heraclea in Thrace, where he was kept under close guard and deprived of any amenities. After two years, Stephen died and was buried by orthodox (i.e. Severan) army officers; his fame spread to Constantinople and he was venerated as a martyr.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
EH 3.1.9.a (SR) 8.7
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܬܫܥܐ “Chapter 9”: Michael’s testimony for this chapter is very trustworthy; a comparison of those parts of the chapter which are preserved in BL Add. 14640 (Brooks 1935, 8.11–9.5) with Michael shows that he did not, as so often, merely include a summary, but that he quoted the chapter extensively and very accurately. Only minor parts are omitted in Michael, most notably the last lines which state that Stephen came to be venerated as a saint after his martyrdom; see 3.1.9.o. Michael’s demonstrably faithful quotation of this chapter renders it likely that he also quite accurately preserved the first few lines of the chapter, which are lost in BL Add. 14640 (Brooks 1935, 8.8–11).

div.head: EH 3.1.9.b (SR) 8.8–9
div.p: ܣܒܐ ܐܣܛܦܢܐ ܩܲܫܝ̣ܫܐ. ܓܒܪܐ ܕܚܪܝ̣ܙ ܒܛܢ̣ܢܐ ܐܠܗܝܐ “the old man Stephen, a presbyter and man who was adorned with divine zeal”: This Stephen is not known from any other source. In the preserved part of 3.1.9 itself, he is never named, his name only occurring once at the very beginning of the summary / quotation of this chapter in Michael the Syrian Chabot 1899–1910, 2:293.b.34; 4:337.a.38. As explained in the previous note, Michael appears to have preserved a quite faithful version of the whole of 3.1.9. Hence we can be confident that the protagonist of the chapter was indeed called Stephen also in John’s original account. This Stephen, a priest, is the first concrete example in the EH of a person who was targeted and suffered during the suppression of the Severan clerics in the early 570s. Neither his name nor the position of this story at the start of John’s treatment of the persecution are coincidental: as noted by Bunce (2023, 68–71), John composed his account in Book 1 very carefully as an apology and did not necessarily observe a strict chronological order. The story of Stephen serves as a literary device; it is likely that with it John wanted to create a parallel to the protomartyr Stephen, traditionally regarded as the first Christian to suffer martyrdom according to the Acts of the Apostles 7:54–60 (Bunce 2023, 48–49). For this literary purpose, John’s Stephen evidently needed to die as a result of the persecution, just like the protomartyr. Indeed, in the whole EH, this Stephen is the sole Severan who is described as having died in the hands of the Chalcedonians (see 3.1.9.l). This hints at the possibility that the content of 3.1.9 might at least partly be fictional and that John placed the story there so that he could start off his account with the martyrdom of a saintly priest, thereby underlining the cruelty of the Chalcedonian oppressors (Shepardson 2019, 285–286). This constructed parallel to the first Christian martyr Stephen enabled John to present his co-religionists as experiencing a fate very similar to that of the first Christians in Jerusalem; with such a martyr story, he may have further hoped to strengthen the group identity of his Severan community (a strategy often used in late antiquity, as noted by Shepardson (2021, 199) and Gaddis (2005, 96)). Even if the events around Stephen really did happen as described, it was very probably not Stephen, but John and his episcopal peers, the leaders of the Severan movement in Constantinople, who were the first target. John’s deliberate placement of the story of Stephen at the beginning of his account means that we also need to be skeptical of his order of events elsewhere in Book 1, as has been argued by Roggo (2022, 103–111).

div.head: EH 3.1.9.c (SR) 8.12–13
div.p: ܡ̇ܢܐ ܝܬ̇ܒ ܐܢܬ ܐܝܟ ܟܪܣܛܝܢܐ܇ ܘܠܥܒ̈ܕ̣ܘܗܝ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܕܐ̇ܢ ܐܢܬ ܚܢܦܐܝܬ “why do you sit as a Christian […] and judge in a pagan way the servants of God?”: John sometimes compares the suppressors of the Severan anti-Chalcedonians to pagans. In EH 3.1.37 and 3.3.12, for instance, he has Tiberius complain to John Scholasticus that persecuting the Severans makes him, Tiberius, appear like a new Diocletian. Similarly, in EH 3.2.26 , the violence instigated by John Scholasticus is compared to the suppression of the Israelites by the Pharaoh of Egypt. In a passage of the second part of the EH preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle and set in the late 520s, the anti-Chalcedonians of Amida call bishop Abraham Bar Kaili a pagan because he tortured and executed those who rejected the council of Chalcedon Chabot 1933a, 33–34 Witakowski 1996, 33. By likening the Chalcedonian leaders to the pagan suppressors of old, John casts doubt on their Christianity and delegitimazes their actions since they behaved, so he claims, in a way no proper Christian would. The Severans are thus presented as innocent victims who have fallen into the hands of suppressors who are Christians in name only.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.d (SR) 8.13–15
div.p: ܠܝܬܝܟ ܒܒ̈ܢܝ ܐܢܫܐ ܕܚ̇ܝ̣ܝܢ܇ ܐܢ ܠܐ ܒܥܓܠ ܡ̇ܘܩܕ ܐܢܬ ܠܝ ܠܣܒܐ ܡܚ̣ܝܠܐ܇ ܘܐ̇ܟܠ ܐܢܬ ܠܝ ܒܛܘ̇ܝܐ “you are not among the living, unless you immediately set me, a weak old man, on fire and eat me roasted”: This curse formula is only attested in the EH. It seems to convey a desire for martyrdom in a situation of oppression and imprisonment; parts of it occur in two other instances of the EH, in 3.2.4.e (uttered by John of Ephesus himself against envoys of John Scholasticus who wanted to talk him into accepting the Chalcedonian communion) and in 3.2.9.j (by Andreas, an imprisoned Severan member of empress Sophia’s court, against an envoy of the emperor).

The phrase ܐ̇ܟܠ ܒܛܘܝܐ (“to eat roasted”) is exceedingly rare in Syriac. It is, however, used on one occasion in the Chronicle of Pseudo-Joshua Trombley and Watt 2001, 94 Chabot 1927, 300.8 where, importantly, it is also associated with cannibalism, as in Stephen’s curse: during the siege of Amida in 502/503, some women are said to have resorted to overpowering, killing, and then “eating boiled or roasted” (ܐܟ̈ܠܢ ܠܗ ܒܫܠܩܐ ܐܘ ܒܛܘܝܐ) random other inhabitants of Amida. The phrase appears thus to have a connotation of describing horrors in the historiographical tradition in which John is writing. It is conceivable that John, originating from the region of Amida, was aware of this cruel story and that the curse with this phrase, which he used three times in the EH, emerged from it.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.e (SR) 8.16
div.p: ܗܘܦܪܟܐ “the prefect”: The identity of this prefect is not clear. In all likelihood, the magistrate in question was the urban prefect of Constantinople who, for the year 571, is unknown (PLRE 3:1428–1429 (Martindale 1992)). Throughout the EH, when referring to either the urban or the praetorian prefect, John always uses ܗܘܦܪܟܐ hupparḵā, a transliteration of the Greek ὕπαρχος; see, for instance, EH 3.1.10 and 3.3.32 for the urban prefect and 3.3.9 for the praetorian prefect. However, the term ὕπαρχος is, in the 6th century, not usually employed to denote these prefects (the official title of the urban prefect being ἔπαρχος τῆς πόλεως; of the praetorian prefect ἔπαρχος τῶν πραιτωρίων). Yet Syriac does not differentiate between ἔπαρχος and ὕπαρχος (Butts 2016a, 60). After all, both these words can, in a broader sense, simply mean “prefect” (Lampe 1961, 511 and 1435); see also Leppin (2019, 120 n.26).

div.head: EH 3.1.9.f (SR) 8.17
div.p: ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ “the bishop”: Very often, John refers to the patriarch of Constantinople, John Scholasticus, simply as “the bishop.” Here, as throughout the first two books of the EH, he is depicted as the one mainly responsible for the violence directed against the Severan clerics (van Ginkel 1995, 107–108); see 3.1.5.e.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.g (SR; NK; PMF) 8.18
div.p: ܡܢ ܡܢܝܐ ܕܝܠܗ “out of his madness”: ܡܢܝܐ maniā is a transliteration of the Greek μανία, a term which was not frequently used as a loanword in Syriac. Tellingly, in the margin of BL Add. 14640, the copyist, apparently not understanding the Greek loanword, added ܚܡܬܗ “his rage,” thus providing his readers with a Syriac explanation. In the main text, ܡܢܝܐ is erased, leaving only the letter ܡ‍, but the whole word is still discernible. Michael the Syrian (Chabot 1899–1910, 4:338.b.4) also preferred ܚܡܬܐ in his rendering of John’s chapter. John used ܡܢܝܐ for a second time in EH 3.6.34, referring to the “madness” with which the Persians “barbarously” (ܒܪܒܪܐܝܬ) destroyed the surroundings of Amid.

Parallel expressions of the second instance of ܡܢܝܐ may offer insight into what nuance this Greek word held for John. The word ܒܪܒܪܐܝܬ “barbarously” is preceded in two other places in the EH by the phrase “in a violent rage” – either ܒܝܕ ܚܐܦܐ ܕܚܡܬܐ (EH 3.4.14) or ܒܚܐܦܐ ܕܚܡܬܐ (EH 3.4.41) – where the Syriac phrase “a violent rage” ܚܐܦܐ ܕܚܡܬܐ seems to be used in place of the Greek μανία. This accords with the definitions given to this word in a modern Syriac–Syriac dictionary: “madness” (ܫܢܝܘܬܐ) and “intense rage” (ܚܡܬܐ ܥܙܝܙܬܐ) Audo 1897–1901, 2:64; cf. R. Payne Smith 1879, 2:2172 Yuyakim d’beth Yahkub 2016, 1035.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.h (SR) 8.18–19
div.p: ܒܗܪܩ̣ܠܝܐ̣ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ ܕܬܪܩ̣ܝܐ “in the city of Heraclea in Thrace”: Heraclea (prior to the 4th century known as Perinthus) lay 90 km to the west of Constantinople and was the capital of the province Europe in eastern Thrace. The bishop of Byzantium had originally been a suffragan of the metropolite in Heraclea. Yet after the first council of Constantinople in 381 at the latest, Constantinople became independent of Heraclea ecclesiastically and already before the council of Chalcedon in 451, the metropolites of Thrace had come under the dominion of the church of Constantinople (Dagron 1974, 418–419). Canon 28 of Chalcedon confirmed the right of the bishops of the capital to ordain the metropolites of the Thracian diocese Price and Gaddis 2005, 3:75–76 Schwartz 1965a, 88.28–89.17. This together with the close proximity of Heraclea to Constantinople renders it unsurprising that John Scholasticus was able to send Stephen into prison there.

Note that Michael the Syrian (Chabot 1899–1910, 4:338.b.5) calls the place of Stephen’s imprisonment ܐܪܡܝܐ ’Eramyā instead of Heraclea, but this mistake is easily explained: Michael (or his sources) frequently replaced initial hē (ܗ) with ālap̄ (ܐ); for instance, he also wrote ܐܘܦܪܟܐ instead of John’s ܗܘܦܪܟܐ (“prefect”) in the same passage. Moreover, the combination of the letters qāp̄ and l­āmaḏ (ܩܠ‍) can conceivably be mistaken for a mim (ܡ‍) as they can appear quite similar if the upper stroke on the mim is extended high.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.i (SR) 8.19
div.p:
ܗ̣ܘܐ ܬܡܢ ܙܒܢܐ ܕܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ “he was there for a period of two years”: This is the sole chronological indication in this chapter; Stephen died after two years of imprisonment in Heraclea. John’s assertion in EH
3.1.5 that the period of religious violence started on 21 March 571 (see 3.1.5.d) provides us with the terminus post quem for Stephen’s confinement; his death can thus have occurred in spring 573 at the earliest. However, as noted above in 3.1.9.b, John may have had his own literary reasons for placing the account of Stephen at the beginning of the EH. Consequently, it is conceivable that this period of two years may have occurred slightly later. In any case, it is very likely that Stephen died before the end of 574 since there seems to have been a relaxation of the anti-Severan measures when Tiberius rose to power; according to EH 3.2.7.f, John himself was allowed to return to Constantinople at that time Roggo 2022, 118–119 Booth 2017, 169–171 Allen 1981, 26–27; see 3.1.31.h. The terminus ante quem for Stephen’s imprisonment in Heraclea is thus autumn 572.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.j (SR) 8.20
div.p: ܠܐ ܡܫܬܒܩ ܕܢܚ̣ܙܐ ܐܢܫ ܡܢ ܝ̇ܕܘ̈ܥܘܗܝ “without being allowed to see any of his acquaintances”: John’s complaint that the imprisoned Severans were deprived of social interactions with their friends and colleagues is a recurrent theme that was in all likelihood already addressed in the lost chapters 3.1.5-8.j.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.k (SR) 8.21–22
div.p: ܘܪܦܝ̇ܬ ܗܘܐ ܒܩ̇ܠ̈ܡܐ ܕܠܐ ܣܟ̣ܐ “was covered with innumerable lice”: John used exactly the same formulation in EH 3.2.5.d when describing the hardship he suffered during his own confinement in the hospice of Eubulus.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.l (SR) 8.24
div.p: ܘܒܬܪܟܢ ܐܬܟܪܗ ܕܢܡ̣ܘܬ “afterwards, he grew ill to the point of death”: The presbyter Stephenis the only person in the EH who is described as actually having died as a result of the imprisonment of the Severan clerics in the 570s. The only other Christian deaths due to religious violence John recorded all took place outside the Roman empire, during the Sasanian persecution of Christians in Armenia and elsewhere in the Persian Empire in the early 570s (EH 3.2.19). As noted above in 3.1.9.b, John may have wanted to start his account with a casualty he could frame as a martyrdom in order to construct a parallel to the early Christian martyrs, hence his choice of a story with a protagonist called Stephen, like the protomartyr. This literary motivation does not rule out the possibility that Stephen indeed died imprisoned in exile; on the contrary, it is even likely that not all the Severan bishops and priests, many of whom were already advanced in age like Stephen and John himself, survived the prolonged period of incarceration (see Shepardson (2019, 286) and Hillner (2015, 253–254) for the understanding of exile and imprisonment as a form of martyrdom). Yet the death of the leaders never was the aim of the measures directed against the Severans. The conspicuous absence of other casualties in Book 1 of the EH suggests that John Scholasticus and Justin II were aware of the danger of creating martyrs for the anti-Chalcedonian movement. The persecutors’ main hope was that by imprisoning the Severan clergy, they could eventually be forced into accepting the shared communion with the Chalcedonians Shepardson 2019, 277 and 283 van Ginkel 1995, 174–176.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.m (SR; NK) 8.26–27
div.p: ܘܗܟܢܐ [ܪ̈ܒܝ] ܚ̇ܝܠܐ ܕܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ ܕܐܫܬ̣ܟܚܘ ܬܡܢ “Thus, orthodox officers of the Romans, who happened to be there”: Brooks (1935, 8.26) conjectured ܒܢ̈ܝ in front of ܚܝܠܐ to close a small lacuna caused by a stain in the manuscript, resulting in “soldiers.” However, in the context of a public burial with a funerary procession, it makes more sense to read ܪ̈ܒܝ ܚܝܠܐ “officers” or “commanders,” as it may not have been possible for simple soldiers to organize such an official ceremony. The phrase ܪ̈ܒܝ ܚܝܠܐ is attested, for instance, in the Chronicle of Pseudo-Joshua Trombley and Watt 2001, 74 Chabot 1927, 286.10. The presence of officers at Heraclea is not surprising, given the city’s strategic location on the way to Constantinople and close to the Long Walls which covered the capital from the west (on the Long Walls and their important function in the 6th century, see Meier (2009, 141–148)).

Often (for instance in EH 3.2.14.b), John simply writes ܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ rhomāyē “Romans” when talking about members of the armed forces; the sense of the present passage is thus most likely “orthodox army officers.” When John uses the term “orthodox,” he always refers to members of his own community, the Severan anti-Chalcedonians. By describing these officers as Severans, John implies that an important pillar of the imperial government did not fully share in the fury of Stephen’s Chalcedonian oppressors and was not completely hostile towards the anti-Chalcedonians. The oppressors are thus presented as not even having the full support of the state apparatus. We are not well informed about potential Severan sympathies in the army, but John indicates in EH 3.2.9–3.2.12 that there were such sympathies at least at the imperial court. Given the popularity of anti-Chalcedonian beliefs in large regions of the Roman east, we may assume that a considerable number of Severans served in the Roman army. Furthermore, it is probable that many lay people did not sharply differentiate between opponents and defenders of Chalcedon (Menze 2008b, 159), especially in regions such as Thrace that were not hotly contested between the two groups. After all, Severans were usually not treated like heretics in imperial legislation, and both Justinian and Justin II had repeatedly attempted to achieve reconciliation with them (see the literature in3.1.5.a). The sudden turn against the Severan leaders, as described by John, was therefore probably not shared by the whole population even in traditionally pro-Chalcedonian regions. EH 3.2.14 provides a fine example of how a predominantly Chalcedonian population came to regard a Severan ascetic as a venerable holy man and prevented his imprisonment; the case of Stephen may have been similar to this.

In this passage, some letters in the manuscript are smudged and thus not recognizable, necessitating conjectures on three occasions. Apart from the first gap, which, as noted above, probably should be filled in with ܪ̈ܒܝ, there are two more additions needed. The first two letters of the word ܐܖ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ are missing, and we have completed it by preferring Cureton’s (1853, 6.23) suggestion over Brooks’s (1935, 8.27) ܐܘܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ. In the third gap, Cureton (1853, 6.23) suggests ܬܡܢ, while Brooks (1935, 8.27) offers ܗ̣ܢܘܢ, which, however, requires four letters. Since there is only space for three letters in the gap, we have again followed Cureton’s conjecture.

div.head: EH 3.1.9.n (SR) 9.1–2
div.p: ܟܕ ܐܝܟ ܕܠܣܗܕܐ ܪܒܐ ܡ̇ܥܦܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܘܡ̇ܙܝܚܝܢ ܦܓܪܗ “while they were enshrouding and processing his body like a great martyr”: John creates the impression that the (Severan) officers in Heraclea treated Stephen’s body like that of a martyr, worthy of special veneration, and criticized the punishment of such saintly men. The EH being the only source for Stephen, the authenticity of John’s report is impossible to ascertain. Starting in the mid-4th century, relics of saints (many of whom were martyrs) were brought to Constantinople, often in a solemn procession (Klein 2006). John was undoubtedly familiar with this since both Justinian and Justin II continued adorning Constantinople with relics, and he may have wanted to evoke this practice here, although he never says that Stephen’s body was actually transferred to the capital (see 3.1.9.o).

div.head: EH 3.1.9.o (SR) 9.4–5
div.p: ܐܬ̇ܚܫܒܘ ܕܝܢ ܐ̈ܢܫܐ ܡܢ ܡܗ̈ܝ̣ܡܢܐ ܕܒܡܕܝܢܬܐ܆ ܕܢ̇ܦܩܘܢ ܘܢ̇ܣܒܘܢ̣ܝܗܝ ܠܦܓܪܗ ܕܩܕܝܫܐ “some of the faithful in the city determined to go retrieve the body of the holy one”: John claims that the Severans in Constantinople wanted to venerate Stephen as a saint after his martyrdom, as his death in prison is framed in the EH, and to collect his body as a relic. However, it is very interesting that John never actually says that Stephen then really came to be venerated. He does not inform us whether the Severans managed to retrieve his body. In line with the observation by Bunce (2023, 71) that John had a strong literary interest in this passage to start his account of the Severan hardship with a story of martyrdom (see 3.1.9.b), we may suspect that he added the information about Stephen’s supposed veneration as a saint for this very purpose and that it is just fiction. In all likelihood, this is also the reason for why Michael the Syrian, who reproduced chapter 3.1.9 quite accurately in his Chronicle, decided to end his version with Stephen’s funeral and to leave out this final part of John’s story: Michael did not know of any martyr called Stephen from the 570s who came to be regarded as a saint. Therefore, he skipped all allusions of John with regard to Stephen’s martyrdom and sainthood and just focused on Stephen’s maltreatment in prison and on his death.

div.head: Summary (SR; HL)
div.p: This chapter treats the closure of the Severan monasteries in Constantinople; other chapters addressing the pressure exerted on monks and nuns are EH 3.1.11; 3.1.36; 3.2.39; 3.2.47; 3.3.19. John asserts here that there were, in the capital, some quite sizable Severan monasteries, both for men and for women, many of which dated back to the 530s and 540s when Theodora (d. 548) had protected the Severans. John then focuses on monasteries for women and claims that clerics, assisted by lay officials, entered those convents and demanded that the inhabitants take communion from them. The nuns complained heavily about being forced to receive Chalcedonian communion and many refused to do so. Some eventually gave in as a result of the violence that was applied, yet others kept resisting and were removed from their monasteries and separated from each other in various prisons, where they were cruelly mistreated.

The rhetorical strategy employed in this chapter is noteworthy. Despite the initial and concluding references to monasteries for both men and women, the narrative predominantly concentrates on female convents. This emphasis is accentuated in Syriac, where gender distinctions are frequently discernible through verb conjugations. Apparently, the author intentionally directs the reader’s attention towards a notably vulnerable group. Their adversaries are subjected to dehumanization, depicted as animal-like and barbaric – a rhetorical technique consistently upheld by John throughout the EH.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chron. 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:294.b.23–295.b.3; 4:338.b.13–25: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.10.l

div.head: EH 3.1.10.a (SR; NK) 9.8–9
div.p: ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܬܘܒ ܕܓܒܪ̈ܐ ܘܕܢܫ̈ܐ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܝܗܝܢ ܒܗ̇ ܒܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ: ܘܒܟܘܠܗܘܢ ܚ̣ܕܪ̈ܝܗ̇ ܘܦ̣ܢ̈ܝܬܗ̇ “the monasteries of men and women which were in the imperial city, in all its suburbs and its districts”: Ever since the second half of the 4th century, numerous monasteries had been founded in Constantinople; in the 6th century, over 200 monastic establishments are known to have existed in the city, with the majority being located in the outer quarters, close to the city walls, and not in the very center Hatlie 2007, 187; 210; 457–472 Janin 1969. In this passage, John only refers to monasteries leaning towards Severan Christology, and their exact number is impossible to assess. The monastic communities housing exiled Severan clerics in the palace of Hormisdas and in Sycae (on which see 3.1.10.b) are well known, and it is probable that there were a few more, although John may create an exaggerated impression in this chapter. John names Severan nunneries in EH 3.1.11; 3.1.36; 3.2.39; 3.2.47; 3.3.19, but all these instances may refer to the same convent (van Ginkel 1995, 155 n.138). A further example of a Constantinopolitan monastery sympathetic towards Severans is the monastery of Rabbula (Janin 1969, 445), as John notes in EH 3.2.13. In all likelihood, Severan monasteries were quite visible in the monastic landscape of Constantinople during the reign of Justinian and prior to their suppression in the early 570s (Hatlie 2007, 141–150).

Since the word ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ (“monasteries”) is feminine plural, the verbal ending should match the feminine form. However, in this instance, it is connected with a masculine verb (ܐ̣ܝܬܝܗܘܢ), thus necessitating a correction to the feminine ܐ̣ܝܬܝܗܝܢ. The same error occurs in EH 3.1.36.a. Apart from these two instances, ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ is regularly construed with a feminine verb form, for example in 3.1.36.f; 3.2.47; 3.5.6. For the ܚܕܪ̈ܐ “suburbs” of Constantinople, see 3.1.4.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.b (SR; NK) 9.11–15
div.p: ܢܫ̈ܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܫ̇ܘܪܝܐ ܪ̈ܕܝ̣ܦܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ: […] ܕܡ̈ܟܢ̣ܫܢ ܗܘ̈ܝ ܠܢܝܚܬ ܢܦܫܐ ܬܐܕܘܪܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܬܐ: ܕܡܢܗܘܢ ܕܥܘܡܪ̈ܐ ܥ̇ܒܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܡܢܝܢܐ ܕܬܠܬܡܐܐ ܐ̇ܚ̈ܘܢ “those of women which at the beginning were driven out […], having been assembled by the deceased empress Theodora, with some of the convents exceeding three hundred sisters in number”: This is a unique passage insofar as it attests the migration from the eastern provinces of numerous female anti-Chalcedonian ascetics to Constantinople; other 6th-century sources only mention men. Starting in the second half of the 530s, many anti-Chalcedonian monks and clerics from the Near East and Egypt established themselves permanently in Constantinople. Many fled to the capital because of the resumption of the persecution in the east by patriarch Ephrem of Antioch in 536 (see 3.1.5.a); EH 3.1.39 and 3.2.46 provide an example of such a monastic community which moved from Cappadocia into the vicinity of Constantinople. In the capital, the Severan anti-Chalcedonians enjoyed imperial protection and repeatedly discussed doctrinal questions with Justinian. Examples include Theodosius of Alexandria (d. 566), the stylite Zʿura, and John himself. Supposedly as many as 500 of them lived as a monastic community in the palace of Hormisdas, just to the south-west of the imperial palace in the city center; others settled in Sycae, located on the other side of the Golden Horn, and founded monasteries there. There is no doubt that these communities were quite sizable, yet the figures of allegedly 300 sisters given in this chapter are possibly exaggerated. The main source for monastic Severan presence in Constantinople during the reign of Justinian is John himself in LES 2 (Brooks 1923, 20–28); 25 (Brooks 1924, 327–336); 36 (Brooks 1924, 428–439); 47 (Brooks 1924b, 474–82); Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.21 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:192-193; 4:278; Zuqnin Chronicle (Chabot 1933a, 138; Witakowski 1996, 122); see (Hasse-Ungeheuer 2016, 261–96; Leppin 2011, 290–92; Hatlie 2007, 142–45 and 183–86; Croke 2006, 31–46; van Ginkel 2005, 39–40; 1995, 177–78).

Theodora (d. 548) was widely thought to hold anti-Chalcedonian sympathies and John therefore identifies her as the main force for protecting those ascetics in Constantinople (LES 47 (Brooks 1924, 474–482)). Yet her influence in this should not be overstated. Justinian was also genuinely interested in keeping the Severan leaders in Constantinople, both for religious discussions and in order to prevent them from setting up a parallel episcopal hierarchy Menze 2008b, 208–228 van Ginkel 1995, 151–152.

There are two gaps in the manuscript at this point, so letters need to be added to two words. The last letter of the word ܢܝܚܬ is illegible and should be completed with ܬ taw according to Brooks (1935, 9.13), while Cureton (1853, 7.11) does not suggest any addition in this gap. In the second gap, about three letters are missing in the middle between ܡ‍ mim and ‍ܐ ālap̄; according to Cureton and Brooks, this should be completed as ܡܢܝܢܐ meaning “number” Cureton 1853, 7.12 Brooks 1935, 9.14.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.d (SR; HL) 9.17–19
div.p: ܗ̇ܢܘ ܕܝ̣ܢ ܕܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܥܡ ܥ̇ܠܡ̈ܝܐ ܘܩܘܡ̇ܢܛܪ̈ܝܣܐ. ܘܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܐܘܛܐ ܕܥܡ ܕܘܡ̣̈ܣܬܝܩܐ ܕܗܘܦܪܟܐ ܕܡܕܝܢܬܐ “that is, of the clergy together with the secular functionaries, namely, the komentaresioi and the taxeotai accompanying the domestikoi of the urban prefect”: John offers us here a rare glimpse at some of the officials involved in the closure and reuse of the Severan monasteries in the capital. The key role played by clerics is unsurprising since patriarch John Scholasticus is portrayed in both EH 3.1.5.e and 3.1.11.e as the main instigator of the suppression. Yet according to John, these Chalcedonian clerics did not act alone, but were assisted by officials who belonged, at least partly, to the staff of the (anonymous) urban prefect. The urban prefect emerges thus as one of the magistrates to whom the implementation of the anti-Severan measures had been entrusted; for his area of competence, see Dagron (1974, 226–239). This passage further strengthens our identification of the prefect who had imprisoned the presbyter Stephen in EH 3.1.9.e as the urban prefect. Yet an imperial magistrate would not have ordered officials of his staff to embark on violent actions against the monasteries without a direct command from Justin II. In EH 3.1.5.e, John indeed asserts that the patriarch managed to arouse anger in Justin so that he consented to suppressing the Severans. This imperial order has not been preserved; Dölger and Müller (2009, 9.18) do list it, but they have omitted the testimony of the EH and only quote Michael the Syrian (see 3.1.5.h).

ܩܘܡ̇ܢܛܪ̈ܝܣܐ qomanṭarisē is a transposition into Syriac of commentarienses, a Latin term that entered into Greek as κομενταρήσιοι and thence into Syriac Lampe 1961, 766 Brockelmann 1928, 673. They belonged to the offices of the praetorian and the urban prefectures and of provincial governors and were, as subordinate judicial officers, responsible for criminal trials, the custody of prisoners, and the staff of torturers Miller and Sarris 2018, 1097 n.74 Jones 1964, 587 Sinnigen 1957, 57–59.

ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܐܘܛܐ ṭakseoṭē stems from the Greek ταξεώτης; it is not attested as a loanword in Syriac prior to the EH Butts 2016a, 60; Brockelmann 1928, 275, where John uses it for a second time in EH 3.3.29. This term denotes a category of official belonging to the staff of an imperial magistrate (Miller and Sarris 2018, 98 n.6); for its broad meaning (rendered in Latin as officialis), see Magie (1905, 114). The taxeotai in the capital were probably civilians, as according to Pfeilschifter (2013, 236) the urban prefect had only few troops at his disposal. They were charged, among other tasks, with supervising arrests Michael Whitby and Whitby 1989, 152 n.425 Lampe 1961, 1372. Socrates, EH 7.14.6 (Hansen 1995a, 359–360) suggests that they also served to protect high-ranking officials. Moreover, they are attested in papyri as functionaries who were sent to enforce official demands (P. Grenfell 1.64.3 (Grenfell 1896); PSI 8.939.2 (Vitelli 1927); SB 4707.3 (Preisigke 1915)). This may explain why below (see 3.1.10.i), John refers to taxeotai as part of the troops, as they must have carried weapons in this role. The Chronicon Paschale (written in the 630s) provides an example of a taxeotes working for the urban prefect; it states that such an official was executed in 610 by the new emperor Heraclius (r. 610–641) upon his accession to the throne (Dindorf 1832, 701). In the present chapter, the participation of komentaresioi and taxeotai shows that the recalcitrant nuns were to be criminalized; these officials were probably responsible for the violence directed against the monastic women and for their eventual imprisonment. The passage 3.1.10.i specifies that nuns were first expelled from their monasteries and then handed over to the taxeotai. It appears thus most likely that only members of the clergy entered the monasteries, whereas the secular officials who had accompanied them remained outside, even if John tries to give a different impression.

Apart from these two categories of functionaries, John notes that there were also ܕܘܡ̣̈ܣܬܝܩܐ ܕܗܘܦܪܟܐ ܕܡܕܝܢܬܐ “domestikoi of the urban prefect” involved. The term ܕܘܡ̣̈ܣܬܝܩܐ domestiqē stems from the Latin domesticus, which, in the late antique civil administration, denoted a staff member of a magistrate such as the urban prefect Jones 1964, 602–603 Sinnigen 1957, 31. Their exact responsibilities are unknown, but at least in some cases included jurisdiction (Miller and Sarris 2018, 1003 n.3). In the context of this passage, their participation may have been practical since they were able to quickly pass judgment on nuns who refused to take Chalcedonian communion. The loanword ܕܘܡܣܬܝܩܐ entered Syriac via the hellenized form δομεστικός Butts 2016b, 156; Lampe 1961, 380 and was also used by John’s contemporary Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 8.1 Greatrex 2011, 279 Brooks 1921, 60.4.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.e (HL) 9.21–22
div.p: ܘܒܕܡ̣ܘܬܐ ܕܣܝ̣ܥܬܐ ܕܕܐ̣̈ܒܐ ܕܥܐ̇ܠܝܢ ܘܢ̇ܦܠܝܢ ܒܛܝ̇ܪܐ ܕܐܡ̣ܪ̈ܐ “as a pack of wolves that enters and falls upon a fold of lambs”: The metaphorical depiction of wolves assaulting lambs bears profound resonance with biblical themes. The Old Testament employs this imagery mostly to characterize coercive dignitaries (Ezekiel 22:27 ; Zephaniah 3:3 ), whereas the Acts of the Apostles primarily employ it to denote pseudo-believers who assail the community of those believing in Jesus Christ (Acts 20:29 ). The expression was common for heretics (Bornkamm 1942, esp. 313), cf. Matthew 7:15 ; also John of Ephesus, LES 10 (Brooks 1923, 140.8–9), for adversaries of the anti-Chalcedonians in Persia. Eusebius’s EH adopts this metaphor prominently at the beginning of the work in relation to heretics (1.1.1 (Schwartz 1999, 6.8–9) or 1.1.2 (Wright and McLean 1898, 4.15–16) in the Syriac edition, incorporating formulations from the Peshitta). In contrast to the Syriac version of Eusebius, John articulates this metaphor using a distinct wording in the passage in question here, particularly substituting ܡܪܥܝܬܐ (“a flock”) with ܛܝܪܐ (“a sheepfold”).

div.head: EH 3.1.10.f (SR) 9.24–25
div.p: ܟܕ ܛܥ̣ܝܢܝܢ ܩܠܝ̣ܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܩ̣ܘܪ̈ܒܢܝܗܘܢ܇ ܘܡ̇ܓܪܓܪܝܢ ܘܡ̇ܢܬܦܝܢ ܠܗܝܢ܇ ܕܢܐ̣̈ܬܝܢ ܢܬ̇ܩܪ̈ܒܢ ܡܢܗܘܢ “while the clergy were carrying their Eucharistic offerings, they dragged them along and violently compelled them to come receive communion from them”: ܩܘܪܒܢܐ qurbānā is the common term for denoting the consecrated bread of the Eucharist. The clerics in question here are those introduced in 3.1.10.d and belonged to the Chalcedonian patriarchate. This passage shows that the main reason for their entering the convents was the administration of the Chalcedonian Eucharist to the anti-Chalcedonian nuns, thus confirming the authority of patriarch John Scholasticus over monastic life in the capital (Francisco 2011a, 112–113); see 3.1.11.f and 3.1.36.b. In this regard, there is a clear difference between John’s account in the EH about the measures taken against Severan clerics and monastics in the 570s and his earlier ones in the LES and the Zuqnin Chronicle about the persecution in the eastern provinces in the 520s and 530s. The latter two sources do not relate that anti-Chalcedonians were forced to take the Chalcedonian communion, the sole example being Abraham bar Kaili’s treatment of the priest Cyrus; see 3.1.10.h Witakowski 1996, 34–35 Chabot 1933a, 34–36. Van Ginkel (1995, 176) has noted that by the 570s, the Chalcedonians’ objective appears to have shifted: they were no longer content with expelling anti-Chalcedonians from their churches and monasteries, but they actually wanted to convert and to make them accept the Chalcedonian Eucharist, as is highlighted in this chapter.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.g (SR) 9.28–30
div.p: ܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ܇ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܡ̇ܦܠܓܐ ܠܗ ܠܡܫܝܚܐ ܐܠܗܢ ܠܬܪ̈ܝܢ ܟ̈ܝܢܝܢ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ܇ ܘܪܒܝ̣ܥܝܘܬܐ ܚܠܦ ܬܠܝܬܝܘܬܐ ܩܕܝܫܬܐ ܡ̇ܠܦܐ “the Synod of Chalcedon which divides Christ, our God, into two natures after the union, and teaches a quaternity in place of the Holy Trinity”: Opponents to the dyophysite Christology that was accepted at the council of Chalcedon in 451 argued that the profession of two natures (Godhead and manhood) in Jesus Christ equalled a division of the Son of God into two distinct entities. Accordingly, they often accused Chalcedonians of replacing the divine Trinity with a quaternity, as in this example Price 2009, 1–7 Gray 2005. For similar exclamations in John’s oeuvre of women forced to receive Chalcedonian communion, see EH 3.2.39 and LES 12 (Brooks 1923, 182.10–12).

div.head: EH 3.1.10.h (SR) 10.3–4
div.p: ܘܗܟܢܐ ܡܢ ܩܛܝ̣ܪ ܒܦܘܡ̈ܝܗܝܢ ܩ̣ܘܪܒܢܐ ܡܬ̇ܬܪܡܐ ܗܘܐ “thus, by force the Eucharistic offering was set in their mouths”: As noted above in 3.1.10.f, the administration of the Eucharist by force is only very rarely mentioned in the sources treating the plight of the anti-Chalcedonians in the 6th century. Apart from this passage, the most famous exception has been preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle and originally comes from the lost second part of John’s EH. John describes there the atrocities committed by the Chalcedonian bishop of Amida, Abraham bar Kaili (sed. late 520s–late 550s). According to John, Abraham demanded that a certain Cyrus, an anti-Chalcedonian priest, take communion with him. Since Cyrus refused to do so, the Eucharist was forced into his mouth, yet Cyrus spat it out again. After this, Cyrus was executed on the bishop’s orders, and John claims that this scandalous behavior led to considerable difficulties for Abraham Witakowski 1996, 34–35 Chabot 1933a, 34–36. However, John does not go quite as far in his description of the forced communion of the Severan nuns in 571. He emphasizes that they attempted to resist and that they were constantly lamenting and cursing their persecutors, but he never writes that they spat the Eucharist out of their mouths. Crediting the monastic women with such aggressive behavior would probably not have fit in with his comparison of them to lambs and sparrows a few lines above (see 3.1.10.e). On religious women in John’s works, see van Ginkel (1995, 155–157) and Ashbrook Harvey (1990, 108–133).

John’s focus in this chapter on the nuns’ resistance to taking the Chalcedonian Eucharist fits in well with a broader trend in the 6th century. Ever since the council of Chalcedon in 451, the Eucharistic sacrament had developed into a decisive doctrinal marker for both sides of the Christological debate and gradually received a center place for the formation of new religious identities. Anti-Chalcedonian theologians and authors such as John Rufus, Severus of Antioch, and John of Tella repeatedly argued against members of their communities who did not care whether the Eucharist had been consecrated by a pro- or anti-Chalcedonian priest. They stressed in their writings that salvation depended on taking the correct, that is, anti-Chalcedonian Eucharist and on abstaining from the sullied Chalcedonian offerings Booth forthcoming; Menze 2017; 2008b, 158–165 Booth 2014, 38–42.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.i (SR) 10.10–11
div.p: ܘܐܫ̣ܬܠܡ ܒܐܝ̈ܕܐ ܕܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܝܘܛܐ ܕܪ̈ܗ̇ܘܡܝܐ “she was then handed over to the Roman taxeotai”: ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܝܘܛܐ ṭaksiuṭē, in Greek ταξεῶται, were officials responsible for supervising arrests (see3.1.10.d above). John often uses the term ܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ rhomāyē “Romans” to denote soldiers (see 3.1.9.m). The use of this term in this passage to describe the taxeotai (who had been introduced above as accompanying the domestikoi of the urban prefect) suggests that they may have served in the Roman army, or, at the very least, have carried weapons. Their task was to arrest the nuns who, despite the violence, kept refusing to partake in the Chalcedonian Eucharist. They, and thus probably also the other secular functionaries who had accompanied the Chalcedonian clergy to the monasteries, appear not to have entered the monasteries since they only seized the nuns outside the monasteries.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.j (SR) 10.12–13
div.p: ܘܡܬ̣ܕܒܪ̈ܢ ܠܡܕܝܢܬܐ܇ ܘܡ̈ܬ̇ܦܠܓܢ ܠܒ̈ܬܐ܇ ܘܠܒܝܬ ܚܒ̈ܘ̣ܫܝܐ ܡ̇ܫ̈ܚ̣ܠܦܐ “they were led to the city and scattered there in different houses and places of confinement”: Some of the Severan monasteries were located in the suburbs of Constantinople, as John notes in 3.1.10.a. For the different places of confinement where Severans were imprisoned, see 3.1.5-8.b–3.1.5-8.g; this information of the forceful displacement of monastics is repeated in EH 3.1.11.j and 3.1.36.b.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.k (SR) 10.14–15
div.p: ܕܐܝܟ ܕܡܢ ܪ̈ܟܫܐ ܐܘ̈ܫ̣ܢܐ ܥܗ̇ܪ̈ܐ ܒܗܝܢ ܡܣܬ̇ܥܪ̈ܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ ܡܢ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ “such things were carried out by many as though by lustful stallions”: John implies that the imprisoned nuns were sexually abused by their oppressors. Apart from this passage, rape and other forms of sexual violence against women are hardly ever mentioned in the accounts of the hardship suffered by the anti-Chalcedonians in the 6th century – perhaps unsurprisingly, given that the measures focused on and monasteries and only very rarely targeted laypeople Menze 2008b; van Ginkel 1995, 161–163. But the inhabitants of female convents, such as in this chapter, may well have suffered from sexual violence.

div.head: EH 3.1.10.l (SR) 10.16–18
div.p: ܐܝܟ ܡ̇ܢ ܕܢܛܝ̣ܪ ܗܘ ܕܝܢ̣ܗܝܢ ܠܕܝ̇ܢܐ ܕܟܐܢܘܬܐ܇ ܕܗ̣ܘ ܢܕ̣ܘܢ ܕܝܢ̣ܗܝܢ܇ ܘܢܬܒ̣ܥ ܬܒܥ̣ܬܗܝܢ “as the verdict on them is reserved for the righteous judge who will pass judgment on them and will demand vengeance for them”: John sometimes states that evil that has gone unavenged in this world will be punished at the Last Judgment. He stresses that there will be retribution for the hardship the Severans have to endure and that the Chalcedonian oppressors will have to pay for their misdeeds (van Ginkel 1995, 186–188). Examples include EH 3.1.36.d; 3.1.38.b; 3.2.25–3.2.27; 3.3.1–3.3.6 (all treating illness and death of patriarch John Scholasticus and Justin II); 3.2.28–3.2.29 (narrating the fate of two particularly brutal oppressors); 3.2.30 (the destruction of the Chalcedonian churches in Thrace by the Avars being a just punishment for the desecration of the miaphysite churches).

The whole chapter 3.1.10 is briefly summarized in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:294.b.23–295.b.3; 4:338.b.13–25. His summary accurately reproduces the main points: the Severan monasteries in Constantinople are targeted, nuns are given the Chalcedonian Eucharist by force despite their resistance, some are handed over to soldiers and become victims of crime. The only major point skipped by Michael is John’s concluding assertion that those who have violated the nuns will be punished at the Last Judgment. This omission fits in well with Michael’s general reluctance to talk about the end of the world (Weltecke 2021, 190–191).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter provides more detail on what the EH has so far said about the measures taken against Severan clerics and monastics by patriarch John Scholasticus and Justin II. It partly repeats the assertion of EH 3.1.5.e that the hatred of John Scholasticus towards the Severans was responsible for the start of their suppression and that it was his obnoxious influence on the imperial couple, Justin II and his wife Sophia, which caused them to turn against the Severan leaders and monasteries in the capital. However, the patriarch was not content with suppressing them only in Constantinople; he wrote to other regions to ensure that also there, steps would be taken against them. John narrates, as in EH 3.1.10.f, that monks and nuns were forced to take the Chalcedonian communion and that they were mistreated and removed from their monasteries in case of refusal. He adds that the patriarch and the imperial couple themselves were also visiting monasteries in an attempt to convert them to Chalcedon; moreover, Chalcedonian priests were installed in these monasteries, and John Scholasticus had portraits of himself put on display there. Some of these points are repeated in EH 3.1.36.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
EH 3.1.11.a (SR) 10.24–25
div.p: ܡ̇ܚܦܛܢܐ ܗܟܝܠ ܘܡ̇ܪܒܝܢܐ ܘܣ̇ܥܘܪܐ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܒ̈ܝ̣ܫܬܐ̣ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ. ܗ̇ܘ ܕܐܬܐ̣ܡܪ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܪܝܫܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ ܕܡܕܝܢܬܐ “the one who instigated, fostered, and carried out all these wicked things was John who, as has been said, was the head of the church of the city”: Patriarch John Scholasticus (sed. 565–577) is portrayed as the one mainly responsible for the hardship John of Ephesus and his co-religionists had to suffer in Constantinople. As in EH 3.1.5.e, where Scholasticus is introduced for the first time, John asserts that the patriarch aroused Justin’s anger towards the Severans.

div.head: EH 3.1.11.b (SR; HL) 11.3–4
div.p: ܘܠܘ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܕܒܡܕܝܢܬܗ ܘܒܫܘܠܛܢܗ܆ ܐܠܐ ܘܐܦ ܠܐܬܪ̈ܘܬܐ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܟܬ̇ܒ ܗܘܐ “not only in his city and his diocese, but he also wrote to other regions”: This is one of very few indications in the EH that the hardship of the Severan clergy may not have been limited to the capital, but perhaps also took place in other regions of the empire (Grumel 1932, 102 N.255). EH 3.2.17 also mentions letters written by John Scholasticus to other ecclesiastical leaders, specifying that they contained orders to act against the Severans, yet we do not know whether these orders were indeed implemented. John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 401) briefly states that there was a persecution of opponents to Chalcedon in Egypt during the reign of Justin II (cf. 3.1.33.b), yet his note is too short and uncertain to connect it to Scholasticus’s letters. Bunce (2023, 79) has remarked that John of Ephesus may have been mainly interested in portraying Scholasticus “as someone with no respect for the institutional processes or traditions of the church” when he mentioned that the patriarch wrote to other regions outside his jurisdiction and hence, so the implication, overstepped his authority.

In fact, in the writings of John, the word ܫܘܠܛܢܐ, translated here as “diocese,” can refer to power in an abstract sense (e.g., EH 3.1.23), but it also denotes a specific sphere of authority (e.g. in this passage and in EH 3.4.43), particularly in relation to bishops or the jurisdiction of a city (e.g., EH 3.3.36). In general, the term is frequently used to denote an administrative district, including ecclesiastical organization, especially when referring to patriarchs (R. Payne Smith 1879, 4179). By employing the term ܫܘܠܛܢܐ “diocese” John distinguishes between a bishop’s legitimate sphere of authority (for a similar usage, see Vita Rabbulae (Overbeck 1865, 187.3)) and what he wants his readers to see as infringements on other dioceses.

Against this background, Bunce’s argument has some logic to it, yet at the same time, it does not appear clearly from this passage that Scholasticus really issued orders to other patriarchs. He may just as well have written to the churches of Thrace, Pontus, and Asia, thus to regions which had their own bishops and were not directly subjected to his episcopal authority, but still belonged to the sphere of influence of the patriarchate of Constantinople. Furthermore, even if he did issue orders, and not just encouragements, with regard to the suppression of the Severan hierarchy and monasteries, such orders need not necessarily have meant an overreach beyond his authority. After all, the other patriarchs could safely assume that instructions they received from the head of the church of the capital also reflected the wish of the emperor.

div.head: EH 3.1.11.c (SR) 11.5–8
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܡܟܝܠ̣ ܗ̣ܘ ܩܢܘ̣ܡܐܝܬ ܢܦ̇ܩ ܗܘܐ܇ ܘܡܬܟ̣ܪܟ ܒܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܕܓܒܪ̈ܐ ܘܕܢܫ̈ܐ ܘܐܦ ܒܒ̈ܬܐ܇ ܘܥ̇ܨܐ ܗܘܐ ܘܐ̇ܠܨ ܠܘܬ ܫܘܬ̇ܦܘܬܐ ܕܥܡܗ ܠܡܐ̣ܬܐ “from that time on he personally went out and toured all the monasteries – as well as the houses – of men and of women, compelling and constraining them to come commune with him”: In the previous chapter EH 3.1.10, John stated that unspecified clerics, assisted by lay officials with connections to the urban prefect, had entered the monasteries of the Severans in Constantinople (see 3.1.10.d). Here, however, he adds that patriarch John Scholasticus himself also participated in the endeavor to force them into accepting Chalcedonian communion (Francisco 2011a, 112–113). The information on John Scholasticus visiting Severan monasteries was integrated by Michael the Syrian into his Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 2:296.a.8–13; 4:339.b.9–12. On the forceful relocation into Chalcedonian monasteries of the monks and nuns who refused to do so and on the dissolution of the Severan monasteries in Constantinople, see 3.1.10.i–3.1.10.k; 3.1.36.b; Hatlie (2007, 183–186).

div.head: EH 3.1.11.d (SR) 11.12–13
div.p: ܘܦܣ̇ܩ ܗܘܐ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܦܣ̈ܩ̇ܐ ܩ̈ܫ̣ܝܐ ܕܡ̇ܘܬܐ “he passed harsh death sentences against them”: It is likely that this claim is a rhetorical exaggeration, perhaps similar to John of Ephesus’s assertion above in 3.1.11.b that John Scholasticus instigated measures directed against the Severans in regions outside his direct jurisdiction. Nowhere else does the EH state that the patriarch of Constantinople passed death sentences against persons who kept rejecting the Chalcedonian communion (van Ginkel 1995, 174 n.51). The first two books of the EH are replete with examples of Severans being imprisoned and separated from their original communities. As noted in the commentary to EH 3.1.9.l, it is not surprising that some died during their confinement, for instance the priest Stephen in Heraclea, yet the Chalcedonian authorities were certainly wary of creating martyrs and of deepening the very rift between opponents and defenders of Chalcedon they attempted to close.

div.head: EH 3.1.11.e (SR) 11.13–14
div.p: ܐܫ̣ܟܚ ܕܝܢ ܕܢ̇ܛܥܐ ܘܢܥܝ̣ܪ ܘܢܥ̣ܒܕ܆ ܘܠܗܘܢ ܠܡ̈ܠܟܐ ܙܟ̈ܝܐ܆ “he managed to deceive, stir up, and force even the victorious emperors”: Frequently, John uses the plural of ܡܠܟܐ (in the Byzantine context: “emperor”) to refer to the imperial couple, Justin and Sophia. In doing so, he copied the practice of Greek writers, for instance Evagrius, EH 2.3 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 41.1 and 15) (referring to Marcian and Pulcheria as βασιλεῖς). Other examples from Book 1 include EH 3.1.24 and 3.1.26. For the epithet ܙܟܝܐ (“victorious”), see 3.1.4.k.

John asserts here that the patriarch managed to deceive the emperors and to push them towards embarking on a policy hostile to the Severan clergy and monasteries. It is noticeable that John tries to protect Justin throughout Book 1 and puts all the blame on the patriarch (on this, see EH 3.1.5.e).

div.head: EH 3.1.11.f (SR) 11.17–21
div.p: ܘܥ̇ܒܕ ܗܘܐ ܟܢܘ̣̈ܫܝܐ ܘܩ̇ܘ̈ܕܫܐ ܒܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ܇ ܟܕ ܡ̇ܟܪܙ ܗܘܐ ܒܗܝܢ ܠܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ ܦܠ̣ܝܓܬܐ܇ ܘܩ̇ܒܥ ܒܗܝܢ ܝܘܩ̈ܢܘܗܝ ܕܝܠܗ܇ ܘܣܐ̇ܡ ܒܗܝܢ ܩܠܝ̣ܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܕܢܗܘܘܢ ܡ̇ܩܪ̈ܒܝܢ ܒܟܘܠ ܚܕ ܒܫܒܐ ܘܟܘܠ ܥܐܕܐ ܘܕܘܟܪܢܐ ܩ̣ܘܪܒܢܐ “held assemblies and performed consecrations in the monasteries, proclaiming the divisive synod in them and affixing images of himself in them. He appointed clergy to make the Eucharistic offering in them every Sunday and every feast and commemoration”: This is a crucial passage in that it highlights that the Chalcedonian “persecutors,” as they are presented by John of Ephesus, did not only focus on bringing the Severan clergy and monastics into communion, even though John concentrates predominantly on the plight of the imprisoned bishops in Book 1 of the EH. It appears that one of the main aims of the Chalcedonian ecclesiastical leadership was to re-establish their control over all sacred space in the capital, in accordance with Canon 8 of the council of Chalcedon, which had placed the monasteries under episcopal authority Price and Gaddis 2005, 3:97 Schwartz 1962a, 159.34–160.4. However, since the arrival of numerous anti-Chalcedonian bishops, priests, and monks in the 530s (see 3.1.5.a and 3.1.10.b), there had been communities in Constantinople which enjoyed imperial protection despite rejecting the dyophysite doctrine of the capital’s patriarch, thereby challenging his authority. On the possible reasons for why emperor and patriarch turned against long-tolerated places of anti-Chalcedonian communities in 571, including the necessity to react to pressure exerted by intransigent Chalcedonian hardliners, see Roggo (2022, 100–118).

Not only did the Chalcedonian clergy attempt to bring the monks and nuns in these monasteries into communion, as described in detail in EH 3.1.10. According to John, patriarch John Scholasticus also held ܟܢܘ̣̈ܫܝܐ “assemblies” there, probably in the sense of “congregations” for divine worship, and he performed ܩ̇ܘ̈ܕܫܐ “consecrations,” which may refer to his celebrating the Eucharist or to ordinations to the priesthood or the diaconate. Francisco (2011, 112–113) interpreted this passage as indicating a re-consecration of the churches themselves, yet this seems less likely: given that John treated the, in his eyes, scandalous and uncanonical reordination of Severan priests by John Scholasticus in much detail in EH 3.1.12–3.1.18, he would hardly have contented himself with mentioning the re-consecration of churches only so briefly, had this indeed happened. On ܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ ܦܠ̣ܝܓܬܐ “the divisive synod,” that is, the council of Chalcedon, see 3.1.10.g; its proclamation in the converted monasteries is also mentioned in EH 3.1.36.b.

One crucial step for visibly demonstrating the subjection of Severan places of worship under the patriarchal authority of John Scholasticus consisted in affixing images of himself there (Tannous 2018, 37–38). The EH is one of the principle literary sources for images of bishops in late antiquity; images of the patriarchs John Scholasticus and Eutychius (sed. 577–582) are also mentioned in EH 3.1.36; 3.2.27; 3.2.34. As they figure more prominently in these later chapters than here, a more extensive discussion of the display of episcopal images in churches and monasteries is found in the commentary to EH 3.1.36.c. However, in the context of the present chapter, it is important to note that bishops had, as functionaries of the Roman empire, a right to portraits ex officio, just as other magistrates Liverani 2019, 297–299 Kovacs 2014, 213–221 Niewöhner 2008, 169–170. Their portraits were displayed in churches, monasteries, and the episcopal palace (Mundell 1977, 72); repeatedly, bishops also used their portraits for self-promotion, as did John Scholasticus in the passage in question here (Grig 2004, 209–215).

Finally, the installation of Chalcedonian clergy in places of worship that had been used by Severans (also mentioned in 3.1.36.b) aimed at preventing the Severans from reclaiming these places. The regular celebration of the liturgy there by these priests, including the proclamation of the council of Chalcedon, ensured that those inhabitants of the monasteries who had entered into communion with the Chalcedonians remained integrated in the ecclesiastical structures of the patriarchate of Constantinople. On the growing importance in the 6th century of the Eucharist as a doctrinal marker, see 3.1.10.h.

div.head: EH 3.1.11.g (SR) 11.22–23
div.p: ܗ̣ܘ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܩܢܘܡܗ ܡܬ̣ܟܪܟ ܗܘܐ ܒܗܝܢ ܒܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ “the emperor personally toured the monasteries”: No other source independent from John of Ephesus attests that Justin II ever visited Severan monasteries. Nevertheless, John’s information here is likely to be accurate. As stated above in 3.1.5.e, John takes the responsibility for the hardship of the Severans away from the emperor in the first two books of the EH and puts it all on John Scholasticus. There is thus no reason for why he should have included an account of Justin’s visit to the monasteries, unless the emperor really actively participated in the spreading of the acceptance of Chalcedon in the capital. In some later sources, Justin is indeed presented as very hostile towards the Severans, without any mention of his attempts at reconciliation in the first six years of his reign (on which see 3.1.3.r). See, for instance, Eutychius of Alexandria, Annals 25.260 (Breydy 1985, 112.7).

The information that Justin himself was persuaded by John Scholasticus to visit Severan monasteries was summarized by Michael the Syrian and integrated into his Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 2:296.a.8–13; 4:339.b.9–12.

div.head: EH 3.1.11.h (SR) 11.23–24
div.p: ܘܬܘܒ ܒܬܪܗ ܠܝܘܡ ܐܚܪܝܢ܆ ܘܐܦ ܡ̇ܠܟܬܐ ܗܟܘܬ “the day after that, the empress did the same”: Possibly, Justin visited monasteries for men and his wife Sophia those for women, yet John of Ephesus does not specify this. Empress Sophia (d. after 601) played an important role throughout the reign of Justin II. The sources describe her as very influential and as involved in politics to an extent that was perceived as unusual for an empress; after the onset of her husband’s illness in 573 (on which see the commentary to EH 3.3.1–3.3.6), she may even have acted as regent of the empire and negotiated a ceasefire with the Sasanian empire. On her, see Roggo (2024b); Averil Cameron (1975a); van Ginkel (1995, 153–155); PLRE 3:1179–1180 (Martindale 1992). Sophia was a niece of empress Theodora, who held sympathies for the anti-Chalcedonians (see 3.1.10.b). According to EH 3.2.10, there were also rumors that Sophia herself had secretly taken communion from the Severan party in Constantinople prior to Justin’s accession to the throne in 565. Even if this were true, her doctrinal stance had definitely shifted by 571: while John insists in those parts of EH 3 he wrote first (the majority of Book 1 and 2) that the imperial couple was only acting against the Severans after having been tricked by John Scholasticus, he gives them more responsibility in Book 3, written after Justin’s death and Sophia’s fall from power in 578 (van Ginkel 1995, 107–108).

div.head: EH 3.1.11.i (SR) 11.24–26
div.p: ܟܕ ܐܦ ܡ̇ܘܗ̈ܒܬܐ ܝܗ̇ܒܝܢ ܗܘܘ܆ ܘܕܝܪ̈ܝܐ ܣܝ̇ܡܝܢ ܗܘܘ܇ ܠܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܐܬܛܦ̣ܝܣܘ ܐ̇ܘ ܡܬ̇ܛܦܝܣܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗܘܢ “they gave gifts and established stipends for those who had obeyed or would obey them”: Imperial liberality to the monasteries and to those monks and nuns who had agreed to accept the Chalcedonian Eucharist may have served to further tie the formerly Severan establishments to the patriarchate of Constantinople. Both Brooks (1936, 7.20) and Schönfelder (1862, 9.9) translated ܕܝܪ̈ܝܐ in this passage as “monks,” which is indeed the most frequent meaning of this word, thus resulting in “they gave gifts and appointed as monks those who […].” However, as noted by Butts (2016b, 155–156) and Brockelmann (1928, 152), ܕܝܪܝܐ, here used in the plural, might also stem from the Greek loanword διάριον, itself taken from the Latin diarium, meaning “stipend” or “pay.” In the present context, this meaning, although not very common, seems to fit best. An imperial “appointment of monks” would not make much sense, whereas the distribution of rewards to those who accepted the imperial demand, that is, the participation in the Eucharist with the Chalcedonian clergy, does.

div.head: EH 3.1.11.j (SR) 11.26–28
div.p: ܠܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܝܢ ܕܡܬ̣ܚܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ܆ ܠܐܟ̣ܣܘܪ̈ܝܘܣ ܘܠܒܝܬ ܚܒ̈ܘ̣ܫܝܐ ܐ̈ܠ̣ܝܨܐ ܡ̇ܫܕܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ܆ ܐ̇ܘ ܠܫ̈ܢ̣ܕܐ ܕܒܦ̣ܪ̈ܛܘܪܝܐ ܡܫ̣ܠܡܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܕܠܐ ܪ̈ܚ̣ܡܐ “But they sent those who resisted into banishment and oppressive places of confinement or handed them over to torments in the praetoria without mercy”: The relegation of resisting monastics into various places of confinement and their mistreatment there is also stated in EH 3.1.10.i–3.1.10.k and 3.1.36.b; moreover, from the material preserved in Michael the Syrian, it appears that these different places of imprisonment were listed in the lacuna EH 3.1.5-8.b–3.1.5-8.g. For the ܦ̣ܪ̈ܛܘܪܝܐ “praetoria,” in all likelihood referring to the official residences of both the urban and the praetorian prefect, both of which were equipped with prison cells, see 3.1.5-8.c.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p:
This chapter builds on John of Ephesus’s depiction of the illicit actions carried out by patriarch John Scholasticus in his dealings with Severan monks and nuns in the previous chapters EH
3.1.10 and 3.1.11. It opens a sequence of chapters, until EH 3.1.18, which focus on the patriarch’s sudden and, so John insists, uncanonical demand to have those members of the Severan clergy reordained who had given in to the pressure and accepted joint communion with him. John of Ephesus puts great emphasis on the fact that no Severan cleric had entered voluntarily into communion with the Chalcedonian party. He admits that some had eventually done so, but only after the exertion of severe pressure. These clerics had been accepted in the Chalcedonian clergy in their own rank, that is, priests as priests and deacons as deacons. Moreover, they had celebrated the liturgy together with Chalcedonian priests over a prolonged period. All the more surprising and, in the eyes of John of Ephesus, despicable and contradictory was then John Scholasticus’s sudden order to have these clerics reordained, thereby implying that the ordination they had received as Severans was invalid. This decision caused great turmoil, with its canonicity apparently being doubted even among the Chalcedonian clergy. Despite the resistance, John Scholasticus wrote to other bishops, encouraging them to embark on a similar course of action.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:296.a.13–36; 4:339.b.12–28: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.12.i

div.head: EH 3.1.12.a (SR) 12.9–12
div.p: ܕܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܕܠܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ ܟܗ̈ܢܐ ܕܡܢ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ: ܒܟܘܠܗ̇ ܛ̣ܪܘܢܘܬܐ ܒܥ̇ܪܝܪܬܐ ܘܐ̇ܢܢܩܐ ܐܫ̣ܟܚ ܕܢܫ̇ܥܒܕ ܘܢ̣ܕܒܪ ܐܢܘܢ ܒܩ̣ܛܝܪܐ ܠܫܘܬ̇ܦܘܬܗ “with all manner of savage tyranny and compulsion, he had managed to subjugate many priests of the orthodox and to lead them by force to commune with him”: John of Ephesus presupposes here that his readers already know that many Severan priests had entered into communion with John Scholasticus (on him, see above 3.1.5.e). However, such information is nowhere preserved in the previous chapters of the EH as we have them today (EH 3.1.5.h states that priests were imprisoned and Severan places of worship destroyed, but then breaks off). Consequently, we may assume that John first introduced the exertion of force on Severan priests in the lacuna between EH 3.1.5 and 3.1.9. In this case, he might first have written about the actions directed against the Severan clergy with the aim to bring them into communion, then have mentioned at least one example of resistance to this demand (the priest Stephen in EH 3.1.9), before turning his attention to the fate of the Severan monasteries in EH 3.1.10 and 3.1.11. It is noticeable that there appears to have been a sizeable number of Severan clerics who gave in to the pressure, despite John’s repeated claims (for instance in EH 3.1.20 and 3.1.29) that most priests and bishops always persevered in their rejection of Chalcedon. There is a distinct possibility that the period of joint communion described here only took place after the Severan bishops, including John himself, had accepted Justin’s edict on faith (see EH 3.1.24.l).

div.head: EH 3.1.12.b (SR) 12.13–14
div.p: ܐܬ̇ܩܒܠܘ ܡܢܗ ܒܛܟ̣ܣܐ ܕܟܗܢܘܬܗܘܢ: ܟܕ ܩܫ̈ܝܫܐ ܐܝܟ ܩܫ̈ܝܫܐ ܡܢܗ ܐܬ̇ܩܒܠܘ “they were admitted by him into their priestly order, with the presbyters being admitted as presbyters by him”: This was normal practice in late antiquity.
