Clergy in the tradition of Severus of Antioch and Theodosius of Alexandria abandoning their stance and accepting the dyophysite tenets of the imperial church, including the orthodoxy of the council of Chalcedon, were usually allowed to retain their clerical rank (van Ginkel 1995, 115). An important source for this practice is a passage in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.31 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:265; 4:321, probably based on the lost chapters at the start of the EH’s Book 4 Flusin 2010, 297 van Ginkel 1995, 83 n.81. This passage states that most Chalcedonian bishops accepted the priesthood of converted Severan priests, with only the “most intolerant and insensible” bishops (probably an allusion to John Scholasticus) considering their ordination invalid and hence insisting on a reordination. In fact, John of Ephesus’s account in EH 3.1.12–3.1.18 is the only example we have from late antiquity of enforced reordinations of formerly Severan priests and bishops. This was not demanded by canon law; there is no mention of reordination (or rebaptism) of moderate anti-Chalcedonian converts in John Scholasticus’s own compilation of the ecclesiastical canons, the Synagoga L titulorum (Beneševič 1937), which he wrote around 550 Troianos 2017, 142–144; 2012, 118–120. Nor did this practice change in the century to follow: Canon 95 of the Quinisext Council of 691/692 Price 2020, 167–170 Ohme 2013, 57–58, dealing with the reception into the church of converts, accepts the Severan sacraments and merely demands that such moderate anti-Chalcedonian converts condemn their former “heresy.” An identical stance is adopted in the heresiological work De iis qui ad ecclesiam accedunt of Timothy of Constantinople, who most probably wrote in the 7th century (PG 86/1:41 and 72 (Migne 1857–1866)). Whether or not converts embracing Chalcedonian orthodoxy were in need of rebaptism or reordination depended on how serious their previous “heresy” was judged from a Chalcedonian perspective Brandes 2013, 78–79 Milaš 1905, 287–289 and 557–560. Severan converts were generally looked upon quite favorably since they already shared the acceptance of the first three Ecumenical councils with the Chalcedonians (cf. Roggo 2022, 108–109). For the debate about the validity of “heretical” sacraments in the Severan communities, see Ford (2016, esp. 112–164).

div.head: EH 3.1.12.c (SR) 12.15–16
div.p: ܘܒܣ̣ܕܪܐ ܥܡܗܘܢ ܒܐ̣ܝܪܛܝܘܢ ܝܬ̇ܒܝܢ ܗܘܘ “and sitting with them in a row in the hierateion”: In the context of a ܣ̣ܕܪܐ “row,” hierateion here refers to the sanctuary of a church, that is, the main apse only accessible to the clergy, where the altar is located. In John of Ephesus’s time, the apses of the patriarchal churches in Constantinople were all equipped with a synthronon (σύνθρονον), a structure of semicircular benches arranged in several rows reserved for the clergy Mathews 1971, 32–33; 85–86; 99; 109; on the synthronon in general, see Altripp (2000).

John emphasizes that those Severan priests who had given in to the pressure and come to celebrate the Eucharist with the Chalcedonian clergy were first treated as equals; they sat on the benches of the synthronon together with their Chalcedonian peers. By highlighting this, John renders the subsequent order of the patriarch to have them reordained (see 3.1.12.f) all the more condemnable since this order appears thus to be at odds with his previous acceptance of the converts’ priestly rank.

div.head: EH 3.1.12.d (SR) 12.18–20
div.p: ܠܘ ܚܕܐ ܙܒ̣ܢ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܘܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ: ܐܠܐ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܬܠܬܝܢ ܘܫܬ ܙܒ̈ܢܝܢ ܒܟܠܗܝܢ ܣܘ̣ܢ̈ܟܣܝܣ ܕܥܐܕܐ “not only once or twice, but up to thirty-six times in all the festal synaxeis”: John of Ephesus further underlines the wrongness of John Scholasticus’s sudden demand for reordinations (Bunce 2023, 79). In John’s view, such a demand would have been more understandable (albeit still against the canons) if the patriarch had insisted on it from the beginning. However, initially, the patriarch seems to have had no problem with the participation of the Severan clerical converts in ecclesiastical life during a prolonged period. Not only were the priests allowed to sit in the sanctuary in a way that they became indistinguishable from the Chalcedonian clergy, John also states (in this chapter and again in 3.1.18.n) that both deacons and priests joined the Chalcedonian clergy up to thirty-six times in synaxeis (a loanword from the Greek συνάξεις Alexopoulos 2023, 533 Lampe 1961, 1302–1303) before the patriarch changed his mind. These synaxeis (celebrations of the Eucharist) are qualified as ܕܥܐܕܐ, that is, “festal”; this indicates that they took place at a high point of the year, and indeed 3.1.18.n specifies that the period of joint celebrations happened during the Easter period.

div.head: EH 3.1.12.e (SR) 12.23–25
div.p: ܐܝܟ ܡ̇ܢ ܕܐܝ̣ܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܘܐܦ ܛܠܝܐ ܥ̇ܠܝܡܐ܇ ܘܬ̇ܙܝܙܐܝܬ ܟܕ ܡܫܬ̇ܩܠ ܒܫܘ̣ܒܗܪܐ܇ ܘܪܘ̣ܐ ܒܫܘܠܛܢܐ ܘܒܡ̇ܫܩܠܘܬܐ “as though he were a young boy pompously elated in his pride and drunk on power and conceit”: The same phrase, comparing John Scholasticus to a young and inexperienced boy unfit for the patriarchal office occurs again in EH 3.2.31. In the latter passage, the turmoil and the lies which had, in John of Ephesus’s view, been characteristic for John Scholasticus’s patriarchate are attributed to his lack of experience and to his unfamiliarity with a clerical office due to his legal background. Similarly, here, the measures he took against the Severan clergy are devalued as unworthy of a patriarch. The EH’s remark is merely defamatory and does not allow us to infer that he was still relatively young when he became patriarch in 565. We are not informed about the birth and death dates of John Scholasticus. Given that he had already embarked on a successful career as a lawyer in around 550 (Troianos 2017, 142–144), he had probably about the same age as his predecessor on the patriarchal throne, Eutychius (sed. 552–565; 577–582), who was born in 512.

div.head: EH 3.1.12.f (SR) 12.25–28
div.p: ܦܩ̣ܕ ܟܕ ܐܡ̇ܪ. ܕܟܠܗܘܢ ܠܡ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܫܠ̣ܡܘܢ ܠܢ ܡܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܠܩ̣ܘܒܠܢ܇ ܦܩ̇ܕܝܢ ܕܢܫ̣ܬܪܘܢ ܡܢ ܟܗܢܘܬܗܘܢ ܩܕܡܝܬܐ ܘܢܗܘܘܢ ܟܗ̈ܢܐ ܡܢܢ ܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ “he issued an order, saying: ‘All of those who were opposed to us who have now acquiesced to us are ordered to be deposed from their original priestly rank and to become priests again through us’”: This order of John Scholasticus is listed in Grumel (1932, 102–103 N.256). As stated above in 3.1.12.b, the ecclesiastical canons do not prescribe reordination for Severan anti-Chalcedonian clergy accepting the orthodoxy of the council of Chalcedon; John Scholasticus’s order is unique in late antiquity. Van Ginkel (1995, 175 n.56) hypothesized that the patriarch may have wanted to reordain those clerics because they had received their original ordination from anti-Chalcedonian bishops who had been expelled from their sees after the accession of Justin I and Justinian and were thus regarded as illegitimate (see 3.1.5.a and 3.1.10.b). However, this does not explain why John Scholasticus first accepted the converts among his own clergy, apparently even for a prolonged period. John Scholasticus had been involved in negotiations with Severans for several years prior to 571 (Averil Cameron 1976a) and he was therefore certainly aware of the fact that there had been many ordinations to the priesthood performed by bishops who were not recognized by the imperial church (see 3.1.5.e). An example of this is the ordination of dozens of priests by the deposed Severan bishop John of Hephaestopolis (Honigmann 1951, 165–167) with the assistance of John of Ephesus himself in the early 540s (LES 25 (Brooks 1924, 336–337)).

Roggo (2022, 105–110) has presented an alternative explanation for the patriarchal order: he places this order after the debate regarding Justin’s edict on faith (EH 3.1.19–3.1.24) and argues that after the Severan bishops had agree to the union, John Scholasticus was forced to give in to the pressure of an important faction in Constantinople, consisting of intransigent Chalcedonian hardliners (for instance the circle around the quaestor Anastasius; see EH 3.1.19.j). These hardliners may have insisted that all anti-Chalcedonians be indiscriminately equated to the followers of Eutyches (fl. c. 450), even including the moderate ones who were, like John of Ephesus and his peers, in the tradition of Severus of Antioch and Theodosius of Alexandria. Eutyches was regarded as an extreme miaphysite denying Jesus Christ’s consubstantiality with mankind (Price and Gaddis 2005, 1:25-29). Imperial legislation (Codex Iustinianus 1.5.8; (Blume and Frier 2016, 194–199)) decreed that the ordinations of his followers were to be regarded as invalid, and this constitution might have served as the legal foundation of John Scholasticus’s demand. Roggo holds that by suddenly insisting on reordinations, the patriarch endorsed the demands of that pressure group and ensured that they remained loyal to him. This led to the failure of the union and to the withdrawal of the Severan bishops from the joint communion (see the summaries to EH 3.1.19 and 3.1.26–3.1.28). Yet in this way, John Scholasticus may have prevented a further weakening of his authority which had been challenged ever since his accession in 565 because of the disputed deposition of Eutychius who may still have had supporters (see 3.1.38.e).

div.head: EH 3.1.12.g (SR) 12.31–13.2
div.p: ܘܥܒ̣ܕ ܐܢܘܢ ܗ̣ܘ ܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ ܠܟܠܗܘܢ ܕܐܫ̇ܬܘܬܦܘ ܠܗ܇ ܟܕ ܫܓܝ̣ܫܝܢ ܘܡ̇ܕܠܚܝܢ ܘܡ̇ܚܪܡܝܢ ܠܗ ܘܡ̇ܓܕܦܝܢ ܥܠܘܗܝ܇ ܘܥܠ ܟܗܢܘܬܗ ܕܕܠܐ ܢܡ̇ܘܣ “he reordained all those who had communed with him, even as they, agitated and troubled, condemned and reviled him and his lawless priestly ordination”: The controversial reordination to the priesthood of Severan clergy is the central topic of the following chapters EH 3.1.14–3.1.18. In these chapters, John of Ephesus discusses the fate of three bishops in particular, who were all confronted with the demand for reordination and resisted to a various degree: Paul of Aphrodisias (3.1.14), Elisha of Sardis (3.1.15), and Stephen of Cyprus (3.1.16). The culmination of John’s account on the reordinations is reached in 3.1.18, when the Severan bishops confront John Scholasticus in his palace and prove to him in a heated discussion that his demands are contrary to the ecclesiastical canons and hence worthy of condemnation. On the literary construction of this part of the EH, see Bunce (2023, 82–83).

div.head: EH 3.1.12.h (SR) 13.3–6
div.p: ܟܕ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ ܘܡܢ ܒ̈ܢܝ ܨܒܝܢܗ ܡ̇ܬܥܕܠܝܢ ܗܘܘ܇ ܒܥ̣ܒܪ ܢܡ̇ܘܣܐ ܘܕܠܒܪ ܡܢ ܟܠܗ ܛܟ̣ܣܐ ܩܢܘܢܝܐ ܕܒܝ̣ܫܐܝܬ ܗܘ̣ܐ ܡܢܗ ܘܒܥ̇ܪܝܪܐܝܬ “while many – even some of his partisans – condemned the transgression of the law and that which had happened wickedly and savagely through him against the canonical order”: John of Ephesus asserts here that the patriarch’s demand for reordination of the Severan converts was not even accepted unanimously by his own clergy. As the EH is the only source for the ecclesiastical events of 571, the reliability of this piece of information is impossible to judge; Bunce (2023) and Roggo (2022, 100–112) have shown that there are good reasons for assuming that John’s account is a careful literary construction and far from trustworthy. The EH repeatedly highlights that both John Scholasticus and, after 577, Eutychius encountered opposition to their plans within the imperial church (for instance, apart from the present chapter, in EH 3.2.34–3.2.36; 3.2.40; 3.2.51–3.2.52; 3.3.17–3.3.19). In this way, John effectively put the validity of their actions into question and undermined their legitimacy, denigrating them as innovators with a propensity for doctrinal positions he considered as heretical. However, despite these clear literary motifs in the EH, it remains possible that John Scholasticus was never able to win the full support of the church of Constantinople due to the continuous claim to the patriarchal throne of his deposed predecessor Eutychius Roggo 2022, 6–16 and 177–81 Averil Cameron 1988. His religious policy may thus indeed have been disputed among the Chalcedonians themselves.

div.head: EH 3.1.12.i (SR) 13.7–9
div.p: ܘܐܦ ܬܘܒ ܟܬ̣ܒ ܠܐܬܪ̈ܘܬܐ܆ ܘܚ̇ܦ̣ܛ ܠܐ̈ܦܝܣܩܦܐ܇ ܕܐܦ ܗܢ̣ܘܢ ܒܕܡ̣ܘܬܗ ܘܐܟܘܬܗ ܢܬ̇ܚܫܚܘܢ “he also wrote to other regions and exhorted the bishops to behave like and as him”: In the previous chapter EH 3.1.11, John of Ephesus states that patriarch John Scholasticus wrote to other bishops, encouraging them to crack down on the Severan monasteries just as he himself did (see 3.1.11.b). The present passage is similar in that John asserts that the patriarch again wrote to other bishops, this time asking them to join him in his endeavor of reordination of the Severan clergy (Grumel 1932, 103 N.257). As in the previous chapter, the EH does not specify to whom exactly John Scholasticus wrote these letters, whether to bishops belonging to the patriarchate of Constantinople or to the other patriarchs. The dispatch of these letters is presented in such a way as if John Scholasticus had already known that his demand for reordinations was against the canons and as if he deliberately wished not to be the only wrongdoer, but to implicate other bishops in his illicit action. John Scholasticus may well have encouraged other bishops to embark on a suppression of the Severan clergy (see EH 3.2.17), yet his supposed motivation for doing so is clearly just a malicious attempt by John to denigrate him.

Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:296.a.13–36; 4:339.b.12–28 preserved an accurate summary of this chapter of the EH. Just as the EH, he highlighted the excessive pride of John Scholasticus and the depravity of his idea to suddenly insist on reordinations after first having accepted the Severan converts among his own clergy for thirty-six celebrations of the Eucharist. Michael also included short mentions of the two orders of John Scholasticus listed in Grumel (1932, 102–103 N.256–257).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: The positioning of this chapter within John’s narration in Book 1 of the EH is somewhat peculiar. It focuses on the vision of an anonymous holy man (inspired by the Book of Ezekiel 40:2 ) of a broad mountain with many splendid churches. He then saw an attack by patriarch John Scholasticus who destroyed and suppressed the thriving Severan communities on that mountain. The truthfulness of this vision, so John claims, became apparent only a few days later when John Scholasticus indeed started his violent measures. The short account of this vision interrupts the sequence of chapters EH 3.1.12–3.1.18 which deal with the patriarch’s order to have the Severan clerics reordained. Moreover, the account of the hardship of the Severans because of John Scholasticus’s hatred towards them already starts in EH 3.1.5. From a chronological point of view, we might thus have expected the narration of this vision earlier, for instance after EH 3.1.4 with its quotations from the Old Testament.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:296.a.37–297.a.5; 4:339.b.28–33: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.13.a

div.head: EH 3.1.13.a (SR) 13.14
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܬܠܬܥܣܪ̈ “Chapter 13”: This chapter is briefly summarized in the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:296.a.37–297.a.5; 4:339.b.28–33. Michael succinctly states that a blessed man had a vision of a mountain with many embellished churches which were then all destroyed by John Scholasticus. Unlike the EH, Michael does not explicitly mention that this vision immediately came to be fulfilled, but as in the EH, this short narration awkwardly interrupts the passages dealing with the reordination of the Severan clerics.

div.head: EH 3.1.13.b (SR) 13.17
div.p: ܠܐܢܫ ܗܟܝܠ ܛ̇ܘܒܢܐ ܡ̇ܝܬܪܐ “a certain blessed and excellent person”: The identity of the person who is said to have had this vision is not revealed. The account may evidently be a literary creation by John of Ephesus, especially given its biblical allusions (on which see 3.1.13.c). Another example of a vision seen by an anonymous pious and God-loving person, with regard to the death of Justinian and his punishment because of his heterodoxy, is preserved at the very end of Book 9 of Michael’s Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 2:281; 4:331. This is possibly taken from the lost second part of John’s EH (see 3.1.3.m above) (van Ginkel 1995, 54–66). In the third part of the EH itself, there is just one other example of a vision, namely the apparition to John of Ephesus himself of an angelic youth when he was imprisoned in the hospice of Eubulus (EH 3.2.6) (van Ginkel 1995, 193–194).

div.head: EH 3.1.13.c (SR) 13.19–22
div.p:
ܚ̣ܙܐ ܗܘܐ ܓܝܪ܆ ܕܛܘ̣ܪܐ ܚܕ ܐܝܬ ܗܘ̣ܐ ܪܒܐ܆ ܕܡܬ̣ܚܐ ܐܝܬ ܗܘܐ ܠܗ̣ ܣܓܝܐܐ. ܒܓ̇ܒܗ ܕܝܢ ܕܗܢܐ ܬܝܡܢܝܐ܆ ܫܬ̣ܠܐ ܪܒܐ ܐܝܬ ܗܘܐ̣ ܕܥܕ̈ܬܐ ܣܓܝܐ̈ܬܐ “this is what he saw: There was a single large mountain which was very broad. On the southern side of this mountain, there was a large grove of many churches”: Van Ginkel (1995, 84) wrote that this passage has “a biblical ring” to it. However, neither he nor Brooks (1936) and Schönfelder (1862) noticed that John of Ephesus was inspired for this vision by the Book of Ezekiel 40:2
where the New Jerusalem is promised and revealed to the prophet (Joyce 2009, 222–223); for the Peshitta version, see (Gillian et al. 2015, 240–241). Crucially, both the city in Ezekiel’s vision and the many churches seen by John’s anonymous blessed man are located on the southern slope of a high mountain. The large number of splendid churches have no parallel in Ezekiel’s vision, yet the fact that John located these in the vision in exactly the same place on the mountain where Ezekiel saw the New Jerusalem is evidently meant to highlight that the Severans are favored by God. As van Ginkel (1995, 193) noted, this vision conveys a divine warning about the impending hardship to the anti-Chalcedonians and thus shows that God is on their side. John’s allusion to the Book of Ezekiel fits in well with the focus he lays on eschatology in EH 3.1.3.e and 3.1.4.b).

div.head: EH 3.1.13.d (SR) 13.24–26
div.p: ܘܚ̣ܙܐ ܗܘܐ ܕܡܢ ܫܠܝ܆ ܗܐ ܡ̣ܛܐ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ ܕܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ܆ ܥܡ ܩܠ̣ܝܪܘܣ ܘܥܡܐ ܣܓܝܐܐ ܕܥܡܗ “he saw John, bishop of the imperial city, suddenly come along with his clergy and a multitude of people alongside him”: This part of the vision links back to events that John of Ephesus has already discussed in previous chapters of the EH. On patriarch John Scholasticus and his instigation of measures directed against the leadership of the Severan movement in Constantinople, see EH 3.1.5.e–3.1.5.h and 3.1.11.a–3.1.11.f. By focusing here on the patriarch and the people, but leaving the officials, who had played a major role in EH 3.1.10, unmentioned, John once again emphasizes the interpretation given in EH 3.1.11 that all evil came from John Scholasticus.

div.head: EH 3.1.13.e (SR) 13.27–29
div.p: ܘܫ̇ܪܝ ܡܥ̇ܩܪ ܘܡ̇ܣܚܦ ܠܟܠܗܝܢ ܥܕ̈ܬܐ ܗܠܝܢ. ܘܥܩ̣ܪ ܘܣ̣ܚܦ ܐܦ ܡܕܒ̈ܚܝܗܝܢ ܕܟܠܗܝ̣ܢ ܥܕܡܐ ܕܫ̇ܠ̣ܡ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ “he began uprooting and demolishing all the churches. He tore out and cast down all of their altars until he had obliterated the churches”: The destruction and desecration of the Severan places of worship is a recurrent topic in the EH when John of Ephesus describes the various instances of persecution in the 570s. Other passages where John mentions that churches were uprooted and altars torn out include EH 3.2.37; 3.3.16; 3.3.20 (referring to persecutions of the Severans under patriarch Eutychius (sed. 577–582)).

div.head: EH 3.1.13.f (SR) 14.1–3
div.p: ܠܟܠܗܘܢ ܟܢ̈ܘ̣ܫܝܐ ܣܒ̈ܝ̣ܣܐ ܕܥ̈ܕܬܐ ܕܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ܇ ܕܒܗ̇ ܒܡܕܝܢܬܐ ܘܒܟܠܗܘܢ ܚ̣ܕܪ̈ܝܗ̇ “all the densely populated communities of the churches of the faithful which were in the city and all its suburbs”: The Severan communities in the area of Constantinople had benefited from a relatively peaceful period during most of the reign of Justinian and the early years of Justin II, as John of Ephesus states in EH 3.1.4.k and 3.1.5.a. In those passages, John used almost the same terms as here, namely ܟ‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍‍ܢ‍̈[ܘ]ܫ̈ܝܐ ܕ[ܟܠܗ̇] ܥܕܬܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ (“communities of the [whole] church of the orthodox”). For this reason, the suggestion of Brooks (1936, 9 n.1) to understand ܟܢ̈ܘܫܝܐ here rather as ܒܝܬ ܟܢ̈ܘܫܝܐ (“buildings” or “gathering places”) appears unnecessary.
For the ܚܕܪ̈ܐ “suburbs” of Constantinople, see 3.1.4.k.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: EH 3.1.12 first introduced John Scholasticus’s campaign to reordain those Severan clerics who had given in to the pressure and entered into communion with the Chalcedonians. The present chapter gives a concrete example of such a Severan bishop, Paul of Aphrodisias, who was arrested, brought to Constantinople, forced into communion by John Scholasticus, then sent back to Caria and reordained by the Chalcedonian metropolitan of Aphrodisias to become (Chalcedonian) bishop of another city, Antioch on the Maeander. The repetition of the ordination, as if Paul had been a layman, proved very controversial and many Severan clerics criticized Paul for having accepted this reordination. The story of Paul is repeated and more details are provided in EH 3.2.42 and 3.2.43.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:297.a.6–15; 4:339.b.33–41: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.14.a

div.head: EH 3.1.14.a (SR) 14.5
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܒܬܥܣܪ “Chapter 14”: Michael the Syrian preserved a summary of this chapter in his Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:297.a.6–15; 4:339.b.33–41. All the essential pieces of information from the EH are repeated, with the exception of the statement that Paul was the bishop of Aphrodisias; Michael merely calls him “bishop” and does not specify the place. At the end of his summary, Michael added that Paul “died from his grief” (ܘܡܢ ܥܩܬܗ ܡܝܬ), yet John did not, in EH 3.1.14, include any information about Paul’s death. However, he did do so in EH 3.2.42, where he repeated many elements given in the present chapter. In all likelihood, Michael took the information about Paul’s death from that chapter. This element on Paul’s death, missing in EH 3.1.14, may also indicate that Paul was still alive when John wrote the start of Book 1 (prior to the death of John Scholasticus on 31 August 577 and possibly as early as 573 or 574), but not anymore when he composed the end of Book 2 (after the death of John Scholasticus, but prior to the death of Justin II on 4 October 578). On the dating of the EH’s single parts, see Roggo (2022, 103–104) and especially van Ginkel (1995, 71–77).

div.head: EH 3.1.14.b (SR) 14.7–8
div.p: ܦܘܠܘܣ ܗܟܝܠ ܗܢܐ ܕܐܦ ܩܕܡ ܩܠܝܠ ܒܥܘܗܕܢܐ ܕܥܠܘܗܝ ܡܢ ܠܥܠ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚܢ “now, as for this Paul, whom we mentioned above a little earlier”: Paul was one of the Severan bishops ordained by Jacob Burdʿoyo in the late 550s, probably in about 558. The sole source for his ordination is a short passage in LES 50 (Brooks 1926, 158), stating that Paul was made bishop of Aphrodisias, the metropolis of Caria in south-western Asia Minor. In the title of the present chapter, he is called “Asian” (ܐܣܝܢܐ), yet below in 3.1.14.c, John of Ephesus states that Paul hailed from the province of Caria. Possibly, the reference to “Asia” in the title is not to the province, but to the wider region or to the civil diocese of Asia, to which also the province of Caria belonged. Paul’s case was similar to that of John himself, nominally bishop of Ephesus, and most anti-Chalcedonian bishops of his time, and he appears not to have resided in the city whose bishop he was. This would not have been tolerated by the Chalcedonian church which was enjoying continuous imperial support. Just as his Severan peers in Syria, he seems to have lived mainly in a monastery in the Carian countryside (on that monastery, see 3.1.14.d). Due to his frequent absence from Constantinople, Paul appears only occasionally in the Documenta Monophysitica, a dossier consisting largely of a series of letters sent between the Severan leaders in Constantinople and Syria in the 560s Chabot 1907, 155.23; 195.26; 204.17 see Van Roey and Allen 1994, 267–303. At the time of the events described in this chapter, in 571, Paul was already advanced in age, and he probably died a few years after his conversion to Chalcedon and his reordination Destephen 2008, 780–781 Honigmann 1951, 218–219. The exact year of his death (mentioned in EH 3.2.42) is unknown, yet his death is likely to have occurred in about 575 or 576 since EH 3.2.42 was written in 577/578 (see above 3.1.14.a). John’s statement at the end of EH 3.2.42 that Paul’s soul descended to hell suggests that he did not repent and remained in communion with the Chalcedonians. It is probable that John throughout Book 1 exaggerated the amount of violence involved and that some Severan clerics did not need much pressure to accept Chalcedonian communion, especially after Justin’s conciliatory edict on faith (introduced in EH 3.1.19) (Roggo 2022, 105–110).

John’s claim that he had already mentioned Paul “a little earlier” is puzzling since the present chapter is the first witness to Paul in the EH. It cannot be a reference to EH 3.2.42 since that chapter was only written after Paul’s death. Van Ginkel (1995, 74) hypothesized that John might have mentioned Paul in the lost second part of the EH, yet this does not really sit well with the phrase “a little earlier.” In all likelihood, John had mentioned Paul in the lacuna which caused the loss of the chapters EH 3.1.5-8. These lost chapters were quite substantial and there would easily have been space for a preliminary discussion of Paul (see 3.1.5.i). This is rendered even more probable since Michael’s short summary of these lost chapters includes the mention of a libellus supposedly written by the Severan converts in which they accepted the faith of Chalcedon (see 3.1.5-8.i). However, the only person in the EH forced to write such a libellus was Paul of Aphrodisias (in EH 3.2.43), and we may thus assume that Michael generalized the case of Paul introduced in EH 3.1.5-8 to something that was required of all the Severan clerics.

div.head: EH 3.1.14.c (SR) 14.8–10
div.p: ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܡܢ ܐܬܪܐ ܕܩ̣ܪܝܐ: ܕܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܥܒ̣ܝܕ ܗܘܐ ܘܡ̇ܫ̣ܡܗ ܗܘܐ: ܕܐܦ̣ܪܘܕܝܣܝܕܐ ܡ̣ܛܪܦܘܠܝܣ ܕܩ̣ܪܝܐ “who was from the region of Caria and had been ordained and named the bishop of Aphrodisias, the metropolis of Caria”: The existence of important anti-Chalcedonian communities in Asia Minor including Caria, is suggested by the fact that seven out of the twenty-nine episcopal ordinations performed by Jacob Burdʿoyo took place in Asia Minor Wood 2010, 174 Jones 1964, 968 Honigmann 1951, 172–173. Additionally, Caria (together with Asia, Lydia, and Phrygia) was one of the regions where John of Ephesus himself had been active since the early 540s as a missionary (see EH 3.2.44; 3.3.36; 3.3.37; LES 43 and 47 (Brooks 1924, 659–660 and 681); Zuqnin Chronicle Chabot 1933a, 77–78 Witakowski 1996, 72), and we may presume that he converted those he identified as pagans and heretics in that area to the faith of the anti-Chalcedonian champions Severus of Antioch and Theodosius of Alexandria Roggo 2022, 155–157 Leppin 2017; Saint-Laurent 2015, 75–79 Destephen 2008, 501–504 Menze 2008b, 256–265 Michael Whitby 1991, 126–131 Trombley 1985, 328–332.

div.head: EH 3.1.14.d (SR) 14.11–12
div.p: ܗܢܐ ܟܕ ܒܕܝܪܗ ܒܫܠܝ̣ܐ ܝܬ̇ܒ ܗܘܐ: ܐܝܟ ܕܩ̇ܕܡܢ ܐ̇ܘ̣ܕܥܢ “while this one was quietly dwelling in his monastery, as we previously related”: This refers again to a probable earlier mention of Paulin the lost chapters EH 3.1.5-8 (see 3.1.14.b). The monastery where Paul usually resided as bishop is unknown. Possibly, it was the so-called monastery of Gordiana mentioned in LES 40 (Brooks 1924, 650–651), where Severan monks were buried who had been active as missionaries in Caria. In favor of this interpretation is the fact that the monastery was, according to LES, located close to the city of Antioch on the Maeander, the bishop of which Paul was made on the orders of John Scholasticus (see 3.1.14.f). However, it remains evidently possible that there were also other monasteries in that region where Paul might have resided Nowakowski and Wielgosz-Rondolino 2021, 120 Destephen 2010, 236 n.164 Ruggieri 2009, 212 n.34 Roueché 1989, 144.

div.head: EH 3.1.14.e (SR) 14.12–14
div.p: ܫ̣ܕܪ ܗ̣ܘ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ܆ ܘܠܒ̣ܟܗ ܘܐ̇ܥ̣ܠܗ ܒܦܪ̈ܙܠܐ܆ ܘܚܒܫ̣ܗ ܒܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ ܕܝܠܗ “the patriarch John sent to have him apprehended and led away in fetters and to be imprisoned in his episcopal palace”: Both EH 3.1.11 and 3.2.17 state that patriarch John Scholasticus wrote to other regions in order to instigate also there the suppression of the Severans (see 3.1.11.b). The imprisonment and the deportation from Caria to Constantinople of Paul of Aphrodisias thus presents an example of this patriarchal order. The civil diocese of Asia, to which Caria with its metropolis Aphrodisias belonged, lay in the sphere of influence of the patriarchate of Constantinople, as had been confirmed by Canon 28 of the council of Chalcedon Price and Gaddis 2005, 3:75–76 Schwartz 1965a, 88.28–89.17.

Upon arrival in Constantinople, Paul was imprisoned in John’s episcopal palace next to Hagia Sophia (Hillner 2015, 284). Possibly, Paul was held in custody there around the same time as his Severan episcopal peers, including John of Ephesus himself, who were also imprisoned in the episcopal palace at one point. In the relevant part of EH 3.1.17, there is twice mention of a Paul being imprisoned in the episcopal palace, and possibly, these two mentions refer to both Paul of Antioch and Paul of Aphrodisias (see however 3.1.17.f). Roggo (2022, 105–110) has argued that John Scholasticus only demanded the reordination of the Severan bishops after their acceptance of Justin’s edict on faith (introduced in EH 3.1.19), thus leading to their withdrawal from the communion and to the definitive breakdown of the negotiations between the two parties. If this hypothesis is correct, then Paul of Aphrodisias might have been held in custody in the episcopal palace at the same time as the other bishops, accepted the edict together with them and entered into communion. Finally, the demand for reordinations would have caused the other bishops to withdraw from the union, yet, if John’s account is accurate, Paul of Aphrodisias appears to have remained in communion with the Chalcedonians.

div.head: EH 3.1.14.f (SR) 14.15–18
div.p: ܘܗ̇ܝܕܝܢ ܫ̣ܕܪܗ ܠܒܪ ܠܗ ܠܐܬܪܐ. ܘܟ̣ܬܒ ܘܦܩ̣ܕ ܠܗ̇ܘ ܕܐܝܬ ܒܗ̇ ܒܡܕܝܢܬܐ܆ ܕܢܫ̣ܪܝܘܗܝ ܡܢ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܘܬܗ܆ ܘܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ ܢܥ̣ܒܕܗ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ ܒܐܢܛܝܘܟܝܐ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ ܕܩ̣ܪܝܐ “then he sent him out to the same region. He wrote and ordered someone in the same city to depose him from his episcopal rank and to reordain him bishop in the city of Antioch in Caria”: John Scholasticus ordered the (anonymous) Chalcedonian metropolitan of Aphrodisias to reordain Paul, yet no longer as bishop of Aphrodisias (the city evidently already having a bishop), but of Antioch in Caria (also known as Antioch on the Maeander), about 20 km to the northwest of Aphrodisias (Grumel 1932, 103 N.258). It was common practice for bishops to be ordained by the metropolitan (archbishop) of the province to which their cities belonged, as was confirmed by Canon 28 of Chalcedon Price and Gaddis 2005, 3:75–76 Schwartz 1965a, 88.28–89.17.

This patriarchal order shows how the Chalcedonian authorities may have attempted to integrate the Severan episcopal hierarchy into their own ranks by installing the converts on episcopal sees in cities where there happened to be a vacancy on the episcopal throne, irrespective of Canon 15 of Nicaea which forbade the translation of bishops to other cities (Alberigo 2006, I:27–28) (see also EH 3.1.27.d). However, this does not explain why Paul was apparently first deprived of his clerical status and treated like a layman. After all, canon law did not demand the reordination of Severan converts (see EH 3.1.12.b and, for John Scholasticus’s possible reasons for demanding reordinations, 3.1.12.f). In the EH, the reordination of Paul is consequently presented as illegal and uncanonical since Paul was already bishop. The episode serves thus to highlight “the illegal actions and depraved character of John Scholasticus” (Bunce 2023, 72) and forms a stark contrast to the ordination of the Severan bishops, including Paul, by the hands of Jacob Burdʿoyo, which John of Ephesus describes in the LES as canonically correct Bunce 2023, 72–73 Brooks 1926, 156–158.

div.head: EH 3.1.14.g (NK) 14.19
div.p: ܘܫܪܘܗܝ “they deposed him”: The manuscript reads ܘܫܕܪ ܘܗܝ “they sent him.” However, given the immediate context, it is quite likely that the additional letter ܕ has crept in by mistake and should be deleted, as already considered by Brooks (1936, 9 n.3). In the manuscript, a small space, like that between two separate words, is inserted between the verbal form ܫܕܪ and the 3rd person singular masculine suffix ܘܗܝ. This is a mistake by the scribe, as the suffixed verb must be written as a single word (Brooks 1935, iv).

div.head: EH 3.1.14.h (SR) 14.22–23
div.p: ܘܩ̇ܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗ ܩܠܝ̣ܪ̈ܝܩܘܗܝ̣ ܨܒܝ̣ܥ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܙܒ̈ܢܝܢ “his clerics called him ‘the twice-dipped’”: Paul’s reordination as bishop and his continued communion with the Chalcedonians appears to have led to strong criticism and mockery from his former co-religionists, although John of Ephesus did not include this piece of information in EH 3.2.42, where he repeated Paul’s story in more detail. The word ܨܒܝ̣ܥ “dipped” can be used with reference to water and baptism (Brockelmann 1928, 620), yet it is clear that Paul was “only” reordained to the priesthood (with oil), and not also rebaptized: in EH 3.2.42, John included Paul’s lament prior to his reordination, where he expressed his lack of understanding why the Chalcedonians wanted to reordain and not also rebaptize him.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter belongs to the unit dealing with the reordinations of Severan bishops (EH 3.1.12–3.1.18). It introduces, after Paul of Aphrodisias in EH 3.1.14, a second bishop, Elisha, whom John Scholasticus wanted to reordain. John of Ephesus asserts that similar to Paul, Elisha was subjected to harsh treatment and imprisonment and therefore entered into communion with the Chalcedonians. John Scholasticus then tried to reordain and send him to Sardis in Lydia. However, Elisha offered fierce resistance, arguing that his previous ordination had been valid and that if he was considered to be in need of reordination, then he should also be rebaptized. The patriarch punished Elisha for his disobedience and had him imprisoned in the monastery of Abraham for a prolonged period. Elisha is mentioned again in EH 3.1.17 and 3.2.4.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
EH 3.1.15.a (SR) 14.24
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܚܡܫܬܥܣܪ̈ “Chapter 15”: Michael the Syrian included a summary of this chapter in his Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:297.a.16–26; 4:339.b.41–340.a.4. Michael does not give a single place name, neither of the monasteries where Elisha was imprisoned, nor does he say that John Scholasticus wanted to send him to Sardis. He merely states that the patriarch wished to reordain Elisha and that the latter resisted and was sent into (monastic) banishment; no mention is made of the episode that Elisha was allowed to visit the baths when he had fallen ill. Yet the basic information, focusing on Elisha’s resistance, is rendered accurately, with one interesting exception: whereas in the EH (see 3.1.15.g below), John of Ephesus has Elisha say “even though I am not worthy, I have been ordained a bishop by the orthodox” (ܕܐܢܐ ܐܦܢ ܠܐ ܫ̇ܘܐ ܐܢܐ܆ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ ܥܒ̣ܝܕ ܐܢܐ ܡܢ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܘܟܣܘ), he says in Michael’s Chronicle “since I am worthy, I have been ordained bishop” (ܐܢ̇ܐ ܟܕ ܫ̇ܘܐ ܐܢ̣ܐ. ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܥܒܝ̣ܕ ܐܢ̣ܐ), probably best explained as a pious alteration on Michael’s part.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.b (SR) 14.25
div.p: ܠܣܒܐ ܗܢܐ ܐܠܝܫܥ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ “the old man Elisha, the bishop”: Not much is known about the Severan bishop Elisha. He is encountered for the first time as a signatory of some of the texts forming the dossier known as the Documenta Monophysitica, consisting largely of a series of letters sent between the Severan leaders in Constantinople and Syria in the 560s Chabot 1907, 155.21; 160.20; 195.25; 204.14 see Van Roey and Allen 1994, 267–303. Consequently, his ordination may have taken place in the first year of Justin II (565/566). We do not know if he was ordained, like most other Severan bishops, by Jacob Burdʿoyo. He is not named in LES 50 (Brooks 1926, 153–158) where John of Ephesus lists many bishops Jacob ordained in the late 550s, yet it remains evidently possible that there were more and that he was ordained later. At the same time, Elisha may also have been ordained by someone else, perhaps even by John himself. After all, John assumed a leading role in the Severan community in Constantinople after the death of Theodosius of Alexandria in June 566 (van Ginkel 1995, 33–34). A further argument pointing towards this possibility is the fact that Elisha had a connection with Lydia, as the present chapter suggests – a region where John claimed for himself a decade-long activity as missionary and for which he might thus have felt a particular responsibility (see 3.1.14.c). EH 3.2.44 provides the example of Deuterius, a bishop ordained by John for Caria, a region where John was equally active.

Apart from the Documenta Monophysitica, the EH (and, dependent on it, Michael’s Chronicle) is the only source providing information about Elisha. It is not entirely clear how the events introduced in the present chapter chronologically relate to the other elements of hardship suffered by the Severans in 571. In all likelihood, this chapter’s mention of Elisha’s imprisonment in the patriarchal residence (see 3.1.15.d) refers to the same instance as the confinement of all the Severan bishops there narrated in EH 3.1.17.b Destephen 2008, 257–258 Honigmann 1951, 230–231. Consequently, John Scholasticus’s attempt to have Elisha reordained (see 3.1.15.g) may only have happened after the bishops had entered into communion with the Chalcedonians based on Justin’s edict (introduced in EH 3.1.19) (Roggo 2022, 105–110). In this case, his imprisonment in the monastery of Abraham (see 3.1.15.i) probably only took place after the withdrawal from the union and the definitive breakdown of the negotiations (EH 3.1.25.d and 3.1.29.f), thus at the same time as when Paul of Antioch was also held in custody there (EH 3.2.2), and John of Ephesus in the hospice of Eubulus. The duration of Elisha’s detention in the monastery of Abraham is unknown. According to EH 3.2.7.a, John was imprisoned in the hospice of Eubulus for one year and nine days, thus well into 572. However, although Elisha’s confinement in the monastery of Abraham is described as long-lasting (see 3.1.15.j), his term clearly was shorter than John’s: when Elisha is mentioned again in EH 3.2.4.c, John states that Elisha was already released from prison while he himself was still in the hospice of Eubulus. Elisha’s release appears to have happened because he agreed to return to the Chalcedonian communion. Thereafter he was, according to Michael, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:300.a.3–4; 4:341.b.20–21, allowed to remain in Constantinople. This is the last mention of Elisha. We do not know if he remained in communion with the Chalcedonians permanently afterwards or if he re-joined the Severans, as did his episcopal peer, Paul of Antioch (EH 3.2.3 and 3.2.8). For chronological considerations with regard to the imprisonment of the bishops in 571 and 572, see Roggo (2022, 100–112) and Destephen (2008, 511–515).

div.head: EH 3.1.15.c (SR) 14.26–27
div.p: ܕܝܪܐ ܕܚܒ̣ܝܫ ܗܘܐ ܒܗ̇ ܕܡܬ̇ܩܪܝܐ ܕܒܝܬ ܕܝܘ̣ܣ “the monastery called Beth Dios in which he was imprisoned”: The monastery of Dios (μονὴ τοῦ Δίου), named after the Syrian monk who founded it towards the end of the 4th century, was one of the oldest monasteries in Constantinople. Because of its early foundation, it usually held the second position in the inner-Constantinopolitan monastic hierarchy, just after the oldest monastery, that of Dalmatius (on which see 3.2.9.k). The monastery of Dios had a reputation of being staunchly pro-Chalcedonian; its archimandrites had been engaged in the fight against both the Henotikon at the start of the 6th century and patriarch Anthimus (sed. 535–536) in 536 Hatlie 2007, 117 Janin 1969, 97–99. The monastery’s unswerving support for Chalcedon rendered it an ideal location for the confinement of anti-Chalcedonian clerics such as Elisha. The monastic internment of clerics was an important penalty prescribed by the legislation of Justinian (Hillner 2015, 314–341) and could be understood as a form of banishment, even when it happened in the same city where the imprisoned clerics had hitherto lived, as they were usually not allowed to leave the monastery (see 3.1.5-8.f).

div.head: EH 3.1.15.d (SR) 14.27–28
div.p: ܒܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ ܕܝܠܗ “in his episcopal palace”: On the episcopal palace of John Scholasticus, see 3.1.5-8.e. John of Ephesus mentions repeatedly that Severan bishops, including himself, were imprisoned there. Elisha’s confinement happened probably at the same time as that of John of Ephesus, Paul of Antioch, and Stephen of Cyprus, narrated in EH 3.1.17.f (see 3.1.15.b).

div.head: EH 3.1.15.e (SR) 15.2
div.p: ܐܢ ܡܨ̣ܝܐ̣ ܠܡܬ̇ܦܠܛܘ ܡܢ ܐ̣ܝ̈ܕܘܗܝ “if it might in some way be possible to escape his hands”: John of Ephesus creates the impression that every Severan bishop who entered into communion with the party of John Scholasticus had only done so under pressure and was eager to withdraw as soon as possible (stressed again in EH 3.1.25; 3.1.29; 3.2.1). However, John is clearly apologetic and attempts to hide the fact that he and his peers assumed that a lasting union might be possible on the basis of Justin’s edict on faith (introduced in EH 3.1.19) (Roggo 2022, 100–111).

John provides another example of a Severan bishop who wished to break away again from John Scholasticus in EH 3.2.3 and 3.2.8, namely Paul of Antioch. However, the case of Paul appears to have happened later than that of Elisha: Paul had first been released from the monastery of Abraham (where he had been held presumably together with Elisha; see 3.1.15.b) and returned into communion with John Scholasticus, and then wanted to break away. The case of Elisha described here occurred prior to his and Paul’s confinement in the monastery of Abraham (on which see 3.1.15.i).

div.head: EH 3.1.15.f (SR) 15.3
div.p: ܨܒ̣ܐ ܕܢܫ̇ܕܪܗ܆ ܠܣ̣ܪܕܐ ܡܛܪ̣ܦܘܠܝܣ ܕܠܘܕܝܐ “John wanted to send him to Sardis, the metropolis of Lydia”: This is the only hint about Elisha’s episcopal see. Honigmann (1951, 230–231) argued that Elisha was the Severan bishop of Sardis, parallel to Paul who was bishop of Aphrodisias, the metropolis of Caria, and therefore sent back there to be made bishop in another episcopal see in that province (see EH 3.1.14.f). In the same way, John Scholasticus would have sent Elisha back to Lydia, and the metropolitan of Sardis would have been expected to appoint him bishop in one of the cities of Lydia. This hypothesis remains plausible, yet there is also a second possible interpretation: it is conspicuous that in EH 3.1.14, Paul is sent to Aphrodisias for reordination as a suffragan bishop by the metropolitan there, whereas according to EH 3.1.15, John Scholasticus himself appears to have wanted to reordain Elisha. It is therefore conceivable that the patriarch planned to make Elisha the Chalcedonian metropolitan of Sardis (presupposing a vacancy on that see at the time). Canon 28 of Chalcedon had confirmed that the ordinations of the metropolitans in the civil dioceses of Asia, Pontus, and Thrace were performed by the patriarch himself Price and Gaddis 2005, 3:75–76 Schwartz 1965a, 88.28–89.17. This may provide an explanation for why the (attempted) reordinations of Paul and Elisha were organized differently.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.g (SR) 15.6–11
div.p: ܕܐܢܐ ܐܦܢ ܠܐ ܫ̇ܘܐ ܐܢܐ܆ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ ܥܒ̣ܝܕ ܐܢܐ ܡܢ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܘܟܣܘ. ܘܐܢܬ ܠܝ ܠܓܡ̣ܪ ܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ ܠܐ ܥ̇ܒܕܬ. ܐܢ ܕܝܢ ܘܣ̇ܒܪܬ ܕܕܛܟ̣ܣܐ ܐ̣ܝܬܝܗ̇ ܗܕܐ: ܕܬܫ̣ܪܝܢܝ ܘܬ̇ܥܒ̣ܕܢܝ ܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ܆ ܡܕܝܢ ܫ̣ܪܝܢܝ ܠܘܩܕܡ ܡܢ ܡ̇ܥܡܘܕܝܬܐ ܕܥ̇ܡܝܕ ܐܢܐ܆ ܘܗ̇ܝܕܝ̣ܢ ܒܗܝ̇ ܕܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚ “As for me, even though I am not worthy, I have been ordained a bishop by the orthodox. But as for you, you are not going to reordain me in any way. But if you still deem it orderly to depose and reordain me, then depose me first of the baptism I received and then do it a second time”: John of Ephesus has various bishops repeatedly complain about the illegality of the attempted reordination and the inconsistency that John Scholasticus demanded a reordination to the priesthood, but not also a rebaptism – either all the sacraments they received as Severans are invalid or none. Similar arguments and complaints are presented by Stephen of Cyprus in EH 3.1.16.j, Paul of Aphrodisias in EH 3.2.42, and all the Severan bishops assembled in the episcopal palace in EH 3.1.18.h. As explained in EH 3.1.12.b, the reordination of Severan converts was not demanded by canon law and the bishops’ complaints are thus understandable. On the patriarch’s order for reordinations and his possible reasons, see 3.1.12.f and Roggo (2022, 107–110). Both Bunce (2023, 72–74) and Francisco (2011, 114) have noted that John of Ephesus’s main literary aim in the description of the attempted reordinations was to highlight the illegality and the depravity of John Scholasticus’s actions. In this passage, John presents the patriarch’s elated and presumptuous character in a stark contrast to the modest Elisha who describes himself as unworthy of the episcopate.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.h (SR) 15.11–12
div.p: ܕܐܢܐ ܠܡ ܡܥ̣ܦܪܐ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܪܡ̇ܐ ܐܢܐ ܠܟ “I am only placing the cloak on you”: John Scholasticus supposedly employed this utterance in an attempt to reassure Elisha that he would not be subjected to a full reordination to the priesthood. Such a full ordination would have included an anointment as the center of the sacrament; a repetition of this procedure was considered as humiliating by the Severan bishops given that they regarded their previous ordination as valid (see EH 3.1.12.f). Instead, so John of Ephesus asserts, the patriarch cunningly hoped to weaken Elisha’s resistance by pretending to be content with the bestowal of the episcopal cloak (ܡܥ̣ܦܪܐ). However, it is quite doubtful that John Scholasticus really would have been satisfied with this symbolic gesture: John of Ephesus presents it as a mere ruse on the patriarch’s part, and it is indeed hard to imagine how John could have insisted to his readership in EH 3.1.12–3.1.18 that the patriarch wanted to have the Severan priests and bishops reordained, if there was, in reality, no sacramental action involved in this at all. Moreover, the fact that according to EH 3.1.14.h, Paul of Aphrodisias became widely known as “the twice-dipped” (ܨܒܝܥ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܙܒ̈ܢܝܢ) shows that repetitions of the anointment happened indeed.

“Cloak” (ܡܥ̣ܦܪܐ) is not an exact term and the liturgical vestment in question is not specified. It must have been a vestment distinguishing its bearer as a bishop and for this reason, the most probable candidate is the omophorion (ὠμοφόριον), a long band wrapped around a bishop’s shoulder and still worn today in the eastern churches (Taft 2004). The great importance of the omophorion in the 6th century as a marker for episcopal status is apparent in the Life of Eutychius 2153–2171 (Laga 1992, 69–70), stating that patriarch Eutychius (sed. 552–565; 577–582) never parted from his omophorion, even when he was deposed and in exile, thereby always upholding his claim to the patriarchal throne.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.i (SR) 15.14–15
div.p: ܒܕܝܪܐ ܐܚܪܬܐ ܕܡܬ̇ܩܪܝܐ ܕܒܝܬ ܐܒܪܗܡ “in another monastery called Beth Abraham”: Elisha’s second term of monastic confinement took place in the monastery of Abraham, in the Greek sources often called “monastery of the Abrahamites” (μονὴ τῶν Ἀβραμιτῶν). The monastery, located just outside the city walls at the Golden Gate, had been founded in the first half of the 6th century by the monk Abraham who eventually, probably at a point after 553, was made bishop of Ephesus. However, there is some debate on the exact date and the identity of the founder, and an establishment of the monastery by another Abraham in the late 5th century has also been suggested Destephen 2008, 71–72 Hatlie 2007, 188–189 Berger 1988, 679–681 Janin 1969, 4–6. Elisha was not the sole Severan bishop to be interned there; according to EH 3.2.2, this fate was shared by Paul of Antioch, and the two bishops probably stayed there at the same time (see 3.1.15.b; 3.1.29.f; 3.2.2.c). Bunce (2023, 73–74) has posited that Elisha’s confinement there was less harsh than his first one in the monastery of Dios, the latter being staunchly Chalcedonian, while the former may have harbored anti-Chalcedonian sympathies. Yet this claim is not supported by any source and there is no reason for assuming that Elisha (and Paul of Antioch) were confined in a monastery that was secretly supportive of their cause.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.j (SR) 15.16
div.p: ܘܗ̣ܘܐ ܬܡ̇ܢ ܒܐܘܠܨܢܐ̣ ܙܒܢܐ ܣܓܝܐܐ “he was in tribulation there for a long time”: The duration of Elisha’s confinement in the monastery of Abraham is not specified, yet as stated above in 3.1.15.b, it is very likely to have lasted less than a year. John of Ephesus was probably imprisoned at the same time, and he was in the hospice of Eubulus for one year and nine days (EH 3.2.7.a), yet Elisha was already released when John was still kept there.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.k (SR) 15.17
div.p: ܠܚܡ̇ܝܡܬܐ “to a thermal bath”: The fact that Elisha was allowed to leave the monastery of Abraham when he fell ill indicates again that the Chalcedonians were wary of creating martyrs (see EH 3.1.9.l). The episode of Elisha’s visit to the bath has been noted briefly by Hillner (2015, 338) and van Ginkel (1995, 174 n.51). The identity of the bath in question remains unknown. Elisha may, for instance, have visited the thermal baths in Pythia (modern Yalova on the south-east coast of the Propontis), a popular spa resort in late antiquity regularly frequented by Justinian and Theodora, as stated in Procopius, Buildings 5.3.16–20 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 4:156.10–22); Malalas, Chronicle 18.25 (Thurn 2000, 368.49–52); Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6025 (de Boor 1883, 186.8–13). These baths are also mentioned in EH 3.2.46; moreover, when John relates in EH 3.1.26.d that Justin II went to the baths, this probably also refers to Pythia.

div.head: EH 3.1.15.l (SR) 15.18
div.p: ܘܐܫ̣ܬܪܝ “and he was released to do so”: John of Ephesus appears to display some irony with his choice of verb here. He uses the Syriac verb ܐܫ̣ܬܪܝ ʾeštri“to be released” in a positive sense to denote that Elisha’s demand for a visit to the baths was eventually accepted. Yet earlier in this chapter (see 3.1.15.g), he employs the same verbal root (ܫܪܝ š-r-y) to convey John Scholasticus’s intention to “depose” Elisha (that is, literally, to “release” him from his episcopal rank). The pun lies in the fact that Elisha was not deposed because of the resistance he put up and therefore was imprisoned, yet then in the end he was nevertheless “released,” albeit from his place of detention and thus not in the sense the patriarch originally intended.

div.head: Commentary to EH 3.1.16
div.p: John of Ephesus introduces in this chapter a third bishop after Paul of Aphrodisias (EH 3.1.14 ) and Elisha (EH 3.1.15) whom patriarch John Scholasticus wanted to reordain, namely Stephen. This chapter forms the center of the unit dealing with the reordinations of Severan bishops (EH 3.1.12–3.1.18) and is a careful literary construction; with regard to the chronological order of events, it would be better placed before EH 3.2.3 (see 3.1.16.d and 3.2.3.b). John of Ephesus first records the violent maltreatment of Stephen on one of the Princes’ Islands close to Constantinople as the result of his having rejected reordination. John highlights that Stephen only agreed to accept the Chalcedonian communion out of fear lest those close to him be subjected to similar torture. Stephen was then brought back to Constantinople and again confronted with John Scholasticus’s demand for reordination. Stephen kept resisting and eventually managed to get access to emperor Justin II. Stephen’s long complaint about John Scholasticus in front of the emperor is at the core of this chapter. He stresses that John Scholasticus’s demand for reordinations violates the ecclesiastical canons and is, above all, inconsistent in that the patriarch should declare invalid either all or none of the sacraments the Severans had received, but certainly not solely their ordination to the priesthood. Justin is then said to have immediately reacted to the speech and to have promulgated a law in which he forbade the reordination of the Severan clerics. In all likelihood, Stephen’s speech is a piece of literary fiction by John of Ephesus; with it, he managed to present his own Severan party as winning imperial approval, whereas their opponent John Scholasticus, the head of the imperial church, is convicted of breaking the canons of the ecumenical councils. John repeats and continues Stephen’s story in EH 3.2.3 and 3.2.4.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:297.a.27–298.a.31; 4:340.a.4–37: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.16.a ## Secondary Literature (SR) Bunce 2023, 75–78 Roggo 2022, 100–111 Francisco 2011a, 114–116 Maraval 1998, 457–458 and 466–467 van Ginkel 1995, 115 and 175 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 508–511 Meyendorff 1989, 262–263 Averil Cameron 1976a, 62–65; Frend 1972, 321–323 Honigmann 1951, 221–224

div.head: EH 3.1.16.a (SR) 15.19
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܫܬܬܥܣܪ̈ “Chapter 16”: Michael the Syrian’s summary of this chapter Chabot 1899–1910, 2:297.a.27–298.a.31; 4:340.a.4–37 mainly focuses on Stephen’s speech in front of the emperor, which is reproduced quite accurately. However, Michael decided not to include the end of the chapter where John fo Ephesus asserts that the patriarch used bribery to have the typos pragmatikos withdrawn (see 3.1.16.r). Similarly, the start of the chapter is rendered in a heavily abbreviated manner; the location of Stephen’s imprisonment (the island of Plateia) is not mentioned, nor is the fact that Stephen actually entered into communion with the Chalcedonians out of fear that persons close to him might be mistreated. Interestingly, in Michael’s version, the officials who beat Stephen up are not called excubitores (see 3.1.16.e), but quaestionarii (ܩܣܛܘܢܪ̈ܐ), that is, torturers or executioners. This Latin loanword is well-attested in Syriac Butts 2016b, 140; Brockelmann 1928, 679, especially because of its use in the New Testament: the Peshitta version of Matthew 27:65–66 and 28:11–12 employs this term four times to denote the soldiers who were guarding the tomb of Jesus, probably as the result of a mistake instead of ܩܣܛܘܕܝ and ܩܘܣܛܘܕܝܐ (both preserving the Gospel’s original κουστωδία, “guard”) used in the Sinaiticus and the Harklean versions Kiraz 2004, 1:448 and 452 Brock 1967, 405. Michael’s use of quaestionarii instead of John’s excubitores is probably explained by the fact that he may not have known what excubitores were, yet correctly assumed from the content of John’s chapter that their function, at least in this passage, must have been similar to quaestionarii.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.b (SR) 15.24
div.p: ܠܓܙܪܬܐ ܕܡܬ̇ܩܪܝܐ ܦܠ̣ܛܝܐ “to the island called Plateia”: Plate or Plateia (Πλάτη / Πλατεῖα), in Turkish Yassiada, is one of the smallest islands in the Princes’ Archipelago in the Sea of Marmara to the south-east of Constantinople. It is quite remote within the archipelago itself, located about 5 km to the south-west of the closest larger island Burgazada / Antigone Janin 1975, 67; 1924, 431–433. People were repeatedly exiled there (and to the Princes’ Islands in general) in the Byzantine period Janin 1975, 61–76; 1924. The practice of relegating high-ranking personalities to islands was not very common until the 6th century, yet then quickly became a frequently employed means of removing potential rivals from Constantinople (Hillner 2015, 212 and 233). The first person securely attested to have been held in custody on one of the Princes’ Islands was patriarch Eutychius (sed. 552–565; 577–582), who, in February 565, spent three weeks on Prinkipo, the largest island of the archipelago (Life of Eutychius 1125–1137) (Laga 1992, 38). The Severan bishop Stephen is the second person known to have been relegated to the Princes’ Islands, and the first one to have been sent to Plateia – this chapter of the EH contains indeed one of the earliest mentions of Plateia, together with a poem by Agathias from the mid-6th century, which he dedicated to a church of St Michael there (Greek Anthology 1.34 (Beckby 1957, 142); we owe this reference to Michael Whitby). Plateia occurs in the sources only again in the early 9th century when it was mentioned by Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6209 (de Boor 1883, 396).

div.head: EH 3.1.16.c (SR) 15.25
div.p: ܐ̣ܣܛܦܢܐ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ “the bishop Stephen”: Stephen was one of the Severan bishops usually resident in Constantinople in the late 560s. He repeatedly figures as a signatory in the Documenta Monophysitica Chabot 1907, 155.17; 160.18; 195.22; 204.12 see Van Roey and Allen 1994, 267–303 after 567 and participated, in 570, in a discussion with adherents of John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine (the so-called “tritheists”) chaired by patriarch John Scholasticus Roggo 2022, 99–100 Van Roey 1982, 487–489 Honigmann 1951, 222. We do not know when exactly and by whom he was made bishop; he does not appear in the list of bishops ordained by Jacob Burdʿoyo in LES 50 (Brooks 1926, 153–158). In the present chapter, John of Ephesus has Stephen himself exclaim that he has been bishop for 20 years (see 3.1.16.n), which would place his ordination in c. 551. However, as discussed below, there are reasons to doubt the accuracy of this statement. John might have wanted to exaggerate the length of Stephen’s episcopate so as to render John Scholasticus’s demand for his reordination all the more contemptible (Honigmann 1951, 223). It is possible that he was only ordained in the mid-560s, yet he quickly became an important member of the group of Severan bishops in Constantinople. This is evident from the fact that he was chosen to represent the Severans in the religious discussion in 570; moreover, he signed letters in the Documenta Monophysitica after John of Ephesus, but before all other bishops, including Elisha and Paul of Aphrodisias (Honigmann 1951, 221). We do not know bishop of which city he nominally was, but since John Scholasticus wanted to reordain him as metropolitan of Cyprus (see 3.1.16.h) and since he was indeed eventually dispatched as Chalcedonian metropolitan to Cyprus (EH 3.2.3.d), it appears most likely that he had already held a Cypriote episcopal see as a Severan.

The EH attests that Stephen joined the Chalcedonian communion in the early 570s, but stresses that he only did so after the exertion of violence. Yet, as Roggo (2022, 105–110) has argued, the Severan bishops may not have needed much pressure to establish the ecclesiastical union on the basis of Justin’s edict on faith (on which see EH 3.1.19). John’s depiction of the events in Book 1 is mainly governed by his wish to present an apology to his co-religionists in the east for the fact that he and his episcopal peers had entered into communion with the Chalcedonians. It is indeed conspicuous that John claims in EH 3.1.25 and 3.1.29 that all the bishops, thus including Stephen, permanently withdrew from the communion with the Chalcedonians after a short time (probably because of the sudden demand for reordinations) and were imprisoned (see 3.1.25.g and 3.1.29.f). But at the start of Book 2, he admits that all his peers in fact again accepted the Chalcedonian communion soon afterwards and were released from prison. According to EH 3.2.3 and 3.2.4, Stephen was one of them and even attempted to convince John to return to the Chalcedonian communion, too.

Stephen’s second conversion to Chalcedon appears to have been lasting; according to EH 3.2.3.d, he had an amiable relationship with Justin II and was eventually sent to Cyprus as the metropolitan of the island. A short passage in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:299.a.25–29; 4:341.b.16–19 asserts that Stephen eventually recanted again, left Cyprus, and definitely returned to the Severans. Honigmann (1951, 224) was inclined to accept this, yet this piece of information is likely to be unreliable. The defection of an important bishop such as Stephen was a hard blow and it is easily imaginable that later Severan authors strove to cover this by claiming that his defection had only been temporary. Moreover, the case of Paul of Antioch shows that Severan lapsi had to undergo a prolonged period of penance (three years in the case of Paul; see EH 3.4.15) (Allen 2013). According to EH 3.2.3 and 3.2.4, Stephen had returned to the Chalcedonian communion even before Paul and consequently, he may have faced an even longer period of penance. There is no recognisable incentive for why Stephen should have given up the prestigious metropolitan see of Cyprus and returned as an outcast into his former community.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.d (SR) 15.25–27
div.p: ܚܕ ܡܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܚܠܝ̣ܡܐܝܬ ܐ̇ܟ̣ܣܘܗܝ ܗܘܘ ܩܕܡ ܩܠܝܠ: ܡܛܘܠ ܫ̇ܪܝܐ ܕܟܪܛܘܢܝܐ “one of those who had a little earlier firmly rebuked him for abolishing the ordination”:This short statement is revealing with regard to the internal order and chronology of Book 1. It indicates that all the events around Stephen which are narrated in this chapter actually need to be placed after John Scholasticus’s demand for reordinations. There is thus a clear difference to the two preceding chapters EH 3.1.14–3.1.15, because in both of them, the reordinations are only introduced in the middle of the narrative, after Paul of Aphrodisias (3.1.14.f) and Elisha (3.1.15.g) were imprisoned in the patriarchal palace and forced to accept the Chalcedonian communion. The passage in question here supports the view that Book 1 of the EH is a careful literary construction and that John of Ephesus rearranged the order of events. After all, the mention of several people firmly rebuking John Scholasticus probably refers to EH 3.1.18, when the assembled Severan bishops, all imprisoned in the patriarchal palace, severely reprimanded the patriarch and showed him how uncanonical his demand for reordinations was. If correct, this would mean that the events narrated in EH 3.1.18 in fact took place prior (ܩܕܡ ܩܠܝܠ “a little earlier”) to Stephen’s hardship in EH 3.1.16, as was indeed assumed by Maraval (1998, 467) and Honigmann (1951, 222). Both Bunce (2023) and Roggo (2022, 103–107) have argued that John’s order of events in Book 1 is artificial and should be taken with a grain of salt. It is imaginable that John did not notice this revealing phrase when rearranging the material to form Book 1.

According to this interpretation, everything mentioned in EH 3.1.16 happened quite late within the internal chronology of Book 1, after all the bishops were brought to the patriarchal residence (EH 3.1.17), discussed with John Scholasticus about the union and the edict of faith (EH 3.1.19–3.1.24), criticized him because of his sudden decision to reordain them (EH 3.1.18), recanted and were separated from each other in different prisons (EH 3.1.25–3.1.29). Stephen’s confinement and torture on Plateia, which caused him to return to the Chalcedonian communion (see below 3.1.16.g), may then have occurred at the same time as Elisha’s and Paul of Antioch’s relegation to the monastery of Abraham (EH 3.1.15.i and EH 3.2.2.c) and John of Ephesus’s own imprisonment in the hospice of Eubulus (EH 3.2.4). Crucially, this understanding appears to be confirmed in EH 3.2.3.b–3.2.3.d, when John returns in his narration to Stephen. John’s narrative there suggests that Stephen’s clash with the patriarch and his complaint to Justin II, both of which are also mentioned in the present chapter (see 3.1.16.j–3.1.16.m), may have taken place only after the failure of the union and the imprisonment of the bishops in separate places as narrated in EH 3.1.29.f.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.e (SR) 15.27–28
div.p: ܥܠ ܗܢܐ ܫ̣ܕܪ ܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܘܣܩ̣ܘܒܝܛܘܪ̈ܣ “he sent clergy and excubitores against him”: Already in EH 3.1.10.d, John of Ephesus asserts that both clergy and secular functionaries (albeit no excubitores) were sent to the Severan monasteries in Constantinopleto force them into accepting Chalcedon. Excubitores existed since the mid-5th century; they were the imperial bodyguard, consisting of a unit of 300 men guarding the palace Mary Whitby 1987, 483–488 Haldon 1984, 136–141 and 161–164 Jones 1964, 658–659. This chapter suggests that John Scholasticus allegedly was able to issue orders to the excubitores. This is quite intriguing if accurate, as they and their commander, the comes excubitorum (at that time in 571 the future emperor Tiberius) normally received their orders from the emperor or the curopalates, who was in overall charge of the security of the imperial palace (Stein 1949, 739–740 n.2). As noted above in 3.1.16.a, Michael the Syrian replaced them with quaestionarii, official torturers, in his rendering of this chapter, and it would be easier to imagine John Scholasticus issuing orders to them than to excubitores. However, as stated above, Michael simply may not have been familiar with the term excubitores. On both these loanwords in Syriac, see Butts (2016b, 140 and 156).

We cannot exclude that there were indeed excubitores involved in Stephen’s maltreatment on Plateia, unless the whole episode was made up by John of Ephesus in order to exculpate Stephen for having accepted the Chalcedonian communion (see below 3.1.16.g). John attests that some high-ranking officials at court (for instance the quaestor Anastasius; see EH 3.1.19.j) were very hostile towards the Severan bishops, and such influential people may, instead of the patriarch, have been decisive for the dispatch of the excubitores.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.f (HL) 15.28–16.1
div.p: ܕܐܣ̣ܦܪܣܬܝܢܐ ܢܥܒ̣ܕܘܢܝܗܝ̣ “to have him tormented with lead balls”: A particular method of torture appears to be conveyed in Syriac through the use of the verb “to be pounded,” which John employs on two occasions in this chapter. There is no doubt that the Syriac word is derived from a Greek word in the aorist passive; but it is unclear from which Greek word it comes. Payne Smith (1879, 1:317) tentatively translated the word with fustibus sive spathis caesus, verberatus and traced the word back to an otherwise unknown Greek verb σπαθαρίζω or σπαρίζω, which, as Payne Smith admits, does not fit the meaning. Brockelmann (1928, 37) argued for σφυρισθῆναι (Liddell, Scott, and Jones 1996, 289), a position subsequently adopted by Sokoloff (2009, 78 and 1057), who translates the term as “to be pounded (with hammers),” and by Butts (2016a, 114–115). However, in late antiquity, this word is attested in alchemistic treatises describing the production of drugs, rather than in the context of torture.

A much more convincing alternative is σφαιρισθῆναι, which can be translated as “to be tormented with lead balls,” as suggested by Brooks (1936, 10 n.4). This meaning is attested in Hesychius Alexandrinus, Lexicon E 6667, s.v. ἐτυμπανίσθησαν (Latte and Cunningham 2020, 277) and seems all the more plausible since whips with lead balls attached to them were common instruments of punishment in late antiquity (see, for example, Codex Theodosianus 2.14.1; 11.7.3 (Meyer and Mommsen 1905, 97–98 and 585–586); Codex Iustinianus 10.19.2 (Blume and Frier 2016, 2496–2499)). John of Ephesus once more portrays a conventional legal process in a manner that suggests the Severans were subjected to unwarranted violence.

The passive infinitive with ܗܘܐ (hwā) as in the second occurrence of ܐܣ̣ܦܪܣܬܝܢܐ in the following sentence can be interpreted as reflecting the light verb strategy (Butts 2016a, 111–116); see 3.1.27.e for another example. But it is unclear whether the use of an aorist passive infinitive combined with ܥܒܕ (ʿḇaḏ) can be understood in the same way, as noted by Butts (2016a, 114 n.43).

div.head: EH 3.1.16.g (SR) 16.8–11
div.p: ܘܗܟܢܐ ܡܢ ܐ̇ܢܢܩܐ܇ ܐܬܬ̣ܪܡܝ ܠܫܘܬ̇ܦܘܬܐ ܕܥܡܗܘܢ. ܘܝܬ̇ܝܪܐܝܬ ܕܐܬܝ̣ܕܥܬ݀ ܐܢ̈ܫܝܢ ܡܢ ܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ ܕܡ̇ܫܕܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗ ܣܘܢ̣ܩܢܗ܇ ܘܐܬܠ̣ܒܟܘ ܘܐܬܚ̣ܒܫܘ ܡܛܠܬܗ “in this way, he yielded to communing with them out of compulsion, especially since it became known that some of the faithful who were sending him what he needed had been both apprehended and imprisoned because of him”: John of Ephesus presents Stephen’s acceptance of Chalcedon as the result of violence and fear lest his supporters be harmed. There is a distinct possibility that this is a literary fiction for apologetic purposes and that in reality, the Severan bishops may have been ready to enter into communion with the Chalcedonians. Already at the negotiations in Callinicum in 567, the bishops had been willing to accept Justin’s conciliatory edict, yet they had been prevented from doing so because of the threats of the monastic hardliners; see Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.1–2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:285–290; 4:333–336; Roggo (2022, 95–98) and the literature listed above in 3.1.3.r. Consequently, they may, in 571, have embraced Justin’s new edict on faith (see EH 3.1.19) without much pressure, given that they were in Constantinople outside the sphere of influence of the eastern monastics. Yet, as Roggo (2022, 105–110) has shown, the union may eventually have failed because of John Scholasticus’s demand for reordinations. The disillusioned bishops then had to explain the short-lived period of communion to their highly critical co-religionists in the east, hence the EH’s repeated insistence that the exertion of violence had been crucial for accepting the union.

Note that there is a certain tension between this passage and Michael’s summary of the lacuna in EH 3.1.5-8.j, according to which the imprisoned bishops were not allowed to have any contact with their acquaintances and supporters. EH 3.1.22 suggests that pressure exerted by Severan sympathizers among the laity may indeed have provided the bishops with an incentive to agree to the union (see 3.1.22.a).

div.head: EH 3.1.16.h (SR) 16.14–16
div.p: ܬܒ̇ܥ ܗܘܐ ܠܗ ܗ̣ܘ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܕܢܫ̣ܪܝܘܗܝ܆ ܘܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ ܢܥܒ̣ܕܝܘܗܝ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ ܠܩ̣ܘܦܪܘܣ ܓܙܪܬܐ “John demanded of him that he depose him and then reordain him as bishop for the island of Cyprus”: For John Scholasticus’s sudden demand for reordaining the Severan clerics after a period of joint communion, see EH 3.1.12.b–3.1.12.g. The fact that John Scholasticus wanted to reordain Stephen as bishop (i.e. metropolitan) of Cyprus is a strong argument for the view that Stephen’s nominal see as Severan bishop had also been a city on Cyprus, probably the metropolis Constantia / Salamis itself (see 3.1.16.c). Because of their small number, the nominal sees of the Severan bishops in the mid-6th century often were the provincial capitals; for instance, John of Ephesus was bishop of the Asian metropolis Ephesus, and Paul (EH 3.1.14) of the Carian capital Aphrodisias. John insists here that Stephen successfully rejected the patriarch’s attempt for a reordination; he adds in EH 3.2.3.d that Stephen was eventually appointed / confirmed as Chalcedonian metropolitan of Cyprus.

Both this chapter and EH 3.2.3.d provide extraordinary evidence of the fact that the patriarch of Constantinople was apparently able to appoint the metropolitan of Cyprus. The testimony of the EH has so far been ignored in scholarly discussions concerning the autocephaly of the church of Cyprus Jones 1964, 873 Janin 1953, 794–795 Vailhé 1910, 8–10. In 431, the council of Ephesus declared that the church of Cyprus should be independent from the patriarchate of Antioch and autocephalous, that is, no bishop from outside the island should be allowed to perform episcopal ordinations there Price and Graumann 2022, 524–531 Schwartz 1929, 118–122. The ecclesiastical independence of the church of Cyprus was confirmed by the emperors Zeno and Justinian and again in Canon 39 of the Quinisext Council in 691/692, explicitly stating that the archbishop of Cyprus was not subjected to the patriarch of Constantinople Price 2020, 119–121 Ohme 2013, 40. The special status of Cyprus was then recognized throughout the Byzantine period and up to this day. However, this passage of the EH clearly attests that the autocephaly of the church of Cyprus was not yet universally accepted prior to the Quinisext Council: both John of Ephesus and the Chalcedonian authorities seem to have taken it for granted that the patriarch of Constantinople was entitled to appoint the metropolitan of Cyprus.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.i (SR; PMF) 16.18
div.p: ܐ̣ܘܒܝܐ “O bia!”: This exclamation, uttered by Stephen here in front of John Scholasticus and shortly afterwards in 3.1.16.l in front of Justin II, is a direct transliteration into Syriac of the Greek ὦ βία, literary translated as “O violence!” The phrase, found in both literary texts and documentary sources (Dosoo and Preininger 2023, 377), appears in conflict situations also in EH 3.2.14.d and 3.4.22 – as well as in LES 6 (Brooks 1923, 114.8) and 16 (Brooks 1923, 243.13). The language of “violence” may not only refer to the physical violence carried out but also suggest a spiritual struggle, as found in the use of the Greek root and the exclamation in Greek monastic writings (Marinides 2012, 245–246).

Beyond John of Ephesus, the word is attested in a small number of martyr acts in Syriac Brockelmann 1928, 7 updated citations in Sokoloff 2009, 12. For example, the Syriac translator of Palladius’s Lausiac History (Bedjan 1890–1897, 7:86.19–20) took it directly from the Greek source text (Bartelink 2001, 126.114). The mid-10th-century lexicographer Bar Bahlul notes that he knew this word through the Lausiac History (Duval 1901, 50), suggesting its limited use in Syriac literature. Consequently, it is conceivable that John did not so much use a word that had entered the Syriac language from Greek, but rather quoted a genuinely Greek expression rendered in Syriac letters. While it must have been evident to his readership that Stephen spoke Greek to both patriarch and emperor, John did not hesitate to use this Greek phrase in presumably non-Greek settings in the LES.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.j (SR) 16.18–20
div.p: ܕܐܢ ܕܬܫ̣ܪܝܢܝ ܠܡ ܡܢ ܟܗܢܘܬܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ ܘܬܥܒ̣ܕܢܝ ܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ ܨ̇ܒܝܬ܆ ܫ̣ܪܝܢܝ ܠܘܩܕܡ ܘܐܦ ܡܢ ܡ̇ܥܡܘܕܝܬܐ “if you want to depose me from the priesthood of the orthodox and reordain me, also depose me of baptism first”: The EH contains various complaints of Severan bishops about John Scholasticus’s intention to reordain them: complaints similar to this one are, for instance, recorded from both Paul of Aphrodisias and Elisha (see 3.1.15.g). They all have in common that the bishops express that if John Scholasticus wants to reordain them to the priesthood, then he should also declare invalid all the other sacraments they received as Severans, in particular their baptism. Everything else they (that is, John of Ephesus as the writer of these exclamations) consider as inconsistent with the canons of the ecumenical councils (see below 3.1.16.p). These fierce debates about the validity of the Severans sacraments in case of a conversion which are reflected in the EH have been noted by Maraval (1998, 457–458), yet he has placed them by mistake in the second patriarchate of Eutychius (577–582).

div.head: EH 3.1.16.k (SR) 16.25–26
div.p: ܗ̣ܘ ܐܣ̣ܛܦܢܐ ܒܚܐ̣ܦܐ ܪܗ̣ܛ ܘܥ̣ܠ ܠܘܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ “Stephen promptly ran and went to the emperor”: Bunce (2023, 76) has doubted that Stephen would have been able to get access to Justin II as easily as claimed in this chapter. In particular, he takes issue with the fact that Stephen did not follow the guidelines provided in Justinian’s Novel 6.3 Miller and Sarris 2018, 103–104 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 41–42 on how bishops should apply for an imperial audience (they were supposed to first state to the patriarch their reasons for why they wished to see the emperor; yet this procedure is evidently useless if the bishop in question wants to complain about the patriarch, as was the case with Stephen). According to Bunce, the whole narrative presented here is fiction created by John so as to evoke the impression that the Severans enjoyed imperial support against the uncanonical measures of John Scholasticus. This is plausible indeed (see 3.1.16.m), yet irrespective of this, it remains possible that Stephen managed to be received by the emperor quite quickly: EH 3.2.3.d asserts that Stephen was on good terms with Justin, and this relationship, possibly dating back to the prolonged doctrinal negotiations of the 560s (on which see above 3.1.3.r), may have allowed him to get easy access to the court.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.l (SR) 16.27
div.p: ܐ̣ܘܒܝܐ̣ “O bia!”: See 3.1.16.i above.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.m (SR) 16.27–30
div.p: ܕܐ̣ܒܕܬ݀ ܠܗ̇ ܟܪܣܛܝܢܘܬܐ. ܐܬܚ̣̇ܒܠܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܝܗ̇ ܕܥܕܬܐ ܕܟܪ̈ܣܛܝܢܐ. ܐܬ̇ܒ̣ܠܒܠܘ ܘܐܬ̣ܕܝܫܘ ܘܐܬ̇ܚܒܠܘ ܟܘܠܗܘܢ ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܝܗ̇ ܘܩ̈ܢܘܢܝܗ̇ ܕܥܕܬܐ ܕܐܠܗܐ “Christianity has been lost! The ordinances of the Christian church have been corrupted! All the ordinances and canons of the church of God have been marred, violated, and destroyed”: Stephen’s long speech (3.1.16.l–3.1.16.p) in front of Justin II forms the center of this chapter. There is a scholarly consensus that John of Ephesus modeled Stephen’s speech in order to express his own views on John Scholasticus’s harmful and uncanonical behavior and to exculpate the emperor from the hardship the Severans were suffering Bunce 2023, 75–78 Francisco 2011a, 114–116 Honigmann 1951, 223. All the blame is put on the patriarch (see 3.1.5.e; 3.1.11.e; 3.1.19.n), whereas Justin is (throughout Book 1 of the EH) presented as upholding the canons (van Ginkel 1995, 107–108): after Stephen’s passionate speech, Justin, so John claims, “was startled as though from a deep sleep” (3.1.16: ܬܘ̣ܗ ܐܝܟ ܕܡܢ ܫܢ̣ܬܐ ܥܡܝ̣ܩܬܐ), apparently even recognized the validity of the Severan sacraments, and strove to put an end to his patriarch’s illegal actions (see below 3.1.16.q). The supposed imperial intervention in favor of the Severans triggered by this speech was particularly important for John as it allowed him to portray his own religious community as enjoying imperial support, whereas the Chalcedonian hierarchy around John Scholasticus was censured and exposed as transgressing the canons.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.n (SR) 17.3–6
div.p: ܗܐ ܓܝܪ ܥܣܪ̈ܝܢ ܠܝ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ܇ ܕܟܕ ܠܐ ܫ̇ܘܐ ܐܢܐ ܥܒ̣ܝܕ ܐܢܐ ܡܢ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ ܩܢܘܢܐܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ܇ ܒܦܘܩܕܢܗ ܕܬܐܕܘܣܝܘܣ ܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ ܕܐ̣ܠܟܣܢܕܪܝܐ “look, twenty years ago I, though unworthy, was canonically ordained as bishop by the orthodox at the order of Theodosius, patriarch of Alexandria”: It is very likely that John of Ephesus considerably exaggerates the length of Stephen’s episcopate Booth forthcoming; Honigmann 1951, 223. As noted above (3.1.16.c), an ordination of Stephen in c. 551 appears questionable as we do not know about any other Severan ordination between 542 (the ordination of Jacob Burdʿoyo himself) and the start of a series of episcopal ordinations by Jacob after 553/554 (Honigmann 1951, 169–173). The fact that Stephen only appears as a signatory in the Documenta Monophysitica after 567 suggests that he might only have been made bishop after the death of Theodosius in June 566. However, John may have had a good reason for this exaggeration: Theodosius, former patriarch of Alexandria, resided in Constantinople from 536 until his death in 566 and had, as the highest-ranking anti-Chalcedonian bishop, interacted repeatedly with both Justinian and Justin II (see above 3.1.5.a).
