According to Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.1, the imperial authorities even allowed a public funeral in Constantinople after his death Roggo 2022, 94–95 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:283. Therefore, the EH’s claim that John Scholasticus declared invalid an ordination performed at the order of such a personality held in high regard by Chalcedonians and Severans alike again underlines his depravity and lack of respect, which is one of his main characteristics in the EH (see also below 3.1.18.e).

div.head: EH 3.1.16.o (SR; NK) 17.16–17
div.p: ܘܥܡܗ ܕܢܚܫ̣ܒܟ ܡܬ̇ܛܟܢ܇ ܒܥ̣ܒܪ ܢܡ̈ܘܣܐ ܥܕ̈ܬܢܝܐ “he is conspiring to count you with him in the transgression of the ecclesiastical laws”: John of Ephesus presents a comparable statement in EH 3.1.12.i: there, he asserts that John Scholasticus was eager not to appear as the only wrongdoer who was insisting on the reordination of the Severan converts and therefore wrote letters to other bishops demanding that they also participate in the campaign. John denigrates the patriarch here in a similar manner by claiming that he wanted to include Justin in his uncanonical actions and thus share both the blame and the responsibility with him. This is yet another example of the negative characterization of John Scholasticus encountered throughout Books 1 and 2 of the EH.

Brooks (1935, 17 n.3) cautiously suggests an emendation here: “Lege fortasse ܕܢܚܒ̣ܫܟ,” which could mean “to involve you (with him).” But the text with the verb ܕܢܚܫ̣ܒܟ (“to count you”) is already intelligible; therefore an emendation does not seem necessary.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.p (SR) 17.21–23
div.p: ܐܦ ܓܝܪ ܩܢܘܢܐ ܕܬܫܬܥܣܪ̈ ܕܬܠܬܡܐܐ ܘܬܡܢܬܥܣܪ̈ ܐܒܗ̈ܬܐ: ܥܠ ܗܪܣܝܣ ܡ̇ܚܒܠܬܐ ܕܒܝܬ ܦܘܠܐ ܫܡܝ̣ܫܛܝܐ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܐܟܘܬܗ “the nineteenth canon of the 318 fathers, concerning the corrupt heresy of the followers of Paul of Samosata and others like him”: Paul (sed. 260–268) taught as bishop of Antioch that Jesus had originally been a mere human who was only inspired by the divine Logos at his baptism. He was deposed because of these views and later widely seen as a precursor of Arian and Nestorian theology Grillmeier 1990a, 296–299 Brennecke 1984. Canon 19 of the council of Nicaea, to which Stephen is referring here, decrees how repenting former followers of Paul are to be received in the church (Alberigo 2006, I:30). The canon states that their sacraments are invalid and that they consequently need to be rebaptized, and their clergy reordained to the priesthood. This canon is also found in John Scholasticus’s own compilation of canon law, the Synagoga L titulorum, Title 38 (Beneševič 1937, 114).

In the EH, John of Ephesus has the Severan bishops (both in this passage and in EH 3.1.18.i) exclaim that John Scholasticus’s order for reordinations violates this canon because he only demands reordinations, and not also rebaptisms. A reordination, so John has the Severans argue, can canonically only be justified if none of the sacraments they received are considered valid, thus including the baptism. They are evidently opposed to being reordained as they consider themselves orthodox, yet the patriarchal inconsistency and apparent contempt for the canons renders the situation even worse. The argument presented here against John Scholasticus indeed makes sense since there is not a single example where canon law “only” demands the reordination of formerly heretical clerics and not also their rebaptism. As stated above in 3.1.12.b, whether or not the sacraments heretics had received were recognized as valid depended on the perceived gravity of their former heresy; some, for instance the followers of Paul of Samosata mentioned here, needed to receive all the sacraments again, whereas others such as the Severans only needed to officially condemn their former beliefs. As for the reasons which might have prompted the formerly tolerant John Scholasticus to have the Severan clerics reordained, see 3.1.12.f.

div.head: EH 3.1.16.q (SR) 18.7–13
div.p: ܘܗ̇ܝܕܝܢ ܦܩ̣ܕ܆ ܕܬܘܒ ܗܕܐ ܒܥܕܬܗ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܠܓܡ̣ܪ̣ ܠܐ ܬܗܘܐ. ܘܡ̣ܚܕܐ ܘܛܘ̣ܦܘܣ ܦܪܓܡ̇ܛܝܩܘܢ ܥ̣ܒܕ܇ ܕܠܐ ܢܗܘܐ ܫܠܝܛ ܠܐܢܫ ܕܬܘܒ ܢܡ̇ܪܚ ܥܠ ܫ̣ܪܝܐ ܕܟܗܢܘܬܐ܇ ܐܠܐ ܐܢ ܓ̣ܕܫ ܕܠܗܪ̈ܣܝܣ ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܦܩ̇ܕܝܢ ܩ̈ܢܘܢܐ. ܘܐܢ ܡܫܬ̇ܟܚܝܢ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܕܥ̇ܒܕܝܢ ܠܗ̇ ܠܗܕܐ܆ ܬ̇ܗܘܐ ܩ̇ܬܪܣܝܣ ܕܝܠܗܘܢ̣ ܘܢ̇ܫ̣ܬܕܘܢ ܠܐܟ̣ܣܘܪܝܐ “he then ordered that this not take place in the church of God in any way and immediately made a typos pragmatikos that it was not permitted for anyone to venture to abolish the priesthood again, unless it should happen in regard to heresies concerning which the canons give an order. If bishops happen to be doing this, their deposition would take place and they would be cast into banishment”: Justinissued his command that the priesthood of the Severan clergy was to be recognized in the form of a typos pragmatikos (τύπος πραγματικός; sanctio / lex pragmatica). This was one of different types of legislation at the disposal of the emperor in late antiquity. It differed from a general law / edict (ἤδικτον) in that it was usually only promulgated at the request of individuals. Yet at the same time, a lex pragmatica was not identical to an imperial rescript (rescriptum) issued in reaction to a petition since it was considered to be generally valid and not only in one single case (Dölger and Karagiannopulos 1968, 78–82).

This definition exactly fits to the case of Stephen: in his speech, he was informing the emperor of the patriarch’s illegal actions and demanded that this be remedied, yet evidently he wished that all the Severan clergy, and not only he himself, be spared from reordination. According to John of Ephesus, the imperial answer to Stephen’s complaint (Dölger and Müller 2009, 10.20) not only aimed at putting an end to John Scholasticus’s reordinations, but it also confirmed the ecclesiastical canons in that it specified that the reordination of heretical priests should continue where canon law demanded it, for instance with regard to the followers of Paul of Samosata addressed in Canon 19 of Nicaea (Alberigo 2006, I:30) (see above 3.1.16.p). In this passage, John of Ephesus effectively manages to break up the Chalcedonian front of emperor and patriarch to his readers (see above 3.1.16.m). He asserts that Justin recognized that canon law supported the Severans and that John Scholasticus was found to be in need of correction and even threatened with deposition (ܩ̇ܬܪܣܝܣ; καθαίρεσις) (Bunce 2023, 75–78).

div.head: EH 3.1.16.r (SR) 18.14–15
div.p: ܐܬ̇ܦܪܣ ܗ̣ܘ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܘܒ̈ܢܝ ܡܠ̣ܟܗ ܕܢܬ̣ܠܘܢ ܫܘܚܕܐ “John and his advisors schemed to make a bribe”: The EH’s claim that John Scholasticus managed to have the imperial command withdrawn by means of bribery is hard to imagine (and it is indeed not repeated in EH 3.2.3.c, where Justin’s typos pragmatikos is mentioned again), unless one presupposes that this happened before the law was promulgated publicly. The outrage among the Severan clergy and their supporters about the reordinations was probably such that they would not have allowed for this imperial order to disappear quietly. Yet there also is the possibility that Justin himself may have changed his stance and withdrawn the typos: as argued by Roggo (2022, 105–113), John Scholasticus may have demanded reordinations only to acquiesce hardcore Chalcedonians opposed to the edict on faith (introduced in EH 3.1.19) so as to prevent further damage to his unstable position within the Chalcedonian church (see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.12.h). Roggo further shows that Justin may have found himself in a similar position after his initial resistance to the reordinations which caused the end of the short-lived ecclesiastical union: in a period when he was increasingly challenged by both internal and external pressure, he had to prioritize the stabilization of his own standing over compromise with Severans and therefore gave in to the demands of a staunchly Chalcedonian interest group (consisting of people such as the quaestor Anastasius; see EH 3.1.19.j).

If this hypothesis is correct, then John of Ephesus, eager to depict John Scholasticus as the only one to blame for the hardship the anti-Chalcedonians suffered (see 3.1.5.e), might have taken recourse to an act of supposed bribery in order to explain why Justin’s initial rejection of reordinations did not improve their situation.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter describes the first imprisonment of the Severan bishops in the patriarchal palace and interrupts the sequence in EH 3.1.12–3.1.18 which treats the command of patriarch John Scholasticus to have them reordained. As so often in Book 1, the chronological order of events is not completely clear (see 3.1.16.d). Yet the content of the present chapter very probably took place prior to the patriarch’s demand for reordinations (see 3.1.17.a). By connecting the chapters dealing with the reordinations to the first imprisonment of the bishops in the patriarchal palace, John of Ephesus creates the impression that the reordinations happened quite at the beginning of the whole chain of events, before any serious talks about an ecclesiastical union, which is improbable (Roggo 2022, 103–108).

Already in EH 3.1.14–3.1.15, John mentioned briefly that some bishops were, for a certain time, confined in the patriarchal residence in Constantinople. In this chapter, he further specifies that there were four or five bishops confined there and that he himself also was one of them. He records that John Scholasticus and his advisors sent them a document containing the formula of union which had enabled the reconciliation between Cyril of Alexandria and John of Antioch in 433. The Chalcedonians demanded that they enter into communion with them on the basis of that formula. John of Ephesus, however, claims that he himself and his episcopal peers replied that in that case, the Chalcedonians also needed to accept the condemnation of Chalcedon, for also in the union of 433, both sides had accepted the condemnation of Nestorius. The EH does not come back to this proposition for a union in later chapters. Probably this tentative should be understood as a prequel to the edict on faith presented in EH 3.1.19, on the basis of which the ecclesiastical union was then indeed realized for a short time.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:298.a.32–35; 4:340.a.38–40: summary of parts of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.17.f

div.head: EH 3.1.17.a (SR) 18.22–23
div.p: ܒܚ̣ܘܫܒܐ ܗܟܝܠ ܢܟܝ̣ܠܐ ܘܕܕܓ̇ܠܘܬܐ ܡ̇ܛܥܝܢܝܬܐ܇ ܐܝܟ ܡܐ ܕܗ̣ܘ ܣܘ̣ܥܪܢܐ ܐ̇ܠ̣ܦ ܒܚܪܬܐ “hence, with a cunning thought and misleading duplicity – as the event itself imparted at the end”: This is the first chapter in which John of Ephesus explicitly states that the Chalcedonians hoped to achieve an ecclesiastical union with the Severans (see 3.1.17.c below). Prior to this chapter, he narrated the events in such a way as to create the impression that the Severans were violently maltreated, forced to join the Chalcedonian communion, and subjected to uncanonical reordination. However, there are good reasons to doubt John’s depiction and especially his chronological order of the events (the most recent contributions on this topic are Bunce (2023) and Roggo (2022, 100–118)). Ever since the contentious council of Chalcedon, various emperors had tried to overcome the division the council had created, and Justin II was no exception to this. It is thus unsurprising that propositions for a union also were crucial in 571, as John reveals here, yet it is noticeable that he is eager to immediately disqualify them as cunning and dishonest. Large parts of the Severan movement, including John himself, still hoped for a reconciliation in the mid-6th century Leppin 2019; van Ginkel 1995, 114–115 and 166–167, and accordingly, they were initially willing to accept the two edicts on faith Justin proposed in 567 and 571 (see 3.1.17.c). It was only after the failure of the short-lived union that had come about after the second edict (the main topic of EH 3.1.19–3.1.29) that their hopes were shattered and John had to present an apologetic version of the events in which he himself was involved (see EH 3.2.6.d). In order to exculpate himself, he insists therefore in Book 1 of the EH that he and his peers had only joined the communion because they were forced to do so, and in particular, he presents the Chalcedonian proposals as inherently deceiving from the beginning.

In line with these observations, the passage in question here can be interpreted in two different ways: 1) John may refer to the proposal based on the union of 433 between Cyril of Alexandria and John of Antioch, the main topic of this chapter. He claims that the proposition was a trick since the Chalcedonians demanded that John and his peers play the part of Cyril and restore the union, while they themselves remained unwilling to emulate John of Antioch’s concession by not accepting the condemnation of Chalcedon ((Bunce 2023, 80–81); see also 3.1.17.h below). 2) It is also possible that John already has the outcome of the whole negotiations in mind, that is, the eventual failure of Justin’s edict (introduced in EH 3.1.19). In line with his apologetic interests, John asserts in EH 3.1.24.g and 3.1.24.l that he and his peers were deceived and only entered into communion with the Chalcedonians on the basis of the edict after they had received the promise that the condemnation of Chalcedon would follow suit – yet this evidently did not happen.

div.head: EH 3.1.17.b (SR) 18.23–26
div.p: ܫ̣ܕܪ ܗ̣ܘ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܥܡ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܒ̈ܢܝ ܡܠ̣ܟܗ܆ ܘܐܝܬ̣ܝܘ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܢܗ ܫ̣ܕܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܐܟ̣ܣܘܪܝܐ܇ ܒܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܘܒܕܘ̈ܟܝܬܐ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܝܬܐ “John along with his advisors sent for and had the bishops brought whom he had cast into banishment in monasteries and other places”: The Severan bishops (probably including the dissident bishop Conon, see 3.1.31.c), are brought from their various locations of confinement and assembled on the orders of John Scholasticus in his patriarchal residence (on the different places of confinement see 3.1.5-8.b–3.1.5-8.g). Arguably, this chapter reflects an early period of the persecution, relatively soon after the initial separation of the bishops in different prisons, which may have happened on 21 March 571 (see EH 3.1.5.d). In all likelihood, the bishops’ imprisonment in the patriarchal residence mentioned here antedated John Scholasticus’s demand for reordinations introduced in EH 3.1.12–3.1.16 (which, according to Roggo (2022, 105–110) only took place after the bishops had accepted Justin’s edict on faith). John has already narrated earlier in the EH that both Paul of Aphrodisias (3.1.14.e) and Elisha of Sardis (3.1.15.d), the latter after being imprisoned in the monastery of Dios, were brought to the patriarchal palace and forced to enter into communion with the Chalcedonians, before eventually being confronted with the reordination. Consequently, these earlier mentions of episcopal confinement in the patriarchal palace probably refer to the same instance as in this chapter, and all the Severan bishops may have been held there simultaneously Destephen 2008, 512 Maraval 1998, 466–467 Honigmann 1951, 199 and 212.

div.head: EH 3.1.17.c (SR) 18.27
div.p: ܡܛܠ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ “about the union”: In his ecclesiastical policy, Justin II largely followed the example set by his uncle Justinian, with his main aim being the achievement of a lasting union between defenders and opponents of the council of Chalcedon. For this reason, Justin had, together with John Scholasticus, kept negotiating with anti-Chalcedonian representatives, including John of Ephesus, ever since his accession to the throne in late 565, and an edict on faith (partly copied in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:289–290; 4:335–336) had been presented to the anti-Chalcedonian leaders at Callinicum in Syria in 567, yet to no avail (see 3.1.3.rand 3.1.5.e). The negotiations then continued in Constantinople and eventually led to Justin’s second edict on faith (see EH 3.1.19), with which he hoped to come to a solution acceptable to both sides. Justin’s continuous effort for overcoming the divide between Chalcedonians and Severans was also acknowledged by John of Ephesus, who wrote in EH 3.1.3.r that he had treated Justin’s great concern for the unity of the church in the (now lost) second part of the EH.

div.head: EH 3.1.17.d (SR) 18.28
div.p: ܠܦܘܠܘܣ ܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ “Paul, the patriarch”: Paul was from 564 until his death in 581 nominally Severan patriarch of Antioch and, after the death of Theodosius of Alexandria in 566, in theory the highest-ranking anti-Chalcedonian bishop (on him, see also 3.1.41.m). Like his episcopal peers including John of Ephesus, he never resided in the city whose bishop he was, instead being usually based in monasteries in the Syrian countryside and in Constantinople. Of Alexandrian origin, his hope had been that he would become the successor of Theodosius in 566, all the more so because the real Severan authority in Syria and Mesopotamia lay with Jacob Burdʿoyo, the most senior bishop. These ambitions quickly contributed to his growing unpopularity in both Egypt and Syria (Booth forthcoming), yet at least the Chalcedonian authorities acknowledged him as one of the most important Severan leaders in the late 560s and early 570s. He participated in the talks with the adherents of John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine (the so-called “tritheists”) chaired by John Scholasticus in 570, and the present chapter suggests that he was the first bishop to be brought to the patriarchal residence for talks about the union in 571. His actions in 571 further damaged his status among the Severans: Paul entered twice into communion with John Scholasticus, recanted twice, and therefore had to undergo a prolonged period of penance during three years until c. 575 before the eastern Severans around Jacob Burdʿoyo accepted him again (EH 3.4.15). Yet he then attempted to have a new Severan patriarch, Theodore, consecrated in Alexandria, a move which brought him the lasting enmity of most Egyptian Severans (see 3.1.40.d). Finally, even Jacob Burdʿoyo turned against him and he was forced to hide in Constantinople. Already before his ignominious death in 581 (EH 3.4.57), Peter of Callinicum (sed. 581–591) was ordained as new Severan patriarch of Antioch. Both John of Ephesus and the author of the Documenta Monophysitica were supporters of Paul in this inner-Severan conflict. The most important sources on Paul are the Documenta Monophysitica Chabot 1907; see Van Roey and Allen 1994, 267–303 and the EH itself, where Paul figures prominently in EH 3.2.2–3.2.8 and in the whole of Book 4 Booth forthcoming; Blaudeau 2022, 63–70 Allen 2013, 190–196; 2011, 32–33 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 199–200 Maraval 1998, 471–473 Blaudeau 1997, 333–343 Honigmann 1951, 195–205 Brooks 1930; Hermann 1928.

div.head: EH 3.1.17.e (SR) 18.28–29
div.p: ܕܝܪܐ ܕܐܩ̣ܝܡܛܘܢ “the monastery of the Akoimetoi”: The Akoimetoi (Ἀκοιμήτοι), literally “the sleepless ones,” were a monastic community founded in Constantinople in the early 5th century. Their name stemmed from the fact that around the clock, there was always a part of the community engaged in divine worship. In the 430s, the Akoimetoi settled on the Asian side of the Bosphorus, at a location called Eirenaion (Janin 1975, 13–15). Quickly, their monastery became very popular, with Akoimetoi monks eventually being dispatched to populate other monasteries in Constantinople, for instance the Studios monastery (Hatlie 2007, 102–105 and 109). After the council of Chalcedon and up to the reign of Justinian, the Akoimetoi monks were fervent supporters of dyophysite Chalcedonian orthodoxy, denouncing, for instance, the Enkyklikon of Basiliscus and Zeno’s Henotikon in the 470s and 480s. They even dared to oppose Justinian’s move towards theopaschism in the early 530s, with which he hoped to render dyophysite orthodoxy more acceptable for the anti-Chalcedonians. Because of their opposition on that occasion, the Akoimetoi monks were targeted in imperial legislation and excommunicated by the Roman bishop John II (sed. 533–535) Hasse-Ungeheuer 2016, 194–195 Pfeilschifter 2013, 432–433 Hatlie 2007, 141–142 and 168–169 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 357–359. Afterwards, the influence of the monastery was reduced, yet it remained staunchly Chalcedonian and was therefore an ideal place of internment for a prominent opponent of Chalcedon such as Paul of Antioch. On the monastic confinement of clergy, increasingly popular since the time of Justinian, see (Hillner 2015, 314–341).

div.head: EH 3.1.17.f (SR) 19.1–3
div.p: ܘܠܗܠܝܢ ܕܫܪܟܐ܆ ܚܕ ܒܬܪ ܚܕ ܐܝܬ̣ܝ ܘܚܒ̣ܫ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܘܬܗ. ܗ̇ܢܘ ܕܝ̣ܢ. ܠܦܘܠܘܣ ܘܠܝܘܚܢܢ ܘܠܐܣ̣ܛܦܢܐ̣ ܘܠܐ̣ܠܝܫܥ “he brought and imprisoned the rest of them, one after another with him: that is, Paul, John, Stephen, and Elisha”: In addition to Paul of Antioch, some other Severan bishops mentioned so far (Stephen of Cyprus in EH 3.1.16; Elisha of Sardis in EH 3.1.15) and John of Ephesus himself are brought to the patriarchal residence of John Scholasticus adjacent to Hagia Sophia for talks about the ecclesiastical union. John of Ephesus states that they were all assembled there from their various places of banishment – Paul of Antioch had been held in the monastery of the Akoimetoi and Elisha in that of Dios (see 3.1.15.c). John does not mention where he himself and Stephen had been confined previously.

There has been some confusion as to how many Severan bishops were gathered by John Scholasticus, four or five. This depends on whether or not one assumes that Paul of Antioch is named once or twice in this passage (in the former case, the second Paul is Paul of Aphrodisias (introduced in EH 3.1.14)). Payne Smith (1860, 23) decided that Paul of Antioch must be listed twice and even took the liberty of rendering the passage as “until all four [sc. bishops] were confined in the same prison.” Brooks’s (1936, 12) translation equally creates the impression that Paul of Antioch is named twice, and most scholars followed him Destephen 2008, 512 Maraval 1998, 466–467 van Ginkel 1995, 34 Honigmann 1951, 199 and 212. The Syriac of this passage is indeed ambiguous; Schönfelder (1862, 14) assumed that it refers to five bishops. It depends on whether we understand ܗ̇ܢܘ ܕܝ̣ܢ (“that is”) as explaining ܗܠܝܢ ܕܫܪܟܐ (“the rest of them”; thus excluding Paul of Antioch) or ܚܕ ܒܬܪ ܚܕ (“one after another,” which might include Paul of Antioch as the first one who was brought to the patriarchal residence). Both interpretations are possible. Yet EH 3.4.15, where John speaks of “Paul [of Antioch] and three other [bishops] with him” (ܦܘܠܘܣ ܘܬܠܬܐ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܝܢ ܕܥܡܗ) when referring back to this episode, supports the view that Paul of Antioch is named twice; similarly, a Florilegium against the Paulianists preserved in British Library, Add. 14533 names four bishops in total who were imprisoned in the patriarchal palace (transcribed by Lontie (1994, 2:20 cf. Booth forthcoming). The testimony of EH 3.1.14.e, stating that Paul of Aphrodisias, too, was imprisoned in the patriarchal residence, does not necessarily speak against this interpretation. While it appears probable that the confinement of Paul of Aphrodisias and Elisha in the patriarchal residence mentioned in EH 3.1.14 and 3.1.15 refers to the joint imprisonment of the bishops treated in this chapter (see 3.1.17.b), John (and the Florilegium) might have focused only on those four who participated in the discussions about the edict. Consequently, there might have been more Severan bishops, and Paul of Aphrodisias might have been one of them. In support of this view, John even admits in 3.1.31.c that another anti-Chalcedonian bishop, the “tritheist” leader Conon, also was in the patriarchal residence around the same time.

Like most scholars, Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:298.a.32–35; 4:340.a.38–40 only listed four bishops: Paul of Antioch, John of Ephesus, Stephen, and Elisha. His rendering of this chapter in his Chronicle is so short that it cannot be qualified as a summary: he merely states that John Scholasticus assembled those four bishops by using lies and pretending to be interested in a union. Yet neither the union of 433 nor any other information given in this chapter is mentioned.

div.head: EH 3.1.17.g (NK) 19.3–6
div.p: ܘܫ̣ܕܪܘܢ ܠܗܘܢ ܟܪܛܝܣܐ ܚܕ ܕܟܬ̣ܝܒ ܗܘܐ ܒܗ܇ ܥܠ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܕܗ̣ܘܬ ܒܝܬ ܩܘܪܝܠܘܣ ܕܐܠ̣ܟܣܢܕܪܝܐ ܘܠܝܘܚܢܢ ܕܐܢܛܝܟܝܐ “they sent them a sheet on which something was written about the union that took place between Cyril of Alexandria and John of Antioch”: This may refer to an excerpt from the 39th letter (Schwartz 1960a, 15–20) of Cyril of Alexandria (sed. 412–444), sent to John of Antioch (sed. 429–441) in 433. Apparently, this excerpt was written on a papyrus sheet (ܟܪܛܝܣܐ/χάρτης). The original context of this letter was the schism between the churches of Antioch and Alexandria in the wake of the council of Ephesus in 431 and the deposition of Nestorius of Constantinople by the faction of Cyril. The schism was eventually overcome in 433 by means of Cyril’s letter Price and Graumann 2022, 617–618 Price and Gaddis 2005, 1:23; 115; 178–183. It contains, as a formula of union, a creed which was acceptable to both ecclesiastical leaders. This creed originally emerged from the Antiochene context; it was likely authored by Theodoret of Cyrrhus, and was then partially adapted by Cyril. Its use by Cyril may have been helpful to restore the ecclesiastical ties with Antioch (Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 259). It emphasizes the unity of the one person of Jesus Christ under the differentiation of the two natures. For the union of 433, see Krannich 2006; Grillmeier 1990a, 703–707.

This creed was regularly included in florilegia collections and used in discussions for the reconciliation of pro- and anti-Chalcedonians in the 6th century Grillmeier 1990a, 707 n.15 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 253. For example, the Chalcedonian theologian John of Caesarea referred to the union formula of 433 when he sought to reconcile the Chalcedonians and the Severans around 515 (Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 70–72).

div.head: EH 3.1.17.h (SR) 19.9–10
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܟܕ ܗܠܝܢ ܩ̇ܒܠܘܢ ܘܐܬ̇ܒܝܢܘ ܒܝܢܬܗܘܢ ܘܐ̇ܗ̣ܠܘ ܒܛ̇ܪܟܢܘܬܗܘܢ “Upon receiving these things, they consulted with one another and mocked their mischievousness”: This passage refers back to the start of this chapter (see 3.1.17.a), where John of Ephesus already anticipates the outcome of the negotiations by stating that the proposition of the Chalcedonians was insincere. John claims that John Scholasticus and his advisors demanded that the Severans enter into a union, just as Cyril of Alexandria did, yet without removing the primary obstacle to such a union, namely the continued proclamation of the council of Chalcedon as orthodox. In 433, John of Antioch agreed to the condemnation of Nestorius, and now, in John’s time, the Chalcedonians equally have to fulfill their side of the bargain if they want to overcome the division (Bunce 2023, 80–81).

div.head: EH 3.1.17.i (NK) 19.12–14
div.p: ܘܐ̇ܚ̣ܪܡܢ ܘܐ̇ܦ̣ܩܢ ܘܫ̣ܕܝܢܗ̇ ܠܒܪ ܡܢ ܥܕܬܗ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܠܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ: ܐܝܟ ܡܐ ܕܐܦ ܩܘܪܝܠܘܣ ܠܪܫ̣ܝܥܐ ܢܣܛܘܪܝܘܣ “we condemned, expelled, and cast it out from the church of God, that is, the synod of Chalcedon, just as Cyril did the evil Nestorius”: Here, a polemical tone is used to refer to the “evil Nestorius” (ܪܫ̣ܝܥܐ ܢܣܛܘܪܝܘܣ) and his condemnation at the council of Ephesus in 431. Two letters from Cyril to Nestorius formed the basis for Nestorius’s condemnation, namely the so-called Second Letter (Schwartz 1965b, 25–28) and the Third Letter (Schwartz 1965b, 33–42) containing the twelve anathemas from the year 430. Both of Cyril’s letters were read and accepted at the council of Ephesus Schwartz 1960b, 13–31 and 36–37; see Price and Graumann 2022, 115–120; 160–171; 219–220; 231–249; 255–256. The Severan bishops suggest that just as Cyril condemned Nestorius, so do they reject the council of Chalcedon and its decisions. The anathematization of Nestorius by Cyril at the council of Ephesus in 431 serves as a rhetorical device to reject any efforts at reconciliation based on the formula of union from 433.

div.head: EH 3.1.17.j (SR) 19.14–16
div.p: ܥܠ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܫ̣ܪܟܐ ܕܗ̈ܘ̣ܝ ܒܝܢ̣ܝܢ ܘܠܟܘܢ̣ ܠܐ ܡܬܚ̣ܪܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܥܡܟܘܢ. ܐܠܐ̣ ܘܕܠܐ ܟ̣ܠܝܢ ܡ̇ܬܚܝܕܝܢܢ ܠܟܘܢ “concerning the remaining things that happened between us, we do not quarrel with you. Rather, we are united with you without any hindrance”: John of Ephesus presents the council of Chalcedon as the sole obstacle to the union; the Severan bishops would be ready to forgive all the hardship they suffered during imprisonment. If Chalcedon were condemned, John asserts, then he and his peers would immediately restore the ecclesiastical communion with John Scholasticus. There are good reasons to doubt this apologetic picture. Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:285–290; 4:333–336 attests that throughout the negotiations leading up to the edict on faith presented in 567 in Callinicum (see the literature in 3.1.3.r), the Severan bishops did not insist on such a condemnation, and it is unlikely that they would have done so prior to the second edict, contrary to what John claims in 3.1.24.d (Roggo 2022, 103–106). John needed to defend himself to his co-religionists in the east after the failure of the union and consequently, in Book 1 of the EH, he creates the impression that he always was adamantly opposed to any agreement which did not include a condemnation of Chalcedon (see 3.1.17.a and 3.1.20.f).

div.head: EH 3.1.17.k (SR) 19.19–21
div.p: ܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܕܝܠܗ ܘܕܝܘܚܢܢ ܕܗܘ̣ܬ ܒܚܪܬܐ […] ܟܕ ܗ̇ܝ ܩܕܡܝܬܐ ܕܩܘܪܝܠܘܣ܇ ܠܥܒ̇ܕܐ ܠܐ ܢܦ̣ܩܬ݀ “the union between him and John that came at the end, as though the earlier union of Cyril had not come to pass”: John of Ephesus mentions here two unions achieved by Cyril. The one “between him and John that came at the end” evidently is the union of 433 which ended the divide between the churches of Alexandria and Antioch (see 3.1.17.g). The introduction of an “earlier union of Cyril” is more puzzling. The Syriac adjective ܩܕܡܝܬܐ (“earlier”) has a feminine ending and therefore needs to refer back to ܚܕܝܘܬܐ (“union”). The most likely explanation is that John perceived the council of Ephesus as a union. The council had been split in two rival camps around Cyril on the one side and John of Antioch and Nestorius on the other, but according to orthodox tradition, only the assembly of Cyril’s faction represented the ecumenical council (Price and Graumann 2022, 46–56). Since Nestorius had been condemned unequivocally by that faction, it is understandable that John of Ephesus regarded this as a first “union” achieved by Cyril.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus ends the series of chapters (EH 3.1.12–3.1.18 ) mainly dedicated to patriarch John Scholasticus’s demands for reordinations with a lengthy and, in all likelihood, largely fictional speech held by the Severan bishops in the patriarchal residence. Many arguments from earlier chapters, in particular Stephen’s speech in EH 3.1.16, are repeated: the bishops criticize John Scholasticus for the reordinations and show that his command violates canon law, especially because he did not insist on reordinations from the beginning, but first accepted the Severan clergy among his own. Moreover, inconsistently, he only demanded a reordination to the priesthood and not also a repetition of the baptism. According to John of Ephesus, the patriarch recognized that his opponents were right and he proved unable to counter their arguments. The Severans are thus once more depicted as being on the right side of the law, whereas John Scholasticus appears to be breaking the law (Francisco 2011a, 116–118). This whole exchange in the patriarchal palace is presented as directly following EH 3.1.17, where John described how he and his peers were brought to the palace for confinement. However, the reordinations probably only happened at a later point, well after Justin’s edict on faith (introduced in EH 3.1.19) (Roggo 2022, 103–108) (see 3.1.16.d and 3.1.17.a). Yet by placing the reordinations before the edict, John highlights the Chalcedonians’ alleged contempt for the proper ecclesiastical order and claims that reconciliation with the Severans was not their prime interest. They are thus disqualified from the outset, and this literary strategy evidently served John’s own apologetic purpose, namely that he had only entered into Chalcedonian communion for a short time and in extremis, because of the supposedly enormous pressure exerted on him, as he states in EH 3.1.24.l.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
EH 3.1.18.a (SR) 19.22
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܬܡܢܬܥܣܪ̈ “Chapter 18”: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:298.a.35–299.a.11; 4:340.a.40–341.b.6 only included a brief summary of this chapter, probably because he gave a more detailed summary of EH 3.1.16 with its very similar content just before this passage. He contented himself with mentioning the bishops’ criticism that John Scholasticus broke the canons when demanding reordinations and, in particular, that the patriarch only did so after having accepted the Severans among his own clergy for a prolonged period. Michael also indicated that the patriarch was unable to counter this criticism.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.b (SR) 19.29–20.3
div.p: ܠܕܪܫ̇ܐ ܕܥܠ ܐ̈ܦܝ ܗܝܡܢܘܬܐ: ܘܡܛܘܠ ܚܒ̇ܠܐ ܕܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ ܘܥܠ ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܡܢܗ̇ ܐܣܬ̣ܥܪ: ܟܠܝܘܡ ܥܡܗ ܥܐ̇ܠܝܢ ܗܘܘ “they entered into a debate with him everyday concerning the faith, the corruption of the synod of Chalcedon, and the things carried out by it”: John of Ephesus presents this chapter as directly following the discussions about the proposition based on the formula of union of 433 in the previous chapter EH 3.1.17. However, as argued in the summary above, this is rather unlikely: John Scholasticus probably demanded reordinations only after the negotiations about the union when the bishops had accepted Justin’s edict Roggo 2022, 103–108 Maraval 1998, 466–467, and John of Ephesus deliberately introduced the reordinations at an early point so as to highlight John Scholasticus’s disregard for the canons and for correct proceeding from the beginning. As Bunce (2023, 82–83) has argued, the speech of the Severan bishops in this chapter is likely fictional. It is indeed hard to imagine that the imprisoned bishops would have been able to dominate the discussion with the patriarch to the extent presented here; they were not in a position to move the debate away from the patriarch’s main interest, that is the establishment of church unity. The main obstacle for a union was the status of the contentious council of Chalcedon (see above 3.1.10.g). However, the EH’s continuous insistence that the Severan bishops would have been ready to agree to a union only after an explicit condemnation of the council is unconvincing. In all likelihood, this claim is part of John’s apology to his co-religionists in the east, whereas in reality, he and his peers may have been content with a formula of union which simply omitted any mention of the council (see 3.1.17.a and 3.1.17.j).

While the speech itself very likely is John’s invention, the setting in which he placed it probably is not: debates concerning the faith are amply attested throughout the 6th century, and repeatedly, they were held in the format alluded to here, consisting of daily meetings over a period of several days. For instance, the religious talks of 532 Hasse-Ungeheuer 2016, 37–45 Menze 2008b, 58–66 Uthemann 1999, 24–33 Frend 1972, 260–267 lasted three days, and the intra-miaphysite discussions of 570 chaired by John Scholasticus four Van Roey 1982, 487–489 Honigmann 1951, 185–186.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.c (SR) 20.5–6
div.p: ܕܚ̇ܘܐ ܠܢ ܐܘ ܪܒܐ ܘܐ̇ܚ̣ܝܕ ܪܝܫ̣ܢܘܬܐ ܕܥܕܬܐ “reveal to us, O great one and wielder of the ecclesiastical primacy”: The beginning of the Severans’ critical address to John Scholasticus appears somewhat sarcastic. Quite possibly, John of Ephesus as the author of this speech mocked the patriarch. The harassed bishops would not seriously have regarded the main instigator of the persecution, as John depicted him, as ܪܒܐ, that is, either “great one” or “teacher,” especially since they then went on to point out his uncanonical behavior. Yet at the same time, John and his episcopal peers may still have accepted the high rank of the patriarch of Constantinople who, according to Canon 28 of Chalcedon Price and Gaddis 2005, 3:75–76 Schwartz 1965a, 88.28–89.17, was second only to the bishop of Rome and therefore the highest-ranking bishop in the Roman east. Van Ginkel (1995, 115 and 147) has pointed out that John of Ephesus did not yet regard the Severan community as separated from the imperial church and that he condoned the privileges of the patriarchal see of Constantinople, even if the current office holder was, in his eyes, a persecutor of the orthodox.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.d (SR) 20.6–8
div.p: ܕܡܢ ܐ̇ܝܢܐ ܩܢܘܢܐ ܘܛܟ̣ܣܐ ܥܕܬܢܝܐ܇ ܝܠ̣ܦܬ ܘܩܒ̇ܠܬ ܕܬܫ̣ܪܐ ܟܗܢܘܬܐ ܕܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ܇ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܟܗ̈ܢܐ “from which canon and ecclesiastical ordinance you have learned and received to abolish the priesthood of the orthodox bishops and the rest of the priests”: John repeats the argument he has already made in EH 3.1.12 and placed in the mouth of Stephen of Cyprus in EH 3.1.16 that the patriarchal demand for reordinations of the Severan clergy was against the ecclesiastical canons, especially since he did not also demand their rebaptism; see 3.1.12.b; 3.1.12.f; 3.1.16.m.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.e (SR) 20.9–10
div.p: ܗܠܝܢ ܕܩ̇ܫܝܫܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܐܦ ܡܢ ܐܒܘܟ ܒܝ̈ܘܡܝ ܟܗܢܘܬܗܘܢ “who are even senior to your father in the days of their priesthood”: This remark is clearly exaggerated. John Scholasticus’s year of birth is unknown (see 3.1.12.e). However, given that he already was an established lawyer (scholastikos) around 550 (Troianos 2017, 142–144), he may have been born before 520, and his (unknown) father thus very probably before 500. It is evidently highly unlikely that in 571, there were still priests around who had been ordained in the late 5th century. Similar to this passage, John of Ephesus may also have exaggerated the length of Stephen’s episcopate in EH 3.1.16.n. These exaggerations probably served to further highlight the depravity of John Scholasticus who supposedly did not at all take into account the venerable old age of some of the clerics he wanted to reordain against the canons.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.f (NK) 20.11–12
div.p: ܒܟܗܢܘܬܐ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܝ ܟ̈ܝܢܐ. ܗܠ̣ܝܢ ܕܪܒܝܥ̣ܝܘܬܐ ܡ̇ܟܪܙܝܢ ܘܡ̇ܠܦܝܢ܇ ܚܠܦ ܬܠܝܬܝܘܬܐ ܓܢ̣ܝܙܬܐ ܘܩܕܫܬܐ “into the priesthood of the dyophysites who proclaim and teach a quaternity instead of the hidden and holy Trinity”: The accusation of teaching a quaternity against the proponents of the two-natures doctrine originates for the first time in Apollinaris of Laodicea: Apodeixis fr. 82 (Lietzmann 1904, 224.25–27). In his work Apodeixis, Apollinaris makes this accusation against representatives of the doctrine of the two natures, especially Diodore of Tarsus. Apollinaris develops his concept of the one nature or mode of being of Christ and the absolute unity in Jesus Christ. From that point forward, the accusation of the doctrine of the quaternity regularly appeared in polemic against dyophysites; see, for example, Severus of Antioch, Cathedral Homily 22 (Brière and Graffin 1977, 21); cf. (Esmeijer 1978).

Within EH 3, the alleged notion of the quaternity is repeatedly ascribed to the Chalcedonians (EH 3.1.10; 3.1.18; 3.2.35; 3.2.39; 3.3.17). This accusation forms part of the widespread polemic of miaphysites against the proponents of the doctrine of two natures, who – according to their critics – introduce an additional “person” into the Trinity by asserting the double nature in Jesus Christ. For John, this accusation entails the division of Jesus Christ “into two natures after the union” (ܠܬܪ̈ܝܢ ܟ̈ܝܢܝܢ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ), which he, as an advocate for miaphysite Christology, cannot accept (cf. EH 3.1.10.g). From his perspective, a two-nature Christology distorts the mystery of “the hidden and holy Trinity” (ܬܠܝܬܝܘܬܐ ܓܢ̣ܝܙܬܐ ܘܩܕܫܬܐ) and misrepresents the core of the Christian confession. Chalcedonian authors defended themselves against this accusation, categorizing it as a deliberate misinterpretation of their Christology. Justinian, for example, explicitly addressed this accusation in his writing Contra Monophysitas from the year 542/543, portraying it as miaphysite polemic: “For the Holy Trinity does not admit an addition of a fourth person, but only one from the Holy Trinity, the incarnate and humanized God-Logos; and we do not know one God-Logos and another Christ, but we confess one and the same God-Logos, the Lord Jesus Christ (οὔτε γὰρ τετάρτου προσώπου προσθήκην ἐπιδέχεται ἡ ἁγία τριὰς τοῦ ἑνὸς τῆς τριάδος θεοῦ λόγου σαρκωθέντος καὶ ἐνανθρωπήσαντος, καὶ οὐκ ἄλλον τὸν θεὸν λόγον καὶ ἄλλον τὸν Χριστὸν ἐπιστάμεθα, ἀλλ’ ἕνα καὶ τὸν αὐτὸν θεὸν λόγον κύριον Ἰησοῦν Χριστὸν ὁμολογοῦμεν)” (Schwartz 1939, 8.25–28). The accusation of introducing the quaternity into the divine Trinity developed into a “commonplace of anti-Nestorian polemics” in the 6th century (Uthemann 2005, 218 and 285).

div.head: EH 3.1.18.g (SR) 20.20
div.p: ܓ̇ܘܕ̈ܦܝܗ̇ ܕܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܘܛܘܡ̣ܣܐ ܕܠܐܘܢ “the blasphemies of the synod and the Tome of Leo”: From an anti-Chalcedonian viewpoint, the main blasphemy of Chalcedon was its supposed teaching of a quaternity by insisting on the continuous existence of two natures in Christ after the union; see 3.1.10.g and 3.1.18.f. Anti-Chalcedonians regarded the embracement of the Tome of Leo by the council of Chalcedon as a crucial milestone proving that the council was heretical. The Tome, in the western tradition usually called “Tomus ad Flavianum,” was a letter sent by the Roman bishop Leo I (sed. 440–461) to bishop Flavian of Constantinople (sed. 446–449) on 13 June 449. The pope explained that there are two natures in the one person Jesus Christ; at Chalcedon two years later, the Roman delegates were adamant that this formulation be used in the council’s definition of faith Price and Gaddis 2005, 2:1–5 and 14–24 Grillmeier 1990a, 736–750.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.h (SR) 20.30–21.2
div.p: ܕܐܦ ܡ̇ܥܡܘܕܝܬܗܘܢ ܬܫ̣ܪܘܢ ܗܘܝܬܘܢ܇ ܘܡܢ ܕܪܝܫ ܬ̇ܥܡܕܘܢ ܐܢܘܢ ܐܝܟ ܚܝ̇ܠܐ ܕܦܘ̈ܩܕܢܐ ܕܩ̈ܢܘܢܐ “that you also would have abolished their baptism and rebaptized them according to the force of the canonical commands”: John of Ephesus has the bishops repeat a point already made by Elisha of Sardis in EH 3.1.15.g and Stephen of Cyprus in EH 3.1.16.j: John Scholasticus’s demand for reordinations was inconsistent because he did not also insist on their rebaptism. The patriarch’s violation of the canons would not have been so blatant, had he at least declared invalid all the sacraments the Severans had received, and not just a single one. As noted above (3.1.12.b), canon law in no case demands only a reordination to the priesthood of perceived heretics, and not also their rebaptism. The “canonical commands” quoted here refer to Canon 19 of Nicaea (Alberigo 2006, I:30), on which see 3.1.18.i.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.i (SR) 21.2–4
div.p: ܩܢܘܢܐ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܬܫܬܥܣܪ̈ ܕܬܠܬܡܐܐ ܘܬܡܢܬܥܣܪ̈܆ ܥܠ ܗܪܣܝܣ ܡ̇ܚܒܠܬܐ ܕܒܝܬ ܦܘܠܐ ܫܡܝ̣ܫܛܝܐ “the nineteenth canon of the 318 about the condemned heresy of the followers of Paul of Samosata”: Already Stephen of Cyprus referred to Canon 19 of Nicaea (Alberigo 2006, I:30) in his complaint to Justin II about John Scholasticus’s illegal action (see 3.1.16.p). This canon demands both rebaptism and reordination of repentant clergy who had been following Paul of Samosata. Consequently, the Severan critics of John Scholasticus accuse him of breaking the canons since he did not also insist on rebaptisms. The manuscript actually has ܩܢܘܢܐ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܫܬܬܥܣܪ̈ “the sixteenth canon,” most likely a mistake by transposition made by the scribe since only the position of the two letters ܫ and ܬ would have been reversed (ܫܬܬܥܣܪ̈ “sixteenth” vs. ܬܫܬܥܣܪ̈ “nineteenth”).

div.head: EH 3.1.18.j (SR) 21.11–13
div.p: ܒܫܠ̣ܝܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܫ̇ܡܥ ܗܘܐ ܘܝ̇ܕܥ ܕܥ̣̈ܕܝܠܢ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ܆ ܟܕ ܠܝܬ ܗܘܐ ܠܗ ܕܒܐ̇ܝܢܐ ܡ̣ܦܩ ܒܪܘܚܐ ܕܚܠܦܝܗܝܢ ܢܬ̇ܚܫܚ “he listened to all these things in silence and recognized that they were condemnable, since he did not have any way of putting up a defense concerning them”: Both in this passage and again at the end of the chapter, John of Ephesus depicts John Scholasticus as beaten and unable to counter the arguments of his Severan opponents. In the first instance presented here, this happens with regard to the claim that he broke the canons when not accepting the priesthood of the Severans without, however, equally rejecting the validity of their baptism. In the second instance at the end of the chapter, the patriarch has to acknowledge defeat when confronted with the inconsistency that he had first integrated the Severan clerics in his own clergy and celebrated the liturgy with them before suddenly demanding their reordination (see below 3.1.18.n). As noted above (in the summary and in 3.1.18.b), John of Ephesus provides us here with his own, very carefully crafted version of the events. There is no reason to assume that the discussion between the bishops and the patriarch – if there was at all such a discussion after the attempt at reordinations – took place in the manner described here and that the patriarch was so completely defeated. Bunce (2023, 82) has noted that in his narrative, John probably was “picking battles he knew he could win,” and therefore concentrated on the supposedly inconsistent and uncanonical behavior of the patriarch, whereas the real focus must have lain on the union.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.k (SR; HL) 21.23–26
div.p: ܘܐܦ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܐܬܪ̈ܘܬܐ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܡܬ̣ܚܬܘܢܝܗܝ ܠܥ̣ܒܪ ܢܡܘܣܐ ܗܢܐ܇ ܘܠܣܩ̣ܘܒܠܝܘܬܐ ܕܟܠܗܘܢ ܛܟ̣ܣ̈ܐ ܘܕܟܠܗܘܢ ܩ̈ܢܘܢܐ ܥܕܬ̈ܢܝܐ “you also extended this transgression of the law to other regions along with the opposition to all ordinances and all ecclesiastical canons”: This repeats the assertion made in EH 3.1.12.i that John Scholasticus wrote to other bishops, asking them to equally subject the Severan clergy to uncanonical reordination.

A leitmotif of John of Ephesus’s account is his insistence that John Scholasticus, who had written a work on canon law (the Synagoga L titulorum (Beneševič 1937)) was transgressing the law. Other references to “ordinances and canons” (with both terms being in the plural) are found in EH 3.2.32; 3.4.16; 3.5.2. Interestingly, EH 3.1.18 is the sole instance where ܛܟܣܐ occurs in the singular in this context. A similar expression is ܛܟܣܐ ܩܢܘܢܝܐ (“canonical order”), see EH 3.1.12.h; 3.1.40.e; 3.2.25 and seven other times; there is also ܛܟܣܐ ܕܩ̈ܢܘܢܐ (“the order of the canons”) in EH 3.2.32. The combination was common in contemporary Greek, see, for instance, Eustratius, Life of Eutychius (Laga 1992, 24.688–689) κατὰ τάξιν καὶ κατὰ τοὺς θείους κανόνας. The combination of both words in the singular παρὰ κανόνα καὶ τάξιν is found in Canon 18 of Nicaea (Alberigo 2006, I:30.374–375), also quoted by John Scholasticus (!) in Synagoga 13.2 (Beneševič 1937, 80.17–18) with the plural κανόνας in one manuscript. Both nouns in the plural (John of Ephesus’s preferred variant) are found in the 4th-century Greek author Serapion Thmuitanus, Euchologion 12 (M. E. Johnson 1995, 58.12). The two words are particularly prevalent in Greek sources in conjunction with the ordination of deacons. The semantics of the wide range of combinations of the words in the singular and plural, as combinations of noun and adjective, and with changing order deserves further study in both Greek and Syriac.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.l (SR) 22.1–4
div.p: ܕܒܙܒܢܐ ܐ̇ܝܢܐ ܕܗ̣ܘ ܐܡܬܝ ܕܢܩ̣ܪܐ: ܐ̇ܘ ܒܚܝ̣̈ܝܟܘܢ ܐ̇ܘ ܐܢ ܓ̣ܕܫ ܕܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܟܘܢ܆ ܠܒ̣ܨܬܐ ܚܠ̣ܝܡܬܐ ܘܒ̇ܥܬܐ ܩܢܘܢܝܬܐ ܐܝܬ ܠܗܝܢ ܠܟܠܗܝܢ ܕܢܐ̣ܬ̈ܝܢ “at whatever time it is that it summons, either during your life or, should it happen, after your death, all of these things will need to come to an orderly inquiry and a canonical investigation”: The subject of ܢܩ̣ܪܐ (“he / it summons”) is not specified and might either be “time” itself, or, quite likely, God, as was already considered by Brooks (1936, 15 n.3).

The passage’s insistence on eventual retribution for John Scholasticus’s uncanonical reordinations, perhaps only after his death, may refer back to the very start of the EH. In EH 3.1.3.d– 3.1.3.i, John of Ephesus interprets the hardship of the Severans as a sign of the impending Last Judgment; moreover, the divine punishment of persecutors is a recurrent theme in the EH, see van Ginkel (1995, 186–188) and 3.1.10.l. However, it is not certain that John really is thinking about the end of time here: his mention of “an orderly inquiry and a canonical investigation” rather points toward a more earthly procedure. Through the bishops, he expresses his hope that the illegality of the patriarch’s actions will be officially acknowledged and corrected; there is no focus on punishment for John Scholasticus himself here. John of Ephesus was still convinced that Severan doctrine would eventually prevail in the imperial church and find universal acceptance (van Ginkel 1995, 166–167). Consequently, he might, in this passage, have been thinking about a synod with representatives from all the five patriarchates who would officially condemn John Scholasticus – similar to what had happened in 571/572, only a few years before he wrote the EH, when a synod had repeated the anathema on Severus of Antioch (de Halleux 1977a, 302–306).

A further instance of an – equally unsuccessful – call for a canonical investigation is found in EH 3.2.32–3.2.33, when the representative of the Roman bishop in Constantinople demands an inquiry into the legality of Eutychius’s return as patriarch in 577 (Roggo 2022, 6–8).

div.head: EH 3.1.18.m (PMF) 22.5–6
div.p: ܕܚ̇ܫܚܐ ܠܬܡ̣ܗܐ ܘܠܡ̇ܬܠܐ ܘܠܬ̇ܘܢܝܐ ܕܦܘ̇ܫܟ ܐ̣ܝ̈ܕܝܐ “it is fit for wonder, a fable, and a perplexing tale”: The phrase ܘܠܬ̇ܘܢܝܐ ܕܦܘ̇ܫܟ ܐ̣ܝ̈ܕܝܐ “a perplexing tale” or “an astonishing tale” poses interpretive challenges. While the first element ܬܘܢܝܐ can easily be rendered as “tale” or “story,” the translation and meaning of the adjectival phrase that follows – ܕܦܘܫܟ ܐܝ̈ܕܝܐ – is somewhat unclear. Payne Smith (1860, 27) simply renders it literally as “the clapping of hands.” The translations of Brooks (1936, 15) – “narrationi plausum excitanti” – and Loconte (2023, 60) – “all’applauso” – understand it as relating to applause, certainly with sarcastic overtones. Schönfelder’s translation (1862, 17) – “des Ausklatschens werth” – brings out the meaning of clapping that insults those being applauded.

Sokoloff (2009, 1175) suggests “perplexity” for the phrase based on the translation of the Greek word ἀπορία with ܦܘܫܟ ܐܝ̈ܕܝܐ in the Peshitta version of Luke 21:25 . The same rendering appears in the Curetonian version of the Old Syriac Gospels, while the Harklean version has ܦܘܫܟܐ (Kiraz 2004, 3:431). It should be noted that ἀπορίαonly occurs once in the New Testament, and Syriac translations of the six occurrences of ἀπορεῖν do not use the same expression. Syriac authors of the 6th and 7th centuries use this term to mean “perplexity” or “confusion.” In Jacob of Serugh’s Homily on the End 2, the world is described as an elderly person dealing with the pains of old life: “In his sleep, [there is] fear; in his wakefulness, confusion [ܦܘܫܟ ܐܝ̈ܕܐ]” Bedjan and Brock 2006, 5:859.11 Isebaert-Cauuet 2005, 137. Philoxenus of Mabbug lists ܦܘܫܟ ܐܝ̈ܕܝܐ as something into which the demons cast the ascetic in parallel with their efforts to “disturb the stability of his thoughts” Budge 1894, 1:2989.21-22 Kitchen 2013, 221. A similar connection is made in Ishoʿdad III’s Letter 17 where he puts “the trembling of weak thoughts” in parallel with ܦܘܫܟ ܐܝ̈ܕܝܐ Duval 1904, 24.11-12; 1904, 23.

The most important reference point, though, is the use of this term in EH 3.4.58 where John speaks of being in disbelief at the deaths of two competing Severan leaders, describing how one is led to ܕܘܡܪܐ ܪܒܐ “a great marvel” (3.4.58) and ܦܘܫܟ ܐܝ̈ܕܝܐ “perplexity” or “astonishment” (3.4.58). On the basis of John’s use of the phrase and that of the broader Syriac tradition, the passage as a whole and this phrase emphasize the confusing, contradictory acts of the Severans’ opponents.

div.head: EH 3.1.18.n (SR; PMF) 22.14–19
div.p: ܘܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܬܠܬܝܢ ܘܫܬܐ ܩ̈ܘ̇ܕܫܝܢ܇ ܘܠܝ̣ܛܘܪ̈ܓܝܣ ܕܒܟܠܗ̇ ܥܐܕܐ ܕܦܛܝܪ̈ܐ܇ ܘܐܦ ܡܢ ܒܬܪܟܢ ܥܒ̣ܕܘ ܥܡܟܘܢ܇ ܟܕ ܒܗ̇ ܒܟܗܢܘܬܗܘܢ ܩܕܡܝܬܐ ܩ̇ܒܠܬܘܢ ܐܢܘܢ܇ ܘܠܩܫ̈ܝܫܐ ܥܡ ܩܫ̈ܝܫܐ ܕܝܠܟܘܢ ܒܐܝ̣ܪܛܝܘܢ ܐܘܬ̣ܒܬܘܢ܇ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܕܪܘܚ̇ܦܐ ܕܐܬܐ̣ܡܪܘ “they carried out with you thirty-six Eucharistic anaphoras and the liturgies through the entire feast of unleavened bread and afterwards, as you accepted their original priestly rank and had them sit as priests in the hierateion with your priests for all the days with a sanctification, which have been mentioned”: The bishops repeat here points John of Ephesus made in EH 3.1.12.b–3.1.12.d, at the start of his account on the reordinations: John Scholasticus first accepted the Severan clergy among his own after he had forced them into communion, he had them sit in the hierateion (the sanctuary) jointly with his own priests and celebrate the Eucharist together up to thirty-six times, before then suddenly demanding that they be reordained. The Severans mainly criticize him for this inconsistency; if the priesthood they had received as Severans really was such a problem, then why not reordain them from the beginning?

The indication that the thirty-six liturgies in which some Severan clerics participated happened in the period of Easter and afterwards sits well with the start date of the persecution given in EH 3.1.5.d. According to John, the hardship began one week before Easter, on 21 March 571, and it is conceivable that some Severan clerics gave in to the pressure fairly quickly and agreed to concelebrate during Easter (for this establishment of joint communion, less force may have been necessary than John claims; see above 3.1.17.a and 3.1.17.j), while the Severan bishops were presented with Justin’s edict on faith and eventually agreed to enter into a short-lived communion with the Chalcedonians (3.1.24.l). As detailed in 3.2.7.g, the start date of 21 March 571 does not necessarily clash with the chronological information John gives in EH 3.2.7 about the length of his different confinements up to 577.

The Syriac word ܪܘܚܦܐ, translated here as “sanctification” generally refers to the presence and agency of the Holy Spirit, especially during liturgical rites (Brock 1999, 339–344). The term has its origins in the imagery of Genesis 1:2 where a wind or spirit (Syriac: ܪܘܚܐ) is said to have been “hovering [ܡܪܚܦܐ] over the waters” (Jansma and The Peshiṭta Institute Leiden 1977, 1). In the present passage of the EH, the term seems to reflect its use in the Syriac tradition during the Eucharistic epiclesis or invocation of the Holy Spirit (Brock 2001, 395–397). The same word appears in three other places in EH 3. In two of these, the specific liturgical context is, albeit probable, not quite clear (EH 3.2.15.i and 3.2.37), but in the third it clearly refers to a Eucharistic celebration (EH 3.2.47). The same term also occurs in LES 47, where John refers to “the consecrations [ܩܘ̈ܕܫܐ] and sanctifications [ܘܪ̈ܘܚܦܐ]” being celebrated Brooks 1926, 477.1 see Brock 1999, 344.

Our translation of ܪܘܚܦܐ as “sanctification” differs from all other translations of the EH. First, we have not been able to find any external support for the translation of the word as “love feast” (R. Payne Smith 1860, 27) or “Liebe” (Schönfelder 1862, 17). In his lexicon, R. Payne Smith (1879, 2:3887) also expresses some hesitation about translating ܪܘܚܦܐ as festum agapes, even though he cites the EH and the pairing ܒܪ̈ܚܡܐ ܘܒܪܘܚܦܐ in Jacob of Serugh in support of this interpretation. To be sure, in the Peshitta, ܪܘܚܦܐ is used to express concepts like “favor” and “compassion” (Brock 1999, 328): it translates, for example, the Hebrew word חֵן in Zechariah 12:10
(Gelston 1980, 89) and σπλάγχνα in Philippians 2:1
(Juckel and Parlevliet-Flesseman 2023, 211). But this does not provide evidence for a liturgical context. Second, the translation “illapsuum” (Brooks 1936, 15), “descents of the Spirit” (Brooks 1926, 476–477), or “della discesa dello Spirito Santo” (Loconte 2023, 60) does not make clear the activity of the priests as is implied in the passage in the EH. Further, in the Syriac tradition, the Holy Spirit is generally invoked to “come” rather than to “come down” (Brock 1999, 381).

The use of the term in association with the Eucharist just before John’s time can be further demonstrated through the works of Jacob of Serugh. In his Homily on Elisha 1, Jacob writes: “Through [the Spirit’s] hovering/sanctification [ܒܪܘܚܦܗ̇], it consecrates the bread and becomes the body” Bedjan and Brock 2006, 4:279.12 Kaufman 2010, 66. In his Homily on the Partaking of the Holy Mysteries, Jacob describes the effects of the “hovering/sanctification” of the Spirit on participants in the Eucharistic service: “Through its hovering/sanctification [ܒܪܘܚܦܗ], it mixes [the participants] in a consecrating manner, / and they become one with him, as it is written in a mysterious way [cf. John 17:11, 21]” Bedjan and Brock 2006, 4:657.12–13 Harrak 2013, 30. Jacob extends this activity to priests in his Letter 30, where he refers to the actions of the priests in the liturgy as ܒܪ̈ܘܚܦܝܗܘܢ which can be translated “by their sanctifications,” seemingly portraying the priestly acts of sanctification as an extension of the activity of the Holy Spirit (Olinder 1937, 236.25). For a broader discussion of the Holy Spirit’s role in the Eucharistic theology of the Syriac tradition, including a reference to Jacob and other early Syriac thinkers, see Shemunkasho (2021, 37–43).

div.head: EH 3.1.18.o (SR) 23.6–7
div.p: ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܣܘܓܐ̣ܗܝܢ ܘܡܢ ܡ̣ܬܚܐ ܕܬܫ̣ܥܝܬܐ ܕܥܠܝܗܝܢ ܡܢܗܝܢ ܡ̇ܗܡܝܢܢ “which we omit because of their copiousness and the length of the story about them”: John of Ephesus frequently asserts that he could have provided us with even more information, yet refrained from doing so in order to prevent an exaggerate length of his account (see above 3.1.3.u, where more examples are listed).

div.head: Summary (SR; HL)
div.p: This chapter opens a crucial part of Book 1, focusing on the negotiations about the ecclesiastical union which Justin II hoped to achieve by means of his edict on faith (EH 3.1.19–3.1.24). The edict is here introduced for the first time: the Severan bishops, confined in the patriarchal palace, receive a first version and are asked to add amendments so as to render it acceptable from their doctrinal point of view. John of Ephesus admits that he and his peers agreed with large parts of the edict’s definition of faith, yet they added some statements directed against dyophysitism. Upon receipt of these additions, the emperor, so John claims, was pleased and inclined to accept them. This, however, caused the fierce resistance of staunchly pro-Chalcedonian circles around John Scholasticus and the quaestor Anastasius. John of Ephesus, eager to present the emperor in a favorable light, asserts that Justin II was angry at them, even issued threats, and demanded that the edict be published with the amendments. Yet in the end, he nevertheless gave in and allowed that most of the amendments were deleted again, thus rendering it impossible for the Severan bishops to agree to the edict.

By placing the sequence of chapters regarding Justin’s edict only after the treatment of John Scholasticus’s demand for reordinations (EH 3.1.12–3.1.16 and 3.1.18), John of Ephesus very likely distorts the chronological order of events (see 3.1.17.a). In this way, the readers of the EH already know before the introduction of Justin’s edict that the Chalcedonians around John Scholasticus are highly untrustworthy and breaking the canons. However, discussions about the union and the edict must have been the main reason for why the Severan bishops were assembled in the first place. Indeed, some scholars have argued that the bishops were presented with the edict already quite soon after their imprisonment and that the reordinations only took place at a later point Roggo 2022, 103–108 Maraval 1998, 466–467. Probably, the events narrated in the present chapter follow upon EH 3.1.17 with its first attempt at a restoration of ecclesiastical unity based on the formula of 433.

The procedure outlined by John in EH 3.1.19–3.1.21 bears a strong resemblance to what a constitution from 446 determined for the case that a new lex generalis (a law of general application) was made (Codex Iustinianus 1.14.8 (Blume and Frier 2016, 262); see Honoré 1986, 136–137 Dölger and Karagiannopulos 1968, 71–73): Such a new law was to be discussed (tractari) by both the imperial consistorium and the senate. Once a first consensus was reached, it was to be drafted (allegata dictari), then reviewed with the involvement of all parties (recenseri), subsequently presented before the consistorium (recitari), and finally endorsed by the emperor (firmari). The overarching aim appears to have been to achieve the broadest possible consensus.

What John describes in EH 3.1.19–3.1.20 in the preparation of Justin’s edict on faith is presumably adapted mutatis mutandis; however, the senate is replaced by circles of church officials affected by the edict. The fact that John takes issue with the involvement of his doctrinal opponents (see 3.1.19.h and 3.1.19.j) suggests that the steps of this legislative procedure leading towards the finalized edict are not his own construction, but rather reflect the emperor’s attempt to apply a proven consensus procedure to disruptive church policy: The situation as presented in the preceding chapters, particularly EH 3.1.17, indicates that imperial intervention is necessary. EH 3.1.19 then describes the emperor’s intervention, who considers it possible to reach a consensus between the two doctrinal camps by means of an edict (3.1.19.c). Therefore, he crafts a text, which is copied out (the aphʿel verb ܐܨܚ ʾaṣaḥ related to the noun ܨܚܚܐ ṣḥāḥā “draft” which may be the technical term); this draft is then submitted to the ecclesiastical representatives for examination by the experienced negotiator Zacharias (3.1.19.d). The Severan bishops take their task seriously and write down amendments (3.1.19.g). These amendments are brought back to and recited before the emperor (ܐܬܩܪܝܘ eṯqriw; corresponding to the recitari of Codex Iustinianus 1.14.8 (Blume and Frier 2016, 262)), who then asks the quaestor, into whose responsibility such legislative procedures fell, to integrate them into the edict. However, according to John, objections from the opposing pro-Chalcedonian faction thwart this endeavor. Nevertheless, the outcome is a version which Justin II deems definitive (3.1.20.b–3.1.20.c). In fact, the Severan bishops would eventually consent to joint communion with the Chalcedonians on the basis of this edict (3.1.24.l). This suggests that the legislative procedure led to success for a certain period of time, although John depicts the whole attempt at reconciliation as doomed to failure (3.1.24.g and 3.1.24.l).

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:299.b.19–300.b.10; 4:341.a.11–27: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.19.a

div.head: Parallel Accounts (SR)
div.p: Evagrius Scholasticus, EH 5.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 197.28–201.11): Justin II’s edict of 571 Nicephorus Callistus, EH 17.35 (PG 147:308C–313A (Migne 1857–1866)): derivative of Evagrius Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.4 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:295–299; 4:338–341: Justin II’s edict of 571 P‘ilon Tirakac‘i, Chronicle Hakobyan 2005, 963–965 Abrahamyan 1944, 396–397: resistance by some Chalcedonians to Justin II’s religious policy; see 3.1.19.c John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 401): brief mention that Justin II promulgated an edict on faith; see 3.1.19.c

div.head: EH 3.1.19.a (SR) 23.16
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܬܫܬܥܣܪ̈ “Chapter 19”: Michael the Syrian included a short summary of this chapter in his Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 2:299.b.19–300.b.10; 4:341.a.11–27. He records accurately that Justin asked the imprisoned bishops to correct the draft of the edict he had sent them; the Chalcedonians around patriarch John Scholasticus and the quaestor Anastasius were opposed to their suggestions and protested in front of the emperor. As in the EH, Justin first appears inclined to accept the amendments made by the Severans. He threatened the quaestor and demanded that he quickly produce twenty copies of the edict (note that Chabot by mistake translated ܥܣܪܝܢ ܐܝܣ̈ܐ as “ten copies” instead of “twenty”). Yet in the end, Justin gave in and allowed the Chalcedonians to delete most of the amendments. Intriguingly, the final passage of the chapter concerning the sentence “let the customs of the church be retained” is missing in Michael (on this sentence, see 3.1.19.o below). This sentence is also missing when he quotes the full edict in Chronicle 10.4 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:295–299; 4:338–341. The reasons for this omission remain obscure; to include this sentence would have allowed Michael to claim – just as John of Ephesus did – that some Chalcedonians were never interested in achieving a lasting union. Given that Michael’s text of the edict is, with the exception of that last sentence, very close to Evagrius’s version, “we must conclude that the monophysite tradition deliberately omitted the final clause, rather than that Michael’s version is related to the draft edict mentioned by John of Ephesus” (Allen 1981, 213).

div.head: EH 3.1.19.b (SR) 23.18–19
div.p: ܟܕ ܓܝܪ ܚ̣ܙܘ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܣܘܢܗܕܣ: ܕܡܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܐܨܛ̇ܢܥܘ ܢܦ̣ܠܘ ܗܘܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܡܟܝܠ “when the partisans of the synod saw that some of those who had been seduced had then come over to them”: This is another crucial passage for establishing the internal chronology of Book 1. The final ܠܗܘܢ (“to them”) is ambiguous, as it might refer to either the “partisans of the synod” or to the Severans, yet, based on the wider context, the former solution is more probable. The end of the previous chapter EH 3.1.18 appears to be at odds with this interpretation as it depicts patriarch John Scholasticus being unable to counter the bishops’ arguments and all but being forced to tacitly acknowledge that his actions have been contrary to the canons. However, this inconsistency can be explained by bearing in mind that in all likelihood, John of Ephesus rearranged the order of events in Book 1 for his own apologetic purposes (see 3.1.17.a and 3.1.18.b). It makes more sense to place the actions described in this chapter directly after EH 3.1.17 where John reports the first attempt of the Chalcedonians at a union. Crucially, in EH 3.1.17.a and 3.1.17.h, John narrates that the proposition of the Chalcedonians was of “misleading duplicity” (3.1.17; ܕܓ̇ܠܘܬܐ ܡ̇ܛܥܝܢܝܬܐ) and full of “mischievousness” (3.1.17; ܛ̇ܪܟܢܘܬܐ) as they wanted to trick the Severans into a union based on the formula of 433. John is thus probably referring to that proposition when he asserts here that some of his co-religionists were “seduced” (ܐܨܛ̇ܢܥܘ) and joined the Chalcedonians. If this interpretation is correct, the Severan bishops must have been presented with Justin’s edict quite soon after their initial imprisonment in the patriarchal palace treated in EH 3.1.17 and, contrary to the impression John conveys, well before the patriarchal demand for reordinations.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.c (SR) 23.19–21
div.p: ܙܟ̇ܝܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ܆ ܐܬ̣ܐ ܠܗܝ̇ ܕܢܥ̣ܒܕ ܐ̣ܝܕܝܩܛܘܢ܇ ܐܝܟ ܕܣ̇ܒܪ ܕܢܥ̣ܒܕ ܒܗ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ “the victorious emperor Justin came to craft an edict thinking he would create a union through it”: Justin’s edict of 571 and his attempt to establish a lasting ecclesiastical union between the supporters and the opponents of Chalcedon are only poorly attested in sources other than John of Ephesus, Evagrius, and their respective derivatives (Dölger and Müller 2009, 9.19). The initiatives of the first six years of Justin’s reign (565–571) in which the emperor, as John states in EH 3.1.3.r, was actively engaged in securing the unity of the church would be completely unknown, were it not for Michael the Syrian who preserved the lost second part of John’s EH in Chronicle 10.1–2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:283–290; 4:331–336. With regard to the events described here, there is a probable mention of the edict of 571 in the Chronicle of the anti-Chalcedonian John of Nikiu (Zotenberg 1883, 401); on him, see Yirga 2022, 519–522 Booth 2011, 555–560. John of Nikiu, writing in the second half of the 7th century, merely states that Justin issued an edict on faith and that this was followed by a great persecution of the anti-Chalcedonians in Egypt (cf. 3.1.33.b). He omits thus the whole series of negotiations and the conciliatory nature of the edict treated in EH 3.1.19–3.1.29 and only focuses on the results of its eventual failure. Contrary to John of Nikiu, the Armenian chronicler P‘ilon Tirakac‘i, another anti-Chalcedonian writing towards the end of the 7th century, acknowledges Justin’s efforts, albeit in a heavily distorted manner Hakobyan 2005, 963–965 Abrahamyan 1944, 396–397. He does not mention any edict, yet claims that Justin finally came to recognize the heretical nature of Chalcedon and wanted to abolish the council’s dyophysite definition of faith, but died before being able to do so (Greenwood 2008, 242–243).
The epithet ܙܟ̇ܝܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ (“victorious emperor”) appears for the first time in EH 3.1.4.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.d (SR) 23.23–25
div.p: ܒܝܕ ܙܟܪܝܐ ܐܢܫ ܣܘ̣ܦܣܛܐ ܘܐ̇ܪܟܝܛܪܣ ܕܦܠܛܝܢ܇ ܕܒܓܢܣ̣ܗ ܡܢ ܫܘ̣ܪܐ ܕܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ “through a certain sophist Zacharias, the archiatrus palatii, who was, in regard to his family, from Shura of the Romans”: The chief physician Zacharias had already brought the imperial edict to the Severan bishops assembled at Callinicum in 567, as is attested in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:289.b.13–14; 4:335.a.27. In the passage in question, Michael calls him not only archiatrus (“chief physician”), but also archdeacon (ܐܪܟܝܕܝܩܘܢ), and he might consequently have held that ecclesiastical rank. Apart from his involvement in the negotiations with the Severans in 567 and 571, Zacharias repeatedly served as an envoy to the Persians from the mid-560s through to the early 580s; we know about five occasions when he participated in an embassy to Persia (PLRE 3:1411–1412 (Martindale 1992)). John of Ephesus mentions two of these embassies in EH 3.6.12 and 3.6.26. In the former instance, he qualifies him again as “physician and sophist” (ܐ̇ܣܝܐ ܘܣܦܝܣܛܣ), in the latter as “sophist” (ܣܘܦܣܛܐ). “Sophist” needs to mean little more than a person who had enjoyed a traditional education based on rhetorics and classical literature; John also mentions “sophists” together with “physicians” in a passage of the second part of the EH preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle Chabot 1933a, 76.21 Witakowski 1996, 71. As archiatrus sacri palatii (“chief physician of the imperial palace”), Zacharias belonged to the top echelons of society; in late antiquity, court physicians like him repeatedly rose to high administrative posts and enjoyed the rank of a comes. Zacharias’s frequent dispatches as an envoy are, therefore, not extraordinary (Jones 1964, 1012).

Zacharias’s hometown Shura lay in Syria on the river Euphrates, about 25 km to the west of Callinicum. His Syrian origins no doubt rendered him familiar with miaphysitism and may explain why he was twice chosen to convey an imperial edict – he might even have harbored Severan sympathies in the past, as John asserts here. The phrasing “Shura of the Romans” (ܫܘ̣ܪܐ ܕܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ) very likely refers to the fact that Shura was an important garrison town close to the eastern border; see 3.1.9.mabove for John’s use of “Romans” when he talks about soldiers of the imperial army.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.e (NK) 23.26–27
div.p: ܥܡ ܨܚܚ̣ܐ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܐ̣ܝܕܝܩܛܘܢ ܕܐܨ̣ܚܘ “with a draft of the edict that they arranged to have drafted”: Brooks (1935, 23.27) proposed to alter the verb ܐܨ̣ܚܘ“they arranged to have drafted,” attested in the manuscript, from the third person plural to the third person singular ܐܨ̣ܚ. Yet the emperor did not compose the edict on his own but collaborated with his advisors (see the overview of the legislative procedure in the summary of this chapter). Accordingly, the reading in the manuscript can be retained.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.f (SR) 23.28–29
div.p: ܡ̇ܪܚܡܢܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ “the merciful emperor”: John of Ephesus usually only calls an emperor ܡ̇ܪܚܡܢܐ (“merciful”) when he is sympathetic towards him (Arcuri 2008, 602–604). For instance, he repeatedly uses this epithet for Tiberius (r. 578–582), who is depicted as tolerant and as opposed to persecuting the Severans (EH 3.3.6; 3.4.39; 3.4.42). In the light of this evidence, it might first appear surprising that Justin II is addressed as “merciful” here – after all it was he who started the suppression of the Severans in 571. However, John of Ephesus puts this utterance in the mouth of Zacharias, and it is of course to be expected that an imperial envoy would describe his overlord with terms highlighting his moral qualities. Moreover, irrespective of this, Justin’s depiction in Book 1 is quite favorable as John of Ephesus puts all the blame for the persecution on patriarch John Scholasticus (see 3.1.5.e) and, at the same time, acknowledges that Justin has been sincerely working for ecclesiastical unity: in 3.1.19.m–3.1.19.n, he asserts that the emperor would have been willing to accept the Severan amendments to the edict.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.g (NK) 24.6–11
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܟܕ ܗܢܐ ܦܘܩܕܢܐ ܩ̇ܒܠܘ ܐܦܝܣ̈ܩܘܦܐ܆ ܫܩ̣ܠܘ̣ ܘܩ̣ܪܐܘܗܝ. ܘܚܙܘ ܕܠܐ ܡ̇ܫܡ̣ܠܝ܆ ܐܦܢ ܡܕܡ ܡܕܡ ܐܝܬ ܒܗ܇ ܕܢܨ̇ܐ ܥܡ ܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ. ܘܬܘܒ̣ ܘܐܦ ܡܢ ܕܝܠܗ̇ ܘܚܠܦܝܗ̇ ܫܟܝ̣ܚ ܒܗ. ܡܛܠ ܕܝܢ ܕܐܬ̇ܦܩܕܘ ܘܥܒ̣ܕܘܢ ܪ̈ܝܫܐ ܗ̇ܢܘ ܕܝܢ ܬ̇ܘܪ̈ܨܐ “when the bishops received this order, they took the draft, read it, and saw that it was incomplete. Even if there was something in it opposed to the synod of Chalcedon, there was also something found in the draft from the council and in favor of it. Since they had been ordered, they made a set of chapters, that is, amendments”: For the Severan bishops, the edict appeared incomplete; it contained formulations that were directed against the council of Chalcedon, but also some that spoke in favor of Chalcedonian views. Subsequently, they made amendments (ܬ̇ܘܪ̈ܨܐ), formulating them as specific chapters (ܪ̈ܝܫܐ), likely representing theses that reflected their anti-Chalcedonian stance.

As the edict, apparently containing amendments by the Severan bishops, is available to us through Evagrius and Michael the Syrian, some of its doctrinal formulations could be attributed to Severan influence. Presumably, the bishops’ aim was to mitigate the acceptance of the dyophysite doctrine in favor of the unity of Jesus’s person, without completely abandoning the discourse on the two natures: “When we consider his ineffable union, we rightly confess one nature of the God-Logos, which became flesh in flesh animated by a rational and spiritual soul. And when again in theory we grasp the distinction of the natures, we say that they are two, without introducing a separation. For each of the two natures is in him. Therefore, we confess one and the same Christ, one Son, one person, one hypostasis, simultaneously God and human” (Ἐννοοῦντες δὲ αὐτοῦ τὴν ἄφραστον ἕνωσιν, ὀρθῶς ὁμολογοῦμεν μίαν φύσιν τοῦ θεοῦ Λόγου σεσαρκωμένην σαρκὶ ἐψυχωμένῃ ψυχῇ λογικῇ τε καὶ νοερᾷ. Καὶ πάλιν ἐν θεωρίᾳ λαμβάνοντες τὴν τῶν φύσεων διαφορὰν δύο ταύτας εἶναι λέγομεν, διαίρεσιν οὐδεμίαν εἰσάγοντες. Ἑκατέρα γὰρ φύσις ἐστὶν ἐν αὐτῷ. Ὅθεν ἕνα καὶ τὸν αὐτὸν ὁμολογοῦμεν Χριστόν, ἕνα υἱόν, ἓν πρόσωπον, μίαν ὑπόστασιν, θεόν τε ὁμοῦ καὶ ἄνθρωπον)” (Evagrius, EH 5.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 200.18–25)).

This includes the famous “one nature” (μία φύσις) formula, primarily championed by Cyril of Alexandria but originally stemming from Apollinaris of Laodicea. However, in the present formulation, it is directed against Apollinarianism by speaking of “a rational and spiritual” human soul instead. Apollinaris instead posited a “spiritual soul” in which the Logos was present. Nevertheless, such formulations emphasizing the one incarnate nature of the God-Logos may have found their way into the edict, despite the theoretical acceptance (ἐν θεωρίᾳ) of the two natures, particularly emphasizing the unity in the person of Jesus Christ. These formulations could have been made at the behest of the Severan bishops, yet they are also completely in line with neo-Chalcedonian doctrine such as Justinian’s edict on faith from the year 551 (Allen 1981, 24–25 and 212–14).

In Justin’s edict from 571, there is indeed a lengthy excerpt directly taken from the beginning of Justinian’s edict on faith. The new edict expanded its text at two points with interpolations and concluded with an additional closing statement Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 199.5–36; 200.15–25; 200.26–201.11. Interestingly, these expansions notably accommodate the anti-Chalcedonian position, leading to a possible attribution of these additions to the influence of the imprisoned bishops (Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 508–511). In these expansions, the notion of “quaternity” is explicitly refuted (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 199.5–9): “For the Holy Trinity has not received the addition of a fourth person apart from the one from the Holy Trinity, the incarnate God-Logos, but one and the same is our Lord, Jesus Christ” (Οὐδὲ γὰρ τετάρτου προσώπου προσθήκην ἐπεδέξατο ἡ ἁγία τριάς, καὶ σαρκωθέντος τοῦ ἑνὸς τῆς ἁγίας τριάδος θεοῦ Λόγου· ἀλλ’ ἔστιν εἷς καὶ ὁ αὐτὸς ὁ κύριος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦς Χριστός). Yet this passage is also found in the Christological confession, which appears at a later point in Justinian’s edict on faith (Schwartz 1939, 76.36–78.1), albeit without the emphasis “but one and the same is our Lord, Jesus Christ.” This shows the neo-Chalcedonian nature of the Christological confession, which strove to highlight that core miaphysite tenets were in agreement with the faith of Chalcedon.

From as early as 540, Justinian sought to reinterpret the definition of Chalcedon in order to make it acceptable to the anti-Chalcedonians. Through his neo-Chalcedonian approach, he emphasized alignment with Cyril of Alexandria, while condemning texts written by key representatives of Antiochene theology, namely Theodore of Mopsuestia, Theodoret of Cyrus, and Ibas of Edessa. However, this significant concession did not lead to the desired reconciliation (Price 2009, 59–75).

The approach presented here in EH 3.1.19 by Justin resembles the first edict of the emperor from the year 567 presented to the anti-Chalcedonian bishops at Callinicum (see 3.1.3.r); there too, they were able to respond to the original proposal of the edict and suggest changes and additions. They demanded that, besides renouncing the mention of two sons, two persons, two hypostases, also the formulation of “two natures” after the union should be explicitly renounced, or that the Henotikon of Zeno should be declared orthodox instead. They emphasized the formula of “one nature” (μία φύσις), without completely abandoning the mention of two natures before the union: “Out of two natures or hypostases, one single nature or hypostasis of God, the incarnate Word, has emerged” (ܡܢ ܬܪܝܢ ܟܝ̈ܢܝܢ ܐܘܟܝܬ ܩܢܘܡ̈ܐ ܚܕ ܟܝܢܐ ܐܬܬܣܝܡ ܐܘܟܝܬ ܚܕ ܩܢܘܡܐ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܡܠܬܐ ܕܡܒܣܪ) (Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:286.b.18–20; 4:333.b.36–38).
