The request of the anti-Chalcedonians referred to the wording of the edict, which dealt with the Christological formulations: “[We confess] one and the same [being] from two natures [ܡܢ ܬܪܝܢ ܟܝ̈ܢܐ], divine and human [ܐܠܗܝܐ ܘܐܢܫܝܐ]; one hypostasis and one person [ܚܕ ܩܢܘܡܐ ܘܚܕ ܦܪܨܘܦܐ], not two hypostases [ܘܠܐ ܬܪܝܢ ܩܢܘܡ̈ܝܢ], nor two persons [ܘܠܐ ܬܪܝܢ ܦܪ̈ܨܘܦܝܢ], nor two sons [ܘܠܐ ܬܪܝܢ ܒܢܝ̈ܐ]; but one hypostasis of God the Word [ܐܠܐ ܚܕ ܩܢܘܡܐ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܡܠܬܐ]” (Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:289.a.32–38; 4:335.a.41–b.1). This attempt at unification failed due to the resistance of anti-Chalcedonian monastics who exerted pressure on the bishops to withdraw their consent to the edict (see 3.1.3.r). The monastic leaders state in a letter included in the Documenta Monophysitica (Letter from the Eastern Archimandrites to Jacob Burdʿoyo) that they were particularly disturbed by the fact that the bishops appear to have been willing to agree to an edict which did not pronounce the anathema on Chalcedon, on the Tome of Leo, and on all dyophysites Chabot 1907, 175.19–176.15 see Van Roey and Allen 1994, 285–286.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.h (NK; SR) 24.13–15
div.p: ܫܩ̣ܠ ܐܢܘܢ ܕܝܢ ܠܬ̇ܘܪ̈ܨܐ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܐ̇ܝܬ̣ܝܗ܆ ܘܐ̇ܘ̣ܒܠ ܚ̇ܘܝ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܒܪ ܪܐܙܗ ܘܒܪ ܡܠܟ̣ܗ ܝܘܚܢ̣ܢ ܘܠܫܪܟܐ ܕܟܠܗ ܣ̣ܢܕܪܝܘܢ ܕܝܠܗܘܢ “the one who had brought the draft took the amendments, presenting and showing them to his confidant and advisor John and all the rest of those in their council”: John of Ephesus portrays the attempts at reconciliation between the two parties in such a way that the Severans would almost have prevailed with their position, had the Chalcedonians accepted the amendments that were brought back to them by Zacharias. The rhetoric of the “heresy of the dyophysites” is charged with polemics, indicating entrenched positions. John Scholasticus’s confidants and advisors, as well as his whole episcopal council (ܣ̣ܢܕܪܝܘܢ), are subsequently deeply alarmed, fearing the loss of their influence and position.

John of Ephesus is the only Syriac author to use the Greek loanword ܣ̣ܢܕܪܝܘܢ (“council,” derived from συνέδριον). In late antique Greek sources, synhedrion is a standard way to refer to the council of advisors of a patriarch, normally consisting of presbyters and deacons (Lampe 1961, 1314–1315). This council usually gathered in the patriarchal sekreton (σέκρετον; “consistory”), a term also employed in EH 3.1.23.b. The Life of John the Almsgiver 16 (Festugière 1974, 364) underlines this connection between synhedrion and sekreton as it records a meeting of the patriarchal council (συνέδριον) in the consistory (σέκρετον) of the patriarchal palace in Alexandria. There appears to be no reference to the Jewish Sanhedrin in Jerusalem since no Syriac translations of the Gospels actually render Sanhedrin as ܣ̣ܢܕܪܝܘܢ. Instead, the Sinaiticus, Peshitta, and Harklean versions all refer to it as ܟܢܘܫܬܐ or ܟܢܘܫܝܐ, for instance in Matthew 26:59
(Kiraz 2004, 1:417); Mark 14:55; Mark 15:1
(Kiraz 2004, 2:224 and 233); John 11:47
(Kiraz 2004, 4:222).

div.head: EH 3.1.19.i (NK) 24.20–21
div.p: ܦ̣ܠܓܘܬ ܢܣܛܘܪ̈ܝܢܘ܆ ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܢܣܛܘܪ̈ܝܢܘ ܚ̇ܬܝ̈ܬܐ “the semi-Nestorians, the proper Nestorians”: John of Ephesus refers to the proponents of the “two-nature doctrine” as “semi-Nestorians” and genuine “Nestorians”. He directly traces their origin back to Nestorius, who was condemned as a heretic at the council of Ephesus in 431. From then on, he was regarded as the “arch-heretic” of the dyophysite followers. This reflects John’s polemical language. In EH 3.1.17.i, he speaks of the “evil Nestorius” anathematized by Cyril of Alexandria.

The term “semi-Nestorians” pejoratively portrays the Chalcedonians as secret followers of Nestorius. Philoxenus of Mabbug (ca. 450–523) already referred to the Dyophysites derogatorily as “semi-Nestorians” (ܦ̣ܠܓܘܬ ܢܣܛܘܪ̈ܝܢܘ): Commentary of the Prologue of John (de Halleux 1977b, 19.19–20.1) or “Pseudo-Nestorians”: Letter to the Monks of Senun de Halleux 1963, 11.16–17; 22.8; 44.28–29. In EH 3.2.39, John of Ephesus puts a speech into the mouth of a nun, which makes clear what he understood by the term “semi-Nestorians”: They adhere to the council of Chalcedon, but – from John’s point of view – introduce a quaternity into the Holy Trinity. In this way, they prove to be covert Nestorians who, although “condemning Nestorius in appearance” (ܒܐܣܟܡܐ ܡ̇ܚܪܡܐ ܠܢܣܛܘܪܝܣ) along with the council of Chalcedon, confess the “two natures” like this heresiarch.

Philoxenus also accuses the Chalcedonians of introducing the quaternity (ܐܪܒܥܝܘܬܐ) into the Trinity: “And if one dares to count the Son in two natures, one will be forced to separate him also into hypostases, whereby one must define the opinion that the divine essence is four hypostases instead of three, impiously proclaiming a quaternity instead of the Trinity and implying that there were four and not three” (Letter to the Monks of Senun (de Halleux 1963, 44.11–16)).

div.head: EH 3.1.19.j (SR; NK) 24.26–30
div.p: ܟܕ ܫܓ̣ܫܘ ܘܐܦ ܠܗܠܝܢ ܕܦܠܛܝܢ܇ ܕܐܦ ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ ܡܢܗܘܢ܇ ܠܐ ܬܪ̈ܝ̣ܨܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܒܟܪܣܛܝܢܘܬܐ. ܘܝܬ̇ܝܪܐܝܬ̣ ܩܣ̣ܛܘܪ. ܕܥܡ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܚܢܦܐ ܐܦ ܫܡ̇ܪܝܐ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ܆ ܕܫܡܗ ܗܘܐ ܐ̣ܢܣܛܣ ܦܠܣܛܝܢܝܐ “since they also troubled those belonging to the palace many of whom were not orthodox in regard to Christianity and especially the quaestor who in addition to being a pagan was a Samaritan, whose name was Anastasius of Palestine”: People who only seem to be Christians, while secretly upholding pagan beliefs, appear on some occasions in John’s oeuvre. An example of this is found in EH 3.3.27, where John asserts that almost the whole leading class of the Syrian city of Baalbek is still clinging to paganism. Such charges of paganism were widely employed in the 6th century as they were a useful rhetorical means of denigrating opponents (Roggo 2022, 139–143 and 148–151). In a passage from the second part of the EH, preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle and set in the mid-540s, John asserts that he himself exposed the hidden paganism of various high-ranking personalities, including even a former praetorian prefect Witakowski 1996, 71 Chabot 1933a, 76–77. Accusations such as these may have been driven by conflicts over influence at court and by personal enmity (Alan Cameron 2016, 262–265). Similarly, the author of the Life of Symeon the Younger repeatedly equated all those critical of Symeon’s sainthood with pagans, irrespective of the Christian faith of the overwhelming majority of these critics Parker 2022, 164–167 Roggo 2022, 149–150.

John’s defamation of the quaestor sacri palatii Anastasius (PLRE 3:64–66 (Martindale 1992)) as pagan and Samaritan is probably due to the fact that this office-holder turned out to be one of the most outspoken opponents of the edict’s conciliatory Christological approach van Ginkel 1995, 119 Allen 1981, 231. Circles sympathizing with the Akoimetoi (see 3.1.17.e) who demanded an explicit declaration of the council of Chalcedon as orthodox may still have been influential at that time and exerted pressure on emperor and patriarch alike (Roggo 2022, 108–111); see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.16.r. As quaestor sacri palatii (in office from about 565/566 to 572/573), one of Anastasius’s main tasks was the drafting of legal texts Kelly 2004, 209 Jones 1964, 387 and 504–505, and he may therefore have been involved in the composition of Justin’s edict on faith. He was undoubtedly a Christian (EH 3.2.29 describes his participation in a liturgy in Hagia Sophia), yet his Palestinian origin may have facilitated his polemical framing as Samaritan.

The Samaritans revolted on three different occasions during the 6th century, in 529, 556, and again in the early 570s. The suppression of the last revolt, taking place under Justin II, is briefly mentioned in EH 3.1.32.d and 3.3.27. As a result of these rebellions, the legal situation of the Samaritans deteriorated as they were deprived of essential civic rights and no longer allowed to construct new places of worship Roggo 2022, 143–144 Millar 2013, 72–78 Noethlichs 2007; Meier 2003, 209–215 Pummer 2002, 259–319. Pressure to convert to Christianity thus grew considerably, yet Procopius asserts in Secret History 11.24–29 and 27.6–9 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 3:74–75 and 175) that many Samaritans converted only outwardly after the revolt of 529. The testimony of Procopius proves that former Samaritans were suspected of faking their adherence to Christianity. Accordingly, it is imaginable that people hailing from Palestine, even those without any Samaritan connection, were charged with being pseudo-Christians quite easily, and the quaestor Anastasius may serve as an example of this practice.

In the manuscript, a line has been erased at this point and replaced with ܒܟܪܣܛܝܢܘܬܐ ܘܝܬ̇ܝܪܐܝܬ̣ (“in regard to Christianity and especially”). While the text of this line is written smaller than the surrounding text, the letters take the same form and it may come from the main hand of the manuscript or a contemporaneous hand.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.k (SR) 25.3–4
div.p: ܢܬ̇ܟܬܒܘܢ ܒܩܬ̇ܪܐ “written on clean copy”: The expression ܒܩܬ̇ܪܐ corresponds to the Greek ἐν καθαρῷ, literally meaning “in a neat [state]” and refers to a checked and corrected version of a document, free from mistakes. This sense of καθαρός is, in Greek, amply attested in the papyrological record; see, for instance, POxy.1277.13 (Grenfell and Hunt 1914, 218) and P.Ryl.2.163.17 (J. de M. Johnson, Martin, and Hunt 1915, 2:191).

div.head: EH 3.1.19.l (SR) 25.11
div.p: ܕܗܠܝܢ ܩ̈ܦܠܐܐ̣ ܬ̣ܪ̈ܝܨܐ ܐܢܘܢ “these chapters are correct”: It is noticeable that John uses here the Greek loanword ܩ̈ܦܠܐܐ̣ (κεφάλαια) for “chapters,” when quoting an utterance of Justin II. The emperor evidently spoke Greek and consequently, it appears fitting that John chose a Greek word here. In contrast to this, earlier in the chapter, in 3.1.19.g, he introduced the genuinely Syriac word ܪ̈ܝܫܐ to refer to the same “chapters” written by him and his episcopal peers. Similarly, John has Justin use the loanword ܐܝ̈ܣܐ (ἴσα) “copies” further down, whereas at the start of the chapter, in 3.1.19.e, he talks of ܨܚܚ̣ܐ to refer to a “copy.” This lexicographical variation might well be intentional; see also 3.1.16.j above, where John started off the two speeches of the Severan bishop Stephen to the patriarch and the emperor with the Greek exclamation ܐ̣ܘܒܝܐ “O bia!”

div.head: EH 3.1.19.m (NK) 25.11–14
div.p: ܐ̣ܢܬܘܢ ܕܝܢ ܟܠܟܘܢ ܝ̇ܕܥ ܐܢܐ ܗܘ ܕܢܣܛܘܪ̈ܝܢܘ ܐܢܬܘܢ܆ ܘܟ̣ܪ̈ܝܗܝ̣ ܒ̣ܬ̣ܐܪܬܟܘܢ̣ ܘܠܐ ܚ̇ܕܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܒܗܝܡܢܘܬܐ ܬܪܝܨܬܐ “But I know that all of you are Nestorians and sick in your mind, and you do not rejoice in the correct faith”: John of Ephesus attributes the entire speech to Justin II. It is full of polemics against the Chalcedonian party, which makes it surprising that the emperor later allows himself to be persuaded by them. This polemical portrayal of his opponents evidently reflects John’s perspective and presents the emperor in a very positive light (see 3.1.5.e).

John portrays the emperor here as very understanding and striving for unity between both parties (Bunce 2023, 83–84). Thus, the bishops can add without hesitation what is lacking in the “correct” confession. In this way, John highlights that even the emperor acknowledges John and his colleagues as the guardians of orthodoxy, who are given the opportunity to make amendments to the statements in need of correction within the edict.

However, the emperor appears to be easily influenced theologically (see also EH 3.1.11.e and 3.1.11.g). Consequently, he is depicted as very fickle, ultimately being stylized as incompetent in theological matters. In the negative portrayal of the Chalcedonians as Nestorians, who are furthermore “sick in their mind” (ܟ̣ܪ̈ܝܗܝ̣ ܒ̣ܬ̣ܐܪܬܟܘܢ̣), the hardened divide between the two parties is revealed.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.n (SR) 25.14–17
div.p: ܘܐܢ ܠܐ ܬܫ̣ܠܘܢ܆ ܫ̇ܪܐ ܐܢܐ ܘܡ̇ܦܩ ܐܢܐ ܠܗܘܢ ܠܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܦܐ܆ ܘܡܫ̇ܡܪ ܐܢܐ ܠܗܘܢ ܥܠܝܟܘܢ. ܕܐܝܟ ܕܐ̣ܒ̈ܐ ܢܦ̇ܠܝܢ ܥܠܝܟܘܢ܆ ܘܡܢܬܫܝܢ ܠܟܘܢ̣ ܘܠܐ ܩܝ̣ܡܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܩܕܡܝܗܘܢ “unless you keep silent, I am going to release and send the bishops out, dispatching them against you like wolves falling upon you and tearing you in bits and you will not be able to stand before them”: John of Ephesus pursues his strategy of exculpating Justin II by claiming that the emperor was completely convinced by all the amendments to the edict. In this way, John manages again to put all the blame for the hardship he and his peers had to suffer on the circle of alleged Nestorians and semi-Nestorians around patriarch John Scholasticus. Already in EH 3.1.5.e and 3.1.11.e, he asserted that Justin was innocent and that the patriarch persuaded him to embark on a suppression of the Severans in Constantinople. Evidently, this is a rhetorical construction by means of which John of Ephesus aimed to depict his religious group as enjoying particular imperial favor. But contrary to what John claims, Justin, whilst hoping that the neo-Chalcedonian exposition of faith in the two edicts of 567 and 571 might close the divide between supporters and opponents to Chalcedon, never turned against the council Roggo 2022, 91–95 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 508–511 Averil Cameron 1976a, 62–65. His behavior as described by John here is thus completely illusory, and we may be certain that he did not switch his allegiance away from the patriarch to the imprisoned Severan bishops. The power John ascribes to himself and his peers is just wishful thinking; Constantinople was, in its majority, always pro-Chalcedonian, and the anti-Chalcedonian minority that existed during decades in the city (see 3.1.5.a and 3.1.10.b) never developed into a serious threat for the established church.
For another example in the EH of a metaphorical use of wolves, see 3.1.10.e.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.o (NK) 25.25–26.2
div.p: ܟܕ ܡܕܡ ܡܕܡ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܫܒܩ̣ܘܗܝ ܠܡ̣ܣܡ ܡܢ ܬ̇ܘܪ̈ܨܐ ܗܠܝܢ. ܟܕ ܗܪܛܝܩܐܝܬ ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܡܢ ܕܝܠܗܘܢ ܣܡ̣ܘ ܒܫܘܠܡܗ ܕܐ̣ܝܕܝܩܛܘܢ܆ ܕܥ̇ܝ̈ܕܝܗ̇ ܠܡ ܕܥܕܬܐ ܢܬ̇ܢܛܪܘܢ ܠܗ̇. ܐܝܟ ܕܡܛܘܠ ܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ܆ ܕܐܝܟ ܥܝ̇ܕܗܘܢ ܢܗܘܘܢ ܡ̇ܟܪܙܝܢ ܠܗ̇ “only allowing a certain few amendments to be inserted, they heretically inserted on their own at the end of the edict, ‘Let the customs of the church be retained for the church,’ so that, in regard to the synod, they might proclaim it as their custom”: As the edict is handed down in both Evagrius and Michael the Syrian, the similarities can be easily understood. However, the last sentence in Michael is missing, which is attested in Evagrius, EH 5.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 201.8–11): “The custom and practice that has prevailed until now in the holy universal and apostolic Church of God is steadfast and unchanged through everything, and persists for the whole of time hereafter” (τοῦ μέχρι νῦν κρατοῦντος ἔθους τε καὶ σχήματος ἐν τῇ ἁγίᾳ τοῦ θεοῦ καθολικῇ καὶ ἀποστολικῇ ἐκκλησίᾳ διὰ πάντων βεβαίου τε καὶ ἀκαινοτομήτου, καὶ εἰς τὸν ἑξῆς ἅπαντα μένοντος χρόνον). John of Ephesus seems to refer to this addition. According to him, this was inserted by the Chalcedonians so that they could keep pronouncing the orthodoxy of Chalcedon. However, this was not acceptable to the Severan bishops, as they rejected the definition of Chalcedon and could not tolerate it as a basis for a union (Michael Whitby 2000, 261 n.16).

The counter-encyclica of Basiliscus from 476 is another example of a doctrinal document with its final sentence missing in a part of the transmission: Evagrius, EH 3.7 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 107) quotes it without its final sentence which, however, has been preserved in the manuscript Città del Vaticano, Bibliotheca Apostolica Vaticana, Vat. gr. 1431 (Schwartz 1927, 52.17–20). Possibly, the pro-Chalcedonian tradition deleted it in order to strengthen the impression that the counter-encyclica was a Chalcedonian triumph Greatrex 2011, 177 n.22 Michael Whitby 2000, 141 n.28 Allen 1981, 126–127.

div.head: EH 3.1.19.p (PMF) 26.4
div.p: ܘܬ̇ܗܦ̣ܘܟ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܓܝܓܠ̣ܐ “the wheel turn against them”: This phrase is likely an allusion to Proverbs 20:26 which reads, in the Peshitta version: “A wise king scatters the iniquitous, and he turns the wheel against them” ܡܒܕܪ ܠܥܘ̈ܠܐ ܡܠܟܐ ܚܟܝܡܐ܂ ܘܡܗܦܟ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܓܝܓܠܐ (Di Lella 1979, 35). The mention of a ruler in the Proverbs passage strengthens the connection to the EH where the emperor plays a central role in mediating between the pro-Chalcedonian and Severan parties. To use the language of Proverbs, the emperor as a wise ruler is in the position of deciding who the iniquitous are whom he should scatter and against whom he should turn the wheel. If the allusion is intended, John of Ephesus may wish to imply that the pro-Chalcedonians hope that the emperor will not behave as a wise ruler.

The beginning of the same passage is quoted in Severus of Antioch’s letter to Justinian, known as the Defense to the Emperor, which he wrote to defend his anti-Chalcedonian understanding of Christology Brooks 1921, 126.23 Greatrex 2011, 357. Severus calls Justinian to act in accordance with expected wisdom of rulers in separating those who are opposed to him with an eye to “the churches in union” (ܥܕ̈ܬܐ ܒܚܘܝܕܐ), another shared concern with the EH at this point Brooks 1921, 127.1–2 Greatrex 2011, 357. This letter was in circulation among Severans at the time John wrote the EH, as it is only preserved in the EH of John’s fellow Syriac historiographer Pseudo-Zachariah. In this way, both historiographical works use Proverbs 20:26 to discuss expectations regarding the conduct of the emperor in mediating ecclesiastical affairs.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter continues the account of the negotiations about Justin II’s edict on faith. The imprisoned Severan bishops are presented with the edict’s latest version, yet they discover that many amendments they inserted in EH 3.1.19 are no longer included. This is presented as the result of the machinations of a circle of intransigent Chalcedonians around the quaestor Anastasius, who persuaded Justin to delete many amendments. John of Ephesus claims that as a result of these machinations, the edict became weirdly ambiguous, containing both elements in favor of and opposed to Chalcedon. The bishops then inform the emperor that they cannot possibly agree to a union on the basis of this text and suggest the easiest solution from their point of view: the condemnation of Chalcedon – an action which would completely remove any need for an edict and instantly lead to ecclesiastical unity.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:300.b.11–22; 4:341.a.28–35: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.20.a

div.head: Parallel Accounts (SR)
div.p: Evagrius Scholasticus, EH 5.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 197.28–201.11): Justin II’s edict of 571 Nicephorus Callistus, EH 17.35 (PG 147:308C–313A (Migne 1857–1866)): derivative of Evagrius Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.4 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:295–299; 4:338–341: Justin II’s edict of 571

div.head: EH 3.1.20.a (SR) 26.5
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܥܣܪ̈ܝܢ “Chapter 20”: Michael the Syrian summarized this chapter briefly in Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:300.b.11–22; 4:341.a.28–35. He reproduced John’s statement that the latest version of the edict partly seemed to be in favor of Chalcedon and partly opposed to it, and that it would suffice to condemn Chalcedon to establish the union, without any need for an edict.

div.head: EH 3.1.20.b (SR) 26.9–10
div.p: ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܓܝܪ ܕܐܝܟ ܕܦܩ̣ܕ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܐܬܟ̣ܬܒ ܐ̣ܝܕܝܩܛܘܢ ܒܩ̇ܬܪܐ܆ ܡ̣ܚܕܐ ܐܪܡ̣ܝ ܒܗ ܐ̣ܝܕܐ “after, as the emperor had ordered, the edict was written on clean copy, he immediately subscribed it”: John of Ephesus’s statement that Justin himself signed the edict after he had received it in its final form evidently suggests that the emperor agreed with its contents, including its final sentence about the preservation of the customs of the church, irrespective of what John claimed in EH 3.1.19.l–3.1.19.o. The emperor hoped that the edict’s neo-Chalcedonian exposition of faith, together with the absence of any direct reference to the contentious council, might be enough to establish the union Dölger and Müller 2009, 9.19 Destephen 2008, 512 Maraval 1998, 466–467 Van Roey 1982, 491 Allen 1980, 5–7 Averil Cameron 1976a, 62–65; Frend 1972, 321–322.
On the expression ܒܩܬ̇ܪܐ, taken over as a loanword from the Greek ἐν καθαρῷ, see 3.1.19.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.20.c (SR) 26.12–13
div.p: ܕܗܐ ܠܡ ܐܝܟ ܕܒ̇ܥܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܥ̣ܒܕܢܢ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ “now we have created the conditions for a union, exactly as you want”: Again, Justin appears to have thought that many amendments suggested by the Severans actually had been incorporated into the edict and that its latest version was therefore a viable solution to the dispute for both sides (Bunce 2023, 83–84). The theological analysis of the edict (see 3.1.19.g) indeed suggests that it was accommodating towards the Severans, laid even more emphasis on the unity of Jesus Christ and the one-nature formula than Justinian had done in his edict of 551, and avoided any explicitly dyophysite language (Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 508–511). John of Ephesus’s claim (3.1.20.e) that the hardcore Chalcedonians had substantially changed the amendments and inserted their own views is thus essentially unfounded and polemical. The main reason for this distorting description of the edict in the EH was that John had briefly entered into Chalcedonian communion and then recanted again (EH 3.1.24). Consequently, he needed to exculpate himself to his co-religionists for his short-term lapse, and he did so by asserting that he had been deceived – hence his continuous depiction of the Chalcedonians as cunning and untrustworthy (on this motif, see 3.1.17.a and 3.1.20.f). This background also provides a possible explanation for why John, unlike Evagrius and Michael, did not incorporate the edict in the EH: his theologically inclined readers would have noticed that the opponents had not “marred and muddled” (3.1.20; ܒܠܒ̇ܠܘܚܗܝ ܘܕܘܕܘ̇ܗܝ) the edict as John claimed, but that it formed a rather carefully balanced and coherent whole.

div.head: EH 3.1.20.d (SR) 26.15–18
div.p: ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܕܝܢ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ: ܟܕ ܩ̣ܪܐܘܗܝ ܠܐ̣ܝܕܝܩܛܘܢ: ܘܚܙܘ ܕܬ̇ܘܪ̈ܨܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܥܒ̣ܕܘ ܠܝܬ ܒܗ: ܐܠܐ ܢܘ̈ܬܫܐ ܡܕܡ ܡܢܗܘܢ “the bishops read the edict and saw that the amendments they had made were not in it but only bits of them had been inserted”: As discussed above in 3.1.19.g, the edict of 571 is very similar to Justinian’s edict on faith issued in 551, with the main exception that two passages were added which further emphasize that God the Word (that is Jesus Christ) is one incarnate nature. The edict as a whole, including the additional passages, is completely in line with neo-Chalcedonian theology and would also have been acceptable to Justinian (Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 508–511). It is imaginable that these additions are the “bits” (ܢܘ̈ܬܫܐ) of the amendments that had been inserted, yet they might just as well stem from the moderate neo-Chalcedonian camp around the emperor that had first drafted the edict. Possibly, the Severans had made further suggestions that were not included in the edict, for instance that the anathema against Severus and other prominent anti-Chalcedonians be lifted, as they had already demanded in Callinicum in 567 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:286–287; 4:333–334.

div.head: EH 3.1.20.e (SR) 26.23–24
div.p: ܘܡܬܚ̣ܙܐ ܕܢܨ̇ܐ ܥܡ ܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ܆ ܘܬܘܒ ܕܚܠܦܝܗ̇ ܡܬ̇ܟܬܫ “with the edict appearing to oppose the synod and also to fight on its behalf”: Apart from the edict’s final sentence (on which see 3.1.19.o) and the fact that it did not include an explicit condemnation of Chalcedon, there is no element in the edict which could be interpreted as supporting a strictly dyophysite Christology. John’s claim that the edict was ambiguous and unacceptable on these grounds is very likely a polemical exaggeration (see 3.1.20.c).

div.head: EH 3.1.20.f (SR) 26.26–29
div.p: ܕܐܢ ܐܪܐ ܥܠ̣ܬ݀ ܟܫ̣ܠܗ̇ ܘܕܠ̣ܘܚܝܗ̇ ܕܟܠܗ̇ ܥ̇ܕܬܐ: ܗ̇ܢܘ ܕܝܢ ܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ ܡܢ ܡ̇ܨܥܬܐ ܡܫܬ̇ܩܠܐ܆ ܐܦ ܠܐ ܣܢ̣ܝܩܐ ܥܕܬܐ ܥܠ ܗܢܐ “if then the cause of offense and disturbance of the whole church, that is, the synod of Chalcedon, is removed from the midst, the church will also not need this”: John’s insistence that he and his episcopal peers demanded an explicit condemnation of Chalcedon is probably part of his apology to his co-religionists, many of whom lived in the eastern provinces of the empire (John mentions in EH 3.2.6.d that he sent them apologetic letters because of his short-term communion with the Chalcedonians). When Justin’s first edict on faith had been presented to the Severan bishops at Callinicum in 567, monastic groups had started rioting because the bishops were principally ready to agree to the edict (which did not contain such a condemnation), and forced them to withdraw (Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.1–2 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:283–290; 4:331–336). Bearing in mind that after the failed union of 571, John’s peer Paul of Antioch was forced to undergo a prolonged period of penance before (parts of) his community accepted him again in Syria (EH 3.4.15; see 3.1.17.d), it is likely that John had to be very careful about how he presented his own actions in 571 when he was writing Book 1. He might have included the statement about the removal of Chalcedon for this reason (see 3.1.17.j). Yet it is improbable that the Severan bishops in Constantinople would have insisted on this removal to the extent described in EH 3.1.19–3.1.24, and it remains possible that they were willing to enter into communion on the basis of the edict with its careful neo-Chalcedonian definition of faith (Roggo 2022, 105–107). They knew perfectly well that the Chalcedonian side could not possibly agree to the condemnation of the council – to confess allegiance to it had become an identity marker beyond all actual doctrinal content Menze 2008b, 100–104 Greatrex 2007, 291–292 van Ginkel 1995, 179–180 Allen 1981, 104–105, and to turn against Chalcedon would have instantly caused Rome and many other western, pro-Chalcedonian regions of the empire to break off communion with Constantinople. After all, there was still fierce opposition in some of these regions to elements of neo-Chalcedonianism such as the condemnation of the Three Chapters Price 2009, 30–33 Chazelle and Cubitt 2007; Sotinel 2005, 284–286; 1998.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: In the focus of this chapter is the claim of patriarch John Scholasticus that the unyielding stance of the Severan bishops and their continuous rejection of the imperial edict on faith is the only obstacle to a successful ecclesiastical union. The bishops counter that the patriarch and his supporters only pretend to be interested in a union and are able to hide behind the seemingly accommodating edict, whereas in reality, they adamantly refuse to remove the true obstacle, that is the acknowledgement of Chalcedon as orthodox. In the subsequent chapters EH 3.1.22–3.1.24, the imprisoned bishops are repeatedly confronted with such accusations of being not flexible enough, both from the patriarch and their own co-religionists. These chapters probably are to be understood as part of John of Ephesus’s apologetic program in Book 1: by repeatedly highlighting the pressure exerted on himself and his peers from all sides, he already prepares his readers for the culmination of the negotiations around the edict in EH 3.1.24, when the Severan bishops give in (supposedly only after having been deceived into believing that a condemnation of Chalcedon would follow suit) and enter into communion with the patriarch for a short time (see 3.1.24.l).

div.head: EH 3.1.21.a (SR; HL) 27.10–12
div.p: ܟܠ ܝܘܡ ܕܝܢ ܡܣ̇ܗܕ ܗܘܐ ܒܗܘܢ ܗ̣ܘ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܒܐ̈ܦܝܣܩܦܐ: ܥܡ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܦܪܨܘܦܐ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܠܘܬܗܘܢ ܡܫ̇ܬܕܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ̣ “John testified every day against the bishops alongside those sent to them in the name of the emperor”: EH 3.1.23.a specifies that the attempts of patriarch John Scholasticus and his supporters to convince the Severan bishops lasted thirty-three days in total and that they were visited daily in that period. The people “sent to them in the name of the emperor” are not identified. Yet this passage supports the view that Justin II himself was engaged in the debate and hoped that the bishops would accept his edict and thereby restore union. Contrary to what Bunce (2023, 83–85) has argued, this imperial engagement was thus very probably more than just an attempt at arbitration between two quarreling parties (see 3.1.27.b), and Justin was strongly committed to the realization of the union. Bunce may have been misled by the fact that John of Ephesus was, throughout Books 1 and 2, very careful to take all the responsibility from the emperor and to portray patriarch John Scholasticus as the main suppressor of the Severans (see 3.1.5.e and 3.1.11.e). However, John of Ephesus could not completely hide that the emperor also was actively involved in the dealings with the Severans (see 3.1.31.e). Apart from this passage, we have, for instance, encountered functionaries of the urban prefect in EH 3.1.10.d and members of the imperial bodyguard in EH 3.1.16.e, none of whom could realistically have been dispatched against the Severans without an imperial command. In addition, John admits in EH 3.1.11.h that Justin and Sophia themselves toured Severan monasteries, although he claims that they only did so because they had been deceived by the patriarch.

The phrase ܡܢ ܦܪܨܘܦܐ occurs twice in EH 3, and notably in the same narrative: in this passage and again in 3.1.27; a related usage is found in 3.1.24.b. In all three instances, representatives of the emperor inform the Severans of his decrees; therefore, the meaning “in the name of” (Sokoloff 2009, s.v., 1249–1250) seems appropriate, just as in LES 25 (Brooks 1924, 533.5). Lund (2024, 292–295) argues against this interpretation, yet his point is methodologically questionable. The distinction between “in the name of” and “from the viewpoint” is not always as clear as he presupposes. The corresponding Greek phrase ἐκ προσώπου (see Lund 2024, 286) is also known from papyri (Preisigke 1925, s.v., 2:422). It is possible that John is adopting the technical language of his time, as he does in other cases.

div.head: EH 3.1.21.b (SR) 27.14
div.p: ܗܐ ܓܝܪ ܠܚܡܫܝܢ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ ܐܬ̣ܬ “the union already came about fifty years ago”: This refers to the return to a clearly pro-Chalcedonian imperial ecclesiastical policy upon Justin I’s (r. 518–527) accession to the throne. Anastasius (r. 491–518) had been rather more inclined towards an anti-Chalcedonian position and under his reign, it had remained possible for outspoken anti-Chalcedonians to become bishops. The most prominent critic of Chalcedon in that period was patriarch Severus of Antioch (sed. 512–518; see 3.1.41.b). In the years after 518, the Severan episcopal hierarchy that existed in Syria and Mesopotamia was completely replaced with a Chalcedonian one, and many bishops were deposed and fled into exile. This experience of persecution is at the core of many of the Lives that form John’s hagiographical collection, the LES (van Ginkel 1995, 39–44); moreover, he also treated it in detail in the lost second part of his EH, partly preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 20–41 Chabot 1933a, 19–44. Yet from a pro-Chalcedonian perspective, “the union” (in the sense that all the incumbent bishops were in communion with each other) was thus restored in the eastern provinces. However, importantly, also after 518, there was no empire-wide union, contrary to the impression given in this passage: the Egyptian episcopate remained staunchly anti-Chalcedonian almost twenty years longer, until 536/537, when Justinian installed Chalcedonian bishops also in Egypt Menze 2008b, 18–57 Gray 2005, 227–229 Frend 1972, 221–254. At the time treated in this chapter, in 571, the party around John Scholasticus accused the imprisoned bishops of threatening that long-established union by remaining stuck in the past and by still not agreeing to it – which they could do, if they were to accept the edict.

div.head: EH 3.1.21.c (SR) 27.17–20
div.p: ܐ̇ܝܟܢܐ ܚ̣̇ܢܢ ܡ̇ܒܛܠܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ܇ ܗܝ̇ ܕܐܢܬܘܢ ܐܦܠܐ ܒܚ̣ܕܐ ܡܢ ܨܒ̈ܥܬܟܘܢ ܩ̇ܪܒܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܠܗ̇܇ ܐܠܐ ܐܢ ܒܡ̈ܠܐ ܒ̇ܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܘܚ̇ܪܥܘܬܐ “how are we the ones obstructing the union to which ‘you draw near not with any of your fingers’ but rather with exterior words and deviousness”: The bishops’ counter argument refers back to the (according to John of Ephesus) insincere propositions of the Chalcedonians for a union in EH 3.1.17 and 3.1.19 (first based on the formula of 433, then on Justin’s edict). According to John’s presentation of the events, in both cases, the Chalcedonians only pretended to reach out to the opponents of the council, whereas in reality, they were not willing to even slightly adapt their stance. First, they insisted that the Severans restore ecclesial unity, just as Cyril had done in 433, although they themselves did not condemn Chalcedon and thereby failed to fulfill the crucial precondition for such unity (see 3.1.17.h). Similarly, so John claims, they then crafted an edict which seemed to be accommodating towards the Severans. But the insertion of the ambiguous final sentence would have allowed them to uphold the orthodoxy of Chalcedon (see 3.1.19.o). John asserts that their propositions and their apparent openness to debate enabled them to carefully hide their inflexibility in doctrinal matters and to put all the pressure on the Severans who appeared thus solely responsible for why the union had still not been realized. Consequently, he describes the Chalcedonians as being full of “misleading duplicity” (3.1.17; ܕܕܓ̇ܠܘܬܐ ܡ̇ܛܥܝܢܝܬܐ), “mischievousness” (3.1.17; ܛ̇ܪܟܢܘܬܐ), and “deviousness” (3.1.21; ܚ̇ܪܥܘܬܐ). Throughout Book 1, John presents his account in such a way as if neither side would have been willing to move – for the Severans, only the condemnation of the council was good enough, and the Chalcedonians wanted to keep proclaiming the orthodoxy of Chalcedon. However, the moderate (neo-)Chalcedonians who wrote the edict were content with the council not being mentioned at all in the text (see 3.1.19.g), and the opposing side might have been less adamant about condemning the council as John wants to make his readers believe (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f).

In this passage, there is a close textual parallel to the Peshitta version of the Gospel of Luke 11:46 (Kiraz 2004, 3:240), where Jesus accuses the scribes and Pharisees of imposing heavy burdens on the people, yet without “drawing near to those burdens with any of your fingers” (ܘܐܢܬܘܢ ܒܚܕܐ ܡܢ ܨܒ̈ܥܬܟܘܢ ܠܐ ܩܪܒܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܠܗܝܢ ܠܡܘܒ̈ܠܐ). The implication is that the Chalcedonians, unwilling to enter into a sincere union without any duplicity, do not care about the suffering of the divided people, just as the scribes did not care about the hardship caused by their imposition of burdens. John also quotes this verse in LES 31 (Brooks 1924, 584); we owe this observation to Carlo Emilio Biuzzi.

div.head: EH 3.1.21.d (SR) 27.27–28.1
div.p: ܐܢ ܐܪܐ ܒܫܪܪܐ ܝܨ̇ܝܦܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܕܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܬܥ̣ܒܕܘܢ ܐܝܟ ܡ̈ܠܝܟܘܢ܆ ܐ̇ܪܝܡ̣ܘ ܬܘܩ̣ܠܬܐ ܘܥܠ̣ܬܐ ܕܟܫܠ̣ܐ ܡܢ ܐܘܪܚܗ̇ ܫܦܝ̣ܬܐ ܕܗܝܡܢܘܬܐ “if then you truly are taking care to create the union as you say, remove the stumbling block and cause of the offense from the clear path of the faith”: The bishops repeat the point John of Ephesus already had them make in EH 3.1.20.f, namely that a successful union would be easily achieved if everyone agreed on condemning Chalcedon. As discussed in that passage, it is unlikely that the Severan bishops would only have entered into a union after the whole church had pronounced the anathema on Chalcedon, and they might actually have been content with the edict which carefully avoided any mention of the contentious council (Roggo 2022, 105–107).

div.head: EH 3.1.21.e (SR; HL) 28.3–9
div.p: ܥܡ ܣܓܝܐ̈ܬܐ ܕܕܐܝܟ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܠܐ ܣ̇ܦܩܝܢܢ ܠܡܣ̇ܝܟܘ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ ܒܥܘܒܛܐ ܣܓܝܐܐ ܕܟܪ̈ܛܝܣܐ܇ ܠܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܒܟܠ ܝܘܡ ܒܝܢܬܗܘܢ ܒܗ̈ܦ̇ܟܬܐ ܕܕܪܫ̇ܐ ܥܠ ܐ̈ܦܝ ܗܠܝܢ ܡ̈ܬ̇ܡܠܠܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ. ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܡܛܠ ܣܘܓܐ̣ܐ ܕܡ̈ܠܐ ܘܥܘ̣ܒܛܐ ܕܟ̈ܬ̣ܝܒܬܐ ܡܢܗܝܢ ܐ̇ܦܝ̣ܫܢ ܘܐ̇ܗ̣ܡܝܢ܆ ܕܕܡ ܠܙܬ̣ܐ ܢܬܚܫ̈ܒܢ ܠܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܦܓ̇ܥܝܢ ܒܗܝܢ “along with many other such things which we would not be able to relate in full even on a thick stack of sheets, we have left out and omitted those things which were said between them concerning these matters every day in the various exchanges of the debate because of the copiousness of the words and the thickness of the texts, lest they be thought an irritation by those who chance upon them”: This is another example of John’s frequent claims that he has shortened the narration so as to avoid too much detail and an exaggerate length of his work (see 3.1.3.u, where more examples are listed).

An earlier church historian, Socrates in EH 5. pr. 2 (Hansen 1995a) also expresses his concern that he could annoy his audience with the plethora of references to contentious bishops. However, in contrast to John, he defends the substantial inclusion of non-ecclesiastical subjects with this reflection. On the other hand, John’s contemporary Evagrius similarly states in EH 2.10 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 61) that he passed over some material so as to avoid too lengthy an account.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter continues John of Ephesus’s complaint about the shrewd and deceiving strategy of the Chalcedonians by repeating and further elaborating on some points already made in the previous chapter EH 3.1.21. There, John concentrates on their efforts to put all the blame for why the union has still not been realized on the imprisoned bishops. Now, he adds that he and his peers were also put under pressure by influential laymen belonging to his own Severan community. John portrays these people as afraid lest they lose their property, and then focuses on the hardship and the inner conflicts to which he and the other imprisoned bishops were subjected as they were thus urged from two sides to agree to the union. He complains that the Chalcedonians were able to present themselves as reasonable and that even many members of the Severan community believed that they were interested in a sincere union. In reality, John asserts, their promises were cunning and untrustworthy, but there was no possibility for John and his peers to counter their harmful influence as they did not allow the Severans to receive visitors in prison.

div.head: EH 3.1.22.a (SR) 28.14–17
div.p: ܩܕܡ ܙܒܢܐ ܗܟܝܠ ܕܪܕܘܦ̣ܝܐ ܗܢܐ: ܘܢܣ̣ܝ̈ܘܢܐ ܘܐܘ̈ܠܨܢܐ ܘܚܒ̣̈ܘܫܝܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܪ̈ܕܘ̇ܦܐ: ܥܡ ܗܠܝܢ ܬܘܒ ܡܢ ܫܪܟܐ ܕܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܡܬ̣ܪܫܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܩܫ̣ܝܐܝܬ “before the time of this persecution and in addition to the trials, torments, and imprisonments from their persecutors, the bishops had been harshly censured by the rest of the orthodox”: This is a crucial passage in that it indicates that Justin’s edict and his hope for the restoration of ecclesial union with the Severans did not come out of the blue in 571. On the contrary, already in the period before, fierce debates within the Severan movement about the establishment of such a union seem to have involved even the laity. Negotiations between the two parties had indeed been ongoing ever since Justin’s accession to the throne in 565 Roggo 2022, 91–100 Averil Cameron 1976a, 62–65. The failure of his first edict on faith in 567 had only been a temporary setback for these talks (see 3.1.3.r and 3.1.5.a); the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian 10.1–2, for the first years of Justin’s reign largely drawing on the lost second part of John’s EH, indicates that they soon continued in Constantinople until the presentation of the new edict in 571 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:283–290; 4:331–336.

John of Ephesus evidently uses here the pressure exerted by laypeople apologetically for explaining why he and his episcopal peers eventually entered into communion (EH 3.1.24.l). But despite this clear literary motive, it appears probable that a part of those (rich) laypeople sympathizing with the Severan bishops and taking communion from them had indeed been open to compromise already previously, all the more so when they perceived a possible danger for their property and position in society (see 3.1.22.b). As argued above in 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f, the bishops themselves were, in all likelihood, also not completely unyielding in their demand for a condemnation of Chalcedon, but open to a formula with a certain ambiguity and thus potentially acceptable for both sides – something that John could not admit anymore when writing Book 1 after the definitive failure of the union.

Some 6th-century authors attest that there were voices critical of the doctrinal polarization and arguing for more tolerance, for instance Procopius, Bella 5.3.6–9 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 2:15–16) and Evagrius, EH 1.1 and 2.5 Allen 1981, 104–105 Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 6 and 52–53. It is conceivable that the anti-Chalcedonian laypeople introduced here shared this viewpoint and were in favor of a union on the basis of Justin’s edict.

div.head: EH 3.1.22.b (SR) 28.22–25
div.p: ܘܠܐ ܪܡ̣ܐ ܠܟܘܢ܇ ܕܠܢ ܕܝܠܢ ܘܠܒ̈ܬܝܢ ܘܠܒ̈ܢܝܢ ܘܠܐܘ̈ܣܝܣ ܕܝܠܢ ܥ̣ܬܝܕܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܕܬ̇ܘܒܕܘܢ. ܘܠܟܘܢ ܡ̇ܢܐ ܪܡ̣ܐ ܠܟܘܢ܇ ܕܚ̣ܢܢ ܡ̇ܘܒܕܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܡ̇ܪ̈ܗܛܝܢ܇ ܘܗ̇ܘܝܢܢ ܚ̇ܕܘܪ̈ܐ “without being concerned about the fact that you are going to lose us, our houses, our children, and our property. What does it concern you that we are going to lose our assets and become beggars?”: The tension between the Severan clergy and laity put additional pressure on the imprisoned bishops. John of Ephesus asserts that he himself and his peers were willing to defend their faith uncompromisingly and without any regard to personal welfare and property, but the laity, according to his depiction, was bound to the material world and wary of losing their property in case of a further escalation of the doctrinal conflict. John’s disdain for earthly wealth is an important feature of his oeuvre; this is most clearly visible in his account of the Justinianic plague of 541/542 preserved in the Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 74–98 Chabot 1933a, 79–109. There he included anecdotes which highlight how people hoping to benefit materially from the death of their neighbors fell victim to divine punishment and succumbed themselves to the plague (Shepardson 2018).

In the third part of the EH, this dimension is less prominent (Shepardson 2018, 97–100), but it is evident that John expected his readers to understand that the potential lapse of wealthy members of the Severan movement was dangerous (see 3.1.22.e below). As in every Christian community, the Severan clergy was dependent on material support from the laity, and John has them issue threats in this passage that this support might stop – either because they might accept Chalcedonian communion if the bishops do not agree to the union, or because their property might be confiscated. However, it is unclear to what extent the measures directed against the Severan clergy in the early 570s also targeted prominent laymen. It remains doubtful that they were really put into long-lasting poverty, as John claims here. He discusses the fate of some high-ranking individuals in EH 3.2.9–3.2.12, including imprisonment and various forms of maltreatment, but some of these people appear again in important positions later (in EH 3.4.35 and 3.6.12), and they seem to have suffered only a temporary setback of their careers (van Ginkel 1995, 120).

div.head: EH 3.1.22.c (SR) 28.28–29.1
div.p: ܘܐܦ ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܣܘܢܗ̈ܕܝܛܐ ܒܗܘܢ ܡܣ̇ܬ̈ܗܕܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ ܒܟܠ ܝܘܡ: ܕܗܐ ܠܡ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܡܒܛܠܝܢ ܕܠܐ ܬܗܘܐ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ “in addition to them the synodites were testifying about them every day – ‘Now, you are obstructing the union from coming about’”: The Chalcedonian claim that the imprisoned bishops were solely responsible for the failure to realize the union and the bishops’ reaction to this are the main topic of the preceding chapter; see 3.1.21.a und 3.1.21.c.

div.head: EH 3.1.22.d (SR) 29.5–7
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܗܫܐ ܗܐ ܚܒ̣ܝܫܝܢܢ ܒܐܘܠܨܢܐ܇ ܘܕܠܐܢܫ ܡܢ ܝ̇ܕܘ̈ܥܝܢ ܕܢܚ̣ܙܐ ܠܐ ܡܫܬ̇ܒܩܝܢܢ “we, imprisoned in torment, are not even allowed to see any of our acquaintances”: Complaints about the separation in prison from friends and supporters and the impossibility of receiving visitors are a recurrent feature in the EH (see 3.1.5-8.j and 3.1.23.h). In the context of the present passage, this means that the imprisoned bishops were unable to counter the Chalcedonians’ disinformation spreading among those sympathetic to the Severans, according to which the bishops were to blame for the fact that the union had not yet come about.

div.head: EH 3.1.22.e (SR) 29.8
div.p: ܠܘܬ ܪ̈ܘܪܒܢܐ ܘܐܝ̣̈ܕܝܥܐ ܕܡܢ ܕܝܠܢ “to the leaders and the noteworthy among us”: John of Ephesus does not give any names here, but throughout the 6th century, there were high-ranking personalities embracing anti-Chalcedonian tenets or at least providing protection for anti-Chalcedonian clergy. It is hard to assess if all of these people were indeed convinced opponents of Chalcedon, or if their reasons to extend their support to anti-Chalcedonians were rather political in nature. The latter was probably the case for empress Theodora (d. 548) (Menze 2008b, 208–228). However, other members of the imperial family were full-blown anti-Chalcedonians, most notably Theodora’s grandson Athanasius, who supported the adherents of John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine (the so-called “tritheists”) and was even seen as a potential candidate to become patriarch of Alexandria after the death of Theodosius in 566 (Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.1 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:283–285; 4:331–333) (PLRE 3:147 (Martindale 1992)). Moreover, empress Sophia herself was rumored to have received Severan communion until just a few years prior to her accession to the throne in 565 (EH 3.2.10). John assures that some important office holders and members of the top echelons of the imperial hierarchy were Severans and even suffered imprisonment in 571, an assertion which is not unlikely (van Ginkel 1995, 120). After all, the Severans were, at least in Constantinople, mostly spared from persecution during several decades prior to 571, and large numbers of their clergy resided in the immediate vicinity of the imperial palace. This may have rendered it easy for them to spread their views (see EH 3.1.5.a and 3.1.10.b). Examples of such office holders include the <koubikoularios Andreas (EH 3.2.9), the honorary consuls Peter and John, and the komes pribaton Eudaimon (all three introduced in EH 3.2.11).

div.head: EH 3.1.22.f (SR) 29.10–12
div.p: ܘܡܟܝܠ ܟܠܗ ܓ̇ܘܕܦܐ ܕܒܘ̇ܛܠܗ̇ ܕܚܕܝܘܬܐ: ܟܕ ܟܠܢܫ ܣ̇ܒܪ ܕܫܪ̈ܝܪܢ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ ܡ̈ܠܝܗܘܢ܆ ܒܬܪܢ̣ ܣ̇ܝܡܝܢ ܠܗ “with everyone thinking their words are true they cast the entire allegation of obstructing the union on us”: The long and partly redundant lamentation of John himself and the other imprisoned bishops repeats his complaint about the Chalcedonians’ deceiving duplicity already made in EH 3.1.21.c. Both in this passage here and again a few lines down (ܐܝܟ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܡ̇ܛܝ̣ܒܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܕܢܥ̣ܒܕܘܢ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ܇ ܘܚ̣ܢܢ ܙܓ̣ܪܢܗ̇ ܠܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܡܢ ܥܕܬܐ “as though they were prepared to create a union but we kept the union from the church”), John argues that the Chalcedonians managed to create the impression that they were open to the union, whereas the Severan bishops supposedly were not. Given John’s assertion that Severan laypeople exerted pressure on the bishops to finally agree to the union, this strategy may indeed have been successful. However, we should leave room open for the possibility that parts of the Severan movement, including the bishops in Constantinople, were anyway not religious fundamentalists and rather inclined to accept the edict, which was quite accommodating towards Severan doctrine (see 3.1.22.a).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter focuses on the nature of the imprisonment and on the physical hardship to which John of Ephesus and his episcopal peers were subjected. John asserts that the talks about the ecclesiastical union, the edict, and the mutual accusations presented in the previous chapters EH 3.1.17–3.1.22 lasted for a period of thirty-three days in total. During this time, he and his peers were repeatedly led out of their prison in the patriarchal palace for debates with John Scholasticus and his council. Moreover, John of Ephesus describes the various types of posts guarding them and complains that not only he and the other bishops, but also laymen belonging to their communion and even their servants were imprisoned, stripped down, and prevented from receiving provisions from supporters.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
EH 3.1.23.a (SR) 29.29–30
div.p: ܟܕ ܓܝܪ ܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܬܠܬܝܢ ܘܬܠܬܐ: ܒܗ ܒܕܪܫ̇ܐ ܠܘܩܒܠ ܚ̈ܕܕܐ ܣ̣ܕܝܪ̈ܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܚ̇ܪܝܦܐܝܬ “for thirty-three days they ardently engaged in a debate with each other”: Destephen (2008, 512–513) has argued that this period of thirty-three days may have lasted from 21 March to 22 April 571. Indeed, John of Ephesus asserts in EH 3.1.5.d that 21 March 571 was the start date of the measures directed against the Severans, and the chronological indications he gives in 3.2.7.g can be read to agree with this start date. But this does not mean that the debate about the union in the patriarchal palace, and therefore the period of thirty-three days (the number is given for a second time in EH 3.1.24.m), began immediately on 21 March. From EH 3.1.17.b, it appears that most bishops were first imprisoned elsewhere for a certain period before being assembled in the patriarchal residence for the debate about the union. Ultimately, the little chronological information we have about the beginning of the persecution is insufficient to decide on specific dates with much certainty, and Destephen’s (2008, 514) claim (“La seule date à peu près assurée est celle de l’union réalisée le 22 avril 571”) is too confident.

Michael the Syrian did not summarize this chapter in his Chronicle. The only piece of information he included is the chronological indication that the debate about the edict lasted for thirty-three days Chabot 1899–1910, 2:301.a.4–5; 4:342.a.1–2.

div.head: EH 3.1.23.b (SR) 30.4
div.p: ܠܣܝ̣ܩܪܝܛܘܢ ܕܦ̇ܛܪܝܪܟܐ “to the patriarchal sekreton”: The sekreton (the three variants σέκρετον / σήκρητον / σέκρητον are all attested) is the consistory or law court of a governor or a patriarch Färber 2019, 92 Festugière 1974, 543 Lampe 1961, 1230. In the present context, the term may refer either to this body of people advising the patriarch (often called synhedrion [συνέδριον], consisting of other clergy, lawyers, scribes), or to the chamber within the patriarchal palace where its members were used to gathering. Examples of the former are found in the Synodal Letter of Sophronius of Jerusalem (Riedinger 1990, 492.13), the 8th session of the council of Constantinople in 681 (Riedinger 1990, 204.15), and in Leontius of Neapolis’s Life of John the Almsgiver 9 (Festugière 1974, 355.20). The Life of John the Almsgiver 1 and 16 (Festugière 1974, 347.21 and 364.5) also contains examples where sekreton refers to the consistory’s meeting room, which is the original meaning of the term (Färber 2019). It is not entirely clear in which of the two senses John of Ephesus employed this term. We may understand both of them simultaneously, since the imprisoned bishops are led in front of the patriarchal court at the place within the palace where this court usually gathered. But there is one indication suggesting that John may have conceived of the sekreton primarily as a physical location: in EH 3.1.19.h, he did not call the body of patriarchal advisors sekreton, but synhedrion (ܣܢܕܪܝܘܢ), and this variation possibly means that here, he wished to emphasize the fact that the bishops were moved spatially within the palace and brought to the chamber of the consistory for the debate.

div.head: EH 3.1.23.c (SR) 30.6
div.p: ܘܡܐ ܕܐ̇ܘ ܡ̣ܙܕܟܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܡܢܗܘܢ “whenever the partisans of the synod had either been defeated by them”: In the previous chapters, John of Ephesus repeatedly claimed that the Chalcedonians had been unable to counter the arguments of the imprisoned bishops and to convince them to enter into communion. For instance, in EH 3.1.17.a and 3.1.17.h, John of Ephesus and his peers saw through the cunning of John Scholasticus and his advisors who demanded that they accept the formula of 433 as the basis for a union. Similarly, in EH 3.1.20.f and 3.1.21.d, the imprisoned bishops kept rejecting the latest version of Justin’s edict on faith. John asserts that they did so because the edict only outwardly appeared to bridge the doctrinal divide, whereas in reality, it still allowed for the proclamation of the dyophysite faith of Chalcedon as orthodox.

div.head: EH 3.1.23.d (SR) 30.8–10
div.p: ܡܬ̇ܚܒܫܝܢ ܠܓܘ ܡܢ ܬܠܬ ܡ̇ܛܪ̈ܬܐ. ܚܕܐ ܕܐܦܝܣ̈ܩܦܝܢܐ̣ ܘܚܕܐ ܕܐܣ̣ܩܘܒܝܛܪܘܣ. ܘܗ̇ܝ ܒ̇ܪܝܬܐ ܕܐܣ̣ܛܝܪ̈ܐ “being imprisoned within the three guard posts: that of the bishop’s attendants, that of the excubitores, and an external one of the doorkeepers”: John of Ephesus suggests here that he and his peers were guarded by three layers of security during their imprisonment in the patriarchal residence. The ܐܦܝܣ̈ܩܦܝܢܐ̣ (“bishop’s attendants”) are solely attested in this chapter of the EH, nor anywhere else in Syriac literature (Brockelmann 1928, 41). Interestingly, the term appears to be calqued from the Greek ἐπισκοπιανός, yet this word is exceedingly rare and not attested prior to the 10th-century Book of Ceremonies (Dagron and Flusin 2020, 3:413.117 and 425.293). Probably, John refers here to a corps of relatively low-ranking envoys and attendants of the patriarch (Darrouzès 1970, 385 n.1). They might not only have ensured that the Severan bishops remained in their cells, but also provided them with their daily provisions.

The EH is the only source to mention that excubitores, that is, members of the imperial bodyguard, were stationed in the patriarchal palace (on them, see 3.1.16.e above). Their presence was very likely due to the imperial involvement in the debate with the Severan bishops. Justin was much invested in the events and eager to close the ecclesiastical divide by convincing the bishops to accept his edict. EH 3.1.21.a and 3.1.24.b attest that representatives of Justin participated in the daily debates, and these imperial dignitaries might have been escorted to and from the palace by excubitores. Alternatively, it is also conceivable that excubitores permanently remained in the patriarchal residence for as long as John and his peers were held there so as to ensure that they did not escape. After all, the patriarchal residence was not usually used as a place of confinement and consequently, the staff there did not necessarily have much experience in guarding prisoners.

Finally, the ostiarii (“doorkeepers”; ܐܣ̣ܛܝܪ̈ܐ) were a low-ranking part of the clergy (Jones 1964, 906). Justinian’s Novel 3.1 from 535 had limited the number of ostiarii employed by the patriarchate of Constantinople to 100 men Miller and Sarris 2018, 78 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 21. Their task was to oversee the entrances both into the patriarchal churches (first and foremost Hagia Sophia, Hagia Eirene, and the Chalkoprateia) and into the patriarchal palace, and to close the doors after the dismissal of the catechumens during the celebration of the liturgy (Mathews 1971, 151–152). This is the only instance in the EH where John used the loanword ܐܣܛܝܪܐ (which had entered into Syriac via the Greek ὀστιάριος Butts 2016b, 160; Lampe 1961, 976). However, he does mention an ostiarius on one occasion in the LES (Brooks 1924, 464.10–11).

div.head: EH 3.1.23.e (SR) 30.10–13
div.p: ܟܕ ܐܢܫܐ ܕܟܠ ܚܕ ܚܕ ܡܢܗܘܢ ܡܢ ܟܠܕܘܟ: ܠܘ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܩ̣ܠܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܘܕܝܪ̈ܝܐ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܒ̈ܢܝ ܚ̣ܐܪ̈ܐ ܕܥܡܗܘܢ܆ ܐܠܐ ܘܐܦ ܛ̇ܠܝ̈ܝܗܘܢ ܟܠܗܘܢ “the retinue of each of the bishops from everywhere – not only clerics, monks, and the remaining free persons with them, but also all their servants”: Book 1 of the EH focuses on the detention of the Severan bishops and does not treat in much detail the fate of other clergy and laypeople with Severan sympathies who were confined because of their refusal to join the Chalcedonian communion. However, there are some examples in Book 2; this highlights that the bishops were indeed not the only ones to suffer. EH 3.2.9 and 3.2.11–3.2.12 explain how some high-ranking individuals, both men and women, were expelled from court and confined in different places (see also 3.1.22.e above). Similarly, EH 3.2.6.o and 3.2.13–3.2.14 provide information on some servants and Severan presbyters, among them assistants and collaborators of John of Ephesus himself who, so John claims, were imprisoned and maltreated just as the bishops. It is possible that John refers here to (some of) these people, who were in his and the other bishops’ retinue.

div.head: EH 3.1.23.f (SR) 30.13–15
div.p: ܒܝܬ ܚܒ̣ܘ̈ܫܬܐ ܚ̈ܫ̣ܘܟܬܐ ܘܡ̇ܪ̈ܝܪܬܐ ܕܒܟܠ ܕܘ̈ܟܝܢ ܕܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ ܚܒ̣ܝܫܝܢ ܗܘܘ “were imprisoned in dark and bitter places of confinement all over the episcopal palace”: For the patriarchal residence of John Scholasticus, located adjacent to the south-western corner of Hagia Sophia, see the detailed overview of the archaeological structures by Dark and Kostenec (2019, 46–59 2014) and 3.1.5-8.e. Dark and Kostenec show that the palace, although only partly known, was of considerable proportions and extended along the atrium of Hagia Sophia and its south aisle for about 70 meters. It included subterraneous vaults; possibly, this is where John of Ephesus and his peers were confined in 571. Nevertheless, John’s claim that not only the bishops themselves, but also other clergy, high-ranking laity, and their servants were detained “all over the episcopal palace” (ܒܟܠ ܕܘ̈ܟܝܢ ܕܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ) may be an exaggeration. Only few of the people John lists in EH 3.2.9–3.2.14 are reported to have been held in the patriarchal palace, with most of them being imprisoned elsewhere, for instance in monasteries (see also 3.1.5-8.b–3.1.5-8.f and 3.1.29.f for the different places of confinement John mentions). Most Severan bishops were only transferred from their previous places of confinement to the patriarchal palace for the debate about the ecclesial union (see EH 3.1.17.b), and we may expect that only those people were brought there who actually participated in the talks.

div.head: EH 3.1.23.g (SR) 30.16–17
div.p: ܡܢ ܐܦ̣ܝܣ̈ܩܦܝܢܐ ܘܡܢ ܕܩ̈ܢܣܐ “by the bishop’s attendants and the dekanoi”: For the ܐܦ̣ܝܣ̈ܩܦܝܢܐ (“the bishop’s attendants”), see 3.1.23.d. The dekanoi (δεκανοί) were guards in the service of the patriarch; the dekanikon (a guardhouse also serving as prison) is mentioned in 3.1.5-8.d and 3.2.13.d. They might be identical with the dekanoi, tax-exempt undertakers working for the patriarchate of Constantinople (Jones 1964, 911), as known from Justinian’s Novels 43 and 59 Miller and Sarris 2018, 387–391 and 451–460 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 269–273 and 316–324. It is imaginable that the patriarchate used them at times for other tasks of a physical nature, such as for guarding prisoners, yet the dekanoi with guarding duties might also have been distinct from the undertakers. The designation was quite common and referred to different groups; for instance, there was also a subaltern corps of palatine officials called dekanoi. The exact responsibilities of this latter body are unknown, but at least in some instances, they acted as doorkeepers Rollinger 2024, 103–104 Jones 1964, 582. This would also sit well with this passage. Quite possibly, some of the attendants and the dekanoi decided to supplement their wages by robbing the detainees, though it is likely that this happened less systematically than John of Ephesus depicts it here.

div.head: EH 3.1.23.h (SR) 30.21–23
div.p: ܘܟܠ ܡܕܡ ܕܡܫ̣ܬܟܚ ܗܘܐ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܡܫܬ̣ܩܠ ܗܘܐ ܘܡܫ̣ܬܕܝܢ ܥܠ ܐܪܥܐ̣ ܟܕ ܡ̇ܫ̣ܠܚܝܢ ܘܐܠܝ̣ܨܝܢ ܘܐܦ ܠܣܝ̇ܒܪܬܐ “everything that was found on them was removed, and they were cast on the ground being stripped down and tormented, even in regard to their provisions”: John of Ephesus repeatedly complains about the harsh conditions under which the Severan bishops and their supporters were detained, including insufficient provisions, separation from friends, and the danger of falling prey to robbers (see 3.1.5-8.j). While we cannot exclude that there were indeed cases of such maltreatment, it is likely that they were limited to lower-ranking people such as servants. The bishops themselves were probably not subjected to such hardship as this might have been counterproductive (Roggo 2022, 105): the Chalcedonians were eager to come to an agreement and were, at least at times, quite generous towards the Severan leaders (see EH 3.1.27.d). Unsurprisingly however, John of Ephesus depicts the imprisonment in the patriarchal palace in the bleakest colors (see also his description of the various tortures from which he himself suffered in prison in EH 3.2.5).

div.head: Summary (SR; NK)
div.p: This chapter marks the pinnacle of the negotiations regarding the ecclesiastical union that Justin II hoped to achieve with his edict on faith (EH 3.1.19–3.1.24): the desired union of the two parties actually takes place, albeit only for a short time. However, according to the apologetic depiction of the events by John of Ephesus, this only came about as the result of deception: apparently, the party of patriarch John Scholasticus assured the Severan bishops that Chalcedon would be condemned as soon as they entered into union. John of Ephesus affirms that he and the other imprisoned bishops submitted to joint communion only twice, while consistently anathematizing the council of Chalcedon. Moreover, he adds that he and his peers kept insisting that the emperor and the patriarch should remove the Chalcedonian symbol from the church, just as they had supposedly promised. However, this evidently did not happen, and the Chalcedonians apparently asserted that they would first need to ask the bishop of Rome for his permission. As a part of his apologetic strategy, John of Ephesus claims that he had always known that the Chalcedonians just wanted to trick the Severans into accepting the union while never seriously entertaining the notion of removing Chalcedon. John assures his readers that he and his peers only agreed to the short-lasting joint communion because they wanted to prove to their critics accusing them of inflexibility (see EH 3.1.22.b) that it was the Chalcedonian party, and not they themselves who prevented the union from coming about.

The whole chapter is rhetorically carefully constructed as a dialogue between the Severan bishops and the Chalcedonians around patriarch John Scholasticus. As in the case of the speeches presented in EH 3.1.16 and 3.1.18, we may assume that John of Ephesus invented most of this dialogue for his own apologetic purposes (see 3.1.16.m and 3.1.18.j). He cannot hide the fact that he indeed entered into joint communion with the Chalcedonians, although he attempts to downplay this as far as possible by claiming that he and his peers had been deceived, that the union lasted only very briefly, and that they needed to prove to their critics that they were not a priori opposed to any union. His argumentation is not completely convincing, for if John Scholasticus had really promised that Chalcedon would be condemned, why would the bishops not simply have insisted on the inclusion of such a condemnation in the edict? As noted above in 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f, the Severan bishops may have been less adamant in their condemnation of Chalcedon than John makes it appear here, and they might have been content with the fact that the contentious council was not mentioned in the thoroughly neo-Chalcedonian edict. After the failure of the union (possibly because of the demand for reordinations; see the summary to EH 3.1.19 and 3.1.12.f; 3.1.16.d; 3.1.18.b and Roggo (2022, 105–110)), John needed to re-ingratiate himself with his co-religionists in the east who had always rejected any proposal for union which did not include an explicit condemnation of Chalcedon.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:301.a.5–302.a.7; 4:342.a.3–28: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.24.o Bar Hebraeus, Chronicon Ecclesiasticum 47 (Abbeloos and Lamy 1872–1877, 1:237.16–23): short summary based on Michael Chronicle to 1234 2.36 Chabot, Abouna, and Fiey 1916–1974, 2:252.20–30; 4:189.35–190.8: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.24.o

div.head: EH 3.1.24.a (SR) 31.3–4
div.p: ܒܬܪ ܕܝܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܬܘܒ܆ ܒܚܪܬܐ ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪܘ ܘܐܬ̣ܕܒܪܘ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܦܐ ܡܢ ܒܝܬ ܚܒ̣ܘܫܝܗܘܢ “so, after all these things, the bishops were again finally sent out of and led away from their place of confinement”: In the previous chapter EH 3.1.23, John of Ephesus prepared the stage for the heated debate between the Severan bishops and patriarch John Scholasticus which forms the center of EH 3.1.24. There, John of Ephesus stated that he and his peers were, for thirty-three days, repeatedly led from their cells to the patriarch and his council (see 3.1.23.a). He added that these debates did not lead to any lasting result, and he did not include any direct speeches. In the present chapter, however, set at the end of those thirty-three days, this is different, and John presents the debate in the form of a dialogue.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.b (NK; SR) 31.4–7
div.p: ܟܕ ܡܟܝܠ ܐܬܬ̣ܣܝܡܘ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ܆ ܩܫ̣ܝܐܝܬ ܘܚ̇ܨܝܦܐܝܬ. ܘܗ̣ܘ ܪܝܫ ܡ̇ܘܬܒܐ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܘܝܬ̇ܝܪ ܡܢܗ܆ ܘܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܢ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܡܫܬ̇ܕܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܘܦܪܨܘܦܗ ܥܒ̇ܕܝܢ ܗܘܘ “then the leader of the session, John, and even more than him those sent by the emperor and representing him, set themselves harshly and shamelessly against the bishops”: John Scholasticus and the imperial envoys pressure the Severan bishops, urging them towards unity. The speech John of Ephesus places in their mouths repeats their arguments from EH 3.1.21.a and 3.1.22.c and again puts all the blame for why the union has not yet been realized on the allegedly obstinate imprisoned bishops. As a part of John’s apologetic strategy, this speech serves to illustrate the constrained situation of the detainees. John shows how the Chalcedonian party employed all psychological and rhetorical means and thus eventually succeeded in overcoming the bishops’ resistance (Leppin 2020, 127–128).

div.head: EH 3.1.24.c (NK) 31.8
div.p: ܡ̇ܫܕܝܢ “to cast away”: In the manuscript, the participle ܡ̇ܫܕܝܢ, meaning “cast away,” is attested. This word is commented upon by a second hand in Serto script in the margins between the columns using the participle ܡܕܫܝܢ. It originates from the verb ܐܕܫ, meaning “to stop the ears, to be silent.” Given that the sole difference between the two participles is the position of the letters ܕ and ܫ, we may speculate that a later reader thought that the copyist had made a mistake and that ܡܕܫܝܢ was more fitting than ܡ̇ܫܕܝܢ.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.d (SR) 31.16–19
div.p: ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܕܝܢ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܦܐ: ܟܕ ܒܐܒ̣ܠܐ ܘܚܫܐ ܐ̣ܝܬܝܗܘܢ ܗܘܘ̣ ܐܡ̇ܪܝܢ. ܕܐܢ ܟܐܢܐܝܬ ܡ̈ܬܬ̇ܕܝܢܢ ܨܒ̈ܘ̣ܬܐ: ܘܒܚ̣ܘ̈ܫܒܐ ܬܩ̈ܢ̣ܐ܆ ܠܘ ܚ̣ܢܢ ܐ̣ܝܬܝܢ ܡ̈ܥܘ̇ܟܢܝܗ̇ ܕܚܕܝܘܬܐ̣ ܐܠܐ ܐ̣ܢܬܘܢ “but the bishops, who were mourning and suffering, said: ‘If these matters are adjudicated justly and with honest intentions, it is not we who are holding back the union but you’”: John of Ephesus’s rendering of the Severan bishops’ elaborate reply again repeats arguments already presented in EH 3.1.21.c; 3.1.21.d; 3.1.22.f. In a nutshell, John has the bishops accuse the Chalcedonian party of only pretending to be interested in the union; the bishops lament that they are being framed as the obstacle to the union, whereas in reality, so John asserts, the blame should lie with the Chalcedonian party and their unwillingness to condemn the council of Chalcedon. Again, John’s insistence that Chalcedon should be explicitly removed from the church likely is part of his apology. Writing after the failure of Justin’s edict, John claims with hindsight that he had always been adamant in the condemnation of Chalcedon. Yet there is much to suggest that he and his peers had originally been open to a compromise, just as in 567 (see 3.1.17.j; 3.1.20.f; 3.1.22.a and Roggo (2022, 103–106)).

div.head: EH 3.1.24.e (NK) 31.24–25
div.p: ܐܠܐ ܡ̇ܠܘܢ ܕܢܬ̇ܚܝܕ ܠܟܠܗ̇ ܕܓ̇ܠܘܬܐ ܟܠܩܕܘܢܝܬܐ ܬܒ̇ܥܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܠܢ ܢܟܝ̣ܠܐܝܬ “rather, you are cunningly demanding that we enter into a union with the duplicity of Chalcedon”: In the speech of the imprisoned bishops, the council of Chalcedon is identified as the actual point of contention. Ever since 451, the Christological definition of Chalcedon failed to establish unity within the church. The original intention of the symbol of Chalcedon as a compromise formula proved unsuccessful and did not lead to agreement. In the time of John of Ephesus, this symbol remained a stumbling block, which was unacceptable for the Severans due to its emphasis on the two natures in Jesus (see 3.1.10.g). John polemically refers to this symbol as the “duplicity of Chalcedon” (ܕܓ̇ܠܘܬܐ ܟܠܩܕܘܢܝܬܐ) and insists that he has always been opposed to it.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.f (NK) 31.27
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܡܢ ܫ̇ܘܪܝܐ ܐܡ̣ܪܢܢ܆ ܕܐܢ “just as we have said from the beginning: if”: Brooks (1935, 31 n.2) deletes the ܕ before ܐܢ as a correction, which is unnecessary. In the manuscript, the ܕ is used following the verb of speech. Here, the verb of speech followed by ܕ serves as an introduction to the conditional clause and can be rendered with a colon, similar to the Greek ὅτι-recitativum (Nöldeke 1904, 300). Thus, the reading in the manuscript does not need to be corrected.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.g (SR) 32.19–20
div.p: ܐܢ ܓܝܪ ܬܬ̇ܚܝܕܘܢ܆ ܡܚ̣ܕܐ ܢܦ̇ܩܐ ܘܐܦ ܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ܆ ܘܗ̇ܘܝܐ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܡ̇ܫܡ̣ܠܝܬܐ “for if you enter into a union, the synod will immediately be expelled and there will be complete unity”: This is the first mention of this element crucial to John of Ephesus’s apology for why he and his peers joined the Chalcedonian communion. Yet the claim that the Chalcedonian party around patriarch John Scholasticus really would have promised to condemn Chalcedon is not trustworthy. Justin II upheld the orthodoxy of Chalcedon ever since his accession in 565 Roggo 2022, 92–95 Averil Cameron 1976a, and an explicit condemnation of the council is hardly imaginable since this would have immediately caused large parts of the empire, especially in the west, to sever their ecclesiastical ties to Constantinople (see 3.1.20.f and 3.1.24.n). John of Ephesus, resident in Constantinople since the early 540s and repeatedly conferring with both Justinian and Justin II prior to 571, was no doubt aware of this dimension. Consequently, it is equally illogical to assume that the Chalcedonian party might have wanted to trick the Severan bishops into accepting the union with such a promise – both sides knew that the orthodoxy of Chalcedon could not possibly be removed from the doctrine of the imperial church. However, to simply omit any mention of Chalcedon in the edict on faith may have been a viable solution for moderate pro- and anti-Chalcedonians alike, including John of Ephesus. It was only after the failure of the edict that John needed to re-ingratiate himself with the more fundamentalist members of the Severan communion who had always insisted on the condemnation of Chalcedon. This forced him to come up with the excuse that he and his peers had been deceived and presented with false promises (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f).

div.head: EH 3.1.24.h (NK) 32.24–27
div.p: ܚ̣ܪܡܗ̇ ܕܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ ܘܛܘܡ̣ܣܗ ܕܠܐܘܢ ܕܐܝܟ ܚ̣ܕܐ ܡ̇ܦܠܓܝܢ ܠܗ ܠܡܪܢ ܘܐܠܗܢ ܘܦܪܘܩܢ ܝܫܘܥ ܡܫܝܚܐ ܠܬܪ̈ܝܢ ܟܝ̈ܢܝܢ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ܆ ܠܐ ܡ̇ܘܦܐ ܡܢ ܦܘ̈ܡܝܢ “the condemnation of the synod and the Tome of Leo which as one divide our Lord, our God, and our savior Jesus Christ into two natures after the union will not cease from our mouths”: In his apologetic rendering of the events leading up to the short-term union, John of Ephesus asserts that he and the other Severan bishops demanded the explicit condemnation of the Christological definition from the council of Chalcedon and the Tome of Leo. In the definition of Chalcedon, the miaphysite position is rejected and anathematized, thereby placing miaphysites under suspicion of heresy. It states that all “who speak of two natures of the Lord before the union, but after the union devise a single [nature]” (τοὺς δύο μὲν πρὸ τῆς ἑνώσεως φύσεις τοῦ κυρίου μυθεύοντας, μίαν δὲ μετὰ τὴν ἕνωσιν ἀναπλάττοντας) are subjected to anathema (Alberigo 2006, I:136.355–359). Instead, the emphasis in Chalcedon is placed on the distinction of the two natures (ἐν δύο φύσεσιν) in Jesus Christ. Additionally, the Chalcedonian definition asserts that the difference of the natures must not be abolished because of the union (οὐδαμοῦ τῆς τῶν φύσεων διαφορᾶς ἀνῃρημένης διὰ τὴν ἕνωσιν) (Alberigo 2006, I:137.380–382). This positioning appeared to the miaphysites as a separation of Christ “into two natures after the union” (ܠܬܪ̈ܝܢ ܟܝ̈ܢܝܢ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ) (see 3.1.10.g).

The definition of Chalcedon explicitly refers to the Tome of Leo (Alberigo 2006, I:136.332–344). There, the distinction of the two natures is clearly maintained after the union. In this doctrinal letter dated to 13 June 449, the Roman bishop Leo expounded his Christological view in advance of the council of Chalcedon (see above 3.1.18.g). In this letter addressed to Flavian of Constantinople, the two natures remain in the one person, so that the distinctiveness of both natures is preserved and comes together in one person (salva igitur proprietate utriusque naturae et in unam coeunte personam) (Alberigo 2006, I:128.60). The two natures almost appear as two subjects of the one person, for both are acting, the forma domini and the forma servi according to Philippians 2 . In this manner, they each participate in the action, which closely resembles the later doctrine of communicatio idiomatum. In the Tome of Leo, the Antiochene position of Christology prevails, emphasizing the two natures after the union. The anti-Chalcedonians see it as a precursor to the Chalcedonian confession.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.h.2 (NK; PMF) 33.1–3
div.p: ܐܢ ܗܘܠܣ ܘܐܝܟ ܡ̇ܘܡ̈ܬܟܘܢ ܗܕܐ ܡ̇ܛܝ̣ܒܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ ܠܡܥ̣ܒܕ “if you really are prepared to do this in accordance with your oaths”: At this juncture, Brooks proposed an emendation of the word ܗܘ̈ܠܣ, as attested in the manuscript, to ܗܠܘܣ (Brooks 1935, 33.2). The spelling found in the manuscript, with the syāmē, would seem to imply the plural of ܗܘܠܐ from Greek ὕλη “material,” a word that appears in EH 3.2.17 and 3.6.9 . Yet following the word ܐܢ “if,” it seems more likely that the Syriac ܗܘܠܣ reflects the Greek word ὅλως, resulting in the phrase ܐܢ ܗܘܠܣ “if […] really” which corresponds to the well-attested Greek phrase εἰ ὅλως. We have not identified any other Syriac text that contains this Greek loan word, but it is found in at least one other Aramaic dialect. In Christian Palestinian Aramaic, various spellings are attested (ܗܘܠܘܣ, ܗܘܠܝܣ) including that found in the manuscript of the EH: ܗܘܠܣ (Sokoloff 2014, 97). For this reason, we have not modified the consonantal text, but we have eliminated the syāmē which seems to be a scribal error suggesting the plural of ܗܘܠܐ.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.i (NK) 33.6
div.p: ܗܪ̈ܣܝܣ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܝ ܟܝ̈ܢܐ “the heresy of the dyophysites”: The bishops are depicted as demanding a clear repudiation of the dyophysite confession. Any mention of two natures in Jesus Christ is rejected and even stigmatized as the “heresy of the dyophysites.” Only under this condition are the bishops willing to enter into the union. John of Ephesus wants to make it unequivocally clear to his Severan community that he and his peers have taken a firm stance and that they have defended themselves against any attempted deception by the Chalcedonians (see 3.1.24.j and 3.1.24.l). In EH 3.1.18.f and 3.1.18.g, there was already mention of the “heresy of two natures” as well as the “blasphemies” of the Chalcedonian synod and the Tome of Leo, which, from the anti-Chalcedonian perspective, advocate for a quaternity instead of the Trinity within the Godhead.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.j (SR) 33.8–14
div.p: ܚ̣ܢܢ ܓܝܪ ܝܕܥ̇ܝܢܢ܆ ܕܨܒܝܢܟܘܢ ܘܚܘ̣ܫ̈ܒܝܟܘܢ܆ ܕܠܘ ܕܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܐܢܘܢ. ܡܛܠ ܕܐܦܠܐ ܡܬ̣ܚܙܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ܇ ܕܫܪܪܐ ܡ̇ܡܠܠܝܢ ܐܢܬܘܢ܆ ܐܦܢ ܒܡ̈ܠܐ ܐܬ̇ܛܝܒܬܘܢ ܕܬܥ̣ܒܕܘܢ܇ ܕܢܣܬ̣ܒܪ ܡܢ ܟܠܢܫ܇ ܕܚ̣ܢܢ ܒ̇ܛܠܢܗ̇ ܠܚܕܝܘܬܐ. ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܐܝܟ ܕܡܢ ܠܘܬܟܘܢ ܘܡܢ ܟܠܢܫ܆ ܚ̣ܢܢ ܢܬܚ̣ܪܡ ܐܝܟ ܡ̇ܒ̈ܛܠܢܐ “for we know that your will and your thoughts are not of the union, since you do not even seem to be speaking the truth even if in words you are prepared to create it, so that it might be known to everyone that we have obstructed the union with the result that we might be condemned, by everyone just as by you, as those who are obstructing it”: In the Severan bishops’ second elaborate speech, John of Ephesus further prepares his readers for the inconvenient truth that he and his peers had entered into union with the Chalcedonians. John presents this as the result of pressure being exerted on them from all sides, including members of their own communion. However, as noted above in 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f, there is much to suggest that there was less pressure involved than John wants to make it appear and that Justin’s edict on faith was acceptable for the bishops – at least until the union achieved through it collapsed, possibly as a result of the demand for reordinations (see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.18.b). After the eventual failure, the bishops faced the danger of lasting damage to their standing within the anti-Chalcedonian community; one of them, Paul of Antioch (see EH 3.2.8; 3.4.15; 3.1.17.d) had to undergo a period of penance for three years before he was readmitted into his community. Consequently, these potentially disastrous consequences forced John to construct EH 3.1.3–3.1.30 as a very careful apology of his motives (Roggo 2022, 103–107), as he and his peers had transgressed the increasingly rigid boundaries between the imperial church and the emerging Severan ecclesial structures (on the importance of taking the right Eucharist as an identity marker, see 3.1.10.h).

John repeats arguments from the previous chapters EH 3.1.20–3.1.22. Again, he has the bishops complain that the Chalcedonian party, benefitting from the fact that the imprisoned bishops were unable to interact with the wider public, managed to present them as the sole obstacle to union. This had as a consequence that even lay people sympathetic to the Severan clerics became impatient with them and started criticizing them for impeding the union (see 3.1.22.a and 3.1.24.k).

div.head: EH 3.1.24.j.2 (NK) 33.14–16
div.p: ܚ̣ܢܢ ܓܝܪ ܐܦ ܢܦܫ̈ܬܢ ܚܠܦ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܕܒ̇ܚܝܢܢ܆ ܕܟܕ ܥܠ ܡ̈ܠܝܟܘܢ ܘܫ̈ܘܘܕܝܟܘܢ ܡܬ̇ܬܟܠܝܢܢ “we are even sacrificing ourselves for the union as we, trusting in your words and your promises”: In the introduction of the subordinate clause, the manuscript offers the following construction: ܕܟܕ ܕܐܢ ܐܪܐ. Brooks corrected this by deleting ܕܐܢ ܐܪܐ. He posited that this wording may have been erroneously imported from a few lines later, where the formulation ܐܢ ܐܪܐ appears: “quod e l. 18 fortasse irrepsit” (Brooks 1935, 33 n.2). It is possible that the scribe made an error. Indeed, the phrase “if now” (ܕܐܢ ܐܪܐ) after ܕܟܕ is disruptive at this place and can be omitted. This phrase is analogous to the Greek εἰ ἄρα, which is employed to reinforce a conditional clause. For instance, in Mark 11:13 , the phrase εἰ ἄρα τι εὑρήσει is used in this manner. The Harklean text faithfully replicates this in its translation: ܕܐܢ ܐܪܐ ܡܫܟܚ ܡܕܡ ܒܗ̇ (Kiraz 2004, 2:163). John of Ephesus frequently employs this construction; see, for instance, EH 3.1.18; 3.1.21; 3.1.24; 3.1.37; 3.4.12; 3.6.1.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.j.3 (NK) 33.26–27
div.p: ܗܠܝܢ ܥܡ ܣܓܝܐ̈ܬܐ ܕܕܠܐ ܣ̣ܟܐ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ܆ ܘܐܬ̇ܡܠܠ̣ ܘܐܣܬ̣ܗܕ ܒܝܢܬܗܘܢ “such things and many others which are innumerable were spoken and testified to between them”: Brooks (1935, 33.27) corrected the wording of the last two verbs by changing the third person masculine plural ܘܐܬ̇ܡܠܠܘ̣ ܘܐܣܬ̣ܗܕܘ to the third person feminine plural ܘܐܬ̇ܡܠܠ̣ ܘܐܣܬ̣ܗܕ. The emendation proposed by Brooks is justified on the grounds that the subject of the two verbs, ܗܠܝܢ, is either masculine or feminine. Since unspecified “things” in Syriac are typically feminine, ܣܓܝܐ̈ܬܐ is also feminine.
