Thus, the demonstrative pronoun ܗܠܝܢ, in its function as subject of the two verbs, must be considered as plural in the feminine. Since ܐܬ̇ܡܠܠܘ̣ and ܐܬ̇ܡܠܠ̣ are pronounced the same, it is not difficult to imagine that a scribe read ܗܠܝܢ as masculine plural and adjusted the verb accordingly. This explains the erroneous spelling in the manuscript. In light of these considerations, Brooks’s emendation can be accepted.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.k (SR) 34.1–5
div.p: ܟܠܢܫ ܓܝܪ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܡ̇ܓܕܦ ܗܘܐ: ܡܢ ܬܪ̈ܝܗܘܢ ܓ̇ܒ̈ܐ ܟܕ ܐܡ̇ܪܝܢ܆ ܕܗܐ ܠܡ ܡ̈ܠܟܐ ܘܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ ܝܨ̇ܝܦܝܢ ܘܚܦ̣ܝܛܝܢ ܘܡ̇ܛܝ̣ܒܝܢ ܕܢܥ̣ܒܕܘܢ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ܆ ܘܗܠܝܢ ܕܚܒܝ̣ܫܝܢ ܟ̇ܠܝ̣ܢ ܘܡ̇ܥܟܪܝܢ ܠܗܘܢ “for everyone from both sides reviled them, saying: ‘The emperors and patriarch are taking care to, intend to, and are preparing to create a union. But those who are imprisoned are hindering and holding them back’”: John of Ephesus indicates, as he did already in EH 3.1.22, that also some lay people who had been normally sympathetic towards the Severan bishops were now criticizing them. This resulted from the propaganda of the Chalcedonian party around patriarch John Scholasticus and further increased the pressure exerted on the bishops to agree to the union; see 3.1.22.a and 3.1.24.j.
Already in EH 3.1.11.e and 3.1.11.j, John stated that both “emperors” (ܡ̈ܠܟܐ), that is, Justin and his wife Sophia, were engaged in securing the union with the Severan bishops.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.l (SR; NK) 34.6–9
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܒܩܠܐ ܪܡ̇ܐ ܡ̇ܚܪܡܝܢ ܠܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ܆ ܐܬ̣ܪ̈ܡܝܘ ܠܫܘܬ̇ܦܘܬܗܘܢ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܙܒ̈ܢܝܢ: ܐܝܟ ܕܐܫܬ̇ܘܕܝܘ ܘܐܦ̣ܣܘ “while condemning the synod of Chalcedon with a raised voice, they acquiesced to commune with them two times as they had promised and to which they had conceded”: John of Ephesus admits here that he and the other imprisoned bishops entered into communion with the Chalcedonians. He attempts to downplay this union by asserting that this only happened twice and that they kept anathematizing Chalcedon during the joint communion. This presentation of the events serves as an apology to the wider Severan community (see 3.1.24.d and 3.1.24.j). Yet given that the whole of EH 3.1.3–3.1.30 is a careful literary construction, we should be wary of uncritically accepting John’s claim of only two instances of joint communion. As stated in the summary to EH 3.1.19 and 3.1.12.f; 3.1.16.d; 3.1.18.b, it is quite probable that he distorted the order of events and that the whole debate about Justin’s edict and the union (EH 3.1.19–3.1.24) occurred prior to patriarch John Scholasticus’s demands for reordinations treated in EH 3.1.12–3.1.18 (Roggo 2022, 103–10; Maraval 1998, 466–67). There are important implications, should this indeed be the case: this would mean that the situation John describes in EH 3.1.12 (many Severan clerics had given in to the pressure and concelebrated the liturgy together with the Chalcedonians for up to thirty-six times; see 3.1.12.a–3.1.12.d) actually corresponds to the period after the bishops had accepted the edict and entered into union in EH 3.1.24. At least parts of the Severan clergy might thus have remained in the union on the basis of Justin’s edict for longer than for merely two joint liturgies. It is impossible to decide with any certainty if John and the other bishops also remained in communion for a longer period – by claiming that he had taken communion with the Chalcedonians only twice, John of Ephesus may just be attempting to let his lapse appear less reprehensible (see the summary to EH 3.1.25 and 3.1.25.f). The way in which he creates the impression of two separate instances of Severan clerics taking Chalcedonian communion in EH 3.1.12 and 3.1.24 is clearly part of his apologetic strategy: in EH 3.1.12.a, he does not deny that some clerics often concelebrated with the Chalcedonians, yet he presents himself as not at all involved in this and instead vaguely speaks of “many priests of the orthodox” (ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ ܟܗ̈ܢܐ ܕܡܢ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ). He then first talks at length about the patriarch’s demand for reordinations and the debate about the edict before finally conceding in EH 3.1.24 that he himself also briefly entered into union with the Chalcedonians. If this reconstruction is accurate, it underlines again that John’s primary aim was to reject the notion that he had ever voluntarily entered into Chalcedonian communion, hence his continuous improbable insistence in EH 3.1.19–3.1.24 that he and his peers had always been adamant in their condemnation of Chalcedon.

Brooks (1935, 34 n.1) suspected that the verb ܐܟ̣ܣܘ attested in the manuscript may be corrupt (“vox corrupta esse videtur”) and instead proposed the verb ܐܦ̣ܣܘ with the basic meaning “to grant permission, give leave, allow, permit” (Brooks 1936, 23 n.4). The verb ܐܟ̣ܣܘ with the meaning “to rebuke, reprove, confute” indeed seems to be corrupted in this context because, like the previous verb ܐܫܬ̇ܘܕܝܘ “to promise,” it is used in parallel with ܐܝܟ ܕ and strengthens the bishops’ concession. The verb ܐܦ̣ܣ is also used in EH 3.1.19 and 3.4.5 in aphʿel, so that it is clear that it belonged to John’s vocabulary. It is possible that a scribal error occurred in the transmission of the letters ܟ and ܦ. Therefore, Brooks’s emendation can be adopted.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.m (SR) 34.10–11
div.p: ܒܟܠܗܘܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܬܠܬܝܢ ܘܬܠܬܐ ܝܘ̈ܡܝܢ “for all thirty-three days”: This refers back to the debates over Justin’s edict on faith between the Severan bishops and the Chalcedonian party around John Scholasticus in the patriarchal palace introduced in EH 3.1.23. According to 3.1.23.a, these debates lasted for thirty-three days.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.n (SR; NK) 34.14–16
div.p: ܕܟܬ̇ܒܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܠܡ ܠܦܦܐ ܕܪܗܘܡܐ: ܘܐܢ ܡܩ̇ܒܠ܆ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܝܢ ܡ̇ܦܩܝܢܢ ܠܗ̇ ܠܣܘܢܗܕܣ: ܠܐ ܓܝܪ ܠܡ ܡܫ̣ܟܚܝܢܢ ܕܡܛܠܬܟܘܢ ܢܣ̣ܕܘܩ ܡܢ ܪܗܘܡܐ “we are going to write to the pope in Rome and then, if he accepts it, we are going to expel the synod. For we cannot cause a schism with Rome on your behalf”: Since the resolution of the Acacian schism in 519, the patriarchates of Rome and Constantinople were in communion with each other. This had been different in the decades before: in 482, the patriarch of Constantinople, Acacius (sed. 472–489), had formulated a reconciliatory definition of faith with the aim to appease the anti-Chalcedonians by omitting any mention of the council of Chalcedon. Emperor Zeno (r. 474–491) had supported Acacius’s definition and demanded its recognition; hence it became known as Zeno’s Henotikon. However, bishop Felix III of Rome (sed. 483–492) had reacted by formally severing ecclesiastical communion as he insisted on the declaration of Chalcedon as orthodox. The schism persisted throughout the reign of emperor Anastasius I (r. 491–518) and was associated with accusations that the emperors favored the anti-Chalcedonian position. The resolution of the Acacian schism was achieved in March 519 soon after the accession of Justin I (r. 518–527). As a precondition for ending the schism, bishop Hormisdas of Rome (sed. 514–523) demanded a declaration against Nestorianism and Eutychianism and a reaffirmation of the orthodoxy of Chalcedon and the Tome of Leo; Justin agreed to this demand (Price 2009, 1–7).

Thus, regarding John of Ephesus’s depiction of the negotiations in 571, it is evident that the Roman church would never have assented to the condemnation of Chalcedon. John portrays the Chalcedonian party as offering a mere pretext for breaking their supposed promise (van Ginkel 1995, 179–180); see also 3.1.20.f. It is even likely that Rome eventually would have rejected Justin’s edict just as Zeno’s Henotikon because it did not explicitly embrace the orthodoxy of Chalcedon. The strictly pro-Chalcedonian stance of Rome was well known, and accordingly, John of Ephesus reveals at the moment when the Chalcedonians declare that they first have to consult with the Roman church about condemning Chalcedon that he and his peers discovered that they had been tricked (see 3.1.24.g). It is also because of this unswervingly pro-Chalcedonian stance of the Roman church that the story preserved in Michael the Syrian Chronicle10.5 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:295.a.9–296.a.8; 4:338.a.28–339.b.9 about the anti-Chalcedonian bishops’ supposed journey to Rome in the hope of convincing the Roman bishop to agree to a compromise very probably is a later invention (see the introduction to EH 3.1.5-8). For the position of the Roman bishops in the 6th century, see Price 2009, 30–33 Chazelle and Cubitt 2007; Sotinel 2005; 1998.

div.head: EH 3.1.24.o (SR) 34.16–19
div.p: ܘܗܝ̇ܕܝܢ ܦܢ̇ܝܘ ܠܗܘܢ̣ ܟܕ ܐܡ̇ܪܝܢ. ܕܡܟܝܠ ܙܒܢܐ ܘܐܦ ܠܢ ܕܡܠܬܗ ܕܝܘܢܢ ܢܒܝܐ ܢܬ̣ܢܐ܆ ܕܠܘ ܠܡ ܗܕܐ ܗܘܬ ܡܠܬܝ ܟܕ ܐܢܐ ܒܐܪܥܝ “then the bishops replied to them, saying: ‘Now it is also the time for us to recount the word of the prophet Jonah:“Is this not what I said when I was in my land?”’”: When John Scholasticus and his supporters asserted that they could not condemn the council of Chalcedon, contrary to their promise, without the consent of the Roman church, the Severan bishops were confirmed in their suspicion that the Chalcedonians had never wanted to turn against the council. John of Ephesus and his peers had known it all along, yet, so John claims, nevertheless given in to the pressure to make clear that it was not they who were obstructing the union. In his description of this foreseeable outcome, John quotes the Book of Jonah 4:2 , sticking neatly to the Peshitta version (Gelston 1980, 43). According to this story, the prophet Jonah had been in a similar situation when God ordered him to go to Nineveh and announce the city’s impending downfall, yet correctly assumed that God might then want to turn back on his word and spare the city (Magonet 1992).

The contents of this chapter are summarized both in the Chronicle to 1234, 2.36 Chabot, Abouna, and Fiey 1916–1974, 2:252.20–30; 4:189.35–190.8 and, in more detail, in the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:301.a.5–302.a.7; 4:342.a.3–28. Both chronicles accurately mention the promise that Chalcedon would be condemned after the establishment of the union, the pressure exerted on the Severan bishops by both Chalcedonians and sympathizers of the Severans, their entering into communion (with only the Chronicle to 1234 stating that this happened only twice), and the breach of the promise by referring to the Roman church. Michael also included the quotation from the Book of Jonah 4:2 when relating the Severan bishops’ reaction to their deception. In addition to this summary, Michael also stated in three other passages briefly that John of Ephesus and the bishops eventually succumbed to the pressure and accepted the edict, in 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:294.a.31–295.a.5; 4:338.a.13–20 and on two further occasions in 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:299.a.15–17; 4:341.b.10–11 and 2:300.a.9–13; 4:341.b.24–27.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus presents in this chapter how he and the other bishops reacted when noticing that the Chalcedonians had deceived them and that there would be no condemnation of the council of Chalcedon, contrary to the promise they had received. The bishops, so John assures us, were all inconsolable in their grief, mourning and refraining from food, knowing what a reprehensible mistake they had made by accepting Justin’s edict and taking communion together with the Chalcedonians. The image of continuous contrition and lamentation John conveys here evidently is, first and foremost, a literary construction. As a part of his apologetic strategy, it was crucial for him to create the impression that his and the other bishops’ participation in the Chalcedonian communion had only been the result of force and deceit, and merely lasted for a very short time (see EH 3.1.24.g and 3.1.24.l). But there are hints in this chapter that the situation might have been quite different from what John claims: he states that he and his peers were released from prison and allowed to move around freely – this would hardly have been the case, had they withdrawn from the joint communion almost immediately. This rather suggests that there was a more extended period when the Severan bishops were in communion with the Chalcedonians on the basis of Justin’s edict, and that they considered the absence of an explicit condemnation of Chalcedon as unproblematic. It was only after the failure of the union that John needed to describe the events rather differently (see 3.1.24.l). Therefore, he asserts at the end of this chapter that the Severan bishops kept attacking and deriding the Chalcedonians. This, so he states, led to their renewed forceful separation from each other. This last part of the chapter already anticipates EH 3.1.29 and 3.2.1–3.2.7, where this new period of imprisonment of the bishops after their withdrawal from the union is treated in more detail.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:302.a.8–14; 4:342.a.28–33: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.25.a Chronicle to 1234 2.36 (Chabot, Abouna, and Fiey 1916–1974, 2:252.30–253.6; 4:190.9–13): summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.25.a

div.head: EH 3.1.25.a (SR) 35.5–8
div.p: ܡܢ̣ܘ ܓܝܪ ܣ̇ܦܩ ܠܡܟ̣ܬܒ ܘܠܡܬ̇ܢܝܘ ܥܠ ܥܩ̈ܬܐ ܘܐܒ̣ܠܐ ܘܐܠܝ̣ܐ ܘܬܒ̇ܪܐ ܕܠܒܐ܇ ܕܐ̣ܕܪܟ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܗܠܝܢ ܕܒܫܘܬ̇ܦܘܬܐ ܢܦ̣ܠܘ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܕܐܬܢܟ̣ܠܘ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܘܐܬ̇ܚܝܕܘ ܠܗܘܢ܇ ܟܕ ܣ̣ܟ ܠܐ ܐܬ̇ܚܝܕܘ “who is able to write about and recount the adversities, mourning, lamentation, and brokenheartedness that came upon those who had succumbed to communing with the ones who had tricked them and entered into a union with them without being united at all?”: John of Ephesus’s statement that he and his peers were inconsolably afflicted once they realized that they had been deceived and that the representatives of the imperial church did not turn against the council of Chalcedon very likely is part of his apology to the Severan communities in Syria and Mesopotamia (see EH 3.2.6.d). As discussed in 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f, the Severan bishops probably agreed to Justin’s edict although it did not condemn Chalcedon. However, after the failure of the union (possibly because of the attempted reordinations; see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.24.l), they found themselves rejected by both Chalcedonians and their stricter co-religionists who had never accepted the edict. They needed to explain their lapse and for this reason, John of Ephesus keeps claiming that he and the other bishops immediately withdrew from the communion in deep sorrow and that they would never have agreed to joining the Chalcedonians, had they known that the council would not be condemned (Roggo 2022, 103–110).

Both Michael the Syrian and the anonymous author of the Chronicle to 1234 included this episcopal lamentation in their works Chabot, Abouna, and Fiey 1916–1974, 2:252.30–253.6; 4:190.9–13; Chabot 1899–1910, 2:302.a.8–14; 4:342.a.28–33. However, unlike John, neither of them explicitly says that the bishops were set free, and consequently, they also both omit John’s anticipation of EH 3.1.29, where he states that the bishops were eventually re-imprisoned (see 3.1.25.g).

div.head: EH 3.1.25.b (SR) 35.11–13
div.p: ܟܕ ܙܥܘܪܝܐ ܗܝ ܬܚܘܝܬܐ ܕܐܒ̣ܠܐ ܕܐܢܬܬܐ ܕܥܠ ܒܥ̣ܠ ܛܠܝܘܬܗ̇܇ ܕܒܐܒ̣ܠܗܘܢ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܬ̇ܬܦܚܡ “the example of a woman mourning for the husband of her youth being small in comparison to their mourning”: This passage alludes to the Book of Joel 1:8 where a virgin in a sackcloth mourns for the husband to whom she was promised in her youth (ܐܠܝ ܐܝܟ ܒܬܘܠܬܐ ܕܟܒܝܢܐ ܣܩܐ ܥܠ ܒܥܠ ܛܠܝܘܬܗ̇) (Gelston 1980, 17). John’s intention here is to express that the grief of the Severan bishops was such that it could even bear comparison with passages of lamentation from the Old Testament, which is also the main topic of EH 3.1.4 As such, it is very likely part of his strategy to win back the favor of the Severan communities in the east.

div.head: EH 3.1.25.c (SR) 35.13–15
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܡܟܝܠ̣ ܘܐܦ ܡܢ ܡܐ̣ܟܘܠܬܐ ܕܥ̇ܝܕܐ ܦܣ̣ܩܘ ܗܘܘ܆ ܘܕܠܐ ܣܝ̇ܒܪܬܐ ܘܕܠܐ ܒ̇ܘܝܐܐ ܢܟܬ̇ܪ̈ܘܢ “how from that point on they also cut themselves off from regular food and would wait without provisions and without consolation”: Earlier in EH 3.1.23.h and, very probably, in the lacuna between EH 3.1.5 and 3.1.9 (see 3.1.5-8.l), John of Ephesus complained that the imprisoned Severan clerics suffered from insufficient provision with food and drinks. There, he put the blame for this on the Chalcedonian persecutors and described how the Severans were neglected and maltreated. In the present passage, he inverts this motif and assures his readers that it was now he and his peers themselves who refrained from food because of their boundless grief, even though they would have had ample access to it after entering into communion with the Chalcedonians. Again, we should be wary of accepting this claim at face value. John likely greatly exaggerates the bishops’ affliction and their self-chastisement so as to curry favor with his co-religionists (see 3.1.24.g and 3.1.24.l).

div.head: EH 3.1.25.d (SR) 35.22–26
div.p: ܐ̇ܝܟܢܐ ܕܡܟܝܠ ܗ̇ܢܘܢ ܕܢ̣ܟܠܘ ܐܢܘܢ: ܟܕ ܚ̣ܙܘ ܕܐܬ̇ܛܠܩܘ ܗܘܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܡܢ ܟܕܘ ܘܫ̣ܪ̈ܟܘ ܠܚܒ̇ܠܐ܆ ܐܡ̇ܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܘܬܗܘܢ܆ ܕܥܠ ܡ̇ܢܐ ܗܟܢܐ ܢܦܫܟܘܢ ܥܡ ܦܓܪܟܘܢ ܩ̇ܛܠܝܢ. ܐܢܬܘܢ܇ ܐܝܟ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܠܦܬܟܪ̈ܐ ܕܒ̇ܚܬܘܢ “how from that point on those who had tricked them, having seen that they had already perished and been ruined, would say to them: ‘To what end are you killing your soul along with your body in this way as if you were making a sacrifice to idols?’”: The fact that John of Ephesus depicts the Chalcedonians as taking an active interest in the wellbeing of the Severan bishops, who were allegedly wallowing in self-pity, suggests that the period of joint communion may have lasted longer than he admits. It is unlikely that the Chalcedonians would have cared about the bishops, had there indeed only been two joint communions, as John claims in EH 3.1.24.l. On the contrary, the whole chapter EH 3.1.25 with its description of the bishops’ lengthy (and in all likelihood invented) laments and their eventual release from prison (see 3.1.25.f) rather evokes the impression that the union based on Justin’s edict initially was a success and not limited to merely two communions.

At the same time, John also presents the Chalcedonians as failing to grasp the difficulties and the hardship the Severan bishops were in as a result of their lapse. Apparently, they feared lest the Severan self-castigation lead to their committing suicide (“killing your soul along with your body”; ܢܦܫܟܘܢ ܥܡ ܦܓܪܟܘܢ ܩ̇ܛܠܝܢ). They are shown as eager to prevent this, all the more so since from their point of view, nothing at all had happened which might have warranted such excessive grief and self-torture. For the Chalcedonians as John depicts them here, such behavior might be understandable, had the Severans sacrificed to pagan deities, but surely not after successfully restoring the unity of the church.

div.head: EH 3.1.25.e (NK) 36.2–3
div.p: ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܕܝ̣ܢ ܕܠܐ ܒ̇ܘܝܐܐ ܡܟܬ̇ܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ “as they remained inconsolable”: The particle ܕܝ̣ܢ is missing in the manuscript. Brooks (1935, 36 n.1) added it in his edition, proposing that its omission after ܗ̣ܢܘܢ may have been due to a scribal mistake since both words end with the same letter. We have accepted his emendation as it connects this clause to the preceding passage and creates an opposition between the Chalcedonians and the Severan bishops.

div.head: EH 3.1.25.f (SR) 36.4–6
div.p: ܘܡܬ̇ܡܪܡܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܣ̈ܓܝܐܐ܆ ܐܦ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܕܡܢ ܒܝܬ ܚܒ̣ܘܫܝܐ ܐܫܬ̣ܪ̈ܝܘ ܘܢܦ̣ܩܘ “were embittered for many days even after they had been released and gone forth from the place of confinement”: This is another passage (next to 3.1.25.d) which strongly suggests that John of Ephesus and his episcopal peers were in communion with the Chalcedonians for a longer period of time than for merely two joint celebrations, irrespective of John’s apologetic claims to the opposite. John reveals here that the Severan bishops had actually left the patriarchal residence and were free for “many days”(ܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܣ̈ܓܝܐܐ) before recanting and being re-imprisoned (see 3.1.25.g and 3.1.26.a). It is implausible to assume that they would have remained at liberty for what appears to have been an extended period, had they withdrawn again from the communion with the Chalcedonians after two instances. The “many days” during which they were free, although supposedly constantly lamenting, probably allowed for quite many joint liturgical celebrations. This period may thus have been identical to the timespan mentioned in EH 3.1.12 (see 3.1.12.a–3.1.12.d and 3.1.24.l), when some Severan clerics (possibly the bishops themselves) concelebrated with the Chalcedonians up to thirty-six times.

div.head: EH 3.1.25.g (SR) 36.8–11
div.p: ܘܗܟܢܐ ܬܘܒ̣ ܠܚܒ̣̈ܘܫܝܐ ܘܫ̈ܢ̣ܕܐ ܕܩ̣ܫܝܢ ܡܢ ܩ̈ܕܡܝܐ ܐܫܬ̣ܠܡܘ. ܘܒܬܪܟܢ ܠܐܟ̣ܣܘܪ̈ܝܣ ܡ̇ܫ̣̈ܚܠܦܬܐ ܟܠ ܚܕ ܚܕ ܡܢܗܘܢ ܐܫ̣ܬܕܝ̣ ܒܟܒ̣ܕܐ ܡ̇ܪܝܪܬܐ “they were thus handed back over to their prison cells and to torments harsher than the former ones, and each one of them was cast into different places of banishment in a bitter wrath”: The end of this chapter already appears to anticipate EH 3.1.29.f where John of Ephesus describes how he and the other bishops were again separated from each other and imprisoned in different locations after the definitive failure of the union and the breakdown of the negotiations. He thus omits here the chain of events treated in EH 3.1.26–3.1.28, including Justin’s final attempt at achieving reconciliation and restoring the union. The reasons for this omission are mainly rhetorical in nature: in the present chapter, John portrays the events in a very straightforward, uncompromising (and probably ahistorical) manner – he and his peers discovered that they had been deceived, lamented ceaselessly, angrily turned against the perjurious Chalcedonians, and consequently were imprisoned again. In this way, John claims thus yet again that he had always been fervently anti-Chalcedonian and prepared to suffer for the right faith, while in reality, he might have been open for compromise (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus further describes in this chapter what happened after he and the other Severan bishops had been released from prison as a result of their entering into union with the Chalcedonians, when they were constantly lamenting because of their supposed deception (EH 3.1.25). John asserts here that emperor Justin II and empress Sophia themselves took an active interest in the grieving bishops. He records that the emperor ordered the bishops to come to the palace, where he talked to them and expressed his hope that the union might nevertheless be saved. He promised that he would keep working for a solution acceptable for the Severan bishops, yet asked them to wait for about a month because of his impending visit to the thermal baths outside Constantinople.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:302.a.14–24; 4:342.a.33–40: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.26.d

div.head: EH 3.1.26.a (SR) 36.15–17
div.p: ܟܕ ܓܝܪ ܥܠ ܗܢܐ ܐܒܠ̣ܐ ܟܘܠܗ ܩܫܝ̣ܐ ܘܚ̇ܫܐ ܡ̇ܪܝܪܐ ܡ̈ܠܟܐ ܝܠ̣ܦܘܢ܆ ܫ̣ܕܪܘ ܘܕܒ̣ܪ̈ܘ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܠܦܠܛܝܢ “when the emperors learned about all the severe mourning and the bitter suffering, they sent for and had the bishops led to the palace”: The summoning of the bishops to the imperial palace probably happened before they withdrew from the union and were re-imprisoned. After all, in EH 3.1.25, John of Ephesus states that he and his peers first lamented for an extended period, and then gathered their strength, rebuked the Chalcedonians as liars, and were arrested again (3.1.25.f–3.1.25.g). Consequently, since the imperial summons mentioned here is described as occurring during their period of mourning and self-castigation (in 3.1.25, the emperors describe the bishops as “so weak that it is as though you are dead,” ܥܕܡܐ ܕܗܟܢܐ ܬܫܬ̇ܦܠܘܢ ܘܬܗܘܘܢ ܐܝܟ ܡ̈ܝ̣ܬܐ), this most likely took place prior to their new detention. Contrary to this interpretation, Destephen (2008, 513) assumed that the summons only happened after the bishops’ re-imprisonment. Yet this is improbable; he did not consider that the end of EH 3.1.25, where John refers to this re-imprisonment, may be an anticipation of EH 3.1.29. In that passage, John wanted to already make clear to his readers that he and his peers kept resisting the Chalcedonians and preferred incarceration over a prolonged joint communion (see 3.1.25.g).

The “emperors” (ܡ̈ܠܟܐ) are Justin II and Sophia who, according to EH 3.1.11.g, were personally invested in achieving the union with the Severans. Schönfelder (1862, 27) wrongly thought that this refers to Justin and Tiberius, yet the latter was only made Caesar in December 574. John of Ephesus himself had already participated in religious debates with both Justinian and Justin II on some occasions prior to 571 (see 3.1.3.s); the occasion described here was therefore not unusual.

div.head: EH 3.1.26.b (SR) 36.20–22
div.p: ܡܣ̇ܒܪܝܢܢ ܓܝܪ ܒܐܠܗܐ܆ ܕܢܝ̇ܚܟܘܢ ܥ̇ܒܕܝܢ ܚܢܢ܆ ܘܒܚܕܝܘܬܐ ܡ̇ܫܡ̣ܠܝܬܐ ܡܚ̇ܝܕܝܢܢ ܠܟܘܢ “we hope in God that we can satisfy you and enter into a full union with you”: John of Ephesus portrays Justin as eager to mend the difficult situation that had arisen after the Severan bishops supposedly had been tricked with false promises, so John claims, into accepting joint communion with the Chalcedonians (see 3.1.24.l). However, given the strong possibility that there had never been such promises and that the bishops had accepted the union on the basis of Justin’s edict without insisting on an explicit condemnation of Chalcedon (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f), the conversation with the emperor must have focused on another issue (see 3.1.26.c). It appears likely that this may have been connected to the attempted reordination of the Severan clergy described in EH 3.1.12–3.1.18. If we accept the hypothesis that these attempts in fact only took place after the debate about Justin’s edict and that they were one of the main reasons why the initially successful union failed and the Severan bishops recanted (see 3.1.12.f; 3.1.16.d; 3.1.18.b), the emperor may have tried to convince the bishops to stay in communion and therefore summoned them for talks to the palace (Roggo 2022, 107–110). Justin did, after all, put an end to the reordinations by outlawing them in a typos pragmatikos (see EH 3.1.16.q). Furthermore, EH 3.1.28 records that he was angry with patriarch John Scholasticus for causing the failure of the union because of his actions – possibly a reference to the reordinations the patriarch demanded.

div.head: EH 3.1.26.c (SR) 36.23–24
div.p: ܒܣܓܝܐ̈ܬܐ ܪܫ̇ܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗ ܥܠ ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܐܬ̈ܡ̣̇ܠܠܝ ܒܝܢ̣ܬܗܘܢ: ܘܐܬ̈ܕܓܠ̣ܝ ܘܠܐ ܐܬ̣̈ܝ ܠܥܒ̇ܕܐ “they were censuring the emperor at length about the things discussed between them which had been violated and had not come to pass”: John of Ephesus’s vague phrasing here is no coincidence; by not explicitly stating what the bishops discussed with the emperor, readers are led to think that the “violated” things refer to the broken promise about the condemnation of Chalcedon. Yet in line with the interpretation offered above in 3.1.26.b, the real issue may well have been the reordinations patriarch John Scholasticus suddenly wished to perform after repeatedly concelebrating with the Severan clergy (see 3.1.12.a–3.1.12.d). It is probable that John of Ephesus and his peers interpreted this unexpected turn of events as a violation of the terms under which they had entered into the union on the basis of Justin’s edict, and that this was the main focus of their talk with the emperor.

div.head: EH 3.1.26.d (SR) 36.25–26
div.p: ܕܡܛܠ ܠܡ ܕܡ̇ܛܝ̣ܒܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܕܢܐ̣ܙܠ ܠܚܡ̇ܝܡܬܐ܆ ܟܬ̇ܪ̈ܘܢ ܠܝ ܥܣܪ̈ܝܢ ܐ̇ܘ ܬܠܬܝܢ ܝܘ̈ܡܝܢ “since we are preparing to go to the thermal bath, wait for me for twenty or thirty days”: Bunce (2023, 85–87) has argued that Justin’s initiative, with which he hoped to convince the Severan bishops to create a lasting union, is just a literary fiction by John of Ephesus, possibly in order to highlight that he still had direct access to the emperor. According to Bunce, Justin would not have left the issue unresolved and traveled to the baths, had he really been interested in finding a permanent solution. Yet this is not convincing; a period of “twenty or thirty days” of imperial absence is very short and it is likely that no one involved in the doctrinal debate considered such an interruption as an unusual delay. Under Justinian, protracted debates with anti-Chalcedonians had, on at least one occasion, lasted for more than a year, evidently with many interruptions, and John of Ephesus was aware of this Witakowski 1996, 121–122 Chabot 1933a, 136–138. Moreover, Roman law occasionally stipulated a designated reflection period for the emperor, notably in cases involving executions, as attested in the Codex Theodosianus 9.40.13 (Meyer and Mommsen 1905, 503). In a vast pre-modern state such as the Roman empire, where it may have taken weeks, if not months, for news from remote border regions to reach Constantinople, the notion of urgency is likely to have been quite different when compared to ours. Already in the first years of his reign, Justin had been invested in the debate with the Severans and it is thus imaginable that he indeed received the bishops in person in 571 (see 3.1.21.a).

The exact location of the thermal bath Justin visited is unknown, yet it was evidently outside Constantinople (see also 3.1.27.a). Most probably, he traveled to the popular spa resort in Pythia (modern Yalova on the south-east coast of the Propontis), where there was also a palace built by Justinian, as stated in Procopius, Buildings 5.3.16–20 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 4:156.10–22). Imperial visits to these baths were a major affair; Malalas, Chronicle 18.25 records that empress Theodora relocated there in the late summer of 528 or 529 with supposedly 4000 attendants (Thurn 2000, 368.49–52; Mango and Scott 1997, 285–86). Just like Theodora, Justin may have traveled there in summer to spend parts of the hot season outside the capital, yet the hot springs in Pythia may also have allowed for bathing trips in cooler parts of the year. John also mentions the baths of Pythia in EH 3.2.46.

Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:302.a.14–24; 4:342.a.33–40 includes a brief summary. He states that Justin attempted to console the Severan bishops and that he promised to work for the creation of a lasting union after his visit to the baths.

div.head: EH 3.1.26.e (SR; PMF) 36.28–37.1
div.p: ܘܬܗܘܐ ܟܠܗ̇ ܥܕܬܐ ܚ̣ܕܐ܆ ܘܢܚ̣ܕܐ ܟܠܢ “the whole church will be one, and we will all rejoice!”: John of Ephesus creates here a play on words between “one” (ܚ̣ܕܐ) ḥḏā and “we will rejoice” (ܢܚ̣ܕܐ) neḥdē. By doing so, he further highlights his conviction that the ecclesiastical union that will truly bring peace for the whole imperial church needs to be based on the one-nature belief of the opponents of Chalcedon.

Both Schönfelder (1862, 27) and Brooks (1936, 25.22–23) understand the verb “we will rejoice” (ܢܚ̣ܕܐ) neḥde to be “it [i.e., the church] will unite.” There are a couple of problems with this interpretation. First, the diacritical dot found below the verb ܢܚ̣ܕܐ implies it should be understood as a pʿal form, when the meaning of “unite” has only been suggested for the paʿʿel and ethpaʿʿal forms, apparently derived from the word “one” (ܚܕ) ḥad Brockelmann 1928, 215 Sokoloff 2009, 415. Second, the two instances of the verb meaning “unite” identified by Brockelmann and Sokoloff have been called into question and even deemed “simply incorrect” (Hillers et al. 1986). For these reasons, we rather understand John to be playing on the consonance between these words, drawing together and combining their different shades of meaning.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: The absence of Justin II from Constantinople because of his visit to the baths led to an interruption in the talks about the union. John of Ephesus describes here how patriarch John Scholasticus cunningly intercepted the returning emperor and how he put yet again all the blame on the Severan bishops. Emperor and patriarch, so John asserts, then made a register of important cities in need of a bishop, offering to the Severan bishops that they could distribute these bishoprics among themselves. They did this in the hope that they might thus finally tempt John of Ephesus and his peers into a lasting union. However, the Severan bishops apparently immediately rejected this offer, emphasizing that they would never agree to a union unless Chalcedon were finally condemned. This reaction greatly angered the emperor.

The chapter forms a part of John’s apology for why he had briefly entered into Chalcedonian communion, as he narrated in EH 3.1.24, and his insistence that he and his peers always demanded an explicit condemnation of Chalcedon is not trustworthy (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f). The real issue which prompted the Severan bishops to withdraw from the union may well have been the patriarch’s sudden decision to have them reordained (see 3.1.12.f; 3.1.16.d; 3.1.18.b); emperor and patriarch might then have tried to win them back by offering them important episcopal sees. John himself admits in EH 3.2.2–3.2.4 that the condemnation of Chalcedon was not as crucial as he claims, because most of his peers eventually gave in and returned to joint communion.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:302.a.25–303.a.7; 4:342.a.40–343.b.9: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.27.h Chronicle to 1234 2.36 Chabot, Abouna, and Fiey 1916–1974, 2:253.6–13; 4:190.13–20: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.27.h

div.head: EH 3.1.27.a (SR) 37.6–7
div.p: ܒܬܪ ܕܝܢ ܬܠܬܝܢ ܝܘ̈ܡܝܢ܆ ܗܦ̣ܟ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܡܢ ܚܡ̇ܝܡܬܐ ܐܝܟ ܕܐܫܬ̇ܘܕܝ “after thirty days the emperor returned from the thermal bath as he had promised”: Justin II announced in 3.1.26.d that he would be gone for twenty or thirty days. John of Ephesus thus presents the emperor positively as someone who kept his word (see 3.1.5.e; 3.1.11.e; 3.1.19.n) – the very opposite of patriarch John Scholasticus and his advisors who, in EH 3.1.24, broke their alleged promise about the condemnation of Chalcedon.

The fact that Justin, returning from the baths, intended to cross the Bosphorus from Chalcedon further supports the view that he had visited the thermal bath at Pythia, as this lay on the Asian shore of the Propontis. It would have made no sense for him to cross the Bosphorus, had he gone to a bath on the European side, for instance Anchialus (modern Pomorie in Bulgaria) on the Black Sea coast, another popular spa resort visited, among others, by Ino Anastasia, the wife of emperor Tiberius (r. 578–582) (see the discussion of the testimony to EH 3.6.49 in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.21 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:362.b.25–363.b.16; 4:380.b.25–381.a.8).

div.head: EH 3.1.27.b (SR) 37.9
div.p: ܕܒ̣ܪ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܒ̈ܢܝ ܡܠ̣ܟܗ̣ ܘܢܦ̣ܩ ܠܘܬܗ “the patriarch John led his advisors and went out to him”: The ܒ̈ܢܝ ܡܠ̣ܟܗ̣ (“advisors”) of John Scholasticus belonged to the patriarchal synhedrion (see 3.1.19.h and 3.1.23.b). In EH 3.1.19.i, John of Ephesus already disqualified this body as being full of “semi-Nestorians” and as not really interested in achieving a union when he narrated how its members were appalled by the amendments proposed by the Severan bishops. In this chapter, John again has the patriarch and his council act against the union (but see 3.1.5.e for evidence that the patriarch originally sincerely labored for such a union). He describes how they intercepted the emperor in Chalcedon and turned him against the Severan bishops, irrespective of the fact that in EH 3.1.26, Justinhad declared that he would discuss the matter with them personally. Bunce (2023, 85–87) argues that John of Ephesus may have largely invented the interaction between bishops and emperor in EH 3.1.26 and between emperor and patriarch in EH 3.1.27. His view probably is too skeptical (see 3.1.26.d), but it is indeed likely that John chose to present the events in such a way that he could depict Justin once again as being sympathetic towards the Severans, yet being prevented from engaging with them more closely because of the Chalcedonian hardliners (see also 3.1.11.e; 3.1.11.g; 3.1.19.m). Bunce (2023, 85–87) also thinks that the emperor attempted to mediate between the two sides, and that this mediation process was flawed because the patriarch and his advisors were granted privileged access to Justin. However, it seems misleading to understand Justin’s role merely as that of a mediator – after all, he did not have a neutral position, but was very much invested in the debate (see 3.1.21.a). Ever since his accession to the throne, he had labored for a union (see 3.1.4.d) and therefore, it is not surprising that he wanted to ensure that the union finally achieved in EH 3.1.24.l would be saved.

div.head: EH 3.1.27.c (SR) 37.11–12
div.p: ܕܗܠܝܢ ܠܡ̣ ܦܣ̣ܩܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܡܢܢ. ܘܗܦ̣ܟܘ ܡܢ ܥܕܬܐ̣ ܠܓܡ̣ܪ “they have cut themselves off from us and have entirely turned away from the church”: When describing the accusations against the Severan bishops that the patriarch and his advisors made before the emperor, John of Ephesus deliberately creates the impression that they were not interested in understanding why the Severans had recanted. John Scholasticus puts the blame for the failure of the short-lived union achieved in EH 3.1.24.l solely on the Severans; there is no self-criticism and no mention of the broken promise about the condemnation of Chalcedon (see 3.1.24.g). By having them not even discuss the problem which Chalcedon posed for the Severans, John of Ephesus presents the Chalcedonians as opponents of a credible and lasting doctrinal solution and as morally bankrupt – they hope to end the debate by bribing the Severan bishops with material benefits in the form of important and wealthy episcopal sees (see 3.1.27.d).

div.head: EH 3.1.27.d (SR) 37.13–15
div.p: ܘܥܒ̣ܕܘܢ ܓ̣ܢܘܣܝܘܣ ܕܡ̈ܕܝܢܬܐ ܪ̈ܘܪܒܬܐ̣ ܘܫܕ̣ܪܘ ܠܗܘܢ ܒܝܕ ܩ̣ܐܣܛܘܪ “they made a register of all the large cities and sent to them through the quaestor”: John Scholasticus and Justin II hope to convince the Severan bishops to save the union achieved on the basis of Justin’s edict by offering them important episcopal sees. In the narration as John of Ephesus presents it, he and his peers recanted because the Chalcedonians broke their promise and did not condemn the council. However, as stated in the notes to 3.1.20.f and 3.1.24.j, this is likely a part of John’s apology and the Severan bishops were ready to agree to the edict and to enter into a union. Their eventual withdrawal may rather have been connected to the patriarch’s attempts to have them reordained (see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.18.b). It appears that this sudden demand also took Justin by surprise since he accused John Scholasticus of having destroyed the union with his actions (EH 3.1.28.b), and he may have tried to save it with an offer of wealthy bishoprics (Roggo 2022, 103–108).

Such an offer, born out of despair when facing the failure of the long-fought-for union, is indeed imaginable: at a later point, after the trial of the Severan bishops described in EH 3.1.29, Stephen returned to the Chalcedonian communion and was rewarded with an episcopal see in Cyprus (in all likelihood, he became archbishop of Salamis; see EH 3.1.16.c; 3.1.16.h; 3.2.3.d). Similarly, according to EH 3.2.3.i, John Scholasticus suggested to make Paul of Antioch (another Severan bishop who eventually returned) bishop of either Jerusalem or Thessalonica, the metropolitan thrones of both cities being vacant at the moment. Admittedly, these instances are not quite the same as the extremely generous offer described here, where the bishops apparently could even choose themselves their future bishopric, and we may doubt if they really enjoyed such a degree of freedom (Destephen 2008, 513). It is evident that at the very least, the episcopal sees listed on the register had to be vacant. Sticking to the letter of the law, the offer of new bishoprics was uncanonical since Canon 15 of Nicaea forbade the translation of bishops from one city to another (Alberigo 2006, I:27–28). Yet such translations nevertheless happened quite frequently in late antiquity, and the Chalcedonian authorities may have attempted to integrate the Severan episcopal hierarchy into their own ranks with this offer (see 3.1.14.f). John of Ephesus, himself one of the potential beneficiaries, does not seem to have regarded this practice as problematic.

The quaestor conveying the register to the bishops was Anastasius, whom John introduced in EH 3.1.19.j as one of those Chalcedonian hardliners (“Nestorians and semi-Nestorians”) who were heavily opposed to the amendments to the edict. The word ܓ̣ܢܘܣܝܘܣ is a loanword taken over from the Greek γνῶσις; while the meaning of “register” or “inventory” it has here is attested, for instance, in Greek texts such as the Life of John the Almsgiver Festugière 1974, 355.19 see Preisigke 1925, 1:302 for the papyrological evidence, John of Ephesus seems to be the sole late antique witness to it in Syriac (Brockelmann 1928, 126). It is probable that John’s use of this word reflects official terminology, i.e. the register he and the other bishops received was indeed called γνῶσις by the quaestor Anastasius.

div.head: EH 3.1.27.e (SR; HL) 37.16–17
div.p: ܘܒ̇ܥܝܢ ܚܢܢ ܕܣܘ̣ܢܩܪܛܝܣܐ ܢܥܒ̣ܕ ܠܟܘܢ “we want to offer you our steadfast support”: The phrase ܣܘ̣ܢܩܪܛܝܣܐ ܢܥܒ̣ܕ is an example of the so-called “light verb strategy” in Syriac (Butts 2016a, 112–116, with this example on page 113). This describes the process when a Greek verb (here, most probably, συγκρατῆσαι) is taken over into Syriac (ܣܘ̣ܢܩܪܛܝܣܐ), but instead of being conjugated itself, is combined with an auxiliary verb, most often ܥܒܕ (“to do”), which carries the morphological flexion (see EH 3.1.16.f for another example). Brooks (1936, 26 n.3) indicated that the Greek verb in question might be either συγκρατῆσαι (“to hold together”; “to strengthen”) or συγκροτῆσαι (“to strike together”; “to forge”; “to unite”). There are two arguments in favor of the former.

First, for συγκροτῆσαι, the spelling ܣܘ̣ܢܩܪܘܛܝܣܐ would also be possible, with waw after riš, and maybe even slightly more likely. John of Ephesus frequently employs plene spellings, incorporating a mater lectionis to represent vowels when possible. (We are grateful to Aaron Butts for this insight). Second, the corresponding noun ܣܘ̣ܢܩܪܛܣܝܣ (συγκράτησις) appears in EH 3.3.7, where Tiberius rejects a proposal to separate from his wife Ino to marry a woman from Justin’s family. He states that Ino had, by marrying him, “strengthened” (ܣܘܢܩܪܛܣܝܣ ܥܒܕܬ) him when he was weak, prior to his exaltation to the imperial throne. Similarly, there is a clear focus on the offer of support extended to the Severan bishops in the present passage. The imperial offer appeals to the bishops’ honor and aims to entice them with attractive episcopal appointments in renowned cities, thus strengthening their position. John of Ephesus seems to be the only Syriac author to employ this loanword (Brockelmann 1928, 486). It is possible that the message the quaestor conveyed to the bishops contained the verb συγκρατῆσαι and that John included it for this reason.

div.head: EH 3.1.27.f (SR) 37.27–38.3
div.p: ܕܚܢ̣ܢ ܠܘ ܡܛܘܠ ܕܢܗܘܐ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܒܡ̈ܕܝܢܬܐ ܕܒ̇ܚܝܢܢ ܢܦܫ̈ܬܢ: ܐܡ̣ܪ ܠܗ ܠܙܟܝ̇ܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ܆ ܐܠܐ̣ ܥܠ ܣ̣ܒܪܐ ܕܫܘ̈ܘ̇ܕܝܐ ܕܚܕܝܘܬܐ “as for us, we are not sacrificing ourselves so that we might be bishops over cities – tell the victorious emperor this! – but rather in the hope of the promises of the union”: In the Severan bishops’ reply to the quaestor, John of Ephesus re-uses arguments he put in the mouth of the bishops several times in EH 3.1.19–3.1.24. He asserts that he himself and his peers only agreed to taking communion twice so as to counter the Chalcedonians’ slander that they were completely opposed to a union, and only after having received the promise that the council would be condemned (see 3.1.21.c; 3.1.22.a; 3.1.22.f; 3.1.24.b; 3.1.24.g; 3.1.24.l). He repeats that a true union can only be achieved if Chalcedon is removed from the church. Yet it is doubtful whether the Severan bishops were indeed as outspoken against Chalcedon in 571 as John claims here. Most likely, all these arguments are part of his apology for why he entered into union with the Chalcedonians, and the bishops might initially have been inclined to accept Justin’s edict despite the absence of a condemnation of Chalcedon (see 3.1.20.f and 3.1.24.j). Similarly, John’s insistence that the bishops would never again take communion with the Chalcedonians is strongly exaggerated, undoubtedly for the same apologetic purpose: at the start of Book 2, written a few years later in 577/578 Roggo 2022, 104 van Ginkel 1995, 73, John reveals that most of his peers eventually returned to the union without Chalcedon being condemned (EH 3.2.3–3.2.4).
For the title of “victorious emperor” (ܙܟܝ̇ܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ), see EH 3.1.4.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.27.g (NK) 38.4
div.p: ܐܠܐ ܐܢ ܡܫܬܡܠܝܢ “unless […] are fulfilled”: A scribe added a note at this point. The manuscript’s main text is corrected between the two columns by the same hand – framed in red – to ܐܝܟ ܕܢܫܬܠܡܘܢ (“so that [the promises] might be fulfilled”). Brooks (1935, 38 n.4) accepted this correction, whereas Cureton (1853, 42.4) used the uncorrected wording. If we follow Cureton’s reading, John of Ephesus introduces two subsequent subordinate clauses with ܐܢ, resulting in a parallel structure: ܘܐܦܢ ܠܐ ܫ̇ܘܝܢܢ and ܐܢ ܡܫܬܡܠܝܢ. However, it seems that the scribe tried to correct this construction with the difficult to understand wording. He changed ܐܢ to ܐܝܟ ܕ and the verb to the imperfect ܢܫܬܠܡܘܢ. This intervention does not seem necessary, as it makes the connection between the two subordinate clauses less intelligible. We follow the lectio difficilior, assuming that the correction in the margin does not reflect the archetypal text.

div.head: EH 3.1.27.h (SR) 38.15–17
div.p: ܘܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ ܪܓ̣ܙ ܘܐܬܬ̣ܥܝܪ ܒܚܡ̣ܬܐ ܪܒܬܐ܆ ܘܡܬ̇ܠܚܡ ܗܘܐ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܘܓ̇ܙܡ ܩܫ̣ܝܐܝܬ܆ ܗ̇ܝ̣ ܕܐܦ ܣܥ̣ܪ ܒܚܪܬܐ “this one also became angry and was awakened in a great rage, intimidating them and making harsh threats, which he eventually carried out”: As the following chapters EH 3.1.28 and 3.1.29 make clear, Justin’s anger was not only directed at the Severan bishops for their refusal to save the union and his edict, but also at patriarch John Scholasticus, presumably because his demand for reordinations had caused the withdrawal of the Severans from the union (see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.18.b). The “harsh threats eventually carried out” refer to the trial of the Severan bishops, their renewed separation from each other, and their imprisonment described in EH 3.1.29.f.

In both the Chronicle of Michael the Syrian Chabot 1899–1910, 2:302.a.25–303.a.7; 4:342.a.40–343.b.9 and the Chronicle to 1234 (Chabot, Abouna, and Fiey 1916–1974, 2:253.6–13; 4:190.13–20), there are summaries of this chapter. Both chronicles state that a register of available bishoprics was sent to the bishops and that they rejected it, insisting on the condemnation of Chalcedon, which caused the anger of the emperor. But only Michael includes John of Ephesus’s information that John Scholasticus and his advisors intercepted the emperor when he was returning to Constantinople and accused the Severan bishops because of their withdrawal from the union.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This short chapter describes the reaction of Justin II when being informed that the Severan bishops had rejected his attempt to bring them back into communion with the promise of wealthy bishoprics (EH 3.1.27). The emperor’s anger was directed not only at the recalcitrant bishops, but also at patriarch John Scholasticus. Justin accused him of having caused the failure of the union that had been achieved for a short time (see EH 3.1.24.l). John of Ephesus does not specify in his account how exactly the patriarch had done this, but this may well be connected to the attempts to have the Severan bishops reordained (see EH 3.1.12.f and 3.1.18.b). The emperor ordered that senatorial judges were to assemble in the patriarchal residence and to examine everything that had happened between the Severans and John Scholasticus.

div.head: EH 3.1.28.a (SR) 38.18–21
div.p: ܥܠ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܟܕ ܗܠܝܢ ܐܬ̣ܝܕܥ ܠܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܘܐܬ̣ܚܡܬ̇܆ ܦܩ̣ܕ ܕܢܥ̣ܠܘܢ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܪ̈ܝܫ̣ܢܐ܇ ܘܢܬ̣ܬܕܝܢܘܢ ܐܦܝܣ̈ܩܘܦܐ ܒܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܝܘܢ “on the emperor – having come to know these things and having become enraged – commanding all the high-ranking men to enter and the bishops to be judged in the episcopal palace”: The title does not agree with the actual content of the chapter. It is not, or at least not solely, the Severan bishops who are to be judged, but patriarch John Scholasticus. The tension between title and content visible here suggests that (some of) the titles of the EH were not written by John of Ephesus himself, but rather by a later user or copyist of his work. (However, some titles probably are from John, see 3.2.11.a; on the question of the authorship of the titles, see the introduction). While there are no other examples in the EH of a title rendering the content of a chapter as inaccurately as here (see also 3.1.40.c), there is sometimes a certain lexicographical variation between title and chapter. In the example here, those commanded by the emperor to act as judges in the episcopal palace are called ܪ̈ܝܫ̣ܢܐ (“high-ranking men”) in the title, but ܣܘܢܩ̣ܠܝܛܘܣ (“senators”) in the text. The author of the title may have wanted to avoid the loanword ܣܘܢܩ̣ܠܝܛܘܣ (“senators”). As for the content of the title, he may have been misled by the fact that it is the Severan bishops, and not the patriarch, who are sentenced to a renewed period of incarceration in the subsequent chapter EH 3.1.29.f. The discrepancy between title and content has also caused some confusion in modern scholarship: when summarizing John’s account on the debate about the ecclesiastical union, Destephen (2008, 513) appears to have followed the title rather than the actual text of this chapter. In his understanding, the angry Justin ordered both senators and patriarch to judge the Severan bishops, obliging the patriarch to condemn them if they were found guilty. Yet this interpretation is at odds with the text of the chapter, which clearly states that it was the patriarch who was to be judged.

div.head: EH 3.1.28.b (SR) 38.22–24
div.p: ܫ̣ܕܪ ܩ̣ܪܝܗܝ ܠܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ ܕܡܕܝܢܬܐ܆ ܘܦܓ̣ܥ ܒܗ ܩܫ̣ܝܐܝܬ̣ ܒܨܥܪܐ ܩܫ̣ܝܐ “he sent for and summoned the patriarch of the city and met him harshly with harsh words of contempt”: At the end of the preceding chapter EH 3.1.27, John of Ephesus stated that Justin became angry when the Severan bishops rejected the register of important episcopal sees he had sent them (see 3.1.27.h). The bishops were indeed punished for their refusal to restore the union, as the subsequent chapter EH 3.1.29 makes clear (see 3.1.29.e and 3.1.29.f). However, the emperor’s anger appears not to have been exclusively directed at the bishops, but also at patriarch John Scholasticus. Justin blames the patriarch for the failure of the short-lived union, but does not specify what mistake he had made. Bearing in mind John of Ephesus’s account in the previous chapters, the reader is led to believe that this mistake may have been the breach of the patriarch’s supposed promise that he would condemn Chalcedon (see EH 3.1.24.n). Yet this explanation remains unconvincing and the historicity of that promise is very dubious (see 3.1.24.g). Justin always upheld the orthodoxy of Chalcedon, and while he may have thought it an elegant solution to simply not mention the contentious council in his edict on faith, an explicit condemnation was never an option for him, not least because of the consequences this would have had for the relationship between Constantinople and the western churches (see 3.1.20.f). For this reason, he can hardly have been angry at John Scholasticus for not condemning Chalcedon.

A more probable alternative explanation for the imperial anger with John Scholasticus are the reordinations of Severan clerics the patriarch had suddenly demanded after a prolonged period of liturgical concelebrations (EH 3.1.12–3.1.18). As argued in 3.1.18.b and 3.1.24.l, it is quite likely that John of Ephesus distorted the order of events in Book 1 and that John Scholasticus only insisted on reordinations after the Severan bishops had entered into communion on the basis of Justin’s edict. It is possible that John Scholasticus did not demand the reordinations voluntarily, but was rather compelled to do so in order to stabilize his challenged position at the head of the imperial church (see 3.1.12.h). Roggo (2022, 103–110) has suggested that by subjecting the Severan clerics to reordination, the patriarch may have hoped to counter the rising inner-ecclesiastical criticism directed against him from the part of intransigent Chalcedonians because he had supported Justin’s edict which did not explicitly embrace Chalcedon. If this hypothesis is correct, this means that the sudden patriarchal change in the treatment of the Severans prompted them to withdraw from the joint communion, and this may be the reason why Justin perceived John Scholasticus as responsible for the failure of the union.

div.head: EH 3.1.28.c (PMF; NK) 38.26
div.p: ܐܫܬ̇ܘܕܝܘ ܕܢܫܬ̇ܘܬܦܘܢ ܠܢ “promised to commune with us”: Rather than the verb ܐܫܬܘܕܝܘ ʾeštawdiw “promised,” the manuscript has ܐܫܬܘܕܥܘ ʾeštawdaʿ[w], which usually is translated as “to know, recognize, understand, see, perceive” (J. Payne Smith 1903, 188). Brooks (1936, 26 n.6) suggested emending this word in his translation. There are two major arguments in favor of the emendation. The first relates to the grammatical construction found in this passage. The verb in question is immediately followed by the particle ܕ d and a verb in the imperfect: ܕܢܫܬ̇ܘܬܦܘܢ “to commune.” The verb ܐܫܬܘܕܝ ʾeštawdi regularly appears with this construction: see, for example, EH 3.4.6: ܘܐܫܬ̣̇ܘܕܝܬ̇ ܕܟܠܡܕܡ ܬ̇ܣܥ̣ܘܪ and EH 3.4.41: ܘܐܫܬ̇ܘܕܝܘ ܕܢܬ̣ܠܘܢ. By way of contrast, the verb ܐܫܬܘܕܥ ʾeštawdaʿ is not attested with this construction in the EH, where it occurs only once more in EH 3.6.29. But there, the verb is followed by an object suffix: ܠܐ ܐܫܬ̇ܘܕܥܘܗܝ “they did not recognize him.” The second argument in favor of this emendation is that it only requires that a scribe mistook the letter ܝ for ܥ; this would be a simple copying error. With these two arguments, Brooks’s suggested emendation seems reasonable and can be adopted.

div.head: EH 3.1.28.d (SR; PMF; HL) 38.27–39.2
div.p: ܦܩ̣ܕ ܕܬܥ̣ܘܠ ܣܘܢܩ̣ܠܝܛܘܣ ܠܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܝܘܢ܆ ܘܢܬܬ̣ܕܝܢܘܢ ܣ̣ܘܥܪ̈ܢܐ ܕܗܘ̣ܘ ܒܝܢܬܗܘܢ. ܟܕ ܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ̣ ܢܬ̣ܕܝܢ. ܘܐܢ ܡ̇ܟܣܝܢ ܠܗ ܢܗܘܐ ܡ̣ܚܝܒ ܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ “he ordered that the senators enter the episcopal palace so that the matters that had come between them would be adjudicated with the patriarch also being judged and, if they found him culpable, condemned as well”: ܣܘܢܩ̣ܠܝܛܘܣ can be rendered as “senate” or “senator(s)” (Brockelmann 1928, 486). It comes from the Greek σύγκλητος and is a loanword very frequently attested in Syriac (Butts 2016a, 106). Although there were special courthouses, trials could take place anywhere, even in private houses. Given the patriarch’s direct involvement in the debate with the Severan bishops, it does not come as a surprise that his palace was chosen for the trial. What is peculiar is the composition of the court, which, as is specified in EH 3.1.29.a, did not only consist of senators, but also of the quaestor Anastasius. Members of the senate and the imperial consistorium (an advisory body comprising the highest state officials including the quaestor) seem to have met occasionally to discuss judicial matters. This institution was called the silentium cum conventu, as attested in Justinian, Novels 62.1; 124.1 Miller and Sarris 2018, 472–473 and 829–830 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 332–333 and 625–626; Malalas, Chronicle 18.22 (Thurn 2000, 367). It seems possible that a silentium cum conventu gathered for the trial here too. In EH 3.1.29, John of Ephesus may have omitted the other members of the consistorium and focused exclusively on the quaestor Anastasius because of his enmity towards the Severans, which he had already highlighted in EH 3.1.19.j and 3.1.27.d.

Throughout Book 1, John of Ephesus puts all the blame for the hardship he and his peers suffered on patriarch John Scholasticus. At the same time, he is eager to present Justin II more positively and ascribes his anti-Severan actions to the patriarch’s bad influence (see 3.1.5.e; 3.1.11.e; 3.1.19.n). In line with this observation, it is possible that John of Ephesus’s claim here that Justin ordered John Scholasticus to be judged is ahistorical and serves to underline yet again the depraved nature of the patriarch who even incurred the wrath of the emperor. It is indeed interesting that the subsequent chapter EH 3.1.29 focuses completely on the trial of the Severan bishops without any mention of John Scholasticus being judged. Nevertheless, we cannot exclude that Justin’s anger at John Scholasticus (most likely because of the reordinations; see 3.1.28.b) led to a thorough examination of the interactions between the Severans and the patriarch where both sides had to defend themselves. The fact that in EH 3.1.29, John of Ephesus only mentions the trial of himself and his peers is not surprising as he was mainly interested in highlighting that he remained a committed anti-Chalcedonian and gladly took punishment upon himself for the defense of the true faith. Furthermore, there is another passage in the EH which suggests that the Severan bishops’ withdrawal from the union might have strained the relationship between emperor and patriarch for a longer period: EH 3.2.3.i asserts that at a later point in the early 570s, John Scholasticus was afraid lest he be deposed and replaced by Paul of Antioch, who had eventually returned to the Chalcedonian communion (Roggo 2022, 107–108). While we cannot know if this was a real possibility or just another slander made up by John against the patriarch, it does make sense to assume that Justin wanted to hold John Scholasticus accountable for the failure of the union for which he had labored ever since his accession in 565. Yet there is no reason to doubt that the board of judges consisting of, among others, the fervently anti-Severan quaestor Anastasius acquitted the patriarch, if they were indeed asked to judge him.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This chapter forms the end of John of Ephesus’s apologetic account about the renewed suppression of the opponents of Chalcedon, the edict on faith, and the enforced ecclesiastical union. The chain of events described in EH 3.1.3–3.1.29 started on 21 March 571 (but see 3.1.5.d; 3.1.18.n; 3.1.23.a for a possible earlier start date) and must have lasted for at least a couple of months (EH 3.1.12.d and 3.1.18.n mention thirty-six joint liturgical celebrations; EH 3.1.23.a thirty-three days of debate about the edict; EH 3.1.27.a thirty days of absence of Justin II).

In EH 3.1.24–3.1.28, John of Ephesus asserts that the Severan bishops were lured into Chalcedonian communion, that they eventually recanted, and that they rejected a generous offer to win them back. The failure of the union caused the emperor’s anger and in the present chapter, John records that in accordance with the imperial command (see EH 3.1.28.d), the senatorial judges and the quaestor Anastasius gathered in the patriarchal palace to pass judgment on the bishops. John does not tell if they also judged patriarch John Scholasticus (see 3.1.28.d); he completely focuses on the trial against himself and the other Severan bishops. He asserts that he and his peers again rejected the Chalcedonian communion, anathematized the Chalcedonians, and denounced their duplicity. As a result, the judges sentenced the bishops to imprisonment in various places, separate from each other. John presents this as the end of the whole affair, highlighting that he and his peers had remained unshaken in their anti-Chalcedonian conviction and preferred punishment over further dealings with the Chalcedonians. Yet his claim that the bishops were permanently separated from each other and precluded that they would ever again take communion with the Chalcedonians is clearly exaggerated as a part of his apology to his co-religionists in the east (see 3.1.17.j; 3.1.20.f; 3.1.24.j). Contrary to what John claims here, EH 3.2.3–3.2.4 show that some bishops returned to the Chalcedonian communion quite quickly and were allowed to visit John of Ephesus in prison.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:303.a.7–19; 4:343.b.9–17: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.29.e

div.head: EH 3.1.29.a (SR) 39.6–8
div.p: ܟܕ ܓܝܪ ܐܝܟ ܦܘܩܕܢܐ ܠܝܘܡܐ ܕܡ̣ܚܪ܆ ܥܠ̣ܘ ܡܢ ܣ̣ܘܢܩܠܝܛܘܣ ܥܡ ܩ̣ܐܣܛܘܪ ܠܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܝܘܢ: ܘܐܬܩ̣ܪ̈ܝܘ ܐܦܝܣ̈ܩܘܦܐ ܠܕܝܢ̣ܐ “for when, in accordance with the order, on the next day some senators along with the quaestor entered the episcopal palace and the bishops were summoned to trial”: John of Ephesus first introduced Justin’s order that senators should convene in the patriarchal palace in EH 3.1.28.d; the court probably took the form of a silentium cum conventu, consisting of both senators and high state officials. As noted by Bunce (2023, 87–89), the composition of the court is thus clearly different from the patriarchal consistory/synhedrion with whom the Severan bishops debated about the union in EH 3.1.23.b. According to EH 3.1.28.d, the senators were tasked to examine the interactions between John Scholasticus and the Severan bishops and even to judge the patriarch himself. Yet there is no mention anymore of proceedings against John Scholasticus in this chapter. The account is solely focused on the Severan bishops and their resistance against Chalcedon, although the patriarch might also have had to defend himself. Possibly, John of Ephesus deliberately omitted the parts of the trial that were not directed against the Severans, thus creating a black-and-white contrast between Chalcedonian oppressors and unjustly sentenced Severans who were valiantly upholding orthodoxy against all odds.

div.head: EH 3.1.29.b (SR) 39.8–10
div.p: ܟܕ ܡܬ̇ܬܒܥܝܢ ܐܝܟ ܕܡܢ ܦܘܩܕܢܐ܆ ܕܢܟ̇ܬܪ̈ܘܢ ܒܫܘܬ̇ܦܘܬܐ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܕܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ܆ ܟܕ ܫܠ̣ܝܢ ܘܠܐ ܬܒ̇ܥܝܢ ܡܕܡ ܐܚܪܝܢ “when they were required by order to remain in communion with the synodites, keeping silence and without making any further request”: The order that the Severan bishops remain in communion on the basis of Justin’s edict, to which they had agreed for a short time in EH 3.1.24.l), marks the last formal attempt to save the union. In all likelihood, the Chalcedonians were under no illusions as to the prospects of success of this order: the attempts at reordaining the Severan clergy had heavily shaken the Severans’ trust in the Chalcedonian authorities, hence their subsequent withdrawal from the union. The damage was such that they had even rejected the offer of wealthy bishoprics (EH 3.1.27.d and 3.1.27.f), and it was only after renewed incarceration that some of them would return to the Chalcedonian communion (EH 3.2.3–3.2.4).

Bunce (2023, 87–89) understands the process against the Severan bishops as inherently flawed because, according to John of Ephesus’s account, the bishops were ordered to remain in communion without any previous hearing of their case and because they were in the end violently dragged from the court (see 3.1.29.e). However, this is only how John of Ephesus presented the events, and the proceedings may have been more regular. After all, it is possible that John in this chapter deliberately omitted that the actions of the patriarch may also have been under scrutiny (see 3.1.29.a). John was mainly interested in depicting the Chalcedonians as persecutors without respect for proper juridical settings; in this way, he managed to portray himself and his peers as fighters for Severan orthodoxy, which was beneficial for his apology (see 3.1.24.j).

div.head: EH 3.1.29.c (SR; PMF) 39.11–15
div.p: ܗܝ̇ ܕܝܢ ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܩܡ̣ܘ ܘܐܬ̇ܟܬܫܘ ܒܐܓܘܢܐ ܚܝܠܬܢܐܝܬ ܘܓܢܒܪܐܝܬ܆ ܘܕܠܐ ܕܚ̣ܠܬܐ ܐ̇ܟ̣ܣܘ ܓܠܝ̣ܐܝܬ܇ ܥܠ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܫܘ̈ܘ̇ܕܝܐ ܕܢܟ̣ܠܐ܇ ܘܥܠ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܡ̇ܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܕܐܬ̣̈ܕܓܠܝ܇ ܘܥܠ ܫܪܪܐ ܕܐܬ̣ܛܠܡ ܘܐܬ̣ܕܝܫ܇ ܘܥܠ ܫܪܟܐ ܕܕܐܝܟ ܗܠܝܢ “at that time they arose and fought powerfully and valiantly in the struggle: Without any fear they openly decried all the deceitful promises, all the oaths that had been violated, the truth that had been rejected and trampled upon, and other similar things”: The “deceitful promises” (ܫܘ̈ܘ̇ܕܝܐ ܕܢܟ̣ܠܐ) and “oaths that had been violated” (ܡ̇ܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܕܐܬ̣̈ܕܓܠܝ) refer back to John Scholasticus’s alleged promise that he would condemn the council of Chalcedon once the Severan bishops had entered into the union (EH 3.1.24.g). There is much to suggest that there never was such a promise, but John of Ephesus and his peers may have understood the sudden attempts to reordain them after several joint liturgies as a betrayal and as the breach of the terms on which they had agreed to enter into union (see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.20.f).

John describes the bold actions of the Severan bishops in terms of an athletic contest, which is gendered as masculine especially through the adverb ܓܢܒܪܐܝܬ ga[n]bbārāʾiṯ “manly.” John uses the loanword ܐܓܘܢܐ ʾaḡonā “struggle; contest” from Greek ἀγών, a term first used in athletic and legal contexts but then employed by Christian authors for martyrdom and spiritual struggle in general (Lampe 1961, 25). This vocabulary appears frequently in both the EH (e.g., 3.1.18; 3.1.25; 3.1.30; 3.2.11) and LES (for a concentration of the terms found in EH 3.1.29, see LES 23 (Brooks 1923, 304.4–12)), thus forming a link between John’s historiography and hagiography (see further Bruns 2006).

div.head: EH 3.1.29.d (SR) 39.15–19
div.p: ܕܒܚ̣ܪܡܐ ܕܟܠܗ̇ ܗܪܣܝܣ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܝ ܟܝ̈ܢܐ܆ ܓܠ̣ܝܐܝܬ ܠܣܘܢܩ̣ܠܝܛܘܣ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚܘ ܒܦܪܗܣܝܐ ܣܓܝܐܬܐ. ܐܝܟ ܕܒܫܘ̇ܪܝܐ ܘܒܡ̇ܨܥܬܐ ܘܐܦ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܝܢ ܒܚܪܬܐ̣ ܚܠܝ̣ܡܐܝܬ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚܘ܆ ܟܕ ܘܐܦ ܬܘܒ “by anathematizing the entire heresy of the dyophysites openly to the senate, they employed great boldness. Just as they acted in a firm manner at the beginning, the middle, and then at the end, so did they again”: John of Ephesus stresses again his fierce opposition to the Chalcedonians, thus creating the impression that he and his peers always fought against the council of Chalcedon and insisted on its removal from the church. Throughout his account about Justin’s edict and the debate this triggered in EH 3.1.19–3.1.25, John repeats time and again that he and the other bishops demanded a condemnation of the council. For instance, when narrating how the bishops took communion with the Chalcedonians, it was of paramount importance to John to highlight that they only did so whilst anathematizing Chalcedon (EH 3.1.24.l). Much of this insistence might be exaggerated and due to John’s desire to re-ingratiate himself with his co-religionists in the east after the failure of the union. Already a few years previously, in 567, the Severan bishops had been inclined to accept Justin’s first edict on faith even though it did not contain a condemnation of Chalcedon. In 571, they might have been similarly ready to enter into communion on the basis of the new edict with its careful neo-Chalcedonian definition of faith (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f).
On “boldness” (ܦܪܗܣܝܐ), a loanword from Greek παρρησία, see 3.1.5.b.

div.head: EH 3.1.29.e (SR) 39.22–24
div.p: ܥܕܡܐ ܠܐ̇ܝܟܐ ܕܢܬ̇ܚܡܬ ܪܘܓܙܗܘܢ ܕܣܘ̣ܢܩ̈ܠܝܛܩܘ ܘܕܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ ܥܠܝܗܘܢ ܕܐܦܝܣ̈ܩܦܐ܇ ܘܦ̣ܩܕܘܢ ܕܒܓ̇ܪܪܐ ܢ̇ܦܩܘܢ ܐܢܘܢ ܡܢ ܩܕܡܝܗܘܢ “until the point that the anger of the senators and the patriarch grew hot against the bishops and they issued an order that they be expelled and dragged away from their presence”: Three points are noticeable in this passage: (1) John of Ephesus does not ascribe any responsibility for the bishops’ renewed incarceration to Justin II. Yet the senators were not entitled to take a decision of this kind on their own. Justin needed at least to approve it – and we may assume that he had no other choice than to implement more forceful measures after the failure of the attempt to keep the Severan bishops in communion (EH 3.1.26–3.1.27); see 3.1.16.r. As usual in Book 1, John leads the focus away from the emperor and onto evil advisors, mainly John Scholasticus, who is presented as responsible for the hardship the Severan clergy had to endure (see 3.1.5.e; 3.1.11.e; 3.1.19.n). In the first two books of the EH, written before the death of Justin in October 578, John is always very careful not to criticize the emperor. This only changes in Book 3, written after 578, where he portrays Justin as a persecutor equal to patriarch John Scholasticus, on whom all the blame is put in Book 1 (van Ginkel 1995, 107–108).

(2) Patriarch John Scholasticus now appears as a member of the court who was judging the Severan bishops. The patriarch indeed belonged to the silentium, the meeting of the highest officials of state, who were probably conducting this trial together with some senators (see for instance Malalas, Chronicle 18.141 (Thurn 2000, 428.64–65)). Yet in the present case, his participation is somewhat puzzling, given that according to the previous chapter EH 3.1.28.d, it was actually he whom Justin ordered to be judged. One possibility for explaining this apparent inconsistency is the observation noted above in 3.1.29.a that the patriarch and the Severans may both have had to defend themselves. If this was the case, then the acquittal of John Scholasticus was hardly surprising, given that some judges were fervent Chalcedonians such as the quaestor Anastasius, and he might after his own trial have participated in that of the Severan bishops. Alternatively, it is also conceivable that the involvement of John Scholasticus in the trial of the Severans as recorded here is ahistorical. In that case, John of Ephesus may have chosen to include the patriarch in order to stress yet again that he was the one to blame for the persecution of the Severans, as he had already presented him in EH 3.1.5.e and 3.1.11.e. Michael the Syrian clearly accepted the EH’s presentation of the patriarch’s leading role because in his summary of this chapter, John Scholasticus is portrayed as solely responsible for the expulsion and imprisonment of the bishops Chabot 1899–1910, 2:303.a.7–19; 4:343.b.9–17.

(3) The sentence against the Severan bishops might parallel a unique account in the Melkite Chronicle (de Halleux 1977a, 302–306) about a synod of all the patriarchs and their representatives which took place in the early 570s and where the anathema on Severus of Antioch (and presumably his followers) was repeated. De Halleux argued to place this otherwise unattested synod (whose historicity may thus appear doubtful) in the context of the suppression of the Severans recorded by John of Ephesus. However, the attendance list of that synod as preserved in the Melkite Chronicle poses some problems since the patriarchs mentioned there did not all sit simultaneously. In particular, it states the participation of John IV of Jerusalem (c. 571/572–594), yet according to EH 3.2.3.i, the vacancy on the throne of Jerusalem only occurred during the new imprisonment of the Severan bishops. Consequently, if the indications in the Melkite Chronicle are correct, John IV cannot have been bishop of Jerusalem at the time of the trial recorded here, and the patriarchal synod, if historical at all, can only have happened at a later point (Roggo 2022, 115–117).

div.head: EH 3.1.29.f (SR; PMF) 39.26–29
div.p: ܘܢܦ̣ܩܘ̣ ܘܐܬܦ̣ܪ̈ܫܘ. ܘܬܘܒ ܠܚ̈ܕܕܐ̣ ܠܐ ܚ̣ܙܘ. ܘܐܫܬ̇ܕܪܘ ܠܐ̣ܟܣܘܪܝܐ. ܡܢܗܘܢ ܠܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ܆ ܘܡܢܗܘܢ ܠܓܙܪ̈ܬܐ ܕܝܡܐ܆ ܘܡܢܗܘܢ ܠܚ̣ܒ̈ܘܫܝܐ ܐ̈ܠܝ̣ܨܐ ܘܡ̇ܪ̈ܝܪܐ ܕܒܐܟ̣ܣ̈ܢܕܟܘܣ “they were expelled and were separated, and they did not see each other again. They were sent into exile, some to monasteries, some to islands of the sea, some to oppressive and bitter cells in hospices”: The new separation and imprisonment of the Severan bishops were already briefly anticipated at the end of EH 3.1.25.g, where John of Ephesus stated that he and his peers were eventually again separated from each other and incarcerated as a result of their withdrawal from the union. John’s claim that the bishops did not see each other again afterwards is clearly exaggerated and probably intended to serve as a dramatic rhetorical endpoint with which he wanted to close his apologetical account (see 3.1.30.d). Already in EH 3.2.3.e and 3.2.4.c, we learn that Stephen of Cyprus was able to confer with Paul of Antioch and Elisha of Sardis, irrespective of John’s assertion here – all three bishops appear to have returned to the Chalcedonian communion quite quickly and were therefore released. Furthermore, Stephen was even allowed to visit John of Ephesus in his prison in the hospice of Eubulus, where he was held for about a year after the trial described here, as is attested in EH 3.2.4.a.

Alternatively, the statement that they did not see each other again could be understood to suggest that John wrote the first thirty chapters of Book 1 already quite soon after his imprisonment in the hospice of Eubulus, before Stephen was able to visit him. Yet this is not very likely because at least one of those first thirty chapters, EH 3.1.16, refers to events that happened only later, after Stephen’s return to the Chalcedonian communion mentioned in EH 3.2.3 (see 3.1.16.d and 3.1.30.d). Consequently, unless we assume that EH 3.1.16 was inserted at a later point, possibly by someone other than John, this proves that John only wrote the first thirty chapters when he was well aware that the renewed period of incarceration was by no means definitive and that several of his peers had lapsed again (Roggo 2022, 103–104).

The list of different places of detention given here is reminiscent of the passage in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:293.b.5–12; 4:337.a.5–15 which probably stems from the lacuna in EH 3.1.5-8.b–3.1.5-8.g. On the use of monasteries for detaining clerics, see Hillner (2015, 281–341); a list of individual cases, including those in EH and LES, appears on the pages 380–386. EH <3.1.15.b and 3.2.2.c attest that Elisha of Sardis and Paul of Antioch were, possibly at the same time, relegated to the monastery of Abraham. Similarly, islands formed a common place of exile (Hillner 2015, 212 and 233). For a list of clergy exiled to islands, see the examples in the list of banished clergy on the pages 359–373. John of Ephesus himself was sent to an island for an exile of eighteen months after his confinement in the hospice of Eubulus in Constantinople, as noted in EH 3.2.7.c; Stephen of Cyprus suffered a similar fate (see EH 3.1.16.c).

The “hospices” (ܐܟ̣ܣ̈ܢܕܟܘܣ) are a loanword taken from the Greek ξενοδοχεῖα (singular: ξενοδοχεῖον). In the 6th century, the term xenodocheion usually indicates an ecclesiastical institution that served as a hostel, hospital, or both (Hiltbrunner 1991, 617–621). On the use of xenodocheia for detainment of clergy, see Hillner (2015, 283–293). For about a year, John of Ephesus himself was imprisoned in the hospice of Eubulus (see EH 3.2.7.a), located to the north-east of Hagia Sophia, close to Hagia Irene Janin 1969, 558; 1964, 348–349.

div.head: EH 3.1.29.g (SR) 39.30–40.1
div.p: ܘܕܠܐ ܢܬܝ̣ܗܒ ܠܗܘܢ ܐܢܫ ܡܢ ܕܝܠܗܘܢ ܐ̇ܘ ܡܢ ܐܚܪܝܢ ܠܡܚ̣ܙܐ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܚܕ ܡܢܗܘܢ “that it be granted neither to one of their own nor to anyone else to see one of them”: A similar complaint about the desolation of the imprisoned Severans is found in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:293.b.19–22; 4:337.a.23–27; this probably stems from the lacuna in EH 3.1.5-8.j. Moreover, also at the end of EH 3.1.23.h, John of Ephesus laments that the Severan bishops were separated from their friends and not allowed to receive any visitors.

div.head: EH 3.1.29.h (SR; PMF) 40.3–4
div.p: ܘܒܩܛ̣ܪ̈ܐ ܕܥ̣ܘܠܐ܇ ܐܝܟ ܕܥܠ ܩ̈ܛ̇ܘܠܐ “with the fetters of iniquity, as though against murderers”: The phrase “the fetters of iniquity” (ܩܛ̣ܪ̈ܐ ܕܥ̣ܘܠܐ) may draw on the language of Isaiah 58:6 where the same sequence of words appears in the Peshitta version. As stated in 3.1.5-8.b, the comparison of the treatment the bishops received in prison to that of murderers may lie at the root of Michael’s assertion in Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:293.b.6; 4:337.a.7–8 that the Severans were imprisoned together with murderers.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: In this chapter, John of Ephesus closes his account of EH 3.1.3–3.1.30 about the suppression of the Severans in 571. John wrote this account before 577; he added the remaining chapters of Book 1 at a later date in the period 577–581 (see 3.1.31.c and van Ginkel 1995, 73) (see 3.1.31.c and van Ginkel 1995, 73) (see 3.1.31.c and van Ginkel 1995, 73). John highlights the trustworthiness of his narrative. He asserts that he reported everything accurately and in good order since he had been an eyewitness to the events; moreover, he claims that he was completely unbiased and, despite his anti-Chalcedonian stance, did not distort the account in favor of his own doctrinal group. For this appeal to the well-known sine ira et studio motif in historiography, see 3.1.30.f. Evidently, John’s remarks about his supposed objectivity have to be taken with a grain of salt: he was anything but a neutral and uncommitted reporter; the main purpose of his writing was apologetic, and he presented the events and his own role in them accordingly (see Roggo (2022, 103–107) and the notes to 3.1.17.a; 3.1.17.j; 3.1.20.f; 3.1.24.j).

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
EH 3.1.30.a (HL) 40.5
div.p: ܪܝܫܐ ܕܬܠܬܝܢ “Chapter 30”: In this chapter, John of Ephesus defends himself against possible accusations of partiality and exaggeration. Even though the chapter is not a preface (prooemium), it takes up topoi from prefaces in classical literature (Lieberich 1898) which were also used by Greek authors of John’s time. However, it refers to an earlier, unpreserved section of the EH, possibly to the preface of the third part (see 3.1.30.g), and at the same time clearly closes a narrative unit, namely the report on the failure of Justin II’s negotiations for ecclesiastical union. It reinforces thus the impression that the first thirty chapters of Book 1 form a unit that could also stand alone.

Another apologetic reflection on John’s own role can be found in EH 3.2.50, where he defends his repetition of certain topics, and in EH 3.3.1, where he explains his treatment of Justin’s divine punishment Riad 1988, 82 Bunce 2023, 57–58. Three points, which also appear in other contemporary prooemia, deserve special attention: John claims special knowledge on the basis of his eyewitness status (3.1.30.c; cf. Procopius, Wars 1.1.3 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 1:5.7–11); John of Epiphanias (FHG iv.272–276), Frg. 1 (K. Müller 1868, 272); LES pr. (Brooks 1923, 3.2–4)). Furthermore, he claims neutrality and thus truthfulness for himself (3.1.30.f; also in EH 3.2.25; 3.3.22; 3.4.46; cf. Procopius, Wars 1.1.3–4 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 1:5.7–11)). Third, he rejects the charge of linguistic elegance (3.1.30.b). In doing so, he addresses a specific problem of ecclesiastical historiography, whereas classicizing authors strove for an elevated style; cf. LES pr. (Brooks 1923, 3.13–4.8); Socrates, EH 6.pr. 1–5 (Hansen 1995a, 310.4–17), who justifies the clear but simple style; cf. on the topos (Riad 1988, 135 and 225–226). The similarities in this passage with contemporary Greek authors do not prove dependence on them, but rather suggest that there was a common tradition (Riad 1988, 13) and that John had some familiarity with the conventions of ancient historiography.

div.head: EH 3.1.30.b (SR) 40.8–14
