div.p: ܟܒܪ ܕܝܢ ܢܣܬ̣ܒܪ̈ܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܕܐܬܐ̣ܡܪ ܘܐܬܟ̣ܬܒ ܠܡ̈ܠ̣ܝܛܝ ܒܐ̣ܝܕܥܬܐ ܪܡ̇ܬܐ: ܘܚ̇ܪ̈ܝܦܝ ܒܗܘܢܐ ܕܕܝ̇ܢܝܢ ܠܗܠܝܢ ܡ̈ܠܐ ܩ̇ܛܝܢܐܝܬ܆ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܕܐܬܐܡ̣ܪ ܘܐܬ̣ܟܬܒ ܡܢ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܐ̇ܟ̣ܬܒ܆ ܕܐܝܟ ܝܬܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܘܒܛܟ̣ܣܐ ܕܣ̇ܝܘܡܘܬܐ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܐܟ̣ܬܒ. ܐܝܟ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܒܨ̣ܘܒܬ ܡ̈ܠܐ ܐܝܟ ܕܡܢܗ ܐܬܟ̣ܬܒ ܗܠܝܢ “perhaps all the things that have been said and written will be deemed by those well-trained in lofty knowledge and swift-of-mind who judge such words sharply to be things said and written by the author superfluously and as the author’s exercise of the literary craft, as though it was in an embellished style that they were written by him”: It appears that this verbose, lengthy, and syntactically complicated sentence would actually have supported the critics against whom John of Ephesus argues here (cf. Booth forthcoming). Brooks (1936, 28 n.1) remarked “sic confuse currit clausula.” In general, John tends towards an intricate writing style and sometimes changes the construction in the middle of a grammatically complex sentence. The peculiarities of his style have been noted (and criticized) by van Ginkel 1995, 99–100 Harvey 1990, 30–31 and 40–42 Nebes 1990; Brooks 1936, iv.

div.head: EH 3.1.30.c (SR) 40.19–22
div.p: ܐܠܐ ܡܟܝܠ ܗܕܐ ܬܬܝ̣ܕܥ ܠܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܒܡܣܒ̣ܪܢܘܬܐ ܗܕܐ ܡܬ̇ܬܚܕܝܢ܆ ܕܡ̇ܟܬܒܢܐ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܟܠܗܝܢ܆ ܠܐ ܡ̇ܒܪܝ ܗܘܐ ܡܢܗ ܕܐܓܘܢܐ܆ ܘܠܐ ܪܚ̣ܝܩ ܗܘܐ ܡܢܗ ܕܬܟܬܘܫܐ “but it should now be known to those who hold to such an opinion that the author of all these things was not an outsider to the struggle and was not far from the battle”: John of Ephesus was, according to his own testimony, at the center of the events he describes in EH 3.1.3–3.1.29. As one of the leading Severan bishops in Constantinople, he, together with his episcopal colleagues, was imprisoned (EH 3.1.5), brought to the patriarchal residence (EH 3.1.17), presented with Justin’s edict on faith (EH 3.1.19), and involved in the debate leading to its eventual acceptance by the Severan bishops (EH 3.1.20–3.1.24). Together with the other bishops, he withdrew from the joint communion with the Chalcedonians (EH 3.1.25), probably after having faced the sudden demand for reordinations (EH 3.1.12–3.1.18), rejected Justin’s attempt to save the union (EH 3.1.27), and received a new prison sentence because of his refusal to remain permanently in Chalcedonian communion (EH 3.1.29) (Roggo 2022, 100–111). His own experience of all this, so John argues, should give credence to his account and silence his critics. Yet as repeatedly highlighted in the commentary to those chapters, John was undoubtedly quite selective in how he presented the events and his involvement in them, nor did he refrain from distorting the chronological order, particularly with regard to the reordinations (see 3.1.12.f; 3.1.16.d; 3.1.18.b).

div.head: EH 3.1.30.d (SR) 40.26–28
div.p: ܘܒܬ̇ܟܬܘܫܐ ܪܒܐ̣ ܥܡ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܫܪܟܐ ܐ̇ܘ ܝܬܝܪ ܣܒ̣ܠ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܚ̈ܫܐ ܓܢܒܪܐܝܬ܇ ܘܣ̇ܝܒܪ ܐܢܘܢ ܠܐܘ̈ܠܨܢܐ ܕܖ̈ܕܘܦ̣ܝܐ ܘܕܚܒ̣̈ܘܫܝܐ “and in the great battle along with the others he valiantly endured the sufferings even more and underwent the tribulations of persecutions and imprisonments”: John of Ephesus’s claim that his own suffering surpassed that of the other Severan bishops is confirmed at the start of Book 2. Despite his insistence in EH 3.1.29.f that he and his colleagues permanently separated themselves from the Chalcedonian communion, EH 3.2.3–3.2.4 show that Paul of Antioch, Stephen of Cyprus, and Elisha of Sardis all eventually returned to the Chalcedonian communion. John describes in EH 3.2.4–3.2.7 that he alone refused to do so and therefore remained imprisoned – a statement that is impossible to verify, given that the EH is the only source for these events. The fact that John alludes here in EH 3.1.30 to the release of his episcopal peers further supports the view that his assertion in EH 3.1.29 that they all permanently withdrew from the communion is merely rhetorical. It appears that John knew very well that his colleagues had relapsed when he wrote the first thirty chapters of Book 1.

div.head: EH 3.1.30.e (SR) 40.28–41.6
div.p: ܘܡܢܗ ܘܡܢ ܫܪܟܐ ܐܬ̈ܡ̇ܠܠܝ ܘܐܬ̣ܕܪܫ ܠܘܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܘܠܘܬ ܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ܆ ܠܘ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܗܠܝܢ ܥܘܗ̈ܕܢܐ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܐ ܕܐܫ̣ܠܡ ܒܟܬܒܐ ܗܢܐ܆ ܐܠܐ̣ ܘܒܡܐܐ ܥ̈ܦ̣ܝܢ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܝܢ. ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܛܠ ܡ̣ܬܚܐ ܣܓܝܐܐ ܕܬܫܥ̣ܝܬܐ: ܘܥܘ̣ܒܛܐ ܕܡ̈ܠܐ ܕܠܐ ܣܟ̣ܐ: ܟܕ ܡܢ ܣܓܝܐܘܬܗܝܢ ܐܗ̣ܡܝ܆ ܒܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ ܕܡܢܗܝܢ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚ “it was not only the few records that he transmitted in this book that were said and uttered in the debate with the emperor and to the patriarch, but a hundred times more. Omitting an abundance of these things due to the great length of the story and the density of the innumerable words, he only made use of a few of them”: John of Ephesus frequently asserts that he omitted many elements from his account so as to prevent an exaggerate length of his work; he does so again at the end of this chapter. On this motif, and on its use in other Ecclesiastical Histories, see 3.1.3.u and 3.1.21.e.

In the passage of Michael the Syrian’s Chronicle Chabot 1899–1910, 2:303.a.19–24; 4:343.b.17–21 based on this chapter, there is the similar statement that John only recorded a part of the debate with emperor and patriarch, and of the hardship the Severans suffered.

div.head: EH 3.1.30.f (SR) 41.9–11
div.p: ܢܛ̇ܪ̣ ܕܠܐ ܡ̣ܨܛܠܝܢܘܬܐ. ܐ̇ܘ ܛ̇ܠܘܡܘܬܐ ܘܥ̇ܫܘܩܘܬܐ ܕܚܕ ܓ̇ܒܐ ܕܠܩ̣ܘܒܠܗ: ܐܝܟ ܕܠܩ̣ܘܝܡܐ ܕܓܒܐ ܕܝܠܗ “he maintains neutrality, without defaming and slandering the one side that was against him so as to establish his own side”: An author’s claim to remain impartial in his work and to provide an accurate description without favoring one side, even if he himself was involved in the events he describes, is a topos in ancient historiography. A famous example of this is Tacitus’s assertion in Annales 1.1 (Fisher 1906, 1.15) to report on the reigns of the first Roman emperors sine ira et studio (“without anger and partiality”), irrespective of the fact that Tacitus was highly critical of the principate since it led to the waning of the senate’s influence. In this passage, John of Ephesus adopts a similar stance in order to win the favor of his potentially critical readers (van Ginkel 1995, 184). Yet as noted above in 3.1.30.a and 3.1.30.c, the appeal to his neutrality cannot hide the fact that he was definitely no impartial observer of the events. As he was implicated in the short-lived communion with the Chalcedonians, his position within the Severan movement was severely shaken, and one of the main aims of his account was to provide an apology, to put all the blame completely on the Chalcedonian side, and thus to ensure that his co-religionists accepted him again after the failure of the union (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f).

div.head: EH 3.1.30.g (SR) 41.11–13
div.p: ܐܠܐ ܐ̇ܝܟܢܐ ܕܒܪܝ̣ܫܗ ܕܣܦ̣ܪܐ ܗܢܐ ܕܡ̇ܟܬܒܢܘܬܗ܇ ܐ̇ܘ ܐܝܟ ܕܩ̣̇ܕܡ ܐܡ̣ܪ ܘܐܫ̣ܬܘܕܝ “rather as at the beginning of this volume of his composition or as he previously said and promised”: John of Ephesus appears here to refer back to the lost start of Book 1. According to this passage, it seems that he also included an assertion of his impartiality at the very beginning of EH 3, either in chapters EH 3.1.1–3.1.2 or in the lost start of EH 3.1.3 (Brooks 1936, 28 n.4). The ܛܒܥܐ ܕܫܪܪܐ (“seal of truth”) introduced in just afterwards is not mentioned anywhere else in the preserved parts of the EH, and it might indeed go back to its lost beginnings. Similarly, John only uses ܡܟܬܒܢܘܬܐ (“composition”), the term he employed to refer to the EH, in EH 3.1.3.m and 3.1.3.x and here in EH 3.1.30. This might suggest that at the end of his account on the hardship he and the other Severan bishops had to endure, John linked back to its start by repeating that he had reported all the events truthfully and accurately.

div.head: Summary (SR; NK)
div.p: John of Ephesus added this chapter and the following ones until EH 3.1.39 in 577/578, a few years after his composition of the first thirty chapters (see 3.1.31.c). He grouped those new chapters into thematic units: 3.1.31 and 3.1.32 are centered around the important official Photius, 3.1.33–3.1.35 deal with Egyptian matters, and 3.1.36–3.1.39 with the fate of the Severan communities in and close to Constantinople.

This chapter introduces the anti-Chalcedonian bishop Conon of Tarsus for the first time in EH 3. Conon was a leader of the so-called “tritheists,” an anti-Chalcedonian subgroup which, influenced by John Philoponus’s conceptual reformulation of trinitarian theology, separated from the Severans in the 560s Roggo 2022, 100–102 dell’Osso 2020; Sorabji 2010, 71–72 Destephen 2008, 508–511 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 279–291 Van Roey and Allen 1994, 122–143 Van Roey 1985. John of Ephesus expresses his rejection of their doctrine throughout the EH, yet particularly so in Book 5. He asserts that they understood the three persons of the Trinity to be three different gods, hence their name. However, this is an external imposition and a misinterpretation of their theology. John Philoponus and Conon themselves never advocated belief in three gods. The “tritheists” eventually split in two groups when John Philoponus came to doubt the resurrection of the flesh at the Last Judgment and postulated the creation of new bodies instead (this is further discussed in the commentary to EH 3.2.36). Conon and his followers (the “Cononites”) did not accept that doctrine.

According to the account of John of Ephesus, Conon was also apprehended during the persecution and, like the other anti-Chalcedonian bishops (EH 3.1.17), imprisoned in the patriarchal palace. The Chalcedonians demanded that he produce a written statement of his faith (a so-called libellus; see 3.1.5-8.i). However, Conon refused to comply, provoked the anger of Justin II, and was handed over to the infamous warrior-monk Photius, stepson to Belisarius. Photius took Conon with him to Palestine and confined him there in the monastery of the Nea church, whose abbot he was. Conon spent three years there before being released; he then traveled back to Cilicia, the main focus of his missionary activities. Conon and his followers again figure prominently in EH 3.5.1–3.5.12.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
Secondary Literature (SR)
EH 3.1.31.a (SR; NK) 41.18
div.p: ܩܢܘܢ ܪܝܫܗ̇ ܕܗܪܣܝܣ “Conon, the head of the heresy”: Conon, since the early 550s formally bishop of Tarsus in Cilicia, was an important anti-Chalcedonian leader in the second half of the 6th century. He was arguably one of the first bishops to be ordained by Jacob Burdʿoyo. Together with Eugenius of Seleucia, he also emerged after 566/567 as one of the leaders of those anti-Chalcedonians who adhered to John Philoponus’s conceptual reformulation of trinitarian doctrine (the so-called “tritheists”). This group, originating in the mid-550s in circles influenced by the Aristotelian philosopher and theologian John Philoponus, definitely separated from the Severans in the late 560s after various initiatives recorded in the Documenta Monophysitica to heal the divide had remained unsuccessful Chabot 1907; see Van Roey and Allen 1994, 122–143. Severans and “tritheists” eventually anathematized each other, hence John of Ephesus’s outspoken condemnation of them throughout the EH (see especially EH 3.5.1–3.5.12) (van Ginkel 1995, 143–146). There were even attempts by the Chalcedonian authorities to mediate between the two anti-Chalcedonian factions; in 566 a series of talks took place in the imperial palace after the death of the Severan patriarch Theodosius, and in 570 patriarch John Scholasticus chaired a debate between them (EH 3.5.3) dell’Osso 2020; Destephen 2008, 508–511 Van Roey 1982, 487–492; 1985 Honigmann 1951, 179–188.

Conon’s rise to become “the head of the heresy,” as he is depicted in the EH, started quite late. As Honigmann (1951, 183–184) pointed out, John of Ephesus still praises him in LES 49 (Brooks 1921, 697), thus indicating that he only embraced “tritheist” doctrine after 566/567. Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.30 (Chabot 1899–1910, 2:254) relates that it was the monk Athanasius, grandson to empress Theodora, who convinced both Conon and Eugenius to embrace this doctrine after the death of Theodosius. They were the first bishops to profess John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine and thus quickly came to be perceived as leaders of the “tritheist” movement. This chapter attests that in 571, the Chalcedonians sought to suppress the entire anti-Chalcedonian hierarchy; Conon was imprisoned just as the Severan bishops around John of Ephesus (see 3.1.31.c). After his release (see 3.1.31.h), Conon continued to spread his doctrine in Asia Minor and Constantinople for a number of years, as recorded in EH 3.5.1–3.5.12; he died probably in the late 570s or early 580s.

div.head: EH 3.1.31.b (NK) 41.21–23
div.p: ܕܡ̇ܡܪܚܝܢ ܕܢ̣ܡܢܘܢ ܟܝ̈ܢܐ ܘܐܘ̈ܣܝܣ ܘܐܠܗ̈ܘܬܐ ܘܐ̈ܠܗܐ ܒܬܠܝܬܝܘܬܐ ܩܕܝܫܬܐ ܘܫܘ̣ܝܬ ܒܐܘܣܝܐ “who venture to enumerate the natures, substances, divinities, and gods in the holy and consubstantial Trinity”: John of Ephesus accuses Conon and his followers of speaking not only of natures and substances but also of divinities and gods in their doctrine of the Trinity. This attribution reflects a polemical intention to classify this group within anti-Chalcedonianism as the “tritheist heresy” (Schönfelder 1862, 282).

Timothy of Constantinople also provides an interesting confirmation regarding the doctrine of the “Cononites.” Timothy was likely a presbyter at the Hagia Sophia. Around the year 600, he wrote a work called De receptione haereticorum, in which he discusses the stance of Conon and his followers on trinitarian doctrine. According to Timothy, they “confess that the holy and consubstantial Trinity is one essence or nature, one divinity or God, not in terms of number, but in the undifferentiated identity of divinity”; Μίαν δὲ οὐσίαν, ἤγουν φύσιν, καὶ μίαν θεότητα, ἤγουν Θεὸν τὴν ἁγίαν καὶ ὁμοούσιον Τριάδα ὁμολογοῦσιν, οὐκ ἀριθμῷ, ἀλλὰ τῇ ἀπαραλλάκτῳ τῆς θεότητος ταυτότητι (PG 86/1:61A7–10 (Migne 1857–1866)).

Furthermore, Timothy cites their belief that “in truth, according to essence and nature, each one is God, namely Father, Son, and Holy Spirit; the Holy Trinity consists of three hypostases and three persons”; οἴτινες καὶ αὐτοὶ ὁμολογοῦσι Θεὸν εἶναι κατὰ ἀλήθειαν, καὶ οὐσίαν καὶ φύσιν ἕκαστον· τὸν Πατέρα λέγω, καὶ τὸν Υἱὸν, καὶ τὸ ἅγιον Πνεῦμα τρεῖς ὑποστάσεις τὴν ἁγίαν Τριάδα καὶ τρία πρόσωπα (PG 86/1:61A1–5 (Migne 1857–1866)). However, according to Timothy, what distinguishes “Cononite” doctrine from orthodoxy as he perceived it is that “they also speak of three substances and natures numerically, which are equal without any distinction within the divinity”; ἔτι μὴν καὶ τρεῖς τινας τῷ ἀριθμῷ οὐσίας καὶ φύσεις ἴσας ἀπαραλλάκτως κατὰ τὴν θεότητα λέγοντες (PG 86/1:61A5–7 (Migne 1857–1866)). The three οὐσίαι and φύσεις are therefore to be understood as undifferentiated and perfectly equal (ἀπαραλλάκτως) within the divine unity dell’Osso 2020, 205 Krausmüller 2017; 2012 Sorabji 2010.

Timothy adds that the “Cononites” shared their trinitarian beliefs with the second “tritheist” faction (PG 86/1:64B (Migne 1857–1866)), from whom they had separated because they did not accept John Philoponus’s doctrine regarding the creation of new bodies at the Last Judgment (see EH 3.2.36).

As the “tritheists” speak of three substances and natures within the divine Trinity, they go beyond what was established as the binding confession of μία οὐσία or φύσις – τρεῖς ὑποστάσεις in the Godhead at the council of Constantinople in 381. Their terminological determination of the divine Trinity led to the accusation of “tritheism” in their conception of God (Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 279–291).

div.head: EH 3.1.31.c (SR) 41.23–24
div.p: ܘܗ̣ܘ ܗܢܐ ܐܬܠ̣ܒܟ ܘܐܬܚ̣ܒܫ ܘܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ ܒܒܝܬ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܝܘܢ ܙܒܢܐ̣ ܥܡ ܫܪܟܐ ܕܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ “this one was likewise apprehended and imprisoned in the episcopal palace for a time with all the others”: John of Ephesus indicates here that it was not only the Severan bishops who were brought to the patriarchal palace for talks about the union. Earlier in the EH, in 3.1.17.f, he only referred to himself and his Severan peers, yet based on the testimony of this passage, we may assume that Conon (and perhaps other non-Severan bishops) was also present together with the Severans when debating about Justin’s edict on faith (on which see 3.1.23.a). We do not know for sure if Conon eventually also entered for a short time in Chalcedonian communion; the fact that he refused to write a libellus (see 3.1.31.d) may suggest that he did not.

The first thirty chapters of Book 1 form a unity and have a clear apologetic purpose insofar as John wanted to prove that he and his colleagues had always insisted on the condemnation of Chalcedon and had thus always remained faithful to their Severan orthodoxy (see 3.1.17.j and 3.1.20.f). In this clear-cut dichotomy between Severans and Chalcedonians, there was no room for representatives of other doctrinal groups. On the contrary, it might have been counterproductive to admit that the Severan bishops had been in the palace together with Conon – after all, a condemned heretic. This would all the more have been the case if Conon, unlike the other bishops, did not enter into communion with the Chalcedonians. It appears that John wrote the present chapter under different circumstances at a later point (he knows already that Conon was set free after three years, see 3.1.31.h), probably in 577/578, and then added it (and the subsequent chapters) to the original thirty chapters of Book 1 (van Ginkel 1995, 73). By that time, his apology may have been accepted by his co-religionists in the east and consequently, it was unproblematic to mention that Conon had also been present in the patriarchal residence in 571.

div.head: EH 3.1.31.d (SR) 41.25
div.p: ܬ̇ܒܥܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܠܗ ܕܝܢ܆ ܠܝ̣ܒܠܘܢ ܕܢܥ̣ܒܕ ܐܝܟ ܗܪܛܝܩܐ “they demanded that he produce a libellus as a heretic”: As stated in 3.1.5-8.i, John of Ephesus mentions only in the cases of Conon and Paul of Aphrodisias (see EH 3.2.43) that anti-Chalcedonian bishops were expected to subscribe to Chalcedonian orthodoxy by composing a statement of the correct faith in the form of a libellus. However, it is possible that John and his peers also had to do this when entering into communion with the Chalcedonians (see 3.1.24.l). He may deliberately have chosen not to include this in his account in the first thirty chapters of Book 1, as this would evidently have contradicted his claim that the Severan bishops always insisted on a condemnation of Chalcedon.

div.head: EH 3.1.31.e (SR) 41.26–27
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܟܕ ܝܠ̣ܦ ܙܟ̇ܝܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ܆ ܝܡ̣ܐ ܒܝ̇ܠܕܬ ܐܠܗܐ̣ “as soon as the victorious emperor learned this, he swore by the God-bearer”: This passage offers yet another contrast to the first thirty chapters of Book 1. Whereas there, John of Ephesus creates the impression that it was really patriarch John Scholasticus who was the driving force behind the suppression of the anti-Chalcedonian bishops (see 3.1.5.e and 3.1.11.e), it becomes clear here that Justin II was just as engaged as the patriarch to overcome the ecclesiastical divide. The emperor insists that Conon write a libellus with a profession of the orthodox (Chalcedonian) faith and issues threats against (Chalcedonian) churches which might nevertheless receive him. This suggests that Conon might have been ready to enter into communion with the Chalcedonians on the basis of Justin’s edict (see 3.1.24.l), yet refused to write such a libellus in which he would have had to explicitly reject his (“tritheist”) beliefs. The way how Justin is depicted in this passage sits well with what we know from his activities in the first years of his reign, when he hosted talks in the imperial palace and tried to find a lasting solution with the edict he presented at Callinicum (see 3.1.3.r and 3.1.21.a) (Roggo 2022, 91–100).

Michael the Syrian summarized parts of this chapter in two different passages in his Chronicle. In 10.7 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:306.a.6–13; 4:344.a.44–345.b.5, he called Conon a leader of the “tritheists” and recorded that Justin insisted that he produce a libellus. Already in the previous chapter 10.6 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:300.a.24–27; 4:341.b.33–36, he reported that Justin handed Conon over to Photius, who imprisoned him in Palestine (see 3.1.31.g).
For the epithet ܙܟܝܐ (“victorious”) for the emperor, see 3.1.4.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.31.f (SR) 42.2
div.p: ܦ̣ܘܛܝܘܣ ܒܪ ܐܢܬܬܗ ܕܒ̣ܠܣܪܝܣ “Photius the son of Belisarius’s wife”: The career of Photius (PLRE 3:1037–1039 (Martindale 1992)), the stepson of Justinian’s most famous general Belisarius, can be traced from the mid-530s to his death, probably in the second half of the 570s. It may have been the news of his death in 577/578 (see EH 3.1.32.l) which prompted John of Ephesus to add new material to the first thirty chapters of Book 1 (van Ginkel 1995, 73). By highlighting Photius’s role in the detention of Conon, he linked his original account of the anti-Chalcedonian persecution to the story of the violent suppressor Photius as narrated in EH 3.1.32.

Photius’s career under Justinian and Justin II is intriguing as he wielded considerable influence in both the secular and the ecclesiastical sphere, reminiscent of later patriarchs such as Gregory of Antioch (570–592; see 3.1.41.k) and Cyrus of Alexandria (630–642) Lin forthcoming; Greatrex 2007, 288. Procopius asserts in Secret History 1.31–3.29 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 3:10–24) that Photius collected military experience under Belisarius in the wars against the Goths and Persians in the 530s and early 540s, even rose to the rank of an honorary consul, yet then came to oppose his mother Antonina because of her infidelity to Belisarius. However, she ensured that her ally, the empress Theodora (d. 548), apprehended Photius and held him imprisoned for three years before he was able to escape to Jerusalem, where he entered into a monastery. In all likelihood, this was the monastery belonging to the Nea church; Photius eventually became the abbot of this new and influential foundation (see 3.1.31.g).

Recently, Sihong Lin (forthcoming) has made a compelling case that Photius turned into an important supporter of Theodora’s extended family after her death, among whom were Sophia and her husband, the future emperor Justin II. This connection to Justin and Sophia is even remembered in Gregory of Tours’s Glory of the Martyrs 5 (Krusch 1969, 42), and it explains his astonishing career after 565. Despite being a monastic figure, Justin entrusted him with wide-ranging secular powers, as John of Ephesus attests in EH 3.1.32 (Hatlie 2007, 202–203). His elevated position in the administration of the Roman east is confirmed in other sources. The Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 111.24–27) from the early 7th century states that after Justin’s accession, he received “authority to deal with all people and issues” (ἐξουσίαν […] κατὰ παντὸς προσώπου καὶ πράγματος) in Palestine; this may have been connected to his appointment as abbot of the Nea monastery in Jerusalem (see 3.1.31.g). Similarly, the Chronicle of Theophanes AM 6058 (de Boor 1883, 242.4–6) holds that in the same period, Justin dispatched him to Egypt “to pacify the churches” (εἰρηνεῦσαι τὰς ἐκκλησίας), probably in order to prevent anti-Chalcedonian groups from creating a parallel episcopal hierarchy (Booth 2017, 164–165). This might explain his very negative depiction by John of Ephesus (see 3.1.32.d).

Soon afterwards, perhaps in the early 570s, he was one of the officials ordered to suppress a Samaritan uprising in Palestine (together with Theophilus introduced in EH 3.3.27; see 3.1.32.d) Roggo 2022, 144 Pummer 2002, 378–379. Photius’s campaign against the Samaritans is also reported in the Chronicle of John of Nikiu (Zotenberg 1883, 401), yet, probably by mistake, dated to the reign of Justinian (Mango and Scott 1997, 355 n.3). Due to his close connections to Justin, Photius visited Constantinople at times, and it was on one of these occasions that Conon was given in his power. This may have happened in 571; this would make sense with regard to the start of the measures directed against the anti-Chalcedonians in that year (see 3.1.5.d and 3.1.31.g). There is further mention of Photius’s activities in EH 3.1.32.

div.head: EH 3.1.31.g (SR) 42.4–5
div.p: ܘܫܩ̣ܠܗ ܥܡܗ ܠܦܠܣܛܝܢܐ. ܘܚܒܫܗ ܒܕܝܪܐ ܕܡܬ̇ܩܪܝܐ̣ ܚ̇ܕܬܐ “Photius took him along to Palestine and imprisoned him in the monastery called the ‘Nea’”: John of Ephesus repeats the deportation of Conon to Palestine in EH 3.5.3. It is interesting that none of the other anti-Chalcedonian bishops were removed so far from Constantinople. John of Ephesus and his Severan colleagues all were detained in or close to the capital (see 3.1.29.f). One possible explanation for this is to assume that the Chalcedonian authorities wished to separate the representatives of the two quarrelsome anti-Chalcedonian factions from each other. In the years prior to 571, repeated attempts at reconciliation between the adherents of John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine and the Severans had failed (see 3.1.31.a), and the subdivision complicated talks with the Chalcedonians since a faction willing to enter into a compromise might have found itself accused of betraying the anti-Chalcedonian cause by the other (Price 2009, 26).

From about the mid-540s (see 3.1.31.f), Photius lived as a monk in Jerusalem. He eventually became the abbot of the monastery belonging to the grand Marian church known as the Nea (ἡ νέα ἐκκλησία). The Nea church, erected during twelve years 531–543, was Justinian’s second-largest church building and only surpassed by Hagia Sophia. Procopius described its construction panegyrically in Buildings 5.6 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 4:162–165); on it and on Justinian’s rationale when constructing it, see Trampedach 2015; Flusin 1998, 505–506. As its abbot, Photius would have been one of the most influential persons in Jerusalem. According to an inscription (CIIP 1.800) probably dated to 564/565, which names a certain Constantine as abbot of the monastery (Trampedach 2015, 161–162), Photius may have succeeded him only after this point, which sits well with his importance under Justin II and John of Ephesus’s chronological indication in 3.1.32.l.

There has been some confusion in scholarship as to whether Photius’s monastery where Conon was imprisoned was the Nea monastery in Jerusalem Hatlie 2007, 202–203 Honigmann 1951, 186–187 or the Nova Laura of Sabas east of Jerusalem (Hillner 2015, 384). However, EH 3.1.32 explicitly locates it in Jerusalem itself; moreover, the name of Photius’s successor as abbot, Abraham (see 3.1.32.m for a more detailed discussion of this point), also attested in John Moschus’s Pratum Spirituale 68 and 187 (PG 87/3:2917D and 3064D (Migne 1857–1866)), renders it very probable that the monastery in question was indeed the Nea (Lin forthcoming).

div.head: EH 3.1.31.h (SR) 42.5–6
div.p: ܘܗܘ̣ܐ ܒܗ̇ ܙܒܢܐ ܕܬܠܬ̣ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ. ܘܗܟܢܐ ܐܫ̣ܬܪܝ ܘܐ̣ܙܠ ܠܐܬܪܐ ܕܩܝܠܝܩܝܐ “he was there for a period of three years, and he was thus released and went to the region of Cilicia”: According to 3.1.5.d, the suppression of the anti-Chalcedonians in Constantinople started in early 571. Assuming that Conon was deported to Jerusalem after the failure of Justin’s edict and the separation and imprisonment of the Severan bishops narrated in EH 3.1.29.f, his detention of three years in the Nea monastery may have lasted roughly until the second half of 574. In EH 3.5.4, John of Ephesus asserts that Conon was eventually released because some of his followers were influential people at court. We cannot exclude that such support played a role, yet it is noticeable that Conon’s release appears to have happened at about the same time when John himself was transferred from prison to house arrest in Constantinople (EH 3.2.7.f). In all likelihood, this detente was connected with the rise to power of the more tolerant Tiberius Roggo 2022, 118–120 Booth 2017, 169–171; see 3.1.33.e. EH 3.5.4 also provides more information on what Conon did after he was set free. Probably, Cilicia was his native region (he was nominally bishop of Tarsus, its capital); for this reason, he went there and, as John deplores, he kept spreading his doctrine (Honigmann 1951, 187).

div.head: Summary (SR; NK)
div.p: This chapter further discusses the activities of Photius, introduced in EH 3.1.31.f. John of Ephesus provides a short overview of Photius’s career; he recounts that Photius originally served in the army under Belisarius and then – John does not say why – became a monk. John’s description is very negative; he asserts that Photius could not bear a life of contemplation and therefore re-entered into imperial service despite being a monk. Justin II dispatched him to suppress a Samaritan rebellion (see 3.1.31.f and 3.1.32.d), but he also violently extracted large sums of money from the inhabitants of the Syrian provinces, apparently to fund imperial war expenses. John portrays Photius as a cruel plunderer who did not even hesitate to use violence against bishops so that they would comply with his demands. For instance, the bishop of Ascalon was forced to pay three hundred pounds of gold; after an initial refusal, he was hung by a noose and subjected to this torture until the sum was paid. Eventually, magistrates, governors, and other dignitaries extorted by Photius complained to the emperor about his violent methods, yet apparently to no avail. In his lawless entourage, as John of Ephesus depicts them, both monks and Roman soldiers were united to form a gang which terrorized the provinces of the Roman east. After twelve years of his cruel activities, Photius finally met his demise; he was succeeded as abbot of the Nea monastery in Jerusalem by a certain Abraham (see 3.1.31.g).

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.7 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:306.a.14–30; 4:345.b.5–19: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.32.h

div.head: Parallel Accounts (SR)
div.p: Procopius, Secret History 1.31–3.29 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 3:10–24): Photius’s military career and his entry into a monastery; see 3.1.32.b John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 401): Photius’s dispatch against the Samaritans; see 3.1.32.d Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 111.24–27): Photius’s influence in Palestine; see 3.1.32.d Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6058 (de Boor 1883, 242.4–6): Photius’s influence in Egypt; see 3.1.32.d Anastasius Bibliothecarius, Chronicle Tripertita AM 6058 (de Boor 1885, 149.19–21): derivative of Theophanes George Cedrenus, Historiarum compendium 420.1 (Tartaglia 2016, 656.8–10): derivative of Theophanes John Moschus, Pratum Spirituale 68 and 187 (PG 87/3:2917D and 3064D (Migne 1857–1866)): Abraham, abbot of the Nea monastery; see 3.1.32.m

div.head: EH 3.1.32.a (SR) 42.9–10
div.p: ܦ̣ܘܛܝܣ ܗܢܐ ܐܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܒܪ ܐܢܬܬܗ ܕܒ̣ܝܠܝܣܪܝܣ ܦ̣ܛܪܝܩܝܘܣ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܫܡܗ̣̇ ܗܘܐ ܐ̇ܢܛܘܢܝܢܐ “this Photius was the son of the wife of the patrikios Belisarius whose name was Antonina”: Photius (PLRE 3:1037–1039 (Martindale 1992)) was the son of Antonina (PLRE 3:91–93 (Martindale 1992)), a close acquaintance and supporter of empress Theodora (d. 548). Photius was probably born prior to 520, given that he was honorary consul by 541. At one point before 533, Antonina married Justinian’s successful general Belisarius (PLRE 3:181–224 (Martindale 1992)); Photius thus became his stepson. For more information on Photius and his career, see 3.1.31.f.

John of Ephesus calls Belisarius patrikios; he bore this title since the mid-530s, probably because of his victory over the Vandals. This was one of the highest ranks in the imperial hierarchy; via the Greek πατρίκιος, it entered Syriac as a loanword in the 6th century (Butts 2016b, 161). John of Ephesus repeatedly used it in both EH and LES (Brooks 1923, 157.12 and 189.7).

div.head: EH 3.1.32.b (SR) 42.10–13
div.p: ܗܢܐ ܗܟܝܠ ܟܕ ܒܐ̣ܣܬܪܛܝܐ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ: ܘܒܩܪ̈ܒܐ ܥܡܗ ܕܒܝ̣ܠܝܣܪܝܣ ܐܬ̇ܢ̇ܦܩ ܗܘܐ܆ ܒܚܪܬܐ ܡܢ ܥܠ̣ܬܐ ܡܕܡ ܐ̣ܙܠ ܣ̇ܦ̣ܪ ܣܥܪܗ̣ ܘܠܒ̣ܫ ܐܣܟܡܐ ܕܕܝܪܝܘܬܐ “when this one was in the army and had been sent out to war with Belisarius, he eventually for some reason went to cut off his hair and take on the monastic habit”: Photius accompanied his stepfather Belisarius in the war against the Ostrogoths in the second half of the 530s (Procopius, Wars 5.5.5 and 5.10.5–20 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 2:25–26 and 51–53)), and he also joined him on his campaign against the Persians in 541 (Procopius, Secret History 2.1 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 3:12–13)). John of Ephesus does not explain why Photius eventually became a monk, yet Procopius presents an elaborate (and not necessarily trustworthy) account in Secret History 1.31–3.29 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 3:10–24): Photius came to oppose his mother Antonina because she had taken a certain Theodosius as lover. Photius got hold of Theodosius, yet Antonina then turned to her friend, the empress Theodora. She had Photius arrested and tortured; he remained detained for three years before being able to escape to Jerusalem in the mid-540s. There, he entered a monastery to hide from the empress Lin forthcoming; Parnell 2023.

John of Ephesus may have deliberately chosen to omit the role Theodora played and not to give any reason why Photius left the army and became a monk. Anti-Chalcedonian authors such as John himself held Theodora in high regard for the protection she had offered to anti-Chalcedonian clerics in the capital in the 530s and 540s (see 3.1.10.b and (Menze 2008b, 208–228)). Her presentation as a vengeful and cruel tyrant, as she appears in the episode with Photius in the Secret History, might have damaged her saintly image. John also appears to have compressed the time it took for Photius to become a monk: he creates the impression that Photius immediately received the tonsura (the monastic haircut) and the habit of a monk upon leaving military service, yet Justinian’s Novel 5.2 from 535 held that candidates to the monastic life had to wait for three years before having their hair cut and putting on the monastic habit (Schöll and Kroll 1895, 29–31). If the Palestinian monasteries were sticking to the letter of the law, then Photius could only have received his habit three years after his arrival in Palestine.

Photius’s career, from a military officer to a monk and eventually even abbot of the monastery associated with the grand Nea church in Jerusalem, is without direct parallels (Hatlie 2007, 202–203). The career of the hermit Auxentius in the mid-5th century is somewhat comparable (we owe this reference to Michael Whitby): he was a member of the palace guards, yet then he withdrew from secular life and eventually became the leader of a monastic community (Varalda 2017). There are some examples of clerics who were also powerful in the secular sphere, for instance the patriarchs Gregory of Antioch (570–592; see 3.1.41.k) and Cyrus of Alexandria (630–642). Both patriarchs assumed some duties of the imperial government in their regions, Cyrus even acted officially as prefect of Egypt, but neither of them had been in military or civil service prior to being ordained. Some other dignitaries entered the ecclesiastical hierarchy after successful careers in the secular sphere; examples include the patriarchs Ephrem of Antioch (527–545), a former comes Orientis, and John IV of Alexandria (569/570–580/581), a military officer and patrikios (see 3.1.40.a and 3.1.41.h for more information on them). Yet these cases were different from that of Photius in that an imperial appointment was decisive for their change of career. Throughout the 6th century, emperors normally sought to prevent people from leaving public service and becoming a member of the clergy or entering into a monastery. Legislation against this practice (which remained ongoing) includes Codex Iustinianus 1.3.52 from 531 (Blume and Frier 2016, 134–139), Novel 123.15 from 546 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 605–606 Miller and Sarris 2018, 811 n.34, and a law by Maurice from c. 593 (Dölger and Müller 2009, 41–42.110a). The first two laws only targeted city councilors and civil servants, yet Maurice’s law explicitly forbade that soldiers or military officers become monks. This indicates that there may have been many who, like Photius, left the army for a monastic life.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.c (SR) 42.14–15
div.p: ܐܠܐ ܐܣܟܡܐ ܕܕܝܪܝܐ ܒܫܡܐ ܫܐ̣ܝܠܐ̣ ܠܒ̣ܫ ܗܘܐ “but took on the habit of a monk as an assumed name”: Throughout his account about Photius, John of Ephesus makes it clear that Photius was a monk in name only. According to John, he was far from embracing the contemplative lifestyle commonly associated with monastic life and behaved as if he still were a military officer invading a hostile region (see 3.1.32.e). Due to the lack of parallel sources on Photius’s activities, we do not know how trustworthy John’s narrative is. However, he usually depicts persons negatively in the EH when their actions proved harmful to himself or to his Severan community, and we may thus assume that his portrayal of Photius is heavily biased (see 3.1.32.d). It is impossible for us to ascertain what Photius’s motives were when he became a monk, whether he just wanted to escape from imprisonment or whether he was sincerely inclined to monastic life. In any case, he was not the only high-ranking person to become a monk: even members of the imperial family, for instance Theodora’s grandson Athanasius (see EH 3.5.1 and 3.1.22.e) opted for monastic life.

The passage in question here can also be understood slightly differently as “but took on the habit of a monk under an assumed name.” It would make sense to assume that Photius, after his escape from Theodora, did not travel to Palestine and enter into a monastery under his real name. Photius’s use of an alias as a safety measure would not have been surprising in his precarious position. Intriguingly, there is also a passage in John Moschus’s Pratum Spirituale 187 (PG 87/3:3064D (Migne 1857–1866)) from the early 7th century which might support the view that Photius adopted another name for his monastic career, namely Eudoxius. This argument Lin forthcoming; Di Segni 2012, 262 is based on the name of Photius’s successor as abbot of the Nea monastery, a certain Abraham mentioned by both John of Ephesus and the Pratum Spirituale (see below 3.1.32.m for a more detailed discussion of this point).

div.head: EH 3.1.32.d (SR) 42.19–20
div.p: ܡܢ ܥܠ̣ܬ ܫܡ̇ܪ̈ܝܐ ܕܡ̣ܪ̈ܕܘ ܗܘܘ܆ ܠܐܬܪ̈ܘܬܐ ܕܣܘܪܝܐ ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪ “was sent to the regions of Syria because of the Samaritans who had rebelled”: The Samaritans rebelled on three occasions in the 6th century: in 529, c. 556, and again in Justin’s reign, possibly in the early 570s. As a consequence, they were repeatedly targeted in imperial legislation and lost essential civic rights such as the right to inherit property. On the Samaritans in the 6th century and on these rebellions, see Millar 2013, 72–78 Noethlichs 2007; Meier 2003, 209–215 and 305–306 Pummer 2002, 259–269; 283–288; 306–309; 318–319; 378–379. Photius’s dispatch against the Samaritans refers to the third rebellion, which took place during the reign of Justin. This is clear because John of Ephesus mentions a Samaritan uprising under Justin in EH 3.2.29 and 3.3.27, and in the former instance, he names Photius as one of the officials suppressing the rebellion. The Chronicle of John of Nikiu (Zotenberg 1883, 401) also reports that Photius was sent out against the Samaritans; however, as already stated in 3.1.31.f, he dated this mission to the reign of Justinian. But the context of this passage in the Chronicle rather suggests the reign of Justin (Mango and Scott 1997, 355 n.3); Justinian and Justin with their similar names are frequently mixed up in the sources (see also above 3.1.4.b). Note that Honigmann (1951, 186–187) dated Photius’s dispatch against the Samaritans to 572 based on Novel 144 (which was issued on 18 May 572 and restricted the right of Samaritans to bequeath property), but quite possibly, this law was only promulgated after the end of the uprising Miller and Sarris 2018, 941–943 Noethlichs 2007, 64–65.

John of Ephesus’s account of Photius’s outrageous behavior and of his plundering of allegedly the whole Roman east starts with his dispatch against the Samaritans (on John’s depiction of the Samaritans, see van Ginkel 1995, 133–135). Yet considering the fact that the Samaritans settled predominantly in the province of Palaestina secunda close to the Lake of Galilee, it appears that Photius’s powers reached beyond the Samaritan homeland (Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6058 asserts that they even included Egypt in 566; see below) and that his nefarious activities, as John depicts them, were not all connected to the Samaritan uprising. According to the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum Pouderon 1998, 174 Cramer 1839, 111.24–27, he received “authority to deal with all people and issues” (ἐξουσίαν […] κατὰ παντὸς προσώπου καὶ πράγματος) in Palestine very soon after Justin’s accession in 565 and thus probably well before the Samaritan uprising. We do not know what Photius’s official function in the imperial administration of the east was (that he did have one seems certain, see 3.1.32.k), but he clearly managed, with imperial support, to carve out a position of considerable power for himself Lin forthcoming; Greatrex 2007, 294 n.70. His function may have been similar to that of Theophilus, an official mentioned by John in EH 3.3.27. This Theophilus had also been entrusted with the suppression of the Samaritan rebellion, and was then, a few years later, in 579/580, again dispatched to Syria to lead an inquiry against supposed pagans (EH 3.3.27–3.3.29). John of Ephesus is full of praise for him and eagerly recounts that he fulfilled his mission by discovering numerous crypto-pagans among the elites of the Roman east; he used torture and did not even refrain from arresting a provincial governor (Roggo 2024b, 227–228). Theophilus thus seems to have wielded power comparable to that of Photius (cf. van Ginkel 1995, 121). The main difference between the two is that John praises Theophilus’s power as it was directed against a common enemy, supposed pagans, yet heavily criticizes its abuse by Photius in the present chapter. A possible reason for John’s enmity towards him may lie in Photius’s mission to Egyptt in 566 “to pacify the churches” (εἰρηνεῦσαι τὰς ἐκκλησίας), as reported by Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6058 (de Boor 1883, 242.6). This mission remains obscure, but it probably included violent measures against the different anti-Chalcedonian factions in Egypt, among them John’s co-religionists, the Severans Booth 2017, 164–165 Mango and Scott 1997, 355 n.3; see 3.1.40.c.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.e (SR) 42.25–26
div.p: ܥܕܡܐ ܕܐܦ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܐ ܘܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܕܡ̈ܕܝܢܬܐ ܟܠܗܝ̣ܢ ܢܥ̣ܪܩܘܢ ܡܢ ܩܕܡܘܗܝ “to the point that even the bishops and clerics of all the cities fled before him”: John of Ephesus no doubt heavily exaggerates in his description of how Photius was supposedly able to exact money for years. Photius appears to have received considerable power from Justin (3.1.32.d), but it is hard to imagine that he could have terrorized the whole Roman east, as John claims. If, for instance, the bishops of “all the cities” had indeed fled before him, this would even have included the patriarchs of Antioch and Jerusalem. We may assume that such an event would have resonated throughout the imperial church and would be known to us from other sources, especially Evagrius’s EH with its focus on the patriarchate of Antioch – nor would the imperial government have tolerated any large destabilization in a region of such strategic importance. John’s evident enmity towards Photius is best explained on the grounds that he may have been a suppressor of the anti-Chalcedonians (see 3.1.32.d and 3.1.32.h).

Despite these restrictions, it is possible that John’s account is based on a kernel of truth – the empire was in a difficult financial situation at the start of Justin’s reign, and this was soon further compounded by war expenditure (Sarris 2011, 227–229). Moreover, tax revenues were impacted by the plague, and tax-evading aristocratic landowners at times passed the financial burden onto the humbler members of society (Sarris 2011, 237–239). As argued by Lin (forthcoming), Photius was a close supporter of Justin. He might also have been involved in the collection of taxes, if the quote John puts in his mouth in 3.1.32.f is trustworthy, and thus have gained a bad reputation – perhaps somewhat similar to the infamous logothete (financial official) Alexander (PLRE 3:43–44 (Martindale 1992)) who exacted large sums of money both for the imperial treasury and for himself in the reconquered Italian territories in the early 540s (Procopius, Wars 7.1.28–33; Secret History 24.9–10 and 26.31–34) Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 2:302–303; 3:147–148 and 163–164. Although John creates the impression that Photius plundered everyone’s property, it nevertheless appears from his account that he collaborated with the imperial government and particularly targeted the elites (see 3.1.32.h and 3.1.32.i). This might be surprising, given that Justin’s reign has been described as “seigneurial” and characterized by a close alignment of imperial policy with the interests of the senatorial aristocracy as is illustrated, for instance, in Novel 149 Miller and Sarris 2018, 961–965 Sarris 2011, 227–229. However, a crackdown on local elites is imaginable in an unruly region such as Palestine in the context of the Samaritan rebellion, and the provincial aristocracy might have enjoyed more peace in other regions of the empire. John’s assertion that “all the regions great and small of the east” (3.1.32) suffered under Photius is, despite his mission to Egypt in 566, likely an exaggeration.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.f (SR) 43.6–7
div.p: ܕܗ̣ܒ ܙܦܠܢ ܠܝ̣ܛܪ̈ܐ܆ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܣܢ̣ܝܩ ܥܠ ܕܗܒܐ ܕܢܦ̇ܩ ܥܠ ܩܪ̈ܒܐ “‘give so many pounds, for the emperor is in need of gold for going out to war’”: John of Ephesus portrays Photius as an unscrupulous imperial tax collector. At first glance, it appears surprising that taxation should have been among his tasks, as this was normally the duty of the city councilors (Jones 1964, 456–458). However, importance and attraction of the city councils dwindled in late antiquity, and the provincial governors and deputies of the praetorian prefecture became increasingly responsible for tax collection. Such officials not only intervened in the tax collection, but also frequently exacted money for themselves (Jones 1964, 759–760). There were repeated attempts at remedying this abuse; Justinian’s Edict 12 from 535, for instance, was issued after a tax collector had plundered the cities of Hellespont and “abstained from absolutely no form of what amounts to utter rapine” (οὐδενὸς ἀπέσχετο παντελῶς τῶν εἰς ἀρπαγὴν ἐσχάτην ἡκόντων) for his personal benefit Miller and Sarris 2018, 1073–1075 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 779. Photius’s case might have been similar, although John of Ephesus is likely to have exaggerated his depredations. Such problems clearly still existed during Justin’s reign; Novel 149.2 from 569 threatened tax collectors who were overcharging the citizens with confiscation of their property Miller and Sarris 2018, 963 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 724. On the other hand, it is also conceivable that Photius mainly acted against local landowners and administrators who had pocketed tax revenues – a situation Justinian deplored in Edict 13 with regard to Egypt Miller and Sarris 2018, 1076–1077 cf. Sarris 2011, 237–238. This could explain why it was mainly the local elites who seem to have feared Photius (see 3.1.32.i), and why he was apparently able to continue his activities for twelve years (see 3.1.32.l) without interference from the central government.

As noted in 3.1.32.e, the empire was in need of revenues because of Justin’s warlike policy – he stopped payments to both Persians and Avars, yet the ensuing wars no doubt turned out more costly than the subsidies had been (Sarris 2011, 227–231). In addition, the long-term effects of the plague, which had first hit the empire in 541/542, may have affected the economy and the taxes considerably. At least John of Ephesus’s claim that Photius was collecting money for the emperor is thus credible.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.g (SR) 43.7
div.p: ܘܗܟܢܐ ܡ̇ܟܢܫ ܗܘܐ ܩ̇ܢܛܝܢܪ̈ܐ “in this way he would gather hundreds of pounds”: ܩ̇ܢܛܝܢܪܐ (“hundred pounds”) originally stems from the Latin centenarium; it entered into Syriac as a loanword in the 6th century via the Hellenized form κεντηνάριον (Butts 2016b, 154). It is mainly attested in the LES where John of Ephesus used this term several times Brooks 1926, 548.12 and 550.7; 1923, 212.11. The amount of gold John refers to here is very considerable; one pound of gold corresponded to 72 solidi, and if Photius indeed demanded three centenaria (i.e. 300 pounds of gold) from the bishop of Ascalon (see 3.1.32.h), this would have amounted to 21,600 solidi. To put this into relation: according to Novel 123.3 from 546, a newly appointed patriarch was to pay no more than 20 pounds of gold (i.e. 1,440 solidi) as an enthronement fee to the clergy of his patriarchate, and bishops of poorer cities sometimes paid as little as twelve solidi (Miller and Sarris 2018, 804–805). In Egypt in the mid-6th century, a man could live on an annual allowance of a little more than two solidi (Jones 1964, 447). Considering this, John may have exaggerated the amounts Photius extracted, yet these figures are not completely impossible if he was indeed collecting outstanding taxes from local elites (see 3.1.32.f).

div.head: EH 3.1.32.g.2 (SR) 43.11–14
div.p: ܘܐܢ ܐܢܫ ܡܬ̇ܥ̣ܣܩ ܗܘܐ ܥܡܗ܆ ܡܚ̣ܕܐ ܕܠܐ ܦܘ̇ܫܐ ܣ̇ܠܩ ܗܘܐ ܠܚܒ̇ܠܐ. ܐ̇ܘ ܒܬܪ ܪܝܫܗ܆ ܐ̇ܘ ܒܪ̈ܦܫܐ ܕܕܪ̈ܥܘܗܝ܆ ܐ̇ܘ ܒܚܕ ܕܪܥܗ “if anyone challenged him, he would immediately without delay be raised up on a noose, either behind his head, or on his shoulders, or on one of his arms”: This method of torture might refer to the eculeus (also attested in the form equuleus; “the little horse”), one of the most common torture instruments used in the Roman empire. This was a wooden frame on which the victims were hung, with their hands bound together and pulled over their heads, and then stretched, presumably with the help of a winch, until their joints were dislocated (Vergote 1972, 120–123 and 133–137).

div.head: EH 3.1.32.h (SR) 43.14–15
div.p: ܥܠ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܕܐ̇ܫܩܠܘܢ “on the bishop of Ascalon”: The identity of this cleric is unknown. Wood (2010, 172 n.39) assumed that he was an anti-Chalcedonian and that Photius’s treatment of him is to be seen in the context of the persecution of the early 570s. Given Photius’s earlier dispatch to Egypt to end inter-confessional strife (3.1.32.d), this is certainly possible, but not entirely convincing: in the second half of the 6th century, every city in the empire had a Chalcedonian bishop, and the anti-Chalcedonian episcopal hierarchy, where extant, usually resided in rural monasteries. We do not have any evidence for such anti-Chalcedonian bishops at that time in the patriarchate of Jerusalem and the province of Palaestina prima, to which Ascalon belonged. The setting of this episode suggests that it took place in the town of Ascalon itself. The large amount of gold (three centenaria; see 3.1.32.g) Photius demanded presupposes that the church was wealthy, and this further underlines that the bishop probably was the Chalcedonian prelate of the city who had access to all its resources. The presence of wealthy people in Ascalon, who may have helped to assemble the amount of gold, is highlighted by Procopius, Secret History 29.17–25 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 3:177–178).

Photius’s brutal treatment of an orthodox bishop is without parallel in late antiquity. We may speculate that the bishop had supported tax evasion: bishops sometimes issued licenses of indemnity to individuals, thus effectively granting them tax exemption. Justinian legislated against this practice in, for instance, Edict 13.28, where he ruled that the loss to the treasury was to be covered by the property of the church in question Miller and Sarris 2018, 1102 and 1076 n.1 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 794–795. John of Ephesus’s biased report renders it impossible to disentangle the exact background to the incident with the bishop of Ascalon. John’s negative attitude towards Photius suggests that he was no friend of the anti-Chalcedonians, as is also suggested by his mission to Egypt (3.1.32.d). It may have been for this reason that Conon was entrusted to him for imprisonment (see 3.1.31.g).

Interestingly, Michael the Syrian added to his summary of this chapter in Chronicle 10.7 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:306.a.14–30; 4:345.b.5–19 that Photius’s pillaging targeted especially the Severans, yet John never explicitly states this. In all likelihood, Michael inferred this because the whole of Book 1 of EH 3 deals with the suppression of the Severans under Justin II. Michael also added another passage not found in John: according to him, Photius was wounded and suffered horribly before he died. Possibly, Michael likened the end of this persecutor, as he perceived him, to that of John Scholasticus (EH 3.2.26) and the quaestor Anastasius (EH 3.2.29) who, so John asserts, indeed were heavily afflicted before their death. Apart from these additions, Michael produced an accurate summary of this chapter, including Photius’s familiary background, his career, and his violent exaction of money.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.i (SR) 43.21–23
div.p: ܥܕܡܐ ܕܙܥ̣ܬ݀ ܐܪܥܐ ܡܢ ܩܕܡܘܗܝ܇ ܘܟܠܗܘܢ ܐ̇ܪ̈ܟܢ̣ܛܘܣ ܘܗܓ̣̈ܡܘܢܐ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܡ̇ܪ̈ܘܬܐ “until the earth shook before him along with the magistrates, governors, and the rest of the dignitaries”: As stated above (3.1.32.e), John of Ephesus very likely exaggerated his description of Photius’s unchecked tyranny. Yet parallel sources attest that he was active in some official capacity and in touch with the emperor. The case of the imperial commissioner Theophilus (see 3.1.32.d) in 579/580 shows that such an official could severely disrupt local power structures and lead to considerable unrest even among the ruling classes. From John’s biased description, it appears that Theophilus’s main task was to find people who only feigned adherence to Christianity (EH 3.3.27–3.3.29), while Photius was exacting money for the imperial treasury, but both missions might have been quite similar in that local elites were held in check and reminded of their duties.

Importantly, as noted in 3.1.32.d, the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 111.24–27) confirms this chapter’s impression that Photius’s power was mainly centered on Palestine, where his monastery also lay. Consequently, when John talks about “magistrates, governors, and the rest of the dignitaries,” he probably introduces the Roman hierarchy in Palestine and the surrounding regions in descending order. In Syriac, the loanwords ܐ̇ܪ̈ܟܢ̣ܛܘܣ ܘܗܓ̣̈ܡܘܢܐ (ἄρχοντες and ἡγεμόνες) are quite vague and can both mean magistrates and governors. However, John only uses ܐ̇ܪ̈ܟܢ̣ܛܘܣ (ἄρχοντες) to refer to the highest ranks, that is to imperial magistrates in Constantinople (such as members of the consistorium). The only provincial governor whom he calls ܐܪܟܘܢ (ἄρχων) is Anatolius (who figures prominently in EH 3.3.27–3.3.33) (PLRE 3:72–73 (Martindale 1992)). Crucially, this Anatolius outranked “normal” provincial governors (viri clarissimi) in that he served as a deputy of the praetorian prefect and was thus probably a vir illustris (on these ranks, see Jones 1964, 528–531). The term ܗܓ̣̈ܡܘܢܐ (ἡγεμόνες), on the other hand, occurs only on one other occasion in the EH, in the quotation of Matthew 10:17–22
(3.1.3.i), where it clearly means “governors.” This insight corresponds well to the administrative division of Palestine in three provinces: as outlined in Justinian’s Novel 103 from 536, the province of Palaestina prima was to be governed by a proconsul of spectabilis rank, whereas Palaestina secunda and tertia had an “ordinary” governor, a vir clarissimus. Moreover, the military commander of the Palestinian provinces, the dux Palaestinae, was also a vir spectabilis Miller and Sarris 2018, 679–684 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 496–499. Consequently, it appears likely that ܐ̇ܪ̈ܟܢ̣ܛܘܣ (ἄρχοντες) here refers to the proconsul of Palaestina prima and the dux Palaestinae (and to other people of the highest ranks who may have lived in Palestine and in the neighboring provinces, if Photius’s powers extended to other regions), whereas ܗܓ̣̈ܡܘܢܐ (ἡγεμόνες) designates the other two provincial governors. The “rest of the dignitaries” would then have included the provincial elites holding power in the cities.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.j (NK) 43.27
div.p: ܚܛܗܐ ܗܢܐ̣ ܒܪܝܫ̣ܝ ܕܝܠܝ “this sin be on my own head”: Brooks emended the manuscript at this point, altering the original wording from ܒܪܝܫ̣ܗ ܕܝܠܗ (“on his head”) to ܒܪܝܫ̣ܝ ܕܝܠܝ (“on my head”). Given that this is part of a dialogue between the emperor and Photius, Brooks’s emendation suggests a shift in responsibility or emphasis, changing the statement from a third-person note about Photius (“on his head”) to a first-person exclamation (“on my head”). The edition accepts Brooks’s proposition and does not follow the principle of lectio difficilior here, assuming that the text found in the manuscript has been corrupted.

There are, however, two alternative interpretations of the phrase as it stands in the manuscript. First, the manuscript reading could be understood as a side note by John of Ephesus, reflecting a condemnation of Photius by John himself that is not part of the direct speech. This would mean that John, as the narrator, interrupts the dialogue momentarily to pass judgment on Photius. Second, the phrase could have originally been a gloss added by a copyist who wished to clarify or emphasize the guilt of Photius. This gloss may then have slipped into the main text.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.k (SR) 43.27–30
div.p: ܗܘ̣ܘ ܠܗ ܕܝܢ ܥܡܗ ܣܝܥ̣ܬܐ ܕܕܝܪ̈ܝܐ ܕܠܗܠܝܢ ܚ̇ܫܚܝܢ ܗܘܘ. ܘܐܦ ܕܘ̈ܣ̣ܦܘܛܝܩܐ ܘܕܡ̣̈ܣܬܝܩܐ ܘܐܣ̣ܦܬܪ̈ܐ̣ ܘܐܦ ܚ̇ܝܠܐ ܕܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ “he had a troop of monks with him who were suitable for these things, along with despotikoi, domestikoi, and spatharioi as well as an army of Roman soldiers”: The composition of Photius’s entourage suggests again that his exaction of money took place in an official function. The sheer size of the group must have commanded respect and immediately identified Photius as an imperial envoy with full powers, including over the nobility, similar to the mission of Theophilus to Syria a few years later (see 3.1.32.d). That some monks accompanied Photius is hardly surprising given his position as abbot, but it is highly improbable that he would have been surrounded by the other people listed here, had he been the gangster John of Ephesus claims. John already mentioned domestikoi in EH 3.1.10.d where they appeared as staff members of the urban prefect. Spatharioi (from σπαθάριος) were high-ranking imperial bodyguards or officers of the imperial bodyguard Rollinger 2024, 86–88 Haldon 1984, 155–160 and 182–190 Jones 1964, 567–568 and John indeed employed the term in this sense in EH 3.6.29–3.6.31. Yet in this passage, it is more likely that he used it to refer to the (commanders of the) bodyguard of Photius. The Syriac word only seems to appear in the EH and in later chronicles dependent on it (Brockelmann 1928, 37). In addition, Photius was apparently also protected by a detachment of soldiers (see 3.1.9.m for John’s tendency to write ܪ̈ܗܘܡܝܐ [“Romans”] for “soldiers”), and the presence of army units close to him is to be expected since one of his tasks was the suppression of the Samaritan rebellion (3.1.32.d). Finally, the exact function of the despotikoi is unknown; the word stems from the adjective δεσποτικός (in the context of secular power meaning “official” or “imperial”) and John is the only Syriac author known to have used it. Brooks (1936, 30.19) plausibly supposed that it also referred to Photius’s bodyguard.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.l (SR) 44.2
div.p: ܙܒܢܐ ܕܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܬܪ̈ܬܥܣܪܐ “a period of twelve years”: This chronological information allows us to establish 577/578 as the time of Photius’s death. Van Ginkel (1995, 73) has noted that the chapters EH 3.1.31–3.1.39 were most likely written in 577/578, as there is mention in them of the death of patriarch John Scholasticus (31 August 577), but not of that of emperor Justin II (4 October 578). Consequently, Photius must have passed prior to Justin’s death after having spent twelve years in his dominant role. Sihong Lin (forthcoming) has argued compellingly that Photius was a close supporter of Justin II, and it is thus no coincidence that he re-emerges in the sources directly after the change on the throne in 565/566, for the first time since his escape from Theodora’s prison, with both the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum and Theophanes noting that Justin gave Photius authority in Palestine and Egypt, respectively de Boor 1883, 242.4–6 Cramer 1839, 111.24–27; see 3.1.31.f and 3.1.32.d. Moreover, an inscription (CIIP 1.800) that is probably dated to 564/565 still names a certain Constantine as abbot of the Nea monastery in Jerusalem (Trampedach 2015, 161–162). Taking all this evidence together, it appears plausible that Photius was appointed abbot of the Nea monastery very soon after Justin’s accession in late 565 and that John of Ephesus’s “twelve years” refer to the period 565/566–577/578 when he had “authority to deal with all people and issues” (ἐξουσίαν […] κατὰ παντὸς προσώπου καὶ πράγματος) in Palestine (Cramer 1839, 111.24–27). Photius’s influence seems thus to have been connected to his position as the abbot of the Nea monastery (on which see 3.1.31.g). This monastery was, together with its church, one of the most important and wealthiest imperial foundations in Palestine, and its leadership was undoubtedly a position of great prestige. However, we do not know if any other of its abbots ever wielded power similar to that of Photius. His case may be unique in that his authority as abbot was further enhanced by his close connection with Justin II.

PLRE 3:1039 (Martindale 1992) states that Photius died either in 578 or in 585. The compilers of the PLRE arrived at 585 by considering that John’s “twelve years” (Brooks (1936, 30.22) by mistake translated “thirteen years”) may have started as late as 572 when Photius was tasked to suppress the Samaritan rebellion (yet this may have taken place earlier, see 3.1.32.d). However, they did not know about the testimony of the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum, which places his dispatch to Palestine in Justin’s first year; moreover, the dating of EH 3.1.32 as explained above renders the late death year of 585 impossible.

div.head: EH 3.1.32.m (SR) 44.4–6
div.p: ܘܩ̣ܡ ܡܢ ܒܬܪܗ ܐܚܪܢܐ ܪܝܫ ܕܝܪܐ ܕܕܝܪܐ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܡܬ̇ܩܪܝܐ ܚ̇ܕܬܐ ܕܒܐܘܪܫܠܡ܆ ܕܫܡܗ̣ ܐܒܪܗܡ “he was succeeded by another abbot of the monastery called the ‘Nea’ in Jerusalem whose name was Abraham”: There are two references to an abbot Abraham of the Nea monastery in John Moschus’s Pratum Spirituale (in stories 68 and 187) written in the late 6th to early 7th century (PG 87/3:2917D and 3064D (Migne 1857–1866)), and it is likely that the same Abraham is introduced here as Photius’s successor. Intriguingly, however, Pratum Spirituale 187 (PG 87/3:3064D (Migne 1857–1866)) states that this Abraham became abbot “after Eudoxius” (μετὰ Εὐδόξιον). Eudoxius is otherwise unknown, yet Lin (forthcoming) and Di Segni (2012, 262) have embraced the argument that this might have been Photius’s monastic pseudonym. As noted in 3.1.32.c, John of Ephesus also seems to indicate that Photius assumed another name upon entering the monastery, and the Pratum Spirituale’s mention of an Eudoxius as Abraham’s predecessor could further strengthen this hypothesis.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This is the first of three subsequent chapters where John of Ephesus deals with matters which involved Severans from Egypt. He reveals here that the pressure to enter into communion with the Chalcedonians was not only directed towards the Severan bishops in Constantinople. The Chalcedonian authorities apparently also attempted to persuade a number of important lay people from Egypt: members of the Alexandrian elite, including the naukleroi responsible for the grain fleet to Constantinople, were brought to Constantinople and detained there for a prolonged period, some of them up to three years before being released. John asserts that not a single one of them gave in to the Chalcedonians and that all remained faithful to their Severan conviction.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.7 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:305.a.34–306.a.5; 4:344.a.38–44: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.33.e

div.head: Parallel Accounts (SR)
div.p: Zuqnin Chronicle ad an. 871 Witakowski 1996, 121–122 Chabot 1933a, 136–138: similar talks with Severans from Egypt under Justinian; see 3.1.33.b

div.head: EH 3.1.33.a (SR) 44.10–11
div.p: ܒܡ̇ܨܥܬܗ ܗܟܝܠ ܕܪܕܘܦܝ̣ܐ ܐܬܟ̣ܬܒ ܘܐܫܬ̇ܕܪ ܠܐܠ̣ܟܣܢܕܪܝܐ “then in the middle of the persecution the Chalcedonians wrote to and sent to Alexandria”: John of Ephesus used an impersonal construction and his phrasing literally translates as “it was written and sent to Alexandria” (see 3.1.35.a for a similar impersonal construction). He is clearly referring to the Chalcedonian authorities in Constantinople, most likely to emperor Justin II himself (see 3.1.33.b). John places this chronologically “in the middle of the persecution,” yet there are good reasons to assume that it happened already quite early, in 571/572, as it was probably the detente after the accession of Tiberius as co-emperor in late 574 which led to the release of the Alexandrians after no more than three years (see 3.1.33.f).

div.head: EH 3.1.33.b (SR) 44.11–12
div.p: ܘܣܠ̣ܩܘ ܣܘ̈ܦ̣ܝܣܛܐ ܘܣܟ̈ܘ̣ܠܣܬܝܩܐ ܘܢܘܩ̣ܠܝܪ̈ܐ܇ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ “sophists, scholastikoi, naukleroi, and many others went up”: John of Ephesus introduces here the educated Alexandrian elite and presents these circles as supporters of Severan Christology (cf. van Ginkel 1995, 120). “Sophists” (see also 3.1.19.d) were rhetoricians or, more broadly, people from the upper ranks of society who had enjoyed a rhetorical education. Possibly, John refers here to teachers of rhetoric active in Alexandria. Alexandria was a city famous for its educational centers, which may explain the inclusion of “sophists,” but legal studies were, since Justinian, only taught in Constantinople and Berytus (the modern Beirut) (Jones 1964, 512–513 and 999). Consequently, when mentioning scholastikoi (σχολαστικός was a widespread title for legal scholars and people with legal education), John probably thought of practicing lawyers and officials with a legal background. Note that scholastikoi is incorrectly written in the manuscript (ܣܟ̈ܠܘ̣ܣܬܝܩܐ instead of ܣܟ̈ܘ̣ܠܣܬܝܩܐ); the Greek loanword was probably unknown to the scribe and he therefore transposed the two consonants ܘ and ܠ by mistake. However, in the chapter heading, the word is spelled correctly.

The naukleroi (a loanword taken from the Greek ναυκλῆρος) were shipowners and thus also belonged to the wealthier layers of society. The Egyptian naukleroi were organized in an association and particularly important: they were responsible for the grain transports from Alexandria to Constantinople and thus played a crucial role in the capital’s food supply. As a recompensation for their efforts, they received payment and enjoyed privileges such as exemption from curial duties (Jones 1964, 827–829).

There is an interesting passage in the Zuqnin Chronicle which provides more background for this chapter Witakowski 1996, 121–122 Chabot 1933a, 136–138. The Chronicle’s anonymous compiler dated this passage, which he took from John’s lost second part of the EH, to the year 871 of the Seleucid era (corresponding to 559/560). John asserts there that all the Alexandrian naukleroi were “believers” (ܡܗܝ̈ܡܢܐ), that is, Severan anti-Chalcedonians (likely an exaggeration, although it is not doubtful that at least some of them had Severan sympathies). Justinian wished to debate over the correct faith with them when they had arrived in Constantinople with the grain from Egypt. They suggested the emperor better talk to learned men such as monastic leaders and scholastikoi about doctrinal questions, and they promised to bring some of them for that purpose the next time they would come to Constantinople. This, so John’s account continues, indeed happened and the representatives from Egypt repeatedly debated with Justinian during a whole year; the emperor finally dismissed them when realizing that he could not convince them of the orthodoxy of Chalcedon. However, John closes this story by mentioning that similar discussions with such groups happened again.

It is indeed conceivable that such debates took place more often, also under Justin in the context of the attempted union of 571, and that John took his earlier account from the second part as an example when writing the present chapter, hence the many parallels. Ever since the imposition of a Chalcedonian episcopal hierarchy in Egypt in 536 (see 3.1.5.a and 3.1.21.b), Justinian (and Justin II) suppressed attempts by anti-Chalcedonians to ordain their own bishops in Egypt (see 3.1.32.d for Photius’s involvement in this in c. 566). This policy changed only in the mid-570s with Tiberius’s rise to power (Booth 2017). Given Egypt’s crucial importance for the food provision of Constantinople, it would only be logical if the emperors attempted to win the Alexandrian elites for Chalcedon so as to minimize the potential for religious unrest which might endanger the smooth grain transports. Note that John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 401) briefly mentions a suppression of anti-Chalcedonians in Egypt under Justin. Taken together with the evidence of this chapter, this might suggest that the attempts to impose Chalcedonian orthodoxy at least among the elite were not limited to Constantinople (see 3.1.11.b and 3.1.19.c).

div.head: EH 3.1.33.c (SR) 44.12–15
div.p: ܒܚܘ̣ܫܒܐ ܡ̇ܢ ܟܣ̣ܝܐ܆ ܐܝܟ ܕܢܬ̇ܒܥܘܢ ܐܢܘܢ ܕܢܬ̇ܩܪܒܘܢ ܒܣܘܢܗܕܣ. ܒܐܣܟܡܐ ܕܝܢ ܒ̇ܪܝܐ܆ ܘܡܛܘܠ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ. ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܐܦ ܒܥ̇ܒܕܐ̣ ܒܝܕ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܗܝܢ ܐܬ̇ܚܫܚܘ ܠܘܬܗܘܢ “with on the one hand the hidden intention of demanding that they receive communion with the synod, but on the other hand with the outward appearance of having done this for the sake of the union, while having in fact both of these intentions in regard to them”: This complicated sentence does not introduce a new subject after the preceding “sophists, scholastikoi, naukleroi, and many others,” and from a grammatical point of view, it was thus they who demanded to receive the Chalcedonian communion. However, this does not make any sense since the following passage 3.1.33.d underscores that they actually rejected the Chalcedonian communion, which is in line with their usual behavior in the mid-6th-century as reported in the Zuqnin Chronicle (3.1.33.b). Consequently, the inner logic of the chapter rather demands to introduce the Chalcedonian authorities in Constantinople as the implied subject. As shown in the previous note, Justinian strove to win the Egyptian elite for Chalcedon, and we may assume that both his successor Justin and patriarch John Scholasticus shared that goal. John of Ephesus’s argument here is in line with his description of the debate about Justin’s edict on faith earlier in Book 1: also there, he asserts that the Chalcedonians only feigned to be interested in a sincere union (which, according to John, would have needed to include a condemnation of Chalcedon), whereas they never intended to condemn the council and instead wanted to trick the Severans into communion (3.1.21.c; 3.1.22.f; 3.1.24.d).

div.head: EH 3.1.33.d (SR) 44.15–18
div.p: ܟܕ ܒܚܪܬܐ̣ ܕܢܬ̇ܩܪܒܘܢ ܡܬ̇ܬܒܥܝܢ ܗܘܘ. ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܕܝܢ ܐܬܚ̣ܪ̈ܝܘ ܘܕܪ̈ܫ̣ܘ ܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ: ܐܝܟ ܫ̇ܢܬܐ ܚܕܐ̣ ܓܢܒܪܐܝܬ. ܟܕ ܣܟ̣ ܠܐ ܡܬ̇ܪܦܝܢ ܐ̇ܘ ܡܬ̣ܪܡܝܢ ܗܘܘ “when the Chalcedonians ultimately demanded that they receive communion, they resisted and debated valiantly for many days, about one year, without growing timid or yielding at all”: John of Ephesus repeatedly assures readers of the EH that all attempts to bring Severans into accepting the Chalcedonian communion were futile. This is likely exaggerated and the more moderate ones on both sides of the debate may have been ready to accept solutions for the doctrinal divide, for example Justin’s edict on faith (see 3.1.17.j; 3.1.20.f; 3.1.24.j). Unlike in his account in the Zuqnin Chronicle about the debates that took place with Justinian (3.1.33.b), John does not specify here with whom the Egyptians actually discussed their faith. The sources do not attribute to Justin II the same predilection for theological debate as to his predecessor Justinian. While the participation of Justin himself cannot be excluded, it is more probable that their interlocutors were Chalcedonian representatives of the same rank, such as clerics and lawyers working for the imperial court or the patriarchate.

In the summary of this chapter in his Chronicle 10.7, Michael the Syrian similarly states that the discussions lasted for a whole year and that no one gave in Chabot 1899–1910, 2:305.a.34–306.a.5; 4:344.a.38–44. However, he omits the naukleroi and only speaks of sophists and scholastikoi. The reader who does not know about the naukleroi in John’s chapter is thus left rather puzzled when Michael then tells us that those sophists and scholastikoi had to be liberated because of the grain transports.

div.head: EH 3.1.33.e (SR) 44.19–22
div.p: ܒܚܪܬܐ ܕܝܢ ܡܢܗܘܢ ܐܫ̣ܬܪܝܘ܆ ܡܛܠ ܕܕܚ̣ܠܘ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܫܠܝܛܝܢ ܠܡܥ̣ܒܕ ܐܢܘܢ ܒܩ̣ܛܝܪܐ܇ ܡܛܘܠ ܡ̇ܣܩܬܐ ܕܚ̈ܛܐ ܕܡܢ ܬܡ̇ܢ ܠܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ ܡܫܬ̇ܕܪ̈ܢ “but eventually some of them were released because those who were capable of doing this by force feared for the shipment of wheat which was sent from there to the imperial city”: Those who were released after about one year were probably the naukleroi. After a prolonged absence from Alexandria, they would have needed to return in order to prevent eventual difficulties with the transport of the new harvest to Constantinople. This passage indicates that John of Ephesus was aware of the importance of Egypt for the grain supply (Leppin 2019, 121–122). The provision of ample food to the capital’s inhabitants was of utmost importance to any emperor. Shortages could easily lead to riots and for this reason, Constantine had already implemented the system of annual grain shipments from Egypt. Some inhabitants received their rations for free, yet many more were able to purchase grain for subsidized prices Van Dam 2022; Jones 1964, 696–701. According to some estimates, the grain imported from Egypt in the mid-6th century satisfied the demand of 600,000–800,000 Constantinopolitans Miller and Sarris 2018, 1082 n.28 Jones 1964, 698.

Justinian’s Edict 13.24 Miller and Sarris 2018, 1098–1100 Schöll and Kroll 1895, 792–793 specified that the grain for Constantinople had to be brought to Alexandria by 10 September. The grain fleet would then arrive at the capital in October, before the onset of winter. These indications may allow us to establish a tentative chronological time frame for the stay of the Egyptian sophists, scholastikoi, and naukleroi in Constantinople. In all likelihood, they arrived with the grain fleet in autumn, either in 571 or in 572; the naukleroi may then have been released in late summer of the following year to be back in Alexandria in time for the new shipment. Considering the information in 3.1.33.f about the release of all the Egyptians after three years, a start of the discussions in 571 (thus quite soon after the imprisonment of the Severan bishops narrated in 3.1.29.f) seems slightly more likely, as the end of the three years would then have coincided with the detente associated with Justin’s illness and the rise of the more tolerant Tiberius in 574 Roggo 2022, 118–120 Booth 2017, 169–171.

div.head: EH 3.1.33.f (SR) 44.22–23
div.p: ܘܡܢܗܘܢ ܐܬܠܒ̣ܟܘ̣ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܬܠܬ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ. ܘܡܕܡ̣ ܠܐ ܗ̣ܘܐ. ܘܗܟܢܐ̣ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܐܫܬ̣ܪܝܘ “some of them were apprehended for as long as three years, but nothing happened. In this way everyone was released”: As argued in the previous note 3.1.33.e, the release of the Egyptians might have taken place towards the end of 574 when the ascendancy of the more tolerant Tiberius became irreversible. John of Ephesus’s assurance that the frequent discussions with them had been unsuccessful and that not a single one of them had abandoned Severan orthodoxy repeats the same claim he makes in the Zuqnin Chronicle (see 3.1.33.b) about the debates that had occurred under Justinian. The end of their detention in Constantinople forms a parallel with John’s own release into house arrest after three years of imprisonment (EH 3.2.7.f) and the release of Conon, which likewise happened after three years (3.1.31.h). All these instances are indicative of a changing approach towards the anti-Chalcedonians under the new Caesar Tiberius; the situation of the opponents to Chalcedon appears to have improved considerably around the mid-570s (see 3.1.36.f).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: This short chapter offers an anticipatory glimpse into the schism which erupted among the Egyptian Severans in 575, when two rival patriarchs of Alexandria were ordained, Theodore and Peter. John of Ephesus discusses this schism and its momentous consequences for the Severans in the whole empire at length in Book 4. Here, he does not provide any detail with regard to the reasons for the schism, but he gives a crucial piece of information about how the authorities reacted to it. On imperial orders, leading representatives of the Egyptian Severans were arrested and brought to Constantinople. There, they remained for at least a year and were finally released after the return of patriarch Eutychios in late 577. In EH 3.4.37, John continues the account of those clerics and reveals that they were deported to Constantinople again already shortly afterwards, in May 579.

div.head: EH 3.1.34.a (SR) 44.27
div.p: ܐܬܟ̣ܬܒ ܠܡ̇ܠܟܐ “it was written to the emperor”: The identity of the emperor in question here is not entirely clear. The two rival patriarchs Theodore and Peter were ordained in summer 575 (possibly on 23 June and 4 August 575 Brooks 1930, 474 n.4 but see Booth 2017, 167 n.82). “The emperor” could hence be either Justin II or Tiberius, who had been elevated to the rank of Caesar in December 574. Yet it appears more likely that Justin is meant here: John of Ephesus has not yet introduced Tiberius at this point in the EH. He first mentions him in 3.1.37, and there, he calls him Caesar to distinguish him from Justin. It is true that Justin often was incapacitated after 573 and considered unfit to rule, but despite his illness, he still had occasional lucid moments when he was able to act as emperor; for instance, the sources attest that he managed to give a moving speech when crowning Tiberius (see EH 3.3.5). Moreover, as noted in 3.1.33.e and 3.1.33.f, the rise of Tiberius led to a detente in the relations with the anti-Chalcedonians; some imprisoned Severans were released and the ordination of Severan bishops in Egypt in large numbers became possible in the mid-570s (Booth 2017, 169–171). Consequently, it makes more sense to ascribe to Justin the imperial order reported in this chapter. However, the responsibility of Tiberius cannot be completely ruled out. Later in his reign, in 578, he condoned reimprisoning Severan leaders including John of Ephesus (EH 3.3.15) in order to counter the rising criticism of his tolerant attitude towards religious minorities (Roggo 2022, 125–127).
