Given the high prestige of the see of Alexandria, it is imaginable that he reacted to similar pressure when news of the Severan ordinations there reached Constantinople; see 3.1.34.c on the importance to prevent the ordination of rival patriarchs in Alexandria.

div.head: EH 3.1.34.b (SR) 44.27–29
div.p: ܕܐ̈ܦܝܣܩܦܐ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ܆ ܥܒ̣ܕܘ ܚܠܦ ܛ̇ܘܒܢ̣ܐ ܬܐܕܘܣܝܘܣ. ܠܘ ܒܠܚܘܕ ܚܕ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ܆ ܐܠܐ̣ ܬܪ̈ܝܢ “that the orthodox bishops had ordained not only one bishop but two as the successor of the blessed Theodosius”: Theodosius (d. 566) did not have a direct successor as the Severan patriarch of Alexandria. Possibly, the dispatch of Photius to Egypt (3.1.32.d) early in Justin’s reign served to prevent the Severans there from ordaining a new patriarch and creating parallel ecclesial structures (Booth 2017, 164–165). Phil Booth (2017, 169–171) has argued that it was only after the ascendancy of Tiberius to imperial power in late 574 that pressure on the Severans loosened and that they could envisage ordaining a successor to Severus. Booth’s conclusions remain convincing although he was unaware of the present chapter which indicates that the imperial authorities still exerted some pressure on the Severans in the mid-570s. John of Ephesus presents the intricate events which divided the Severans in Book 4 for an overview, see Booth 2017, 165–168 Allen 2013; Brooks 1930; Hermann 1928. At this point, it suffices to note that Theodore of Rhamnis, the abbot of a monastery in the Scetis valley close to Alexandria, was ordained Severan patriarch in summer 575 with the approval of Paul of Antioch (on him, see 3.1.17.d) (EH 3.1.40.d and 3.4.10). However, an important faction of the Alexandrian Severans soon resented what they perceived as interference by the see of Antioch into the affairs of Alexandria (EH 3.4.11–3.4.12). This criticism was all the stronger because of the personal past of Paul of Antioch, who had accepted Chalcedonian communion in 571, recanted, and repented for a period of three years (EH 3.2.8 and 3.4.15). As a result of this lapse, his authority among the Severans was irrevocably damaged. The Alexandrian deacon Peter was hence ordained as counter-patriarch to Theodore about six weeks later. Brooks (1930, 474 n.4) established the date of 23 June 575 for the ordination of Theodore, and 4 August for that of Peter, yet this is not completely certain (Booth 2017, 167 n.82). Throughout Book 4, John of Ephesus laments the Alexandrian schism; he considers Peter’s ordination as uncanonical and supports the side of Theodore (see EH 3.1.40.e and 3.4.12).

div.head: EH 3.1.34.c (SR) 45.2–3
div.p: ܘܐܬܠܒ̣ܟܘ ܘܣܠ̣ܩܘ܆ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܕܝܘܚܢܢ ܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ “they were apprehended and went up until after the death of the patriarch John”: Patriarch John Scholasticus (on him see 3.1.5.e) died on 31 August 577. This is the first passage in the EH which claims that anti-Chalcedonians were also targeted outside the patriarchate of Constantinople. Earlier instances of imprisonment of Severan bishops all took place in Constantinople itself or in Asia Minor. John of Ephesus reports twice (see 3.1.11.b and 3.1.12.i) that John Scholasticus wrote in 571 to other regions in order to incite the bishops there to also act against the anti-Chalcedonians, but we do not have any concrete evidence whether the persecution, as it is presented by John of Ephesus, indeed happened in the other patriarchates. Possibly, a short note in John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 401) about a persecution of opponents to Chalcedon in Egypt during Justin’s reign refers to this instance.

This chapter does not reveal who the “chief clerics” deported to Constantinople were. There is no evidence that the two rival patriarchs Theodore and Peter, nor any of the bishops who ordained them, were ever brought to the capital. John’s claim that “all the chief clerics” were apprehended is thus certainly exaggerated. In EH 3.4.38, he mentions that one of the leaders of the Alexandrian Severans who were again (in 579, that is, after the instance narrated in the present chapter) confined in Constantinople was the archpriest Theodosius (on him, see Booth (2017, 165–166)). According to EH 3.4.9, this Theodosius was crucial for the establishment of a new Severan patriarch in Alexandria in that he invited and encouraged the bishops who were to perform the ordinations in 575. Considering the imperial anger with the new ordinations, we may safely assume that the authorities tried to get hold of those clerics who were considered as responsible for them.

As noted in 3.1.5.e; 3.1.11.e; 3.1.19.n, John of Ephesus puts all the blame for the hardship the Severans had to suffer on John Scholasticus and minimizes the responsibility of Justin. However, this chapter shows that the emperor may also have been invested in the debate and may have taken a personal interest in preventing the new Severan ordinations in Alexandria. John of Ephesus’s phrasing suggests that it was the fact that two rival bishops, Theodore and Peter, had been ordained which caused the emperor’s anger, and implies that a single ordination would have been accepted. Yet this is not very probable, at least if the emperor in question was Justin (see 3.1.34.a): ever since his accession in 565, he had suppressed tendencies of Egyptian anti-Chalcedonians to establish a parallel episcopal hierarchy (Booth 2017, 164–168). A new ordination in the see of Alexandria was particularly dangerous in that regard since the patriarch of Alexandria traditionally had the right to ordain all the other Egyptian bishops, and this is exactly what the new Severan patriarch Peter did (EH 3.4.12 and 3.4.16).

div.head: EH 3.1.34.d (SR) 45.3–4
div.p: ܘܗܝܕܝܢ ܐ̣ܘܛܘܟ ܗܘ̣ܐ܇ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܩܕܡܘܗܝ “then came Eutychius, who had been patriarch before him”: Eutychius had been John Scholasticus’s predecessor as patriarch of Constantinople from 552 to 565 (see 3.1.36.e). He became the victim of a coup in early 565, when a circle who wanted to assure the accession to the throne of the future emperor Justin II plotted against him Roggo 2022, 72–78; 2019, 440–445 Averil Cameron 1988, 234–235. John Scholasticus, a close ally of Justin, was personally involved in these machinations Roggo 2022, 72–73 Lin 2021, 138 van den Ven 1965, 320–323. The plotters managed to get the old Justinian, who had recently fallen out with Eutychius because of a doctrinal dispute, to replace Eutychius with John Scholasticus. Eutychius was sent into exile, yet, due to his good connections with the Caesar Tiberius, allowed to return after John Scholasticus’s death on 31 August 577. John of Ephesus (EH 3.2.31–3.2.34) attests that Eutychius was, during his second patriarchate 577–582, full of hatred towards John Scholasticus, whom he perceived as a usurper, and attempted to undo all his actions and erase everything that served as a reminder of his patriarchate Roggo 2022, 6–8 and 172–81 Averil Cameron 1990, 215; 1988, 239–242; see also 3.1.36.e.

div.head: EH 3.1.34.e (SR) 45.4–5
div.p: ܘܥ̣ܠ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܘܫ̣ܪܐ ܐܢܘܢ܆ ܒܬܪ ܫ̇ܢܬܐ ܚܕܐ “he arrived after John’s death and released the bishops after one year”: Eutychius arrived in Constantinople from his place of exile in Amasea in northern Asia Minor on 3 October 577, a little over a month after John Scholasticus’s death on 31 August. The way John of Ephesus phrased this passage does not make it clear whether the Egyptian Severans were released one year after Eutychius’s reinstallation as patriarch (hence in October 578) or after having spent one year in total detained in Constantinople (which would mean that they arrived in Constantinople in late 576 and that Eutychius released them in late 577). While this question cannot be answered securely, the latter option appears to be more likely. If Eutychius had released them in October 578, one year after his return, this would admittedly sit well with the death of Justin (on 4 October 578), who was probably responsible for their deportation to Constantinople (see 3.1.34.a). However, it would not sit well with the reports (EH 3.3.13 and 3.3.26) that Tiberius agreed to a crackdown on minoritarian Christian groups, including Severans, soon after his coronation as Augustus on 26 September 578 (Roggo 2022, 125–126). Moreover, EH 3.4.37 attests that the same Severan clerics were again deported to Constantinople and arrived there in May 579. To assume a release date in late 578 would only allow for little time for their travel to Egypt, their re-imprisonment, and their journey back to Constantinople. In contrast, a period of one year for their detention in Constantinople from late 576 to late 577 seems more realistic: the two rival patriarchs Theodore and Peter had been ordained in summer 575, this news and the imperial order needed to travel to Constantinople and back, and the clerics such as the archpriest Theodosius (see 3.1.34.c) first needed to be found and arrested. This explains why they may have arrived in Constantinople only more than a year after the contentious ordinations. Their release soon after Eutychius’s return in late 577 would also make sense considering that John Scholasticus, together with Justin, had been one of the main actors of the anti-Severan repression in the early 570s. After his death, it might have been easier for Tiberius to assert himself against the incapacitated Justin and to set them free, which would be in line with his relative disentanglement from doctrinal politics Roggo 2022, 118–120 Booth 2017, 169–171. In this case, Eutychius would merely have executed Tiberius’s orders and not have been responsible himself for their release, which is indeed likely given his staunchly anti-Severan stance as noted by John of Ephesus (EH 3.2.35–3.2.39 and 3.3.15–20).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus records that in addition to the people mentioned in the two previous chapters, there were also solitaries, holy men of great reputation, transported from Egypt to Constantinople. However, this did not happen with the aim of bringing them into Chalcedonian communion; in fact, John does not indicate whether those solitaries were pro- or anti-Chalcedonians. They were rather supposed to predict the future to Justin and Sophia, possibly in connection with Justin’s illness (see 3.1.35.d). Some of them refused and declared that they were unable to do so, but others gave in and were treated with much honor in Constantinople. However, John does not reveal what exactly they predicted to the emperor.

div.head: EH 3.1.35.a (NK) 45.9
div.p: ܙܒ̈ܢܝܢ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܢ ܬܘܒ ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪܘ ܠܐܓ̣ܝܦܛܘܣ “now, many times envoys would be sent to Egypt”: Similar to 3.1.33.a, this sentence is construed without an explicit subject. In his edition, Brooks (1935, 45.9) emended the verb to the singular form (ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪ), interpreting the sentence as impersonal. However, the manuscript clearly reads the third person plural, “they were sent” (ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪܘ). Brooks’s emendation to the singular is unnecessary because the third person plural can naturally convey an unnamed or unspecified subject, rather than an impersonal construction. The critical point is that the verb in this case is passive, emphasizing that envoys were sent without explicitly naming who sent them.

Thus, Brooks’s revision does not account for the possibility that the plural form might have been used to indicate the involvement of multiple envoys. John of Ephesus may have intended to emphasize that several individuals were sent on several occasions, and the plural form (ܐܫܬ̇ܕܪܘ) preserves this nuance.

div.head: EH 3.1.35.b (SR) 45.9–11
div.p: ܘܐܬܩ̣ܪ̈ܝܘ ܘܐܬ̣ܘ ܝܚ̈ܝܕܝܐ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܣ̣ܒܝܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ܇ ܕܝ̇ܕܥܝܢ ܟ̣ܣܝ̈ܬܐ܇ ܘܡܬ̇ܒܝܢܝܢ ܥܠ ܥܬ̣̈ܝܕܬܐ “many solitaries would then be summoned and come to the imperial city who were thought to know hidden things and to have an understanding of things to come”: Holy men were often thought to possess special powers by the grace of God because of their ascetic lives of prayer and their eagerness to imitate Jesus Christ in their contempt for the material world. Such qualities were accessible to ascetics throughout the empire, but Egypt may have held a special place in that regard since it was the birthplace of the first famous Christian hermit, Anthony (d. 356), who was revered throughout the empire. Emperor Theodosius was in close contact with the holy man John of Lycopolis (d. 394) who is credited with having accurately predicted the emperor’s victories in war (Augustine, De civitate Dei 5.26 (Dombart and Kalb 1955, 161); Rufinus, EH 11.32 (Schwartz 1908, 1036); Palladius, Lausiac History 35.1 (Bartelink 2001, 168); Theodoret, EH 5.24.1–2 (Hansen and Parmentier 1998, 324)). Yet despite such early luminaries of Egyptian monasticism, John of Ephesus does not usually praise the Egyptian ascetics and monks of his own time. He rather sees them as prone to conflict and inconsistent in their behavior, especially with regard to the Alexandrian schism that erupted in 575 (see 3.1.34.b). Interestingly, as observed by van Ginkel (1995, 193), John does not specify whether the solitaries in question here were pro- or anti-Chalcedonians. Given his criticism of those who agreed to predict the future for Justin (3.1.35.e), we may assume that there were a few Severans among them – if there had only been pro-Chalcedonians, he might have denounced the recourse of the “synodites” to astrological-heretical practices more specifically.

In addition to this Christian dimension, Egypt was famous in antiquity as a source of arcane knowledge. An example of this is the wide appeal of Hermetism (a set of beliefs centered around Hermes Trismegistos, the hellenized name of Thoth, the Egyptian god of wisdom) and its astrological and magical practices (Moreschini 2018). Of course, nothing in this chapter suggests that the solitaries were secretly engaging in pagan hermetic practices. Yet the strong influence Egypt had exerted throughout antiquity on Greco-Roman astrology may partly explain why John of Ephesus depicted Egypt, in the words of Leppin (2019, 122), as a region with “somehow exotic peculiarities” and why he ascribed in particular to Egyptian solitaries the capability of foreseeing the future.

div.head: EH 3.1.35.c (SR) 45.11–13
div.p: ܐܝܟ ܕܢܘ̇ܕܥܘܢ ܕܟܡܐ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܚ̇ܝܐ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܘܡ̇ܠܟܬܐ܇ ܘܡܢܐ ܥܬ̣ܝܕ ܕܢܗܘܐ ܒܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܗ܆ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܕܐܝܟ ܗܠܝܢ “in order that they might make known how many years the emperor and empress would live, what was going to happen during his reign, and other such things”: Astrological inquiries into the lifespan and the death date of individuals, and particularly the emperor, had been forbidden since the time of Augustus. Yet by addressing these questions to revered holy men who were thought to have received their special knowledge through the grace of God, Justin is not presented as having resorted to astrology, which had been outlawed by increasingly harsh legislation since the 4th century (on these laws, see Mace 2018). Holy men were often credited with predicting the reign, or the length of the reign, of an emperor, and unlike “pagan” astrological practices, this does not seem to have been problematic. On the contrary, (rumors of) such predictions rather underlined their claim to holiness. For example, according to their hagiographies, patriarch Eutychius (d. 582) and Symeon Stylites the Younger (d. 592) predicted the reigns of Justin and Maurice Laga 1992, 60.1850–61.1881 and 61.1900–1929 van den Ven 1962, 177.203 Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 217.3–7 cf. Roggo 2022, 74; in addition, Eutychius is also said to have foreseen the accession of Tiberius (Laga 1992, 61.1883–1900). Moreover, a few decades later, the hagiographer of Theodore of Sykeon (d. 613) asserted that the holy man foresaw both the accession and the death of Maurice and, at a later point, announced a series of calamities and a reign of thirty years for Heraclius (r. 610–641) Festugière 1970, 46.54; 95.119; 106.134; 153.166.

div.head: EH 3.1.35.d (SR) 45.15–17
div.p: ܡ̇ܡܠܠܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܕܝ̣ܢ ܥܠ ܡ̇ܪܬܝܢܘܬܐ ܘܕܝܢ̣ܐ ܘܙܕܝܩܘܬܐ. ܕܟܕ ܗܠܝܢ ܢܣܬ̇ܥܪ̈ܢ܆ ܗܢ̣ܝܢ ܡ̇ܖ̈ܥܝܢ ܠܐܠܗܐ̣ ܘܡ̇ܩܪ̈ܒܢ ܠܘܬܗ “they would rather speak about admonition, judgment, and righteousness: that should these come about, they would please God and be as offerings brought before him”: John of Ephesus has some solitaries declare that Justin and Sophia should rather reconcile themselves with God through pious behavior and thus, knowing Him by their side, be prepared for all eventualities, instead of wishing to learn what exactly the future holds in store for them. It is tempting to read this episode in connection with Justin’s illness and with attempts by the imperial couple to find remedies. The emperor’s health took a decisive turn for the worse in 573 after news about the defeat against the Persians at Dara reached Constantinople, forcing him to proclaim Tiberius Caesar at the end of 574 Pfeilschifter 2013, 142–147 Averil Cameron 1976b. The situation was no doubt dangerous for Justin’s hold on the throne since an incapacitated emperor had to live in constant fear of an overthrow Roggo 2024a, 100–104; Pfeilschifter 2013, 142–147. Finding a cure or learning something about their future would therefore reasonably have been a top priority for the imperial couple in the midst of an existential threat, and this context might explain the consultation of solitaries. Crucially, another source, the Life of Symeon the Younger 208–210 (van den Ven 1962, 179–181), also relates to what lengths those close to Justin went in order to improve his health: they had him examined and treated by a Jewish doctor and even allowed that a female soothsayer was introduced to him in order to learn about his future. The Life is evidently highly biased; its main aim in this episode is to show that Justin would have been healed, had he exclusively trusted on the force of Symeon’s prayers. Yet it still offers an interesting parallel to the consultation of solitaries with knowledge about the future. Moreover, the insistence of some of them on righteous and just behavior and pleasing God sits very well with the reason for Justin’s illness as presented by John of Ephesus: at the start of Book 3 (EH 3.3.2 and 3.3.4), John does not give the shock about the defeat in battle as its cause, but rather Justin’s unrighteous and unjust behavior as a persecutor of the Severans, which triggered divine retribution.

div.head: EH 3.1.35.e (SR) 45.21–22
div.p: ܐܚܪ̈ܢܐ ܕܝܢ ܟܕ ܫ̇ܦܪܝܢ ܠܒ̈ܢܝܢܫܐ܆ ܐܬܪ̈ܡ̣ܝܘ ܠܪܦ̣ܝܘܬܐ ܕܥܠ̣ܬܐ ܗ̇ܝ ܕܒܚܪܬܐ ܐܬܟܣ̣ܬ݀ “but others, who were people-pleasers, acquiesced to weakness in this matter, which eventually would be rebuked”: As noted in 3.1.35.b, the absence of any doctrinal criticism by John of Ephesus can be read to suggest that there were possibly also Severan solitaries among those who acquiesced and told the emperor about the future. John does not say what they told him, but he clearly disagrees with them. He might have objected to predictions of the future in general; moreover, his disagreement might also have stemmed from hindsight: he wrote this chapter in late 577 or in the first half of 578, before the death of Justin in October of that year (van Ginkel 1995, 73), but by that time, Justin had been ill for about four years without any lasting improvement to his situation. Consequently, most of their predictions had probably already turned out to be wrong, and this may have been a further inducement for John to disassociate himself even from those solitaries who perhaps shared his Severan convictions. The failure of those solitaries to deliver accurate predictions might also explain the short phrase ܕܒܚܪܬܐ ܐܬܟܣ̣ܬ݀ “which eventually would be rebuked”: it is conceivable that their renown as solitaries suffered because they had taken advantage of the situation and indulged in a comfortable life in Constantinople without actually living up to the expectations.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus returns to events in and close to Constantinople in EH 3.1.36–3.1.39. In this chapter, he again discusses the suppression of the Severan monasteries in Constantinople, a subject he already treated in EH 3.1.10 and 3.1.11. Again, he mainly focuses on the fate of the female convents (see 3.1.10.b; 3.1.10.h; 3.1.10.k). He repeats some points he made in those chapters – Chalcedonian clerics were installed in all the monasteries; the Severan monastics were forced to accept the Chalcedonian communion; those who resisted were relegated to other, Chalcedonian, convents; patriarch John Scholasticus had images of himself put up (see 3.1.10.f; 3.1.10.j; 3.1.11.c; 3.1.11.f; 3.1.11.j). Yet in the second part of the chapter, he takes the narration further to the period after the death of John Scholasticus in 577. The new patriarch Eutychius (sed. 577–582) ordered that all the images of John Scholasticus be taken down and replaced with his own portraits. Moreover, John of Ephesus indicates that there also was a detente of the measures directed against the Severans: the majority of the monastic women eventually reverted to the Severan communion, and only a small number remained faithful to Chalcedon. These “bi-confessional” monasteries are further treated in EH 3.2.47.

div.head: EH 3.1.36.a (SR; NK) 45.29–46.1
div.p: ܟܕ ܓܝܪ ܐܬܕܒ̣ܪ̈ܝ ܩܛܝ̣ܪܐܝܬ ܘܕܠܐ ܢܡ̇ܘܣ: ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܟܠܗܝܢ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܐܬܐ̣ܡܪ ܡܢ ܠܥܠ “when all the monasteries mentioned above were being treated violently and lawlessly”: This refers back to EH 3.1.10 and 3.1.11 where John of Ephesus describes the suppression of the Severan monasteries in Constantinople in 571 (see 3.1.10.f; 3.1.10.j; 3.1.11.c). The present chapter was, together with EH 3.1.31–3.1.39, written some years after the first thirty chapters of Book 1, after the death of John Scholasticus (31 August 577) and the return of Eutychius as patriarch (3 October 577), but before the death of Justin II (4 October 578) (van Ginkel 1995, 73). As indicated in EH 3.2.7.f, John of Ephesus was liberated from house arrest after the death of John Scholasticus, and it appears that by composing this chapter, he updated his account on the fate of the Severan monasteries that he had written during his captivity (perhaps as early as 572/573 (Roggo 2022, 103–104)). For monasteries opposed to Chalcedon in Constantinople in the 6th century, see 3.1.10.a.

Since ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ (“monasteries”) is feminine plural, the ending of the verb “were being treated” must be corrected from the masculine ܐܬܕܒ̣ܪ̈ܘ, attested in the manuscript, to the feminine form ܐܬܕܒ̣ܪ̈ܝ. The same error occurs in EH 3.1.10.a. Apart from these two instances, ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ is regularly construed with a feminine verb form, for example in 3.1.36.f; 3.2.47; 3.5.6.

div.head: EH 3.1.36.b (SR; HL) 46.1–6
div.p: ܘܙܥܘܪ̈ܐ ܕܒܗܝܢ ܐܫܬ̇ܘܬܦܘ: ܘܫܪܟܐ ܐܫ̣ܬܕܝܘ ܒܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܐܚܪ̈ܢܝܬܐ܆ ܘܒܟܠ ܦ̣ܢ̈ܝ̣ܢ ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܩܘ ܐܬܣ̣ܝܡܘ. ܕܒܟܠ ܚ̣ܕܐ ܚ̣ܕܐ ܡܢܗܝ̣ܢ ܢܗܘܘܢ ܡܩ̇ܪܒܝܢ ܩ̣ܘܪ̈ܒܢܐ܆ ܘܝܗ̇ܒܝܢ ܠܗܠܝܢ ܕܫ̇ܠܡܝܢ. ܘܫܡܐ ܕܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܒܗܝܢ ܐܬܟ̣ܬܒ܆ ܘܐܬܟ̣ܪܙ “a few of those in them took communion; the rest were cast into other monasteries. Clerics were appointed in all the districts so that they might bring Eucharistic offerings into each and every one of the monasteries and distribute them to those who assented. The name of the synod was recorded within the monasteries and proclaimed”: EH 3.1.10 and 3.1.11 treat the Chalcedonian measures to suppress the Severan monasteries in 571 in more detail. 3.1.10.f and 3.1.11.c record that some monastics were forced into communion. 3.1.10.j and 3.1.11.j describe the relocation of recalcitrant monks and nuns to different places of confinement, presumably Chalcedonian monasteries (on the use of monasteries for imprisonment, see Hillner 2015, 281–341). The appointment of Chalcedonian priests to administer the Eucharist in the formerly Severan monasteries is treated in 3.1.11.f. Finally, John of Ephesus also mentions that the council of Chalcedon was proclaimed in the monasteries in 3.1.11.f. This probably means that the council of Chalcedon was included in the diptychs, the lists of bishops and ecumenical councils acknowledged as orthodox by a community; these lists were standardly read out during the liturgy (for another example, see EH 3.1.41.e). However, as there is no explicit mention of the diptychs here, it is also conceivable that this refers to written confessions of the faith of Chalcedon that may have been carved as inscriptions into the buildings themselves or displayed in them, for instance as banners or as a part of mural paintings. Examples of such practices are known from Syria, where some church buildings were marked with inscriptions indicating their confessional affiliation, for example the trisagion with the anti-Chalcedonian insertion ὁ σταυρωθεὶς δι’ ἡμᾶς Begass 2024; Alpi 2009, 2:50–52 Khoury and Riba 2013, 112–115. John may be alluding to such a practice; another reference to clearly visible doctrinal markers displayed on church buildings is found in EH 3.2.52.

div.head: EH 3.1.36.c (BF) 46.6–7
div.p: ܘܝܘ̈ܩܢܐ ܕܟܘܠܗܘܢ ܐܒܗ̈ܬܐ ܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ̣ ܐ̇ܚܬ̣ ܝܘܚܢܢ܆ ܘܝܘܩ̈ܢܘܗܝ ܕܝܠܗ ܩܒ̣ܥ ܒܟܠ ܕܘܟܐ “John took down the images of all the orthodox fathers and affixed images of himself everywhere”: Discussions of the destruction of episcopal images and the installation of new ones appear in several places in the EH. 3.1.11.f, the present chapter, and 3.2.27 refer to John Scholasticus installing images of himself in monasteries after proclaiming the council of Chalcedon; this chapter, 3.2.27, and 3.2.34 discuss patriarch Eutychius (on whom see 3.1.36.e) taking down the images of John Scholasticus and installing his own. The destruction of images appears in three other chapters: in 3.3.20, Eutychius tears down otherwise unspecified images of the “orthodox” (Severans), while 3.3.16 and 3.3.31 record the destruction of images by large gatherings. The EH is one of the principal literary sources for several practices related to the images of bishops in late antiquity. Since these passages are frequently cited in art historical studies, a brief excursus is warranted.

Setting up images of bishops in various contexts was common practice since at least the 5th century. Quite a number of images of bishops have survived from the period from the 5th until the 7th centuries while others are known from written sources Liverani 2019; Marsengill 2013, 115–136 Deichmann 1989, 315–319 Nauerth 1974, 23–46. The bulk of the material represents examples of monumental art either executed in mosaics or painting decorating the walls or vaults of churches, sometimes even in the main apse. The bishops are usually presented either in the context of episcopal galleries highlighting the tradition of the local church Liverani 2019, 304–306 Deichmann 1989, 318–319, or as acting founders or donors of a specific church. The role as donor is sometimes underscored by the presentation of a church model (Liverani 2019, 291–297). These images testify to their status as leading representatives of the elite, to their authority and occasionally to their capacity as patrons or donors. As such, they were sometimes represented together with provincial governors. This is especially evident in the well-known imperial panel at San Vitale in Ravenna that depicts archbishop Maximian alongside emperor Justinian and high-ranking officials (Dresken-Weiland 2016, 242–253). Precious book covers of the gospels or altar clothes occasionally also included an image of the bishop who had donated the church inventories Deichmann 1989, 317–318 Nauerth 1974, 32–38. Another category of episcopal images is related to their tombs Fourlas 2012, 315–330 Deichmann 1989, 316–317.

As functionaries of the Roman empire, bishops had a right to portraits ex officio, just as other magistrates (see 3.1.11.f and esp. Liverani 2019, 297–299). In EH 3.2.27, images of John Scholasticus are characterized as either painted on walls or on tablets. Furthermore, the text underscores that there were multiple images of John in a large number of towns and villages. This seems to have been common for the patriarchs of Constantinople. For bishop Acacius of Constantinople (sed. 472–489), a large number of his portraits is attested in the churches under his jurisdiction (Marsengill 2013, 127). In general images of bishops were presented in churches upon their inauguration as visual claimants to their authority Marsengill 2013, 188 Deichmann 1989, 315 Kollwitz 1954, 331. In some instances, episcopal images on tablets thus resemble the practice of sending panel paintings of newly proclaimed emperors to the provinces. These were apparently also set up in churches according to some sources Deichmann 1989, 314 Kruse 1934, 23–50, esp. 46 n.3 on the presentation in churches.

Only one wooden panel painting of a bishop from Egypt has survived from late antiquity. It presents the frontal bust image of Abraham who is identified as the fourteenth bishop of Hermonthis and abbot of the Phoibammon monastery around 590/600 and 610/620 Fluck 2010; Krause 1971. The icon-like rectangular panel measures 36.5 x 26.5 cm and the four holes in the corners indicate that it was hung up or fixed somewhere. The image was acquired from the art market in 1904, but it might have been found in the early Byzantine church at Luxor Fluck 2010, 215 Krause 1971, 110. It has been suggested that the panel painting of Abraham represents an official portrait (Amtsbild) of the bishop that was created upon his inauguration (Krause 1971, 110). But this is debatable because of the halo. Some scholars suggest that the halo indicates that the panel represents a posthumous memorial image of Abraham as a venerated saint Liverani 2019, 302–303 cf. Fluck 2010, 216 Krause 1971, 109. However, the panel gives an idea of the possible appearance of the images of John Scholasticus and Eutychius painted on tablets. The Liber pontificalis 89 of Ravenna (Mauskopf Deliyannis 2006, 256) mentions a further possible panel painting (tabula) of bishop Agnellus of Ravenna (sed. 557–570) in the monastery of Saint George at Argenta near Ravenna that he had founded Deichmann 1989, 315–316 Nauerth 1974, 43–44. The presentation of – most likely wooden – panel paintings of bishops was apparently common practice in churches throughout the empire in the 6th century.

The images of John Scholasticus mentioned in the present passage are obviously related to the practice to officially publicize the bishop’s portrait in his subordinate churches. The removal of the images of the “orthodox fathers” implies that John Scholasticus aimed to replace the images of older anti-Chalcedonian bishops by his own to emphasize that he now executed authority over the monasteries. EH 3.1.11 indicates that the setting up of his images in the monasteries was a crucial act connected to the systematic subjection of anti-Chalcedonian places of worship to John’s authority that also included forced communion with and the appointment of Chalcedonian clergy (see 3.1.11.f). The imperial couple supported these measures by rewarding those who accepted the participation in the Eucharist with the Chalcedonian clergy (see 3.1.11.i). Francisco (2011a, 112–113) has suggested that the monasteries were also re-consecrated, but this is rather unlikely (see 3.1.11.f).

However, the transfer of the Severan monasteries in Constantinople to the authority of the Chalcedonian bishop is reminiscent of the transfer of Arian churches and all their property in Ravenna to the Catholic church of the city in the early 560s. In this context, all churches were re-dedicated by archbishop Agnellus (Liber pontificalis 85–89 Mauskopf Deliyannis 2006, 252–256 see Herrin 2022, 235–236 Urbano 2005, 82–86). The measures conducted in Sant’Apollinare Nuovo, originally the Arian palace church of Theodoric (Basilica Salvatoris), are especially insightful. The church was re-dedicated to Saint Martin (Liber pontificalis 86 (Mauskopf Deliyannis 2006, 253–254)). Within the mosaic decoration of the church major alterations were made by Agnellus. Depictions supposedly of Theodoric and members of his court were replaced by a procession of the three Magi as well as female and male martyrs lead by Saint Martin Dresken-Weiland 2016, 195–209 Jäggi 2013, 177–182 fig. 117 with a sketch indicating the modified parts Mauskopf Deliyannis 2010, 164–172 Urbano 2005, 76–82 Penni Iacco 2004, 73–79 Deichmann 1974, 149–152. More importantly, according to most scholars, on the west wall of the main nave a partially preserved mosaic image of the Gothic king Theodoric was modified to represent emperor Justinian e.g. Dresken-Weiland 2016, 209–210 Deichmann 1974, 151–152 Penni Iacco 2004, 79–80 with a summary of the scholarly debate, while an image of the officiating bishop Agnellus was also affixed there (Liber pontificalis 86 (Mauskopf Deliyannis 2006, 253–254)). It is assumed that this latter image replaced the depiction of the Arian bishop who had originally consecrated the church Deichmann 1989, 316 Nauerth 1974, 22–23. If so, portraits of the old authorities that had founded the church were replaced by images of the ruling emperor and the officiating bishop. This contemporary example of the “cleansing” of a heretical church building by an orthodox bishop indicates that the measures taken by John Scholasticus, namely to replace older images by his own ones, are not an isolated case. The case is especially remarkable as the subjugation of the Arian churches of Ravenna to the authority of Agnellus was supported by the emperor.

Recent finds from church no. 3 in Olympos (Lycia) dating to the first half of the 6th century indicate that portraits of bishops were at least in some cases intentionally designed as symbols of orthodox faith. Fragmentary murals from a niche above a cenotaph are reconstructed as a depiction of a bust of Christ Pantokrator and below him bishop Aristocritus besides a quote of Acts 7:55–56
highlighting the manhood of Christ (Evcim 2023, 295–302 fig. 15). As bishop of Olympus, Aristocritus signed the acts of the council of Chalcedon (Schwartz 1936, 74.20). The quote from Acts underscores that the posthumous image presented Aristocritus as a representative of Chalcedonian faith.

Written sources indicate that the removal and destruction of episcopal portraits was a frequent consequence of dogmatic controversies (Liverani 2019, 298). In EH 3.3.16, John of Ephesus describes the tearing down of portraits of the anti-Chalcedonian luminaries Severus of Antioch and Theodosius of Alexandria by a Chalcedonian mob in the context of a violent raid in Constantinople. A parallel to these events can be found earlier in the century: in 511, the Chalcedonian patriarch Macedonius II (sed. 496–511) was exiled due to his opposition to the church policy of emperor Anastasius who leaned towards an anti-Chalcedonian position (Meier 2009, 259–269). The subsequent destruction of portraits of Macedonius by his successor Timothy (sed. 511–518) (Theodore Lector, EH frag. 58 Hansen 1995b, 140; see Marsengill 2013, 196) is reminiscent of John Scholasticus’s treatment of the images of the “orthodox fathers.”

Moreover, the epilogue to the acts of the sixth ecumenical council of 680/681 written by the deacon Agathon offers another example of this practice: During the short reign of emperor Philippicus Bardanes (r. 711–713) monothelitism, which had been condemned by the sixth ecumenical council in 681, was restored as official imperial church policy. The (monothelete) “orthodoxy” was also communicated by circulating images of bishops and councils. The emperor had an image of himself and of patriarch Sergius I (sed. 610–638; he was perceived as the founder of the monothelete doctrine) affixed in the center of depictions of the first five ecumenical councils at the million in Constantinople Riedinger 1992, 900.29–35 cf. Fourlas 2020, 173. At the same time, an image of the sixth ecumenical council in the vestibule of the imperial palace was destroyed (Riedinger 1992, 899.16–19). As images of the councils usually present the emperor presiding the assembly of bishops, this also implies the destruction of images of the emperor on images of councils and their political significance, see Walter 1990; Grabar 1957, esp. 55–61. Furthermore, Philippicus had images of bishops Sergius and pope Honorius (sed. 625–638) restored; both had been condemned as heretics by the sixth ecumenical council and their images had obviously been removed Riedinger 1992, 899.20–24 cf. Fourlas 2020, 174 Liverani 2019, 299–301. The removal of the portraits of Sergius and Honorius seems to have been a direct consequence of the anathema decided by the council. However, after the downfall of Philippicus and the end of the monothelete interlude, the programmatic image of the emperor and the patriarch Sergius at the million was replaced by an image of the sixth ecumenical council Riedinger 1992, 900.29–35 cf. Fourlas 2020, 174–175. It is noteworthy that in opposition to imperial church policy, pope Constantine (sed. 708–715) set up an image of the sixth ecumenical council in the church of Saint Peter and refused to accept images of emperor Philippicus in the churches of Rome due to his heretical confession (Liber pontificalis 90.8 and 10 Duchesne 1886, 1:391–392 cf. Grabar 1957, 48–49 and 58).

Within this “war of images” a badly preserved mosaic in the church of Saint Demetrius in Thessaloniki is also relevant. The image dates from the mid-7th century and depicts two bishops flanked by a deacon and a presbyter (Fourlas 2020, 149–171 fig. 3–10 with detailed discussion). The panel conveys the idea that both bishops are in communion with each other. It was covered by marble revetment most probably after the sixth ecumenical council in 681 because of its significance as an expression of adherence to the monothelete doctrine by the church of Thessaloniki and because it supposedly featured an image of patriarch Paul II of Constantinople (sed. 641–653) who was posthumously anathematized by the council (Fourlas 2020, 173–174). The presence of a heretic (Paul II) condemned by an ecumenical council may explain why this panel was subsequently covered in the church, while other images featuring the bishop of Thessaloniki who was very likely also a follower of monothelitism (but not posthumously anathematized) were not affected (Fourlas 2020, 174–175).

Obviously, the removal of images of bishops who were perceived as leading representatives of “heresy” was common practice while the occasional restoration was due to a change in church policy that led to the reconsideration of previously condemned bishops as “orthodox.” But the removal of the images of John Scholasticus by Eutychius at the start of his second tenure in 577 followed a different logic. Here the images of one Chalcedonian patriarch were removed by another and therefore this act was not motivated by any “heresy” of John. According to EH 3.2.34, Eutychius aimed to obliterate the memory of his predecessor in any possible way, and that also included the removal of his portraits (see 3.1.34.d and 3.1.36.e).

The removal of portraits in the context of such a damnatio memoriae finds an analogy in the political practice of removing, mutilating or destroying images of emperors that were considered illegitimate, and in the proclamation of the portraits of the new legitimate ruler Marsengill 2013, 189 on the damnatio memoriae of imperial effigies, see Elm 2011, 665–670 Krüpe 2011, 40–55 Varner 2004; Kruse 1934, 14–18. The wooden panel painting of the family of emperor Septimius Severus (r. 193–211) in Berlin represents a well-known example. On the tondo, the image of one of his sons was subsequently smeared with excrement; this is interpreted as reflecting the damnatio memoriae of Geta Krüpe 2011, 236–239 Varner 2004, 181–182 fig. 187.

To conclude: the underlying idea of the perception and treatment of episcopal portraits described by John of Ephesus was connected to their significance as images of authority and as important elements of commemorative culture. In this regard, the modes of presenting and removing them follow similar principles as those related to the treatment of images of emperors or other high-ranking officials. Moreover, the perception of episcopal images as symbols of orthodox faith or heretical doctrine was obviously more meaningful during times of doctrinal controversies.

div.head: EH 3.1.36.d (SR) 46.7–8
div.p: ܗܠܝ̣ܢ ܕܐܝܟ ܕܥܒ̣ܕ ܐܬܦ̣ܪܥ ܡܢ ܐܠܗܐ “that which he did was avenged by God”: John of Ephesus presents the apparently gruesome death of John Scholasticus (see EH 3.1.38 and 3.2.26; the EH is the only source for this) as a punishment for the persecution of the Severans upon which he embarked. Divine retribution for persecutors of the orthodox is a common theme in the EH and in Christian tradition; see 3.1.10.l and van Ginkel (1995, 186–188).

div.head: EH 3.1.36.e (SR) 46.8–12
div.p: ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܓܝܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܡ̇ܪܝܪܐ ܘܡ̇ܫܢܩܐ: ܘܡ̇ܥܠܬܗ ܕܐ̣ܘܛܘܟ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܩܕܡܘܗܝ ܕܥܠ̣ ܬܘܒ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ܆ ܡܬ̇ܥܩܪܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܝܘܩ̈ܢܘܗܝ̣ ܘܕܐ̣ܘܛܘܟ ܒܕܘܟܬܗܘܢ ܡܬ̣ܩܒܥܝܢ ܒܥ̈ܕܬܐ “for after his bitter and tortuous death and the arrival of Eutychius who had been bishop before him and who came again after his death, the images of him were torn out and those of Eutychius were affixed in the churches in their place”: After the death of John Scholasticus (on 31 August 577; see EH 3.2.26 and 3.1.37.h for discussion of the EH’s depiction of his death), the Caesar Tiberius called Eutychius back from exile to the patriarchal throne. He was reinstated as patriarch on 3 October 577. As John of Ephesus points out here, Eutychius had already been the predecessor to John Scholasticus from 552 to 565. However, in early 565, Eutychius clashed with Justinian on doctrinal grounds and became the victim of a plot orchestrated by a group of people who took advantage of the tensions between emperor and patriarch. Therefore, Eutychius was deposed and replaced with John Scholasticus. This explains Eutychius’s long-standing enmity towards John Scholasticus – an enmity which, according to this passage, even led to the destruction of John Scholasticus’s images.

As for the background to the plot against Eutychius (see also 3.1.42.g), in all likelihood, a faction supportive of the future emperor Justin II (Justinian died on 14 November 565) wanted to bring John Scholasticus to the patriarchal throne Roggo 2022, 72–78; 2019, 440–445 Averil Cameron 1988, 234–235. For a candidate with imperial ambitions, it was important to have the patriarch’s support because of the role he played in the investiture of a new emperor. John Scholasticus was known as a close ally of Justin already prior to 565 Roggo 2022, 72–73 Lin 2021, 138 van den Ven 1965, 320–323 and consequently, the plotters successfully strove to further undermine Justinian’s trust in Eutychius. He was arrested, and on 1 February 565 John Scholasticus was made the new patriarch. Eutychius was sent into exile to Amasea in northern Asia Minor where he remained until the death of John Scholasticus. He never accepted his successor as legitimate patriarch and therefore, he embarked on a vigorous campaign against every trace of John Scholasticus during his second patriarchate 577–582 Roggo 2022, 172–81 Averil Cameron 1990, 215; 1988, 239–242. The EH is the main source for this as the heated conflict between Eutychius and John Scholasticus is completely glossed over in the hagiographic Live of Eutychius (Laga 1992). In addition to the removal of all the images of John Scholasticus (see 3.1.36.c) mentioned in the present chapter, in 3.2.27, and in 3.2.34, Eutychius declared invalid all the actions John Scholasticus had done as patriarch. Furthermore, he removed his name from the diptychs, the commemorative lists of the former bishops, just as if John Scholasticus had never been patriarch (EH 3.2.31–3.2.34). Eutychius’s actions against the memory of John Scholasticus threatened to tear the church of Constantinople apart; Tiberius eventually put a stop to his campaign (Roggo 2022, 6–8).

div.head: EH 3.1.36.f (SR) 46.12–13
div.p: ܘܟܠܗܝܢ ܕܝܪ̈ܬܐ ܕܢܫ̈ܐ ܗܠܝܢ܆ ܗܦ̣ܟܝܢ ܐܝܟ ܕܒܣܘܓܐܐ̣ ܘܗ̣̈ܘܝ ܐܪ̈ܬ̣ܕܟܣܬܐ “all the monasteries of women came back for the most part and became orthodox”: This passage underlines that there was eventually some relaxation of the anti-Severan measures. The nuns who, in 571, had been dispersed and interned in other monasteries because of their refusal to accept the Chalcedonian communion (see 3.1.10.f and 3.1.10.j) were allowed to return and there were once again Severan communities in Constantinople. EH 3.1.34 similarly attests that some Egyptian Severans were eventually released and allowed to leave for Egypt after the return of Eutychius. Moreover, there are signs that this period of detente already started some years prior to John Scholasticus’s death in 577: some anti-Chalcedonian leaders were released from prison in about 574, for instance Conon (EH 3.1.31.h) and John of Ephesus himself, who was transferred from prison into house arrest at about the same time (EH 3.2.7.f). EH 3.1.38.g also mentions that already in the last years of John Scholasticus’s tenure, the Severans were able to gather without fear of persecution. This change in the religious policy is likely connected to Tiberius’s rise to power; already before his appointment as Caesar on 7 December 574, he was one of the most powerful men at court and effectively ruling the empire instead of the incapacitated Justin (Roggo 2024a, 100–104). Tiberius’s remarkable religious tolerance with regard to the emergence of independent Severan ecclesial structures in Egypt in the mid-570s has been noted by Booth (2017, 169–171 see also Roggo 2022, 118–120, and the testimony of John of Ephesus suggests that his rising influence also considerably improved the situation of the Severans in Constantinople.

div.head: EH 3.1.36.g (SR) 46.13–16
div.p: ܡܢܗܝܢ ܕܝܢ ܦܫ̣̈ܝܢ ܛ̈ܠܝ̣ܬܐ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ܆ ܘܥ̇ܐ̈ܠܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ ܒܟܠ ܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ̣ ܕܡܬ̇ܩܪ̈ܒܢ ܒܣܘܢܗܕܘܣ܆ ܘܠܒ̈ܫ̇ܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ ܐܣܟܡܐ ܕܕܝܪܝܘܬܐ܆ ܘܡܬ̇ܩܪ̈ܒܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ ܡܢ ܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ “but from the women, a few girls stayed behind and entered every day to receive communion with the synod. They took on the monastic habit and received communion from the clerics”: The period of relaxation that started in the mid-570s led to the emergence of confessionally divided monasteries. Those nuns who had kept rejecting the Chalcedonian communion and been interned in other monasteries (see 3.1.10.j and 3.1.11.j) were allowed to return, yet not all of those who had given in and lived in “Chalcedonized” monasteries for a number of years reverted to their original Severan confession. John of Ephesus also mentions these “bi-confessional” convents (according to van Ginkel (1995, 155 n.138), it might just have been one single convent) in EH 3.2.47. There, he specifies that the nuns kept celebrating the whole liturgy together, Severans and Chalcedonians, but that they had different priests who administered the Eucharist to them simultaneously. It would thus be misleading to understand in the present passage ܥ̇ܐ̈ܠܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ (“they entered”) as if the Chalcedonian nuns went to a different place. They entered in the same church as the Severans, yet received communion from a different priest. Probably, those who remained in communion with the Chalcedonians were no small minority since otherwise, it would be hard to explain why they kept the images of Eutychius (see 3.1.36.e). As indicated in 3.1.36.f, the start of Eutychius’s tenure as patriarch 577/578 was quite peaceful for the Severans. EH 3.2.7.f attests that John of Ephesus was released from house arrest in that period; according to EH 3.3.15–3.3.16, the persecution appears to have restarted only towards the end of 578 (Roggo 2022, 124–127). The existence of such bi-confessional monasteries as described here is thus conceivable for the time around 577.

The passage focuses on “a few girls” (ܛ̈ܠܝ̣ܬܐ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ) who had not yet taken the monastic habit. Justinian’s Novel 5.2 from 535 held that candidates to the monastic life were to receive the monastic habit only after a novitiate of three years (Schöll and Kroll 1895, 29–31). Consequently, those girls very probably entered the convents after 571, i.e. when the Severan communities had been suppressed and all the monasteries were in Chalcedonian hands. Unlike the elder nuns, they had thus no Severan past, which might explain why they were unwilling to abandon Chalcedon. Despite John of Ephesus’s deliberate focus on young and inexperienced monastic women, it is clear that they could not have remained in communion with the Chalcedonians, had they not also been joined by more senior members of the convents, who may have organized the bi-confessional structures.

div.head: EH 3.1.36.h (SR) 46.17–18
div.p: ܗܠܝܢ ܕܝܢ ܦܣ̣ܩܝܢ ܓܠ̣ܝܐܝܬ܆ ܘܠܐ ܡܬ̇ܩܪ̈ܒܢ ܗ̈ܘܝ̣ ܘܐܦ ܠܐ ܚܕܐ ܡܢܗܝܢ. ܘܝܬ̇ܝܪܐܝܬ̣ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܕܝܘܚܢܢ “but they cut themselves off openly and not a single one of them received communion, especially after the death of John”: It is not entirely clear who “they” (ܗܠܝܢ) are. Most likely, it refers to “a few girls” (ܛ̈ܠܝ̣ܬܐ ܙܥܘܪ̈ܝܬܐ) who are mentioned just before; this would also make most sense grammatically, ܗܠܝܢ being the near demonstrative pronoun (as opposed to ܗܢܘܢ “those”). However, Brooks (1936, 32 n.4) assumed that it rather refers back to the other nuns who had returned to the Severan communion (which implies that John of Ephesus should have used ܗܢܘܢ [“those”] instead of ܗܠܝܢ). As for the content, both possibilities are conceivable: it was either the Chalcedonian or the Severan nuns who insisted on taking their own communion, thus contributing to the clear Eucharistic split within the convents, as indicated in EH 3.2.47 (see 3.1.36.g). For the growing concern throughout the 6th century to take the correct Eucharist and its development into an identity marker, see 3.1.10.h.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: In this chapter, it is for the first time Tiberius, and not Justin, who features as the emperor taking important decisions regarding the suppression of the Severan episcopal hierarchy. Just as EH 3.1.31–3.1.39, this chapter was written in 577/578 when Justin was still alive (van Ginkel 1995, 73), but effective power had passed to the Caesar Tiberius. According to John of Ephesus, Tiberius’s approach was markedly different from Justin’s: whereas the latter is presented as giving in to patriarch John Scholasticus and consenting to the persecution of the anti-Chalcedonians in 571 (see 3.1.5.e), Tiberius receives much praise throughout the EH because he was opposed to religious violence against the Severans. John of Ephesus records here (and again in EH 3.3.12) that John Scholasticus attempted to persuade Tiberius to suppress the Severans just as Justin had done. Yet, in a stylized and undoubtedly fictional dialogue, the patriarch is then forced to admit that the Severans are true Christians, neither pagans nor heretics, and thus undeserving of persecution. John of Ephesus adds that also on a later occasion, after the return of Eutychius as patriarch in early October 577, Tiberius refused to persecute the Severans and forced the militant Chalcedonian party to be quiet.

div.head: Testimonies (SR)
div.p: Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.7 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:306.a.31–307.a.13; 4:345.b.19–32 and 10.8 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:310.a.29–35; 4:347.b.22–39: summary of the chapter in EH; see 3.1.37.c and 3.1.37.j

div.head: EH 3.1.37.a (SR) 46.22–24
div.p: ܘܩܕܡ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܗܟܝܠ ܕܝܘܚܢܢ: ܟܕ ܒܪܕܘܦ̣ܝܐ ܕܟܪ̈ܣܛܝܢܐ ܪܘ̣ܐ ܗܘܐ ܒܛܢ̣ܢܐ ܕܪܘܓܙܐ: ܘܠܗ̣ܝܩ ܗܘܐ ܐܝܟ ܕܐܒ̣ܐ ܠܕܡܐ ܕܐܡ̣ܪ̈ܐ “now, before John’s death, when he was drunk on the zeal of anger for the persecution of the Christians and thirsting like a wolf for the blood of lambs”: There is a certain tension between this passage and the subsequent chapter EH 3.1.38 which describes that patriarch John Scholasticus was, prior to his death on 31 August 577, opposed to a resumption of the persecution. Both passages are evidently highly stylized: here, John Scholasticus’s thirst for persecution serves to contrast him with the mild Caesar Tiberius who rejected his demand, and in EH 3.1.38, John of Ephesus is mainly interested in showing that the persecuting patriarch received divine punishment, languished horribly before his death, and recognized that his actions had angered God (van Ginkel 1995, 187). Neither passage thus provides secure information about whether or not John Scholasticus wanted to continue the suppression of the Severans in his last years.

However, it is possible that John Scholasticus’s attempt to persuade Tiberius took place quite soon after the latter’s accession as Caesar in December 574. According to EH 3.2.26, John Scholasticus suffered from his excruciating disease for about two years until his death, and this could mean that his thirst for persecution was eventually tempered after mid-575, as described in EH 3.1.38.
For the metaphorical use of wolves in the EH and their biblical connotations, see 3.1.10.e and 3.1.19.n.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.b (SR; HL) 46.24–25
div.p: ܥܠ ܗܘܐ ܠܘܬ ܡ̇ܫܝܢܐ ܘܒܗ̣ܝܠܐ ܩ̣ܣܪ ܛܒ̣ܪܝܣ “he went to the peaceable and serene Caesar Tiberius”: Tiberius was crowned Caesar on 7 December 574, once it had become clear that the ailing emperor Justin in all likelihood would not recover and remain largely incapacitated (see EH 3.3.1–3.3.6 and the discussion in Roggo 2024a, 100–104; Pfeilschifter 2013, 142–147 Averil Cameron 1976b). John of Ephesus presents Tiberius throughout the EH in stark contrast to Justin as peaceful and opposed to the violent oppression of the Severans (van Ginkel 1995, 108). In EH 3.3.22, for instance, John assures his readers that his laudatory description of Tiberius is accurate, all the more so since he had first-hand knowledge of the new emperor. The main reason for Tiberius’s very positive depiction in the EH lies in the fact that the Severans in Constantinople, including John of Ephesus himself, were able to enjoy a period of respite after his accession to power in the mid-570s; see 3.1.31.h; 3.1.33.f; 3.1.36.f; 3.2.7.f Roggo 2022, 118–120 Booth 2017, 169–171.

Tiberius’s resistance to persecuting anti-Chalcedonians is, with regard to the situation in Egypt, confirmed in John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 401–402). Moreover, also Evagrius (EH 5.1–5.2 and 5.13 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 195–197 and 208–209)) and Gregory of Tours (EH 4.40 and 5.19 (Krusch and Levison 1951, 171–173 and 225–227)) are very critical of Justin and laudatory of Tiberius; they contrast both emperors in terms of cruelty vs. clemency and avarice vs. financial liberality (for Tiberius’s liberality, see also EH 3.3.11 and 3.3.14) Michael Whitby 1998, 327–329 and 333–334 Averil Cameron 1977; 1975b, 422–424.

When speaking of emperors, John often uses the common title ܙܟܝܐ (“victorious”; see 3.1.4.k). Here, he uses other epithets that emphasize the peacefulness of the emperor, namely ܡ̇ܫܝܢܐ “peaceable” and ܒܗ̣ܝܠܐ “serene”; the latter a second time in 3.1.37. This is in keeping with the content of this chapter, but also with the development of the imperial title under Tiberius, which emphasizes the aspect of peaceableness more strongly than it was the case under his predecessors (Rösch 1978, 50–51 and 169); see especially Tiberius’s complete title in the preface to Novel 12 (Zepi and Zepi 1931, 19.22–23) where the epithets ἡμερώτατος and εἰρηνικός appear; they are also found on inscriptions and in papyri. The epithet ܒܗ̣ܝܠܐ reappears in EH 3.3.22, 3.3.33, and in the heading of 3.6.25. Payne Smith (1903, 36) erroneously assumed that the word belonged to the title of Caesar, yet EH 3.3.33 proves this wrong. Arcuri (2008) does not discuss these epithets.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.c (SR) 46.28–29
div.p: ܕܨ̇ܒܐ ܐܢܐ ܕܫܪܪܐ ܬ̇ܦܢܐ ܠܝ܇ ܥܠ ܐܝܠܝܢ ܕܡܫ̇ܐܠ ܐܢܐ ܠܟ “I want you to answer me truthfully concerning those whom I am asking you”: The dialogue between Tiberius and John Scholasticus evidently is a fictional composition by John of Ephesus (van Ginkel 1995, 147 n.99). It is repeated, in a slightly shorter form, in EH 3.3.12. However, Tiberius’s unwillingness to suppress the Severans in the mid-570s is well attested (see 3.1.37.b above); we may thus regard the general gist of his account as accurate. In the carefully crafted exchange between the wise Christian ruler and the over-zealous patriarch, John of Ephesus effectively conveys his view that Severans and Chalcedonians still formed part of the same church. This entails he and his peers insisting on being treated as fellow-Christians by the Chalcedonians, and surely not as heretics or even as pagans worthy of persecution. As he saw it, the ways had not yet parted, the alienation between the two camps was not yet irreversible, the gradual development of independent Severan churches which gathered decisive pace in the 570s was not yet completed (see 3.1.3.j). Contemporaneous Severan leaders such as Peter and Damian of Alexandria (see EH 3.4.12) definitely broke away from the imperial church by establishing a parallel episcopal hierarchy in Egypt (Booth 2017), yet John of Ephesus – and probably more Severans of his generation – still upheld the ideal of one united imperial church and had not yet given up hope for a reunion van Ginkel 1995, 114–115; 126; 147; 166–167. After all, such a union had indeed been achieved for a short time in 571 (see 3.1.24.l).

Michael the Syrian included an almost literal quotation of this dialogue in his Chronicle 10.7 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:306.a.31–307.a.13; 4:345.b.19–32. However, in his version, it is not clear that it was Tiberius who discussed with John Scholasticus and refused to persecute the Severans. Michael just vaguely talks of “the emperor,” and given that in the preceding passage of his Chronicle (dealing with the story of Photius, see 3.1.32.h), the emperor still clearly is Justin, it appears that Michael assumed by mistake that it had been Justin who turned John Scholasticus down.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.d (NK) 46.29
div.p: ܚ̈ܢܦܐ ܐܝܬܝܗܘܢ݃ “pagans?”: The final nun contains a symbol that can be interpreted as a question mark. Brooks omits the question mark from the text but mentions it in the note (Brooks 1935, 46 n.1). It is conceivable that he could not reproduce this sign as it was originally written. This is the Syriac character zawgā ʿelāyā (ܙܘܓܐ ܥܠܝܐ), which is to be understood as an interrogative marker Kiraz 2019, 112 Coakley 2012; Segal 2004, 70. This sign indicates that the sequence of words between it and the subsequent punctuation mark constitutes a question: “Are the ones whom you are intent on having me persecute pagans?” The anticipated response can be either affirmative or negative. The question put into the mouth of emperor Tiberius is designed to underscore the definitive assertion that those subjected to persecution are not pagans, but Christians.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.e (NK) 47.1–2
div.p: ܘܐܠܐ݃ ܗܪ̈ܛܝܩܘ ܐ̣ܝܬܝܗܘܢ “if this is not the case, are they heretics?”: Over the last letter of ܘܐܠܐ݃, there is again a symbol in the manuscript which in Brooks’s edition is only indicated in the footnote (Brooks 1935, 47 n.1). This is the Syriac sign zawgā ʿelāyā, which, as in the previous note, is to be understood as an interrogative marker Kiraz 2019, 112 Coakley 2012; Segal 2004, 70. The interrogative marker immediately following the initial word indicates that the entire sentence, up to the next punctuation mark, should be interpreted as a question. In this way, John of Ephesus has Tiberius expose John Scholasticus, who is then forced to admit that his intention is not to persecute heretics, but Christians.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.f (SR) 47.7–8
div.p: ܐܝܟ ܕܝܘܩܠ̣ܝܛܝܢܣ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܕܡ̈ܠܟܐ ܚ̈ܢܦܐ “as Diocletian and the other pagan emperors”: Some emperors who ruled before the conversion of Constantine were remembered in late antiquity as cruel persecutors of the Christians (see 3.1.3.k). Among them, Diocletian (r. 284–305) was most prominent because he had been, in 303, the instigator of the last major persecution. Other emperors who often were reduced to a role of merciless suppressors of the early Christians are Nero (r. 54–68), Domitian (r. 81–96), Decius (r. 249–251), and Valerian (r. 253–260) C. Moss 2013; Lane Fox 2006. John of Ephesus has Tiberius highlight how wrong it would be if he were to give in to John Scholasticus’s demand: by agreeing to persecute the Severans who, as John Scholasticus had to admit, were fellow Christians (see 3.1.37.c), Tiberius would become comparable to the worst of the pagan emperors of old (cf. van Ginkel 1995, 147).

div.head: EH 3.1.37.g (SR) 47.8–9
div.p: ܙܠ ܬ̣ܒ ܒܥܕܬܟ̣ ܘܫܠ̣ܝ. ܘܠܐ ܬܘܒ ܬ̇ܫܚܘܩ ܠܢ ܒܗܠܝܢ “go sit in your church and be silent! Do not burden us with these matters”: This is not the only time that John of Ephesus puts such a command into Tiberius’s mouth. At the end of this chapter, Tiberius replies in very similar terms to Eutychius and the clerics who hoped for a resumption of the persecution ܙܠ̣ܘܢ ܬܒ̣ܘܢ̣ ܘܫ̣ܠܘ “Go sit and be silent!” (see 3.1.37.j). Furthermore, also in EH 3.2.40, Tiberius orders Eutychius to refrain from innovations in the liturgy by exclaiming ܙܠ ܬ̣ܒ ܒܥܕܬܟ̣ “Go sit in your church.” In this way, John of Ephesus portrays John Scholasticus and Eutychius as notorious troublemakers who forget where their place is and interfere in matters which should be left to others, most notably the emperor.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.h (SR) 47.10–12
div.p: ܥܕܡܐ ܕܪܘܓܙܐ ܕܡܢ ܫܡܝܐ ܐܕܪܟ̣ܗ܇ ܐܝܟ ܕܡܢ ܠܥܠ ܐ̇ܘ̣ܕܥܢ܇ ܘܡܢ ܚ̈ܝܐ ܕܬܢܢ ܛܠ̣ܩ “until anger from heaven overtook him, as we have related above, and he passed away from life here”: John of Ephesus described the death of John Scholasticus (on 31 August 577) as ܡ̇ܪܝܪܐ ܘܡ̇ܫܢܩܐ “bitter and tortuous” in 3.1.36.e. His painful end, apparently after more than two years of excruciating illness (for which the EH, however, is the only source), is presented as divine retribution appropriate to a persecutor in EH 3.2.26. There is also mention of John Scholasticus’s long illness in EH 3.1.38.b; John of Ephesus claims there that it caused the patriarch to realize that his actions against the Severans had been wrong and that he was suffering justly. The depiction of John Scholasticus’s gruesome end underscores John of Ephesus’s message that God supports the Severans and that punishment eventually comes to their persecutors (van Ginkel 1995, 187).

div.head: EH 3.1.37.i (SR) 47.12–14
div.p: ܘܒܬܪ ܗܢܐ ܬܘܒ ܐܝܟ ܕܡܢ ܠܥܠ ܐ̇ܘ̣ܕܥܢ܆ ܟܕ ܥ̣ܠ ܐ̣ܘܛܝܟ ܘܫ̇ܪܝܘ ܫ̇ܓܪܝܢ ܠܗ ܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܡܥ̇ܕܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܒܒ̣ܙܬܐ ܘܒܚ̣ܛܘܦܝܐ “then after this one, as we related above, when Eutychius had come and the clerics who were accustomed to despoiling and plundering had begun inflaming him”: John of Ephesus mentions the return of Eutychius as patriarch of Constantinople after the death of John Scholasticus in EH 3.1.34.d and 3.1.36.e. According to the Life of Eutychius (Laga 1992, 69.2130–2132), Eutychius’s reinstatement took place on 3 October 577, and the interaction between Caesar and patriarch most likely has to be placed shortly afterwards. John of Ephesus depicts Eutychius as deeply hostile to the Severans throughout the EH. The EH narrates that despite Eutychius’s initial failure to restart the persecution as stated in this chapter, he eventually succeeded and exerted violence against the Severan communities on several occasions during his second patriarchate (EH 3.2.37–3.2.39 and 3.3.15–3.3.21; see also 3.1.37.j). Moreover, Eutychius had apparently written an anti-Severan treatise during his exile from 565 to 577 (EH 3.2.35). There can thus be no doubt that Eutychius repeatedly turned against the Severans, yet it remains unclear what his motives were.

For John of Ephesus, Eutychius was both a merciless persecutor and an avaricious plunderer of the Severan places of worship. Yet the passage in question here suggests that Eutychius actually reacted to the demand of other clerics when he went to Tiberius. There are indeed good reasons to assume that at least a part of his anti-Severan activities may have been caused by the necessity to give in to pressure exerted by Chalcedonian hardliners. We have encountered a fervently pro-Chalcedonian circle around the quaestor Anastasius in the resistance to Justin’s edict in 571 (3.1.19.j); moreover, such unrelenting hardliners may have been responsible for John Scholasticus’s sudden demand to have the Severan clerics reordained (3.1.12.f; see also 3.1.38.e). Importantly, as Roggo has argued, both John Scholasticus and Eutychius had excommunicated each other in 565. Their position at the head of the church of Constantinople was therefore far from stable (Roggo 2022, 6–8 and 177–181). In 571, John Scholasticus needed to be wary of antagonizing staunchly pro-Chalcedonian sections of the clergy and the elites in the capital as they could otherwise have become supporters of the exiled Eutychius, hence the eventual failure of Justin’s accommodating edict (Roggo 2022, 108–111). Similarly, Eutychius encountered resistance after his return in 577 when parts of the clergy did not recognize him as the new patriarch because of his previous deposition. He needed to prevent the resistance to him from becoming even stronger, and he might therefore have felt compelled to present himself as an uncompromising defender of Chalcedon. For this reason, he might then have given in to those who insisted on a harsher approach towards the opponents of the council (Roggo 2022, 120–122). Eutychius’s strictly anti-Severan stance as described in the EH may thus rather have been due to his attempts to stabilize his contested standing as patriarch, and not so much to his supposedly deep-rooted hatred of the Severans, as John of Ephesus would have us believe.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.j (SR) 47.17–18
div.p: ܦܢ̇ܝ ܕܝܢ ܘܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ ܠܘܬܗ̣ ܟܕ ܐܡ̇ܪ. ܕܣ̇ܦܩܝܢ ܠܢ ܩܪ̈ܒܐ ܕܒܪ̈ܒܪܝܐ܇ ܕܡܢ ܟܠ ܓ̇ܒ̈ܝܢ ܚ̣ܕܝܪ̈ܝܢ ܠܢ “but he also replied to him, saying: ‘The wars of the barbarians which surround us on all sides are enough for us’”: Tiberius’s reply to Eutychius is quite different from that he had made to John Scholasticus a few years earlier (see 3.1.37.c). He still prohibited violent measures against the Severans, yet there is no longer a great focus on their status as fellow Christians which would render their persecution deeply reprehensible. On the contrary, the impression John of Ephesus conveys here is that the emperor was too occupied with other matters (warfare against foreign enemies) and that he had therefore no resources left for opening up another conflict within the empire. Ever since the early 570s, the Roman empire had indeed been involved in an energy-sapping war on two fronts against Persians and Avars (for an overview of these wars, see Sarris (2011, 177–182 and 229–235) and Michael Whitby (1988, 86–89 and 250–268)). Even John of Ephesus, who is very laudatory towards Tiberius (see 3.1.37.b and Averil Cameron (1977)), thus has to admit that the irenic approach towards the religious dispute that had been prevalent in his first years as Caesar Roggo 2022, 118–120 Booth 2017, 169–171 had been replaced with Realpolitik.

This shift in Tiberius’s politics is not surprising given that he increasingly became the focus of criticism which had first still been directed towards the senior emperor Justin (Roggo 2022, 125–127). According to John of Ephesus, discontent with Tiberius’s mildness in regard to his religious policy eventually erupted after his accession as Augustus on 26 September 578; vociferous circles shouted against Tiberius and forced him to consent to the persecution of religious minorities (EH 3.3.13 and 3.3.26) Roggo 2024b, 244–245; Greatrex 2020, 399–400 Pfeilschifter 2013, 328–329. Moreover, Tiberius’s position as emperor appears to have been challenged by members of the Justinianic dynasty Roggo 2022, 120 and 126 Pfeilschifter 2013, 278 n.48 Averil Cameron 1975b, 425–426. Tiberius therefore had to stabilize his hold on the throne and for this, it was important that he did not antagonize important pro-Chalcedonian factions. Very probably, it was this necessity which ultimately led to his agreeing to a new incarceration of the Severan clergy in late 578 (EH 3.3.15). It is possible that he already felt this increasing pressure after the return of Eutychius in October 577 and that it was for this reason that his rejection of Eutychius’s demand, as John of Ephesus presents it here, was quite weak.

The empire’s involvement in many wars could, in fact, also be used for the very opposite of what Tiberius argued here, namely to justify the suppression of the Severans: John of Ephesus puts very similar words in Tiberius’s mouth when describing how Eutychius repeated his demand to the emperor about three years later, in 580/581 (see EH 3.3.21). Yet on that occasion, Tiberius stated that the war business did not leave him any time for ecclesiastical affairs and, therefore, he allowed Eutychius to deal with the Severans as he wished. The patriarch of course immediately proceeded to persecute them (Roggo 2022, 130–131).

Similar to the previous dialogue between Tiberius and John Scholasticus (see 3.1.37.c), Michael the Syrian also included a shortened version close to John’s account of the exchange between Tiberius and Eutychius in his Chronicle 10.8 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:310.a.29–35; 4:347.b.22–39.

div.head: EH 3.1.37.k (SR) 47.23–24
div.p: ܘܗܟܢܐ ܘܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ ܕܫܥܬܐ ܟܡ̣ܣ̣ ܘܫܠ̣ܐ “in this way, Eutychius also receded and has been silent for a time”: The first securely dateable instance of a violent anti-Severan action in Eutychius’s second patriarchate took place more than a year after his return, shortly before Christmas 578 when John of Ephesus and other Severan leaders were imprisoned (EH 3.3.15) (Roggo 2022, 126–127). The present chapter attests that despite the pressure exerted on him, Tiberius still resisted the demand for a harsher approach towards the Severans for a while after Eutychius’s return. This only changed once he had become sole emperor after Justin’s death in early October 578 (see above 3.1.37.j). Given that John of Ephesus does not show any awareness of Justin’s death in chapters 3.1.31–3.1.39, van Ginkel has argued convincingly that they were written before this point in 577/578 (van Ginkel 1995, 73). Consequently, it seems best to translate ܫܠ̣ܐ “he has been silent” as a present perfect, as this entails that Eutychius remained silent and did not embark on a persecution until the time of writing.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus’s message in this chapter is that evil deeds against the Severans do not go unavenged and that divine retribution is eventually meted out to the persecutors – this is also the main theme of chapters EH 3.2.25–3.2.30. The instigator of the hardship the Severans had to suffer, patriarch John Scholasticus, is chastised by God. The exact nature of this punishment is not revealed, yet in a later chapter (EH 3.2.26), John describes in detail how the patriarch was tortured by an excruciating illness and languished for a period of two years until his death in 577. John asserts that the patriarch recognized because of his heavy affliction that he had acted wrongly against the Severans. Accordingly, when some of his collaborators suggested cracking down on them again, he prohibited it and left them in peace for the remainder of his patriarchate. John explains thus the respite which the Severans enjoyed after the ascendancy to power of the more tolerant Tiberius in 574 with a divine intervention in favor of his community. He reports that his co-religionists were again able to gather without fear during the last years of John Scholasticus, and this new liberty also continued after his death.

div.head: EH 3.1.38.a (SR) 47.28–29
div.p: ܗ̣ܢܘܢ ܗܟܝܠ ܟܢܘ̣ܫ̈ܝܐ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܕܐܪ̈ܬܕܟܣܘ ܕܡܢ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܣ̣ܪܡܝܐ ܐܬܥ̣ܩܪ̈ܘ ܗܘܘ ܘܐܬܬ̣ܚܕܘ “so, as for all the communities of the orthodox which had been uprooted and closed by John of Sirmin”: In all parts of the EH written prior to Justin’s death in October 578, John of Ephesus is very careful not to criticize the emperor (van Ginkel 1995, 107–108). For this reason, he puts all the blame for the closure of the gathering places of the Severans and for the hardship they suffered in the early 570s on John Scholasticus (see 3.1.5.e; 3.1.11.e; 3.1.19.n; 3.1.29.e). He claims that the patriarch managed to persuade the emperor to act against the Severan clergy – because evidently, a large-scale campaign against them cannot have happened without imperial orders. In EH 3.1.13.e, in the vision seen by an anonymous holy man, John Scholasticus is also described as “uprooting” the churches of the Severans, with the same verbal stem (ܥܩܪ) used in this passage. For the possible motives of John Scholasticus to turn against the Severans in 571, see 3.1.5.e; 3.1.12.f; Roggo (2022, 106–113). “Sirmin” (ܣ̣ܪܡܝܐ) refers to John Scholasticus’s place of origin close to Antioch, see 3.1.5.e.

div.head: EH 3.1.38.b (SR; NK) 47.29–48.2
div.p: ܟܕ ܫ̣ܕܪ ܗܘܐ ܐܠܗܐ ܥܠܘܗܝ ܡ̇ܪܕܘܬܐ ܕܪܘܓܙܐ ܩܫ̣ܝܐ ܕܡܢ ܫܡܝܐ܆ ܟܠܗܘܢ ܫ̇ܪܝܘ ܡ̇ܥܙܝܢ ܘܦܬ̇ܚܝܢ ܠܗܘܢ “after God sent a chastisement of harsh anger from heaven upon him, they all grew emboldened and reopened”: John of Ephesus gives a detailed account of John Scholasticus’s horrible bodily afflictions, including gout and the slow dissolution of his bowels for about two years, in EH 3.2.26. The fate of the patriarch is presented as a just punishment inflicted by God on a cruel persecutor (see also 3.1.37.h). John of Ephesus is the only author who mentions that John Scholasticus suffered for a long time prior to his death, and it is possible that he invented or exaggerated parts of his account so as to point out that divine retribution for impious deeds committed against the Severans is merciless (van Ginkel 1995, 187–188). For this purpose, a work such as Lactantius’s De mortibus persecutorum could have served as a model. John of Ephesus connects the patriarch’s illness with the respite that the Severan leaders enjoyed in the mid-570s, yet it is more probable to explain the improvement of their situation with the accession of the new Caesar Tiberius in late 574 Roggo 2022, 118–120 Booth 2017, 169–171. Tiberius was opposed to the persecution of the Severans (see 3.1.37.b and 3.1.37.c), and it is hardly a coincidence that in the time of his ascendancy to power, anti-Chalcedonian leaders such as John of Ephesus himself (3.2.7.f) and Conon of Tarsus (3.1.31.h) were released from prison. The resurgence of Severan communities (more specifically, female monasteries) in Constantinople in this period is also attested in 3.1.36.f.

In the manuscript, there is a gap in the word ܘܦܬ̇ ܚܝܢ. It is possible that the letter yuḏ stood in the gap and that the text originally read ܘܦܬܝܚܝܢ, the masculine plural passive participle of ܦܬܚ. In this case, the following word ܠܗܘܢ would have specified the agents opening the communities: “and being opened by them.” However, the erasure of this hypothetical yuḏ results in a more logical reading: the masculine plural active participle ܘܦܬ̇ܚܝܢ (marked as active by the diacritical dot above the taw) parallels the preceding participle ܡ̇ܥܙܝܢ. Both participles depend on ܫ̇ܪܝܘ. ܠܗܘܢ should then be understood as an ethical dative. In sum, the correction – perhaps by the scribe himself – offers the most convincing reading.

div.head: EH 3.1.38.c (SR) 48.5–7
div.p: ܘܡܟܝܠ ܥܦܐ ܗܘܢܗ ܒܢܘܪܐ ܕܡ̇ܘܬܐ ܕܐ̇ܚܝ̣ܕܐ ܗܘܬ ܒܠܒܗ ܘܡܫܬ̇ܠܗܒܐ ܒܡ̣̈ܥܘܗܝ “from that time forward, his mind was engrossed in the deadly fire which had taken hold in his heart and burned in his innards”: EH 3.2.26 may be helpful for understanding this passage which explains why John Scholasticus turned away from persecuting the Severans: John of Ephesus asserts there that the patriarch apparently wanted to suppress the opponents of Chalcedon even more after the onset of his illness, yet then through the endless torture finally came to recognize that he was being punished because he had acted wrongly against them. The passage here likely alludes to this change of mind; the heaven-sent illness, described as “deadly fire” (ܢܘܪܐ ܕܡ̇ܘܬܐ), that was raging in him made him understand the fatal mistake he had made when persecuting the Severans and thus angering God. For this reason, John Scholasticus then expresses his opposition to a resumption of the persecution at the end of this chapter.

div.head: EH 3.1.38.d (SR) 48.7–9
div.p: ܐ̣ܝܟܢܐ ܕܒܚܪܬܐ ܗܠܝܢ ܒ̈ܢܝ ܨܒܝܢܗ ܘܡ̇ܫ̈ܡܫܢܐ ܕܒ̣ܝܫܬܗ܆ ܟܕ ܝ̇ܕܥܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܨܒܝܢܗ ܥܐ̇ܠܝܢ ܗܘܘ̣ ܘܐܡ̇ܪܝܢ ܠܗ “accordingly, at the end his partisans and the servants of his wickedness who knew his will would enter and tell him”: On several instances, John of Ephesus introduces fervent Chalcedonians who were particularly eager to persecute the Severans. We have already encountered the quaestor Anastasius who is presented as the spokesman of the “semi-Nestorians” (ܦ̣ܠܓܘܬ ܢܣܛܘܪ̈ܝܢܘ) opposed to Justin’s accommodating edict on faith (see 3.1.19.j); his militant anti-Severan stance is mentioned again in EH 3.2.29. Other persecutors include the deacon Theodulus (whose zealotism apparently even scared John Scholasticus; see EH 3.2.28) and Probinus (EH 3.2.38), who had originally been close to the anti-Chalcedonians (van Ginkel 1995, 119 and 187–188). As discussed in 3.1.38.e, John Scholasticus might indeed not have been completely free in his decision to act against the Severans in 571 and rather have given in to pressure exerted by staunchly pro-Chalcedonian circles. Such circles may have included the monastic community of the Akoimetoi, who had been famous for their uncompromising commitment to dyophysite Chalcedonian orthodoxy in the first half of the 6th century; see 3.1.17.e.

div.head: EH 3.1.38.e (SR) 48.14–15
div.p: ܗ̣ܘ ܕܝܢ ܒܚܡ̣ܬܐ ܘܒܩܠܐ ܪܡ̇ܐ܆ ܢܨ̇ܐ ܗܘܐ ܥܡܗܘܢ̣ “then, enraged and with a raised voice, he strove against them”: John of Ephesus’s main purpose in this passage is to show how John Scholasticus as a fierce persecutor was brought low by divine punishment so that he completely abandoned his original schemes, clashed with his supporters, and blamed them for the pain he had to endure (cf. van Ginkel 1995, 187–188). Yet despite this clear literary motif, there might well be a kernel of truth in John Scholasticus’s speech when he exclaims that it was really his partisans (that is, staunch pro-Chalcedonians) who were responsible for his actions directed against the Severans which had led to his chastisement. After all, John Scholasticus had, in the first six years of his patriarchate (565–571), been quite far from the uncompromising and fervently dyophysite stance John of Ephesus ascribes to him in the EH. On the contrary, the patriarch had written at least one theological treatise in which he professed the orthodoxy of the “one incarnate nature” formula, completely in line with neo-Chalcedonian Christology as expressed in Justin’s two edicts on faith from 567 and 571 (see 3.1.5.e). His turn towards the suppression of the Severan clergy in 571 therefore appears surprising. It is best explained by the hypothesis that his original reconciliatory approach may have led to considerable criticism from the part of Chalcedonian hardliners. Crucially, such criticism was dangerous for John Scholasticus given that it further undermined his authority at a moment when his hold on the patriarchal throne still was far from secure: his deposed predecessor Eutychius never accepted John Scholasticus as legitimate patriarch, and we may assume that he still had supporters (Roggo 2022, 6–8 and 177–181). Consequently, as already noted in 3.1.37.i, John needed to be wary of antagonizing staunchly pro-Chalcedonian sections of the clergy as they could otherwise have become supporters of the exiled Eutychius. Quite possibly, the patriarch might thus have been compelled to give in to pressure exerted by such pro-Chalcedonian circles in 571 when he suddenly insisted on the reordination of the Severan clerics who had entered into communion with the Chalcedonians (Roggo 2022, 108–111).
