This move may have rendered John Scholasticus more popular among among strict Chalcedonians and thereby stabilized his position as patriarch, but it also caused the failure of Justin’s edict on faith when the disillusioned Severan bishops withdrew from joint communion (see 3.1.12.f and 3.1.24.l).

div.head: EH 3.1.38.f (PMF; NK) 48.17–18
div.p: ܣ̇ܦ̈ܩܢ ܠܘ̈ܛܬܐ܇ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܪܘܓܙܐ ܕܡܢ ܫܡܝܐ ܐ̇ܥ̣ܝܪܝܢ ܘܐ̇ܝܬ̣ܝܝܢ “These curses suffice, which have awakened the wrath of heaven and brought it upon me”: On our interpretation of this passage, ܠܘ̈ܛܬܐ “curses” is the subject of the two verbs in the relative clause: ܐ̇ܥ̣ܝܪܝܢ ܘܐ̇ܝܬ̣ܝܝܢ “have awakened and brought.” Brooks emended the two verbs to ܐ̇ܥ̣ܝܪ ܐܢ̈ܝܢ ܘܐ̇ܝܬ̣ܝ ܐ̈ܢܝܢ, where “anger” is the subject of the verbs and the repeated third person feminine plural object pronoun ܐ̈ܢܝܢ refers back to ܠܘ̈ܛܬܐ “curses.” This would result in a translation of: “These curses suffice, which anger from heaven has awakened and brought upon me.” In favor of Brooks’ emendation, one can note that anger also appears as the acting subject in EH 3.1.37: ܥܕܡܐ ܕܪܘܓܙܐ ܕܡܢ ܫܡܝܐ ܐܕܪܟ̣ܗ “until anger from heaven overtook him.” Yet we have adopted a more conservative approach to emending the text and have followed the manuscript reading which seems equally plausible.

div.head: EH 3.1.38.g (SR) 48.21–24
div.p: ܘܡܢ ܩܕܡ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܕܝܢ ܐܝܟ ܕܐܡ̣ܪܢ܆ ܘܬܘܒ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ. ܗ̇ܘ ܕܗܘ̣ܐ ܒܥܓܠ܆ ܒܝܬ ܟܢ̈ܘ̣ܫܝܐ ܕܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ܆ ܡܟܝܠ ܒܦܪܗܣܝܐ ܣܓܝܐܬܐ̣ ܕܠܐ ܕܚܠ̣ܬܐ ܟܢܫ̇ܝܢ ܗܘܘ “but before his death, as we have said, and also after his death which soon came, they assembled in the gathering places of the faithful in full freedom of assembly, without fear”: The respite the Severans were able to enjoy in the mid-570s was in all likelihood due to the accession of Tiberius as Caesar in December 574. He was, at least prior to the death of Justin in October 578, quite liberal with regard to his religious policy. He was opposed to the exertion of violence against the Severans and prohibited John Scholasticus from acting against the Severan clergy (see 3.1.37.a and 3.1.37.b). John of Ephesus provides ample evidence that the situation of the Severans and of himself improved in Tiberius’s early years: Both John himself and Conon of Tarsus were released from prison (3.1.31.h and 3.2.7.f); Egyptian Severans who had been held in Constantinople for three years were allowed to leave (3.1.33.f); some monasteries in Constantinople returned to the Severan communion (3.1.36.f). As Booth (2017, 169–171) has shown, John of Ephesus’s testimony about Tiberius’s relative disentanglement from doctrinal issues is confirmed by the establishment of a parallel Severan episcopal hierarchy in Egypt in the mid-570s (3.1.34.a and 3.1.34.b; see also Roggo (2022, 118–120)). This favorable situation first continued after John Scholasticus’s death on 31 August 577. Tiberius rejected the demands of the new patriarch Eutychius for a suppression of the Severans, and it was only towards the end of 578, when Tiberius had become the sole focus of criticism, that he had to give in and violence against the Severans in Constantinople was resumed (3.1.37.j and 3.1.37.k; see Roggo (2022, 125–127)).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: John of Ephesus highlights the Christian virtues of Narses, as the conqueror and administrator of Italy one of the most important imperial functionaries of his time. He recalls that Narses provided the means for founding the Kathara monastery in Bithynia and settled monks there who had been expelled from Cappadocia. According to John, Narses’s corpse was, after his death in Italy, brought back and buried there, with even the emperors attending the ceremony. In this chapter, there is no explicit mention that the monastic community Narses supported was anti-Chalcedonian (nor that Narses himself was), yet John suggests as much when he returns to this account in EH 3.2.46.

div.head: Parallel Accounts (SR)
div.p: Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6063 (de Boor 1883, 243.32–244.2): Narses’s foundation of the Kathara monastery Anastasius Bibliothecarius, Chronicle Tripertita (de Boor 1885, 150.12–14): derivative of Theophanes Symeon Logothete, Chronicle 105.8 (Wahlgren 2006, 148.48–50): derivative of Theophanes George Cedrenus, Historiarum compendium 421.5 (Tartaglia 2016, 658.7–9): derivative of Theophanes

div.head: EH 3.1.39.a (SR) 48.27
div.p: ܕܝܪܐ ܗܟܝܠ ܗܕܐ ܕܩ̇ܬܪܐ “the monastery of Kathara”: The exact location of this monastery is unknown, yet EH 3.2.46 and the Life of Theodore of Sykeon 131 (Festugière 1970, 104–105) attest that it was close to Pythia (a town famous for its thermal baths, see 3.1.15.k and 3.1.26.d) on the south-east coast of the Propontis Belke 2020, 653–654 Janin 1975, 158–160 Honigmann 1939, 618–619.

The information provided in this chapter suggests that it was founded prior to 551, when Narses was dispatched to Italy at the head of a Roman army (Procopius, Wars 8.21.6 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 2:601); Malalas, Chronicle 18.110 (Thurn 2000, 412); see Brodka (2018, 125–126)). PLRE 3:927 (Martindale 1992) argues for 545 as terminus post quem because of John’s remark in EH 3.2.46 that Justin (who acceded to the throne in 565) visited the monastery twenty years after its foundation and attempted to win the monks over for Chalcedonian communion. Possibly, this imperial visit took place during Justin’s trip to the baths (of Pythia) recorded in 3.1.26.d and might be connected to his efforts for an ecclesiastical union based on the edict of 571 (see 3.1.26.b). If both this assumption and the indication of twenty years are correct, then the monastery might have been founded only a short time prior to Narses’s dispatch in 551.

There has been some confusion with regard to the name of the monastery. Brooks (1936, 34.8–9) seems to have been misled by the fact that ܩ̇ܬܪܐ also is the Syriac term for “rock” and translated accordingly monasterium Rupis, but John of Ephesus simply transliterated the Greek Καθαρᾶ in this way. Moreover, starting with Schönfelder (1862, 36), some scholars assumed that the Greek name of the monastery stemmed from the adjective καθαρός, “pure,” and sought to establish a connection with the Christian group of the Novatians who were also called καθαροί, “the pure ones” Mango and Scott 1997, 360 n.2 Honigmann 1939, 617–618 Stein 1919, 34 n.17. However, Janin’s (1975, 160) meticulous analysis of the monastery’s name as transmitted in sources from the iconoclast period led him to conclude that it was likely named after a person called Καθαρᾶς, and that its original name was τὰ Καθαρᾶ, “[the house] of Katharas.” His suggestion has been accepted in scholarship Belke 2020, 653 Brodka 2018, 248 Cheynet and Flusin 1990, 205–207.

div.head: EH 3.1.39.b (SR) 48.27–28
div.p: ܡܢ ܢ̇ܪܣܝ ܟܕ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ̣ ܟ̇ܪܛܘܠܪܐ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܐ ܒܦܠܛܝܢ “by Narses when he was the chartularios of the emperor in the palace”: Most probably, the founder of the monastery is identical with the famous general and eunuch Narses who completed the reconquest of Italy in the 550s (the most recent scholarly overview of his career is Brodka 2018, see 248 for the foundation of the Kathara monastery); see also PLRE 3:912–928 (Martindale 1992). This identification is suggested by John of Ephesus himself when he writes that Narses was sent to Rome and emerged victorious from many wars (see 3.1.39.e). However, the compilers of the PLRE seem not to have been in agreement on this question; the foundation of the Kathara monastery is ascribed both to Narses the general (3:927) and to another military officer and eunuch also called Narses (3:930–931), who appears in EH 3.6.30–3.6.32 as a spatharios. The latter hypothesis was proposed by Sevcenko (1970, esp. 4) and has been accepted by some scholars (Mango and Scott 1997, 360 n.1), yet it is highly unlikely since Narses the spatharios only died late in 581, thus after the completion of Book 1 (see 3.1.41.o), and he did so in the Balkans, and not in Italy, as John asserts here. Importantly, the Chronicle of Theophanes AM 6063 (de Boor 1883, 243.32–244.2), the sole other source to report the foundation of the Kathara monastery, also suggests that this was done by Narses the general, and the translators therefore thought that Theophanes had made a mistake (Mango and Scott 1997, 360 n.1). Finally, John calls Narses chartularios, and the same title is also given to Narses the general in some western sources (Marius of Avenches, Chronicle s.a. 553 (Favrod 1993, 76.7); Agnellus, Liber pontificalis 62 (Mauskopf Deliyannis 2006, 232.18–20); Paul the Deacon, Historia Langobardorum 2.1 and 2.3 (Bethmann and Waitz 1878, 72.20 and 73.16)).

Chartularioi originally were lower-ranking administrative and financial officials, yet the title rose in importance throughout late antiquity. However, the exact nature of Narses’s office as chartularios as recorded by John remains elusive, and it is puzzling that this title for Narses is not attested in any other east Roman source. Stein (1920, 243–251) argued that Narses was, in the 530s, a chartularios and sakellarios in the sacrum cubiculum and as such in charge of the private purse of the emperor. If this is correct, Narses would thus (unofficially) still have been referred to as chartularios even after his promotion to the rank of praepositus sacri cubiculi (in charge of, and controlling access to, the private apartment of the emperor (Scholten 1995)) in c. 538 (see Martindale 1992, 3:914). Note, however, the skeptical remarks by Brandes (2002, 432 and 451–453) who points out that Stein’s theory is based on much speculation. It is more probable that the western sources and John of Ephesus called Narses chartularios by mistake instead of koubikoularios (the overarching term for all the officials (and eunuchs) employed in the sacrum cubiculum) (Brodka 2018, 121–122). Indeed, John refers to Narses as koubikoularios when he returns to the monks of the Kathara monastery in EH 3.2.46, and it is thus conceivable that the term chartularios crept into this passage as the result of a scribal mistake.

The word ܟ̇ܪܛܘܠܪܐ “chartularios” with its basic meaning of “archivist” (Butts 2016b, 154) is in Syriac almost exclusively attested in texts from the second half of the 6th century. John used it twice in LES 56 (Brooks 1926, 543.2 and 545.9); moreover, it occurs once in a letter by Jacob Burdʿoyo in the Documenta Monophysitica (Chabot 1907, 188.26).

div.head: EH 3.1.39.c (SR) 49.3
div.p: ܕܝܪ̈ܝܐ ܕܪ̈ܕܝܦ̣ܝܢ ܗܘܘ ܡܢ ܩܦܕܘܩܝܐ “monks who had been driven out of Cappadocia”: John of Ephesus does not express clearly here which Christian group Narses supported. However, he does label the monastic community as belonging to “orthodoxy” in EH 3.2.46, and it is thus likely that the monks were among those anti-Chalcedonians who had been driven out of their monasteries in the eastern provinces of the empire in the 520s and 530s (see 3.1.5.a and 3.1.10.b). John provides more information about this community in EH 3.2.46 where he notes that it originally consisted of seventy monks and that its Cappadocian monastery was called Gordiason. He adds there that the monks settled in different other locations, yet were expelled again. Therefore, a prolonged period may have passed before they could move to the Kathara monastery in Bithynia, and this is reminiscent of the trajectory in the 520s and early 530s of John of Ephesus’s own monastic community after its expulsion from Amida (van Ginkel 1995, 28).

div.head: EH 3.1.39.d (SR) 49.3–6
div.p: ܟܕ ܩܘܪ̈ܝܐ ܠܐ ܙܥܘܪ ܙ̣ܒܢ ܠܗ̇: ܘܥܕܬܐ ܬܡ̣ܝܗܬܐ ܒܢ̣ܐ ܒܗ̇܇ ܘܐ̣ܟܣܢܕܟܝܢ ܥܒ̣ܕ ܒܗ̇܇ ܘܦܪ̈ܘܣܕܢ ܕܠܐ ܙܥܘܪ ܐܩ̣ܢܝܗ̇ “after buying not a few villages for it, building a wondrous church in it, establishing a hospice in it, and allocating it no small amount of revenue”: A parallel to Narses’s rich endowment of the Kathara monastery is found in EH 3.2.41. John notes there that he himself built a monastery together with a church and other buildings on an estate he had received by another praepositus sacri cubiculi and supporter of the Severans, Callinicus. There is a considerable number of monasteries in or close to Constantinople which were founded in the 6th century by aristocrats and high-ranking imperial officials such as Narses, and even more by emperors and members of the imperial family (Hatlie 2007, 187–189). If the short note of Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6063 (de Boor 1883, 243.32–244.2) can be trusted, Narses may even have founded two monasteries, the Kathara monastery in Bithynia and another one in Constantinople itself (Hatlie 2007, 188 n.22).

John’s word for “hospice,” ܐ̣ܟܣܢܕܟܝܢ, stems from the Greek ξενοδοχεῖον; see 3.1.29.f. He also used a second Greek loanword in this passage, ܦܪ̈ܘܣܕܢ (πρόσοδον; “revenue”), and he appears to be the only Syriac author to have employed this term Brockelmann 1928, 601 R. Payne Smith 1879, 3248.

div.head: EH 3.1.39.e (SR) 49.6–8
div.p: ܒܝܕ ܥܘܕܪܢܐ ܕܐܠܗܐ ܕܠܘ̣ܐ ܗܘܐ ܠܗ܆ ܐܬܢܨܚ ܒܙܟ̈ܘܬܐ ܪ̈ܘܪܒܬܐ ܕܩܪ̈ܒܐ ܣܓ̈ܝܐܐ “through the help of God who accompanied him, he was celebrated for great victories in many wars”: Under Narses’s commando, the reconquest of Italy was completed in the first half of the 550s, although some strongholds resisted until the early 560s. He achieved decisive victories over the Ostrogothic kings Totila and Teia, campaigned successfully against invading Franks and Alemanni, and subsequently served as governor of Italy until the late 560s, thereby suppressing several revolts Brodka 2018, 119–207 Martindale 1992, 3:916–925 Stein 1949, 597–611.

The sources stress his great piety; according to Evagrius, EH 4.24 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 171.13–18), he always waited for a sign from the Virgin Mary before joining battle. Yet John of Ephesus is the only author who reports that Narses’s piety apparently was such that he even intended to become a monk in the monastery he had founded. John is very positive towards Narses: he presents him as a faithful anti-Chalcedonian in EH 3.2.46; moreover, in EH 3.5.2, he asserts that the “tritheist” leaders Conon and Eugenius in vain attempted to win Narses for John Philoponus’s trinitarian doctrine in the second half of the 560s. Narses’s supposed anti-Chalcedonian sympathies are also recorded in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.5 (Chabot 1899–1910, 2:295.a.32–296.a.1). Older scholarship Martindale 1992, 3:927 Stein 1949, 356–357 therefore described Narses as (moderately) anti-Chalcedonian, yet this notion conflicts with the fact that western sources stress that he was a “Catholic” (Paul the Deacon, Historia Langobardorum 2.3 (Bethmann and Waitz 1878, 73.17)). He always remained in communion with the Roman church during his time in Italy, and this evidently precluded an openly anti-Chalcedonian stance. It is likely that he did not choose a clear position in the Christological controversy; he may have kept good relations with both sides of the debate so that both could regard him as belonging to their party (Brodka 2018, 247–249).

div.head: EH 3.1.39.f (SR) 49.9
div.p: ܢܦ̣ܩ ܡܢ ܥܠܡܐ “he departed from the world”: Narses died in Rome, supposedly at the age of ninety-five years; the traditional date of his death is 574 (Liber pontificalis 63 (Duchesne 1886, 1:306.1–7); Agnellus, Liber pontificalis 95 (Mauskopf Deliyannis 2006, 260.78–81)). This date was embraced, for instance, by Stein (1919, 34 n.17) who argued that the fact that emperors attended his funeral in the Kathara monastery (see 3.1.39.g) meant that this took place after December 574, when Tiberius had become Caesar. Recently, however, Brodka (2018, 243–245) presented arguments that he may have died a few years earlier, in about 571/572, based on the observation that Agnellus and Paul the Deacon place his death prior to that of the Lombard king Alboin (d. 572). In the end, the question has to remain open since the Liber pontificalis 63 (Duchesne 1886, 1:306.4–7) stresses that Narses died at about the same time as the Roman bishop John III (d. 13 July 574).

div.head: EH 3.1.39.g (SR) 49.10–11
div.p: ܟܕ ܡ̈ܠܟܐ ܐܘܒ̣ܠܘ ܣܡ̣ܘܗܝ ܒܗ̇ ܘܩ̇ܕܫܘܗܝ “with the emperors carrying and placing him in it and consecrating him”: Western sources confirm John’s statement that the remains of Narses were transferred to Constantinople (Liber pontificalis 63 (Duchesne 1886, 1:306.2–3); Paul the Deacon, Historia Langobardorum 2.11 (Bethmann and Waitz 1878, 79.7–8)). Stein (1919, 34 n.17) assumed that the emperors in question had to be Justin II and Tiberius and therefore dated the funeral to after December 574, yet PLRE 3:926 Martindale 1992; followed by Brodka 2018, 243 noted that John’s “emperors” might just as well refer to Justin and Sophia, and indeed this plural is used repeatedly with this sense in Book 1 (see 3.1.11.e). Both options remain possible since we do not know the year of Narses’s death. Irrespective of the identity of the emperors in question, the fact that an emperor participated at all in a funeral of someone who did not belong to the imperial family is highly extraordinary; in 582, for instance, Tiberius chose not to attend the funeral of his close associate, patriarch Eutychius (Life of Eutychius 2678–2680 (Laga 1992, 85)). This testifies to the great esteem in which Narses was held, and it renders the claims of the Liber pontificalis 63 (Duchesne 1886, 1:305–306) and Paul the Deacon, Historia Langobardorum 2.5 (Bethmann and Waitz 1878, 75) very unlikely according to which Narses fell out with Justin and Sophia in the late 560s, was dismissed from his post, and invited the Lombards to invade Italy in order to take revenge Brodka 2018, 238 Martindale 1992, 3:925.

There are no known examples when an emperor personally assisted in the carrying and positioning of a coffin in a church. Moreover, the final verb in John’s account, ܩ̇ܕܫܘܗܝ (“[the emperors] consecrated him”) appears to be unfitting. Evidently, it lay not within the emperors’ power to “consecrate” or “sanctify” someone – and there is no indication that Narses was ever venerated as a saint. Probably, all these actions should not be understood literally, and the emperors simply may have been present when the coffin was carried and laid into the church (for the single elements of the funerary rites, see Koep, Stommel, and Kollwitz 1954, 208–218). According to John, Narses was buried in the church he had founded, and the “consecration” may then just indicate that he was laid to rest ad sanctos, that is, near the relics kept in the church. It is also possible that by writing ܩ̇ܕܫܘܗܝ, John wanted to express that the emperors participated in the celebration of the Eucharist in the memory of Narses after they had accompanied the transferral of his body from Constantinople to the Kathara monastery (see J. Payne Smith 1903, 491 for the use of this verb in an Eucharistic context).

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: The final chapters of Book 1 consist of lists of patriarchs (both Chalcedonian and anti-Chalcedonian) of the two most important episcopal sees from an anti-Chalcedonian perspective, namely Alexandria and Antioch, to which John of Ephesus added a list of the bishops of the empire’s political center, Constantinople, in EH 3.1.42. In the present chapter, John included bishops of Alexandria from different doctrinal groups; he named not only the Severan office-holders, but also the Chalcedonian and Julianist incumbents. John added or kept updating these final chapters until some years after 577/578, when he had completed the rest of Book 1 (van Ginkel 1995, 73). The patriarchs are listed accurately until the year 581, and it seems that he no longer updated these chapters in the 580s. The inclusion of such episcopal lists was a common feature of ecclesiastical historiography as it enabled authors to present the history of the church as an uninterrupted chain stretching back to the apostles, yet they are by far not as central in John’s EH as they are in that of Eusebius Leppin 2019, 133 van Ginkel 1995, 197. Such lists only occur in the chapters at the end of Book 1. Similar (yet less accurate) lists are found in the EH of John’s contemporary Evagrius (for instance, 4.37; 4.39; 5.16; 6.24 Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 186; 190; 212; 240).

div.head: EH 3.1.40.a (SR) 49.14–16
div.p: ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܕܝܢ ܕܡ̇ܠ̣ܝ ܒܐܦܝܣܩܦܘܬܐ ܕܐܠ̣ܟܣܢܕܪܝܐ: ܝܘܚܢܢ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܡܢ ܦܛܪܝܩܝܘܣ ܕܗܘ̣ܐ ܒܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ: ܘܐܫܬ̇ܕܪ ܠܬܡܢ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܘܡܝ̣ܬ “after completing some years in the episcopate of Alexandria John, who had been a patrikios in the imperial city and had been sent there, died”: John of Ephesus starts his overview of the patriarchs of Alexandria relatively late with the Chalcedonian John IV (sed. 569/570–580/581) – in contrast, EH 3.1.41 and 3.1.42 list all the patriarchs of Antioch and Constantinople after c. 520. This might be different in the case of Alexandria because John probably mentioned the patriarchs of Alexandria already in EH 2, for instance in the context of the death of the Severan patriarch Theodosius in June 566, and he may then simply have continued this list in the present chapter. The fact that he put the Chalcedonian patriarchs first may be explained by the absence of a generally accepted Severan claimant to the see of Alexandria between the death of Theodosius in 566 and the election of Theodore in 575 (see Booth 2017, 164–165).

Only very little is known about John IV Booth 2017, 173 Allen 1980, 12 Maspero 1923, 256–258. John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 402), asserts that he had a military background and that he refrained from imposing Chalcedonian doctrine by force (yet there were in his time deportations of Severan clerics to Constantinople; see 3.1.34.c and 3.4.37). Moreover, Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6062 (de Boor 1883, 243.26–27), records that his ordination as patriarch took place in Constantinople and was performed by John Scholasticus – a procedure which led to considerable inner-ecclesiastical tensions, especially with patriarch Anastasius of Antioch, since it could be interpreted as establishing a subordination of the see of Alexandria under that of Constantinople Roggo 2024b, 235 n.55; Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1990b, 63 Allen 1981, 215–216 cf. Maspero 1923, 257 and 259.

The exact dates of his patriarchate are uncertain; the entry in Theophanes suggests that his ordination happened before the deposition of Anastasius of Antioch (which took place in 570). Theophanes attributes eleven years to John’s patriarchate; this together with the information in 3.1.40.b that his successor Eulogius was made patriarch in Tiberius’s third year (26 September 580–25 September 581) points towards an accession in late 569 or early 570. He might have died before September 580 since we may assume that some time elapsed between his death and the accession of Eulogius, and only the latter is securely dated to Tiberius’s third year. John of Ephesus himself does not seem to have known exactly how long John IV had been patriarch when he wrote this chapter, hence his vague formulation ܫ̈ܢܝܐ, “[some] years.”

John of Ephesus is the sole source to record that John IV was a patrikios prior to his ordination as patriarch, yet this sits well with the claim about his military background found in John of Nikiu, Chronicle 94 (Zotenberg 1883, 168.14 and 402): “from the army” (እምሐራ). The appointment of a layman as bishop had become unusual by the 6th century, yet was not without precedent (see 3.1.32.b). It was evidently sensible to dispatch an office-holder with military experience to an unquiet region rife with doctrinal unrest; only some years previously, the abbot Photius had received wide-ranging powers in Egypt “to pacify the churches” (εἰρηνεῦσαι τὰς ἐκκλησίας), as reported by Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6058 (de Boor 1883, 242.6) (see 3.1.32.d).

The phrase ܗ̇ܘ ܕܡܢ ܦܛܪܝܩܝܘܣ, literally “he who [was] from [the rank of] patrikios,” is probably a calque on the Greek use of the preposition ἀπό to denote “the former.” For instance, patriarch John Scholasticus is called ὁ ἀπὸ σχολαστικῶν, “the ex-scholastikos” in Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6057 and 6058 (de Boor 1883, 240.29 and 241.28); the phrase ὁ ἀπὸ ἐπάρχων, “the ex-prefect,” occurs several times in Malalas (e.g, Chronicle 16.16 and 16.19 (Thurn 2000, 332.79 and 333.21)); Evagrius calls himself ἀπὸ ἐπάρχων, “ex-prefect,” at the very end of his EH (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 241.6). The Ordo generis Cassiodororum (Galonnier 1996) offers another comparable example in Latin from the 6th century when it calls Cassiodor “ex patricio.”

div.head: EH 3.1.40.b (SR) 49.16–18
div.p: ܘܐܬ̣ܩܪܝ ܡܢ ܒܬܪܗ܆ ܐܢܫ ܕܫܡܗ̣ ܐܘ̣ܠܓܝܘܣ ܐܟ̣ܣܢܕܟܪܐ ܡܢ ܐܢܛܝܟܝܐ. ܘܗܘ̣ܐ ܬܡܢ ܦܦܐ܆ ܒܫܢܬ ܬܠܬ ܕܙܟ̇ܝܐ ܛܒ̣ܪܝܘܣ “a certain person named Eulogius who had been in charge of a hospice in Antioch was called in his place. He became pope there in the third year of the victorious Tiberius”: This passage provides a secure start date for Eulogius’s patriarchate; the third year of Tiberius lasted from 26 September 580 to 25 September 581. Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6075 (de Boor 1883, 252.22), states that he was patriarch of Alexandria for 27 years, which allows us to establish 607 or 608 as the year of his death. Eulogius was a prolific theologian, engaged in debates with assorted anti-Chalcedonian groups, yet only small fragments of his texts have been preserved. The detailed synopses of some of his writings in Photius’s Bibliotheke 225–227 and 230 (Henry 1959–1991, 4:99–114 and 5:8–64) are crucial for reconstructing his theological thought Booth 2017, 173–174 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1990b, 66–72 Allen 1980, 12–13 Maspero 1923, 258–275.

No other source records that Eulogius was xenodocharios (in charge of a hospice) in Antioch prior to his appointment, yet his Antiochene background is well attested: according to a note in Photius, Bibliotheke 226 (Henry 1959–1991, 4:111.3–5), he was priest in Antioch and the superior of the monastery of the Theotokos. Furthermore, John of Ephesus reports in EH 3.3.29 that Eulogius lived in Antioch in early 580 and became involved, together with patriarch Gregory of Antioch, in the so-called “pagan affair” Roggo 2024b; 2022, 157–164. The fact that he was made patriarch of Alexandria soon afterwards shows that the accusations brought forth against him were quickly dismissed; a statement of faith written by him and sent to patriarch Eutychius is mentioned in Photius, Bibliotheke 227 (Henry 1959–1991, 4:114.14–18), and may belong to this context when he needed to give proof of his orthodoxy before he could be ordained patriarch Roggo 2024b, 233; 2022, 163–164. In all likelihood, the ceremony took place in Constantinople, just as in the case of his predecessor John IV (Maspero 1923, 259).
For Tiberius’s epithet ܙܟ̇ܝܐ (“victorious”), see 3.1.4.k.

div.head: EH 3.1.40.c (SR) 49.19–20
div.p: ܕܡ̣ܪܘܣ ܕܝܢ ܕܝܘ̈ܠܝܢܣܛܐ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ̣ ܕܪܐܬܐܣ܇ ܗ̇ܘ ܕܡܢ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܣܓܝܐ̈ܬܐ ܥܒ̣ܝܕ ܗܘܐ ܘܝܬ̇ܒ ܬܡܢ “but of the faction of the Julianists it was Dorotheus who had been ordained and had resided there for many years”: More than two thirds of the chapter are devoted to the anti-Chalcedonian claimants to the see of Alexandria. This is contrary to what the title claims, which creates the impression that the chapter only deals with the “synodite” (ܣܘܢܗ̈ܕܝܛܐ) patriarchs of Alexandria. The discrepancy between title and actual content suggests that John of Ephesus may not have been the author of the titles and that they may have been added at a later date (see 3.1.28.a for a similar case).

The “Julianists” (ܝܘ̈ܠܝܢܣܛܐ) are the followers of Julian of Halicarnassus, an influential anti-Chalcedonian theologian active in the first decades of the 6th century. Julian fell out with his fellow anti-Chalcedonian Severus of Antioch over the question of the incorruptibility of Jesus Christ when he argued that Christ had been subjected to the wanting human condition only because he chose to do so, and not out of necessity for the aphthartist theology of Julian, the essential study still is Draguet 1924; see also Y. Moss 2016; Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 83–116. Those adhering to his Christology quickly fragmented, yet Julianist thought spread throughout the empire and different groups in his tradition remained influential to the end of the 6th century Yirga 2022; Possekel 2013; Kofsky 2013; Wierzejski 2016. Egypt in particular was a stronghold of the Julianists; they were serious rivals to the anti-Chalcedonians in the tradition of Severus. In 535, they even managed to oust the newly elected Theodosius of Alexandria and to install Gaianus as patriarch in his stead. He was deposed by imperial troops after a few months; after him, the Egyptian Julianists are also called Gaianites Yirga 2022, 527–529 Kofsky 2013, 252–253.

The Julianists still played a dominant role among the anti-Chalcedonian factions in Egypt in the 560s, and Justinian’s interest in aphthartist doctrine at the end of his reign may have contributed to their appeal. Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6057 (de Boor 1883, 241.7–10), notes that they ordained a bishop for themselves, a certain Elpidius (on him, see Roggo 2022, 37–38 with further literature). The imperial authorities detained him and he died soon afterwards, but there is a strong probability that he was replaced rather quickly by the same Dorotheus John mentions here. The fact that Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6057 (de Boor 1883, 241.10–11), introduces Dorotheus as a “Theodosian” (that is, a follower of the Severan patriarch Theodosius of Alexandria) prompted some scholars for instance, Maspero 1923, 214–216; 223; 232 to conclude that there must have been two bishops called Dorotheus, a Severan and a Julianist one. However, Booth (2017, 164–165) has argued convincingly that there was probably just one Dorotheus who managed to unite (some of) the Egyptian Julianists and Severans. This union, attested only in Theophanes, did not last long, and it may have been this complicated situation among the Egyptian anti-Chalcedonians that led to the dispatch of Photius early in the reign of Justin II (see 3.1.32.d).

If Booth’s hypothesis is correct, Dorotheus was Julianist patriarch of Alexandria from c. 565 at least until 580/581, when John wrote this chapter. Nothing more is known about him; as Booth (2017, 164–165) has noted, it was evidently not in the interest of a Severan author such as John of Ephesus to admit that there had been a Julianist claimant to the patriarchal see of Alexandria who had also been recognized by at least some Severans. Dorotheus might be identical with an unnamed Julianist patriarch of Alexandria briefly mentioned in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.31 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:265.b.20–30; 4:321.c.36–322.b.2.

div.head: EH 3.1.40.d (SR) 49.21–23
div.p: ܕܡ̣ܪܣ ܬܘܒ ܕܒܝܬ ܬܐܕܘܣܝܣ ܒܬܪ ܙܒܢܐ ܕܡ̇ܘܬܗ܆ ܠܘܩܕܡ ܗ̣ܘܐ ܐܢܫ ܕܫܡܗ̣ ܬܐܕܘܪܘܣ ܡ̇ܕܒܪܢܐ ܣܘܪܝܝܐ “and of the faction of the followers of Theodosius some time after his death it was first a certain person named Theodore, a monastic superior from Syria”: The followers of Theodosius of Alexandria were the Egyptian Severans. After Theodosius’s death in Constantinople in June 566, there was no Severan claimant to the see of Alexandria until 575 (although some Egyptian Severans probably entered into communion with the Julianist Dorotheus at the start of Justin’s reign, see 3.1.40.c). John of Ephesus refers to this gap here with “some time after [Theodosius’s] death” (ܒܬܪ ܙܒܢܐ ܕܡ̇ܘܬܗ); in EH 3.4.12, he asserts that the vacancy in the Severan leadership lasted “ten years” (ܥܣܪ̈ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ). Document 42 of the Documenta Monophysitica (Chabot 1907, 271.15) states that it lasted “nine years and more” (ܬܫܥ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ ܘܝܬܝܪܐ), and this sits well with the possible date of 23 June 575 proposed by Brooks (1930, 474 n.4) for the ordination of Theodore.

Theodore was of Syrian origin, as John repeats in EH 3.4.10, and the abbot of the (otherwise unknown) monastery of Rhamnis in the Scetis valley to the south of Alexandria. Brooks (1936, 34.29) translated “eremita” instead of “monastic superior,” probably because he read ܡ̇ܕܒܪܝܐ instead of ܡ̇ܕܒܪܢܐ. Yet John calls him ܪܝܫ ܕܝܪܐ, “abbot,” in EH 3.4.10.

His ordination as patriarch of Alexandria in 575 proved highly controversial (see 3.1.34.b). It was performed despite Theodore’s resistance by Syrian bishops acting with the approval of Paul of Antioch, who was heavily criticized and had lost much of his authority because he had entered into Chalcedonian communion in 571. After his recantation, he had to undergo a period of penance of three years; this period had not yet ended at the time of Theodore’s ordination (see 3.1.17.d; 3.2.8; 3.4.15). Theodore was thus seen by some as the creature of a bishop who had lost his legitimacy. Furthermore, the Alexandrian clergy had not been informed about the choice of candidate, did not accept Theodore, and proceeded to the election of another patriarch, Peter IV, about six weeks later Booth 2017, 167–168 Allen 2013, 191 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1990b, 72–75 Honigmann 1951, 200–202 and 227 Brooks 1930, 473–474 Hermann 1928; Maspero 1923, 236–240. John of Ephesus treats the ensuing conflict between the supporters of Peter and Paul and its long-lasting consequences in Book 4 of the EH. The synodal letter Theodore sent to Paul in 575 has been preserved in the Documenta Monophysitica Chabot 1907, 298–308 see the discussion in Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1990b, 73–75 Van Roey and Allen 1994, 299. Yet support for Theodore was marginal in Egypt; consequently, he returned to his monastery (3.4.13). John nevertheless considered him as the legitimate Severan patriarch of Alexandria; he reports that Theodore eventually grew angry with Paul of Antioch, who had brought him into this uncomfortable situation, and traveled to Cyprus in the vain hope of finding Paul there (3.4.55–3.4.56). The year of his death is unknown; the fact that John does not mention his end suggests that he was still alive in the mid-580s.

div.head: EH 3.1.40.e (SR; NK) 49.23–27
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܝܠ̣ܦܘ ܩܠ̣ܝܪ̈ܝܩܘ ܘܫܪܟܐ ܡܢ ܗܢܐ̣ ܗܦ̣ܟܘ. ܘܟܕ ܠܐ ܩܒ̇ܠܘܗܝ ܕܠܐ ܛܟ̣ܣܐ ܩܢܘܢܝܐ܆ ܐ̣ܙܕܪܟܠܘ ܘܥܒ̣ܕܘ ܐܚܪܢܐ ܠܥܠ ܡܢܗ܆ ܕܫܡܗ̣ ܦܛܪܘܣ. ܘܟܕ ܗܢܐ ܡ̇ܠܝ ܘܥܒ̣ܕ ܝܬܝܪ ܡܢ ܬܡ̈ܢܐܝܢ ܐܦܝ̈ܣܩܘܦܝ̣ܢ ܡܝ̣ܬ “when the clerics and others learned this, they turned from him. Since they did not receive him against the canonical order, they brashly ordained another above him whose name was Peter. When this one had completed his time and ordained more than eighty bishops, he died”: The majority of the Severan clergy in Alexandria did not accept Theodore as patriarch (see 3.1.34.c and 3.1.40.d). “Forty days and more” (ܥܪ̈ܒܝܢ ܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܘܝܬܝܪ) (Chabot 1907, 269.20) after the ordination of Theodore, Peter IV was created (according to Brooks’s (1930, 474 n.4) reconstruction of events on 4 August 575). He had served as a deacon in the church of Alexandria; John of Ephesus qualifies him as elderly and “simple and unlearned” (ܦܫܝܛܐ ܘܗܕܝܘܛܐ) in EH 3.4.11. Moreover, John claims in 3.4.11–3.4.12 that Peter was completely under the control of influential Alexandrian clergymen who acted through him, yet it is likely that he denigrated him because he considered Theodore as the legitimate and canonically correctly ordained patriarch on Peter, see Booth 2017, 167–168 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1990b, 72–73 Blaudeau 1997, 336–339 Honigmann 1951, 201 and 227 Brooks 1930, 474–475 Maspero 1923, 238–240 and 245–249.

At first, Peter’s ordination was opposed by leading Severans outside Egypt; Jacob Burdʿoyo attacked him as a “new Gaianus” (ܓܝܢܐ ܚܕܬܐ; 3.4.15), thus comparing the situation to the schism that had occurred among the Egyptian anti-Chalcedonians in 535 when the Julianist Gaianus was elected against Theodosius of Alexandria (see 3.1.40.c). However, Jacob quickly changed sides, traveled to Alexandria, exchanged synodal letters with Peter and eventually even consented to the formal deposition of Paul of Antioch whom the Alexandrines accused of having intruded in their affairs by encouraging the ordination of Theodore (EH 3.4.15–3.4.18). John of Ephesus deplored all these developments in Book 4 of the EH because this resulted in an inner-Severan schism between the Alexandrines around Peter and the supporters of Jacob Burdʿoyo on the one side, and the supporters (including John himself) of Paul of Antioch and Theodore on the other (Allen 2013, 191–196) (see 3.1.41.n).

According to John, Peter also embarked on a large-scale project of creating new Severan bishops throughout Egypt immediately after his ordination. In the passage here, he speaks of eighty bishops ordained by Peter; in EH 3.4.12 and 3.4.16, he mentions seventy. Given that Peter’s patriarchate lasted only about two years, these great numbers are probably exaggerated (Wipszycka 2011, 267). However, there is no reason to doubt the fact that he did perform episcopal ordinations, and Booth (2017, 168–169) has argued that these ordinations constituted the definitive establishment of a Severan ecclesiastical hierarchy in Egypt. Peter brought “the Severan episcopate back from the brink of annihilation” (Booth 2017, 168) after the thorough implantation of Chalcedonian bishops throughout Egypt by Justinian (see 3.1.5.a). This rapid growth of parallel episcopal structures was only possible because the new Caesar Tiberius adopted a more lenient doctrinal stance and preferred to refrain from a policy of persecution (see 3.1.34.b and 3.1.37.b).

The traditional date of Peter’s death is 19 June 577 (Maspero 1923, 278). This does not sit well with the indication in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.13 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:325.17; 4:357.a.35–36 according to which Peter died three years after his ordination. Yet a tenure of three years (from August 575 to August 578) is certainly too long, since his successor Damian was already patriarch and managed to send a long synodal letter to Jacob Burdʿoyo well before the latter’s death on 30 July 578 (see EH 3.4.33 and Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.14 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:325–334; 4:358–363). Peter’s death thus probably indeed occurred in mid- to late 577 (Blaudeau 1997, 334 n.2).

Brooks (1935, 49 n.2) proposed that the length of Peter’s time as bishop may have been omitted following the verb ܡ̇ܠܝ (“he had completed”): “Aliquid excidisse videtur, vel auctor tempus episcopatus consulto vacuum reliquit.” There is a comparable construction about an undefined number of years (ܫ̈ܢܝܐ) following the verb ܡ̇ܠ̣ܝ in the initial sentence in this chapter (3.1.40.a). Here, however, the manuscript does not indicate the absence of such a specific temporal reference. It is therefore reasonable to propose the insertion of “his time.”

div.head: EH 3.1.40.f (SR) 49.27–28
div.p: ܘܥܒ̣ܕܘܢ ܠܐܢܫ ܣܘܪܝܝܐ܆ ܕܫܡܗ̣ ܕܡܝ̣ܢܐ ܘܦܫ̣ܘ̣ ܟܕ ܣܕ̣ܝܩܝܢ “They ordained a certain person from Syria whose name was Damian, and they persisted in causing a schism”: Damian was probably made patriarch shortly after Peter’s death in 577, whom he had served as secretary (Blaudeau 1997, 334 n.2). During his long patriarchate of almost thirty years (577–c. 606), the episcopal structures set up by Peter IV were stabilized; he is one of the founding figures of the Coptic church (Booth 2017, 188–189). Just like his rival Theodore, he hailed from Syria; Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.17 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:344.33–35; 4:369.a.7–11, attests that he had an elite background and that his brother served as the governor of the province of Osrhoene. For an overview of Damian’s activities and his theological stances, see Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1990b, 76–82 C. D. G. Müller 1986; Maspero 1923, 278–317.

One of Damian’s main aims appears to have been the re-establishment of Alexandrine dominance within the Severan community, just as it had been until the death of Theodosius in 566 (Blaudeau 1997, 343–344 and 355–359). Large parts of the Alexandrian clergy considered it as unacceptable that Paul, an Antiochene patriarch of contested legitimacy, had been at least indirectly involved in the ordination of Theodore in 575. We may assume that anti-Antiochene sentiment played a role for Theodore’s rejection and the subsequent ordinations of Peter IV and Damian (3.1.40.d). Damian was from the beginning of his patriarchate in alliance with the Syrian party around Jacob Burdʿoyo who was equally opposed to Paul. Paul had been declared deposed already by Peter (3.1.40.e), and Damian then repeatedly attempted to ordain a new Severan patriarch of Antioch who would replace Paul. John of Ephesus, a supporter of Paul, was heavily critical of Damian and included a detailed account of his machinations in EH 3.4.39–3.4.45: Damian first failed and was almost arrested in Antioch in the winter 579/580, but he managed to escape to Constantinople and participated in a synod chaired by the Arab client king al-Mundhir in March 580. That synod aimed to reconcile the factions of Damian and Paul, and Damian indeed agreed to recognize Paul. However, he recanted as soon as he had returned to Alexandria and eventually succeeded, in 581, in establishing Peter of Callinicum (sed. 581–591) as Severan patriarch of Antioch Roggo 2022, 128–130 Blaudeau 1997, 345–354; see 3.1.41.o. It is for this reason that John states here that the schism (between Paul and Damian and their respective supporters) continued. His anger at Damian because of the failed reconciliation was such that he claimed that Damian and his party “were in league with Satan” (ܥܡ ܣܛܢܐ ܟܕܝܢܝܢ; 3.4.43).

By placing his own candidate on the patriarchal see of Antioch, Damian thus reaffirmed Alexandrian dominance in the nascent Severan church, yet Peter of Callinicum soon proved to be an independent theologian: the two Severan patriarchs clashed in c. 586 over the doctrine of “tritheism” (on which see 3.1.31.b). Damian labeled Peter as a tritheist, and the ensuing schism between Antioch and Alexandria (of which John of Ephesus shows no awareness in EH 3) could only be resolved in c. 616, well after Damian’s death Allen 2013, 196–197 Ebied, Roey, and Wickham 1981, 34–43.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: Among the chapters containing episcopal lists in EH 3.1.40–3.1.42, this one is the most detailed. John of Ephesus presents a fairly elaborate overview of all the patriarchs of Antioch, both Chalcedonian and Severan, who were active during his lifetime (507–c. 589). This chapter is thus an important source for the patriarchate of Antioch in the 6th century, especially since John often does not only list names and numbers of years, but also provides some background information about the individual patriarchs.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.a (SR) 50.3–5
div.p: ܒܐܢܛܝܟܝܐ ܗܟܝܠ ܪܒܬܐ ܕܣܘܪܝܐ: ܒܝ̈ܘܡܝ ܐܢܣܛܣ ܡ̇ܠܟܐ܆ ܗ̣ܘܐ̣ ܦܠܘܝܢܐ. ܗܢܐ ܕܐܬܟ̣ܣܣ ܒܗܪܣܝܣ ܕܬܪ̈ܝܝ ܟ̈ܝܢܐ܆ ܘܐܫ̣ܬܕܝ ܡܢ ܟܘܪܣܝܗ. ܟܕ ܗ̣ܘܐ̣ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ “now, in the great Antioch of Syria in the days of the emperor Anastasius was Flavian who was culpable of the heresy of the dyophysites. He was banished from his see when he had been there some years”: Patriarch Flavian II (sed. 498–512) of Antioch was a moderate Chalcedonian who, at least in the first half of his tenure, also was acceptable to most anti-Chalcedonians: he supported the Henotikon which had declared orthodox the first three ecumenical councils and did not mention Chalcedon for Flavian’s theological position, see Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 304–306 Grillmeier 1991, 306–312. He hailed from Syria and had a monastic background; prior to his appointment as patriarch, he had been the apokrisiarios (representative) of the church of Antioch in Constantinople. After 505, he found himself in an increasingly heated conflict with intransigent anti-Chalcedonian hardliners, first and foremost bishop Philoxenus of Mabbug (d. 523), who accused him of being a Nestorian and demanded his deposition. The main reason for this was that Flavian had broken communion with the new patriarch of Alexandria, John III (sed. 505–516), who had condemned Chalcedon. In the following years, both Philoxenus and his ally Severus lobbied against Flavian and gradually convinced emperor Anastasius (r. 491–518) of their Christological stance. Contrary to what John of Ephesus claims here, Flavian was far from being a hardcore dyophysite. Facing Philoxenus’s agitation, he condemned several dyophysite theologians of the 5th century and even expressed his opposition to the Chalcedonian two-nature-formula. Yet he refused to explicitly condemn Chalcedon itself. As a result of this, there was severe fighting between monastic groups in Antioch; Flavian was eventually declared deposed in 512 by a synod and subsequently sent into exile to Petra. He died in 518 Allen 2011, 25 Meier 2009, 251–259 and 267 Allen 1981, 147–151 Frend 1972, 214–220 Stein 1949, 171–173. The most important sources for his patriarchate are Theodore Lector, EH frag. 497–498 (Hansen 1995b, 141–142); Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 7.10 Greatrex 2011, 266–268 Brooks 1921, 50.1–51.8; Evagrius, EH 3.31–32 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 127–131).

As in the case of John IV of Alexandria (see 3.1.40.a), John of Ephesus does not seem to have known exactly how long Flavian had been patriarch, hence his vague formulation ܟܕ ܗ̣ܘܐ̣ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ, “when he had been [there some] years.” The same observation applies to the subsequent note regarding the length of Severus’s exile in Egypt. Possibly, John wrote these chapters from memory.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.b (SR) 50.6–9
div.p: ܘܒܬܪ ܗܢܐ ܗ̣ܘܐ̣ ܣܐܘܪܐ ܐܪܬܕܘܟܣܐ. ܘܠܒ̣ܟ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ̣ ܫܬ. ܘܒܪܝܫܗ̇ ܕܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܗ ܕܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ ܣܒܐ ܗ̇ܘ ܩܕܡܝܐ܆ ܢܦ̣ܩ ܡܢ ܟܘܪܣܝܗ. ܘܒܬܪ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܕܗ̣ܘܐ ܒܡ̣ܕܒܪܐ ܕܐܓ̣ܘܦܛܘܣ̣ ܡܝ̣ܬ ܬܡܢ “after him was the orthodox Severus, and he held the see for six years. But at the beginning of the reign of Justin I, the elder, he was expelled from his see. After some years being in the desert of Egypt, he died there”: Severus (sed. 512–518) was one of the most influential anti-Chalcedonian theologians Zachhuber 2020, 119–144 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 20–185 still fundamental is Lebon 1909. A large amount of his writings have been preserved, almost exclusively in Syriac. John of Ephesus’s notion of orthodoxy in EH 3 is closely connected to Severus; John himself and other anti-Chalcedonians adhering to Severus’s views on Christology are accordingly called “Severans” throughout this commentary.

Severus hailed from Sozopolis in Pisidia; he was born into a pagan family in the mid-450s. It was only after studying law in Alexandria and Berytus that he became (an anti-Chalcedonian) Christian in the late 480s and entered into a monastery. During the following decades, his influence grew steadily; from 508 to 511, he resided in Constantinople and exerted a profound influence on emperor Anastasius. Together with Philoxenus of Mabbug, he contributed decisively to turning the emperor against patriarch Flavian II of Antioch (see 3.1.41.a). In November 512, Severus was appointed patriarch of Antioch as successor to Flavian, irrespective of the ongoing support for Chalcedon of considerable parts of Syria. During his tenure, he ordained numerous anti-Chalcedonian bishops, yet his position quickly became untenable after the accession of the clearly pro-Chalcedonian Justin I in July 518. Threats were issued against Severus, and the patriarch abandoned Antioch and fled to Egypt in September. He remained there in relative security because it was only after 536 that a Chalcedonian episcopal hierarchy was imposed in Egypt (see 3.1.5.a). During his exile, he developed into a champion of the anti-Chalcedonian cause; for his supporters, he was still the legitimate patriarch of Antioch. Justinian invited him to Constantinople for religious discussions, and he indeed traveled there in 535 and entered into communion with patriarch Anthimus of Constantinople (see 3.1.42.c). Yet after the latter’s deposition, he returned to Egypt and was anathematized by a Constantinopolitan synod in 536. Justinian repeated this sentence in Novel 42 (Miller and Sarris 2018, 379–385); Severus died soon afterwards on 8 February 538 Allen 2011, 26–27 Meier 2009, 256 and 289–292 Menze 2008b, 44 Allen 1981, 151–154 and 176–177 Frend 1972, 201–235 and 269–273.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.c (SR) 50.9–11
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܫ̇ܢܬܐ ܚܕܐ ܗܘ̣ܬ ܐܢܛܝܘܟܝܐ ܕܠܐ ܐܦܝܣܩܦܐ܆ ܒܚܪܬܐ̣ ܢܚ̣ܬ ܦܘܠܐ ܝܗܘܕܝܐ “when Antioch had been without a bishop for one year, Paul the Jew eventually went down”: After the departure of Severus from Antioch in September 518, it lasted more than half a year before the appointment of a new patriarch. At the same time, Justin I and Justinian were busy resolving the Acacian schism that had divided the churches of Rome and Constantinople for thirty-five years (see 3.1.24.n). The schism came to an end in March 519, and it is possible that Justin wanted to involve the legates of the Roman bishop Hormisdas (sed. 514–523) in the selection process and “accept a candidate of whom they approved in order to secure a good relationship” with Rome (Menze 2008b, 48). Paul, who was eventually chosen in June 519, was a staunchly pro-Chalcedonian priest and administrator of the hospice of Eubulus in Constantinople (see 3.1.41.d). Moreover, he had previously spent two years in Antioch and had thus first-hand experience of the eastern provinces. His role as administrator meant that he had some financial skills; given the patriarchate of Antioch’s financial problems, this probably rendered him a good candidate (Menze 2008b, 47–49).

Possibly, Paul only arrived in Antioch towards the end of summer 519 for his ordination, hence John’s remark here that the episcopal see had been vacant for a whole year. Paul was a clear supporter of Hormisdas’s libellus, a document the Roman bishop had written in 515 and in which he demanded the condemnation as heretical of every bishop who had supported the Henotikon and not explicitly upheld the orthodoxy of Chalcedon Menze 2008b, 68–73 Grillmeier 1991, 364–367. This entailed that several moderate Chalcedonian leaders of the past decades, for instance patriarch Flavian II, were to be condemned. Paul’s support for this uncompromising policy quickly embittered many (moderate) Chalcedonians in Antioch; moreover, he did not refrain from using violence when deposing anti-Chalcedonian bishops (see 3.1.41.f). Because of his persecutory zeal, he was labeled “the Jew” (ܝܗܘܕܝܐ), a cognomen also found in the Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 20 Chabot 1933a, 19.1 and in Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 8.1 Greatrex 2011, 281 Brooks 1921, 62.8 on Paul, see Allen 2011, 27–28 Menze 2008b, 49–57 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 306.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.d (SR) 50.11–12
div.p: ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܦ̣ܪܡܘܢܪܐ ܕܛ̇ܘܒܢܝܬܐ ܐ̣ܘܦܡܝܐ ܒܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ “he had been the paramonarios in the church of the blessed Euphemia in Chalcedon”: This is the sole passage to state that Paul had been paramonarios in Chalcedon before he was appointed patriarch. Paramonarioi were presbyters or deacons who were, as church wardens, responsible for the safekeeping of the liturgical vestments and instruments of a church, especially at night (Ritter 2020, 179–185). In other sources, Paul is introduced as having been in charge (xenodocharios) of the hospice of Eubulus in Constantinople (Malalas, Chronicle 17.6 (Thurn 2000, 338.35–36); Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 20–21 Chabot 1933a, 19.7; Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6011 (de Boor 1883, 165.18)); the Collectio Avellana 216–217 simply calls him presbyter (Guenther 1898, 675.12–13 and 677.23–24). Given the testimony of the other sources, it is possible that John of Ephesus made a mistake here. Yet the two possibilities do not necessarily need to contradict each other; it is conceivable that Paul served first as paramonarios and then as xenodocharios. If he indeed was paramonarios in the church of the Saint Euphemia in Chalcedon (the very church where the council took place in 451; see Janin (1975, 31–33)), he may not have been the first one to raise to the episcopal see of Antioch from that position: according to an (uncertain) tradition, he could have been preceded by Peter the Fuller (d. 488), three times patriarch of Antioch in the late 5th century (Malalas, Chronicle 15.1 Thurn 2000, 301.7 see Kosiński 2010, 56).

div.head: EH 3.1.41.e (SR; NK) 50.12–13
div.p: ܗܢܐ ܐܚ̣ܬ ܕܘܦ̣ܛܝܟܘܢ ܕܟܬ̣ܝܒܐ ܒܗ ܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܠܡܕܢܚܐ “he took down the diptych on which the synod for the East was written”: Paul had been sent to Antioch with orders to reintroduce the proclamation of the council of Chalcedon (Evagrius, EH 4.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 155.17–18)). This entailed that Chalcedon was again included in the diptychs (the lists of bishops and ecumenical councils acknowledged as orthodox by a community which were read out during the liturgy; see also 3.2.11.k). Consequently, “the synod for the East” (ܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܠܡܕܢܚܐ) had to be removed; this refers to a gathering of anti-Chalcedonian bishops that took place early in Severus’s episcopate, probably in 513. At this so-called “Oriental synod,” Severus, Philoxenus of Mabbug, and their episcopal supporters condemned Chalcedon, an action which elicited stark criticism, also from emperor Anastasius Grillmeier 1991, 319–322 Honigmann 1951, 15–16. Yet this passage attests that a strictly anti-Chalcedonian Christology prevailed in Antioch until the arrival of Paul.

Two small clarifications regarding the interpretation and text of this passage should be noted. First, Payne Smith (1860, 79) argued that “the synod for the East” refers to the council of Ephesus of 431, yet this is certainly wrong. The imperial church always acknowledged Ephesus as one of the ecumenical councils. Second, the Greek loanword δίπτυχον is rendered in the manuscript as ܕܘܦ̣ܛܝܟܘܢ. Brooks (1935, 50.12) proposed to change this to ܕܦ̣ܛܝܟܘܢ, as he also did in EH 3.2.11.k. However, this alteration is unnecessary, as the manuscript spelling is well attested (J. Payne Smith 1903, 91) and can be preserved.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.f (SR) 50.13–15
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܐܝܟ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܠܒ̣ܟ܆ ܐ̣ܬܝ̣ܕܥ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܢܣܛܘܪܝܢܐ܆ ܘܐܫ̣ܬܕܝ ܘܐܦ ܗ̣ܘ̣ ܘܢܦ̣ܩ “after he held the position for about two years, it became known that he was a Nestorian. He was banished, and he was also expelled”: Paul’s intransigent stance alienated many moderate Chalcedonians who had accepted the Henotikon during the reign of Anastasius (3.1.41.c). Therefore, support for him dwindled, and it did not help his case when rumors spread that one of the new bishops he had ordained commemorated Nestorius in the liturgy. As a result of this, Justin I ordered to carry out an investigation, and Paul also lost imperial support (Menze 2008b, 51–52). Unsurprisingly, he himself was also labeled as Nestorian, an accusation also found in Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 8.1 Greatrex 2011, 281 Brooks 1921, 62.9 and John of Nikiu, Chronicle 90 (Zotenberg 1883, 382).

Moreover, his heavy-handed approach towards anti-Chalcedonian bishops who refused to leave their sees caused widespread resistance (Menze 2008b, 49–55). In one instance, soldiers dragged bishop Paul of Edessa out of the baptistery by force; this resulted in riots and slaughter in Edessa (Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 25–26 Chabot 1933a, 24.12–25.20 see Menze 2008b, 50). There are detailed descriptions of the persecution and of the suppression of anti-Chalcedonian monasteries which he triggered in LES and the Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 22–32 Chabot 1933a, 21.1–32.16; the latter account ultimately goes back to a lost work by John of Ephesus about the anti-Chalcedonian hardship in the east in the 520s and 530s. John then included (parts of) this work in EH 2, whence it found its way into the Zuqnin Chronicle (van Ginkel 1995, 87–88).

Paul’s position eventually became untenable and he resigned. The exact date is unknown, but he must have done so shortly before May 521 when Justin I informed the Roman bishop Hormisdas about Paul’s resignation (Collectio Avellana 241 (Guenther 1898, 740–741)). Contrary to what John asserts here, Paul had thus not been deposed or exiled; his end is unknown. He had not been in office long enough – in fact less than two years – to depose all the anti-Chalcedonian bishops of his patriarchate. He appears to have concentrated his efforts on the Syrian provinces, whereas the regions to the east of the Euphrates were largely spared Wood 2010, 166 Menze 2008b, 49–53 van Ginkel 1995, 28 n.10.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.g (SR) 50.15–17
div.p: ܘܒܬܪ ܗܢܐ܆ ܐ̣ܘܦܪܘܣ ܒܪ ܫܡ̇ܪ̈ܝܐ. ܘܒܫܢܬܐ ܫܒ̣ܝܥܝܬܐ ܕܝܠܗ̣ ܐܬܗܦ̣ܟܬ݀ ܐܢܛܝܘܟܝ ܒܙܘ̇ܥܐ̣ ܘܡܝ̣ܬ ܒܗ̇ “after him was Euphrasius, a Samaritan. In his seventh year, Antioch was overthrown by an earthquake, and he died in it”: Only very little is known about the background of Euphrasius (sed. 521–526). Malalas, Chronicle 17.11 (Thurn 2000, 342.25–26) and Evagrius, EH 4.4 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 155.21–22) state that he hailed from Jerusalem. The claim of John of Ephesus that he was a Samaritan also points towards Palestine, yet this supposed background evidently is just a way of denigrating a persecutor of the anti-Chalcedonians – just as in the case of the quaestor Anastasius, whom John calls a Samaritan, presumably because of his Palestinian origins (3.1.19.j).

Euphrasius’s accession to the patriarchate of Antioch after the contentious tenure of Paul coincided with a detente in the relations with the moderate Chalcedonians who had been opposed to Paul’s intransigent stance. Formally, Hormisdas’s libellus (3.1.41.c) remained the cornerstone of orthodoxy, yet some of its more problematic demands (mainly the condemnation of those Chalcedonian patriarchs who had, like Flavian II, accepted the Henotikon) were probably dropped silently (Menze 2008b, 86–89). However, this relaxation did not extend to the anti-Chalcedonians: Euphrasius is remembered as a persecutor and during his time in office, pro-Chalcedonian bishops were imposed throughout the patriarchate of Antioch, also in the regions to the east of the Euphrates that Paul had still spared (Malalas, Chronicle 17.11 (Thurn 2000, 342.25–26); John of Nikiu, Chronicle 90 (Zotenberg 1883, 383); Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 23 and 26 Chabot 1933a, 22.18–20 and 26.3–7 see Menze 2008b, 50 and 53 van Ginkel 1995, 28 n.10. Also the monastic community which the young John of Ephesus joined at that time was targeted and had to leave Amida (LES 4 Brooks 1923, 84.8 cf. Destephen 2008, 496–497).

Euphrasius died in the earthquake which destroyed Antioch on 29 May 526 (Allen 1981, 177–178). He was thus not in his seventh year as patriarch, contrary to what John asserts here: he was appointed in May 521 at the earliest (3.1.41.f) and therefore cannot have been in office for more than five years. Evagrius, EH 4.5 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 156.9–10) registers fairly neutrally that he died during the collapse of the city; Malalas, Chronicle 17.22 (Thurn 2000, 352.67–68) and John of Nikiu, Chronicle 90 (Zotenberg 1883, 385) state that he was burnt during the earthquake. However, anti-Chalcedonian authors starting with John of Ephesus himself present his death as the deserved punishment for a persecutor Allen 2011, 28 Debié 2004b, 167–168. According to them, he fell in a burning cauldron of pitch or wax and suffered horrible pains (Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 46–47 Chabot 1933a, 50.14–51.21; Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.16 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:182–183; 4:273.b.5–13; Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 8.1 and 8.4 Greatrex 2011, 282 and 296 Brooks 1921, 62.9–11 and 76.16–19).

div.head: EH 3.1.41.h (SR) 50.17–19
div.p: ܒܬܪ ܕܝܢ ܗܢܐ ܬܘܒ܆ ܗ̣ܘܐ ܐܦܪܝܡ ܐܡ̇ܕܝܐ ܒܪ ܐ̇ܦܝܢܐ ܪܕܘ̇ܦܐ ܒܝ̣ܫܐ ܝܬ̇ܝܪ ܡܢ ܦܘܠܐ ܘܐ̣ܘܦܪܘܣ. ܘܚ̣ܝܐ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ̣ ܘܡܝ̣ܬ “then after him was Ephrem of Amida, the son of Appianus, a persecutor more wicked than Paul and Euphrasius. He lived for some years, and he died”: Ephrem (sed. 527–545) was, prior to becoming patriarch, as comes Orientis one of the highest-ranking Roman civil functionaries in the east, responsible for all the provinces of the Dioecesis Orientis in Syria, Mesopotamia, and Palestine. His origin from Amida is confirmed in Malalas, Chronicle 17.12 (Thurn 2000, 343.42–43); Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 8.4 Greatrex 2011, 296 Brooks 1921, 76.19; Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 37 Chabot 1933a, 38.24; John of Nikiu, Chronicle 90 (Zotenberg 1883, 385). According to Photius, Bibliotheke 228 (Henry 1959–1991, 4:114.21–23), Ephrem’s mother tongue was Syriac and he learned Greek only at a later point. His father Appianus is otherwise unknown; he is also named in the Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 37 Chabot 1933a, 38.24.

In his function as comes Orientis, Ephrem proved a skilful administrator and he immediately organized help for Antioch and supervised the cleanup after the earthquake that had struck the city on 29 May 526 (Malalas, Chronicle 17.22 (Thurn 2000, 352.65–69); Evagrius, EH 4.6 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 156); see the brief overviews in Allen (2011, 29–30 1981, 179–180)). Even the anti-Chalcedonian Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 8.4 Greatrex 2011, 296 Brooks 1921, 76.20–22, offers some praise. The grateful population of Antioch soon demanded that Ephrem become patriarch; Justin I and Justinian agreed to this soon after the latter had become Augustus on 1 April 527, irrespective of Ephrem’s status as a layman (see 3.1.32.b). Yet Ephrem turned out to be an able theologian (some of his treatises are summarized in Photius, Bibliotheke 228–229 Henry 1959–1991, 4:114–174; for a discussion of his theology, see Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 357–373).

He was a convinced Chalcedonian, and the fact that he hailed from one of the anti-Chalcedonian strongholds east of the Euphrates evidently rendered him an ideal candidate for the patriarchate. His first years on the episcopal throne were relatively peaceful since Justinian put an end to the suppression of the anti-Chalcedonians in the east. However, this changed in the winter of 536/537, after the failure of the religious talks with Severus and the condemnation of him and other anti-Chalcedonian leaders at a synod in Constantinople (see 3.1.5.a). On imperial orders, Ephrem embarked on a policy of persecution; he himself traveled from Antioch to the eastern provinces in Mesopotamia (the so-called “descent to the east”), and numerous monastic communities, including that of John of Ephesus, were again expelled (Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 37–41 Chabot 1933a, 38.22–44.8; Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 10.1 Greatrex 2011, 398–401 Brooks 1921, 174.14–176.29; LES 35 Brooks 1924, 620–622 see Menze 2008b, 110–111 and 118–120 Destephen 2008, 498 van Ginkel 1995, 29). It is because of this experience that John describes Ephrem here as even worse than his predecessors. The same or similar statements, probably all deriving from EH 2, are found in Zuqnin Chronicle Witakowski 1996, 48 Chabot 1933a, 53.5–6 and Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.16 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:181; 4:272.a.2–15. In LES 21 (Brooks 1923, 293.12), John even identifies Ephrem as the likeness of the Antichrist.

Ephrem died in 545 (for the dating of his patriarchate, see Stein 1949, 638 n.1). Just as in 3.1.40.a and 3.1.41.a, John does not know for how many years he had been patriarch and does not seem to have had access to that information at the time of writing.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.i (SR) 50.19–20
div.p: ܘܒܬܪ ܗܢܐ ܗ̣ܘܐ ܕܘܡ̣ܢܝܢܐ ܪܗܘܡܝܐ̣ “after him was Domninus, a Roman […]”: The patriarchate of Domninus III (sed. 545–559) is badly attested in the sources. In both Evagrius’s EH and the Zuqnin Chronicle, he is only encountered in the context of episcopal lists; no further information is provided. In particular, the silence of the Antiochene Evagrius seems deliberate since he mentioned both Domninus’s predecessor Ephrem and his successor Anastasius in a laudatory manner (3.1.41.h and 3.1.41.j). Domninus hailed from the far west of the empire from an Antiochene perspective, from Lychnidos, nowadays Ohrid in Northern Macedonia (Allen 2011, 30). The Life of Symeon the Younger 72 (van den Ven 1962, 62–63) is the sole source containing detailed information about Domninus: he was director of a hospital (πτωχεῖον) in Lychnidos; his accession to the patriarchal throne of Antioch is presented as the result of a coincidence. Apparently, it happened that Domninus was on business in Constantinople when the vacancy in Antioch occurred. If we can believe the Life of Symeon, he was introduced to Justinian, who then immediately decided that he should become Ephrem’s successor in Antioch. It is clear that there must have been more to his surprising appointment, yet it is no longer possible to reach any certainty on this matter.

John of Ephesus’s statement here that Domninus was a “Roman” (ܪܗܘܡܝܐ̣) might point towards a military background before he became a cleric and served as the head of the hospital. This would be consistent with how John usually employs the adjective ܪܗܘܡܝܐ̣ (3.1.9.m). Yet it could also refer to his place of origin, which may have lain in the Latin-speaking parts of the Balkan peninsula (Parker 2022, 123 n.59). The word which immediately follows on ܪܗܘܡܝܐ̣ is illegible in the manuscript; its context remains thus unclear.

According to the negative depiction in the Life of Symeon the Younger 72 and 204 (van den Ven 1962, 62–63 and 177–178), Domninus was unpopular from the very beginning of his patriarchate and the population rejoiced when he died. He held the urban poor in contempt and was therefore punished by God with bodily afflictions. Parker (2022, 123–124) has shown that this story sits well with the Life’s general hostility to wealth; possibly, the patriarch enjoyed a luxurious lifestyle which evoked criticism. A short note in Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.32 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:267; 4:322.a.42–323.a.4 supports this view: apparently, Domninus was solely interested in food.

Domninus’s theological activities remain unknown. He participated as one of the chairmen in the ecumenical council of Constantinople in 553 (Life of Eutychius 811 (Laga 1992, 28)), yet the conciliar acts do not record any particular initiative of his. Stein (1949, 689 n.1) established 559 as the year of his death.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.j (SR) 50.20–23
div.p: ܘܒܬܪ ܗܢܐ ܗܘ̣ܐ ܐܢܣܛܣ. ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܐܦܩܪܝܣܪܐ ܕܐܠܟ̣ܣܢܕܪܝܐ. ܘܥܠ ܗܢܐ ܪܟ̇ܒ ܥܠܘܗܝ ܥ̈ܠܠ̣ܬܐ ܝܘܣܛܝܢܐ ܗ̇ܘ ܐܚܪܝܐ܆ ܘܐܦܩ̣ܗ “after him was Anastasius who had been the apokrisiarios of Alexandria. Justin II formulated various accusations against him and expelled him”: Anastasius (sed. 559–570 and 593–598) appears to have been the most accomplished theologian among all the sixth-century patriarchs of Antioch. A number of his theological treatises and homilies have been preserved; they show him as a committed Chalcedonian who defended the contentious Tome of Leo (see 3.1.18.g). Moreover, he was one of few Chalcedonians to critically engage with “tritheist” doctrine (3.1.31.b), and his reflections about the energiai in Jesus Christ proved influential for the turn towards monenergism in the early 7th century for an overview of his theology, see Krausmüller 2023; Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 374–402 Weiss 1965; cf. Allen 2011, 31–32; 1980, 13–15.

According to the Life of Symeon the Younger 204.14–17 (van den Ven 1962, 178), Anastasius hailed from Palestine (cf. Weiss 1965, 6–10). The Life and the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 111.6–7) confirm John of Ephesus’s statement that Anastasius served as the apokrisiarios (representative) of the church of Alexandria in Antioch. He was thus already known in Antioch prior to his accession as patriarch in 559, and the Life of Symeon the Younger records that the population rejoiced when it became known that he was to succeed to the unpopular Domninus (see 3.1.41.i).

We know of two occasions when Anastasius clashed with an emperor during his patriarchate. First, in 565, he rejected Justinian’s edict on aphthartism and assembled a large synod which produced a theological statement against that doctrine (Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 9.34 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:272–281; 4:325–330; see Roggo 2022, 29–80; 2019 with further literature) His resistance to the imperial “lapse into heresy” and his qualities as a defender of orthodoxy are also praised in the Life of Eutychius 1175–1182 (Laga 1992, 39) and in Evagrius, EH 4.40 Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 190–191 cf. Michael Whitby 1998, 331–332. Evagrius claims that Anastasius would have been deposed, had it not been for Justinian’s death and the ensuing end of imperial aphthartism.

Eventually, Anastasius was indeed deposed, in 570. It is evident, also from the passage in question here, that he came into conflict with Justin II, yet the exact reasons for his downfall remain obscure. The account of Evagrius, EH 5.5 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 201) is not wholly convincing; he gives Anastasius’s unsuitable expenditure of holy monies and his insulting remarks directed against Justin as the official reasons for the deposition. There is a broad scholarly consensus that these are just pretexts Roggo 2024b, 235 n.55; Michael Whitby 2000, 261 n.17 Allen 1981, 214–217 Weiss 1965, 23–32. An entry in the Chronicle of Theophanes, AM 6062 (de Boor 1883, 243.24–28), suggests that Anastasius may have taken issue with the ordination of the Alexandrian patriarch John IV by John Scholasticus in Constantinople (see 3.1.40.a). It is, after all, unlikely that Anastasius as a former apokrisiarios of the church of Alexandria would have condoned such a Constantinopolitan intrusion into another patriarchate. This may have proved decisive for his downfall; importantly, John Scholasticus was a close ally of Justin, and Anastasius’s complaints possibly prompted the emperor to find pretexts to depose him.

Weiss (1965, 33–34) showed that Anastasius spent the period of his exile in Constantinople. There, he made the acquaintance of the Roman apokrisiarios Gregory, the future bishop (sed. 590–604), who still addressed him as patriarch despite his deposition Roggo 2024b, 235–236; 2022, 116–117. Gregory appealed to the emperor Maurice on behalf of Anastasius in the early 590s; Anastasius was finally allowed to return as patriarch to Antioch in 593, after twenty-three years of exile (Evagrius, EH 6.24 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 240.17–19)). During his second patriarchate, he engaged in conversations with anti-Chalcedonians; he died in 598.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.k (SR) 50.23–24
div.p: ܘܐܫܬ̇ܕܪ ܚܠܦܘܗܝ ܐܢܫ ܪܝܫ ܕܝܪܐ ܕܛܘܪ ܣܝܢܝ ܕܫܡܗ ܓܪܝܓܘܪ “there was sent as his successor a certain abbot of Mount Sinai named Gregory”: Of all the sixth-century patriarchs of Antioch, Gregory (sed. 570–592) is the one who is best attested in the sources. He features prominently in Evagrius’s EH; this is due to the fact that Evagrius belonged, as a scholastikos, to the legal team of the patriarchate of Antioch in the time of Gregory Michael Whitby 2000, xiv–xv Allen 1981, 1–4. We refrain deliberately from giving an exhaustive account of all of Gregory’s activities that have been transmitted in the sources and instead focus on those instances which are important in the context of John’s EH. For a broad overview of Gregory as patriarch, see Roggo 2024b, 228–237; Allen 2011, 33 Lee 2007; Michael Whitby 1998, 329–331 Allen 1981, 217–218; 1980, 15–16.

Relatively little is known about Gregory’s career before he ascended to the episcopal throne of Antioch in 570. John of Ephesus reports here that he was abbot of the monastery of Saint Catherine on Mount Sinai; Evagrius, EH 5.6 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 202.3–5) states that Justin II appointed him as abbot there and that he defended the monastery against Arab nomads. Evagrius adds (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 202.2–3) that prior to that, Gregory had entered monastic life in the monastery of the Byzantines (located in Jerusalem) and that he had also been abbot there. Gregory’s connection to the monastery of the Byzantines is confirmed in Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6062 (de Boor 1883, 243.28–29). The Pratum Spirituale 139 (PG 87/3:3002–3004 (Migne 1857–1866)) of John Moschus places Gregory at the head of even a third monastery, that of Pharan north of Jerusalem. The episode is dated to six years before he became patriarch, thus to 564. If all this information is correct, then Gregory had already had an impressive monastic career, including interactions with the emperor, before his accession as patriarch (cf. Allen 1981, 217–218). His appointments by Justin first to a monastery in an unquiet region such as the Sinai and then to the patriarchate of Antioch which was torn by inter- and inner-confessional strife are reminiscent of the abbot Photius who received ill-defined, but wide-ranging powers in Palestine and Egypt (see 3.1.32.d).

Evagrius offers a long eulogy on Gregory’s generosity, forgivingness, and popularity as patriarch in EH 5.6 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 201–203); he depicts him, in short, as the perfect ecclesiastical leader. Similar praise is found in the Pratum Spirituale 140 (PG 87/3:3004 Migne 1857–1866; see Lee 2007, 99–100 Michael Whitby 1998, 329–331 Allen 1981, 218). Michael the Syrian, Chronicle 10.3 Chabot 1899–1910, 2:292; 4:336.a.18–26 adds that Gregory was interested in a good relationship with the anti-Chalcedonians and that he did not persecute. However, this alleged tolerance does not sit well with EH 3.4.42, according to which Gregory received imperial orders to stop the suppression of the anti-Chalcedonians in 580. Moreover, Gregory also nearly managed to catch Damian of Alexandria in the winter of 579/580 when the latter had come to Antioch to ordain a Severan counter-patriarch to Paul (see 3.1.40.f and 3.4.41).

There is, in fact, much to suggest that Gregory was less popular than Evagrius claims, even among the Chalcedonians. He was involved in a number of scandals and there have been plausible arguments that the population of Antioch was probably divided during his patriarchate; his deposed predecessor Anastasius may still have had supporters who did not accept Gregory Roggo 2024b, 235–236; Parker 2022, 122 Lee 2007, 106 Liebeschuetz 2001, 268 Michael Whitby 1998, 331. During the “pagan affair” of 580, people rioted in Antioch against the patriarch as soon as the rumor had spread that he had participated in human sacrifice (see John of Ephesus, EH 3.3.29 and Evagrius, EH 5.18 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 213.7–13)). It is indicative of the discontent with the patriarch that there was apparently a substantial number of people who were prepared to believe such an accusation Roggo 2024b, 228–237; 2022, 161–164 Parker 2022, 45–47 Lee 2007, 103–104. It seems that there was always some degree of opposition to Gregory in Antioch; this came again to the fore in 588 when he clashed for unknown reasons with the comes Orientis Asterius and was eventually accused of sexual misconduct (Evagrius, EH 6.7 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 225–226)) and involvement in pagan rites (John of Ephesus, EH 3.5.17). Gregory was forced to travel to Constantinople and was finally acquitted by a synod of representatives of the other patriarchal sees Roggo 2024b, 234–235; Lee 2007, 104–106.

Despite these scandals, Gregory also proved an important pillar of the Roman government in the eastern provinces (Lee 2007, 99–100). For instance, he acted as mediator when the Roman army mutinied in the late 580s, and he was involved to bring the Persian king Khosrow back on his throne in 590/591. He also enjoyed good connections with the Arab tribes and played an important role in the arrest of the client king al-Mundhir in the early 580s (see EH 3.4.40). After the peace with Persia in 591, he was able to travel through the border regions of the empire and to convert many to the faith of Chalcedon (Evagrius, EH 6.22 Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 238.22–28 see Greatrex 2007, 292 Allen 1981, 261–262). His death in 592 was caused by a malworking drug for gout according to Evagrius, EH 6.24 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 240.11–14). His predecessor Anastasius was then allowed to return to Antioch as patriarch (see 3.1.41.j).

div.head: EH 3.1.41.l (SR) 50.24–29
div.p: ܘܬܘܒ ܗܠܝܢ ܕܠܘܩܒܠ ܣܘܢܗܕܣ ܕܟܠܩܕܘܢܐ ܠܘܩܕܡ ܒܬܪ ܙܒܢܐ ܣܓܝܐܐ܆ ܥܒ̣ܕܘܢ ܒܕܘܟܬ ܣܐܘܪܐ܆ ܠܣܪܓܝܣ. ܐܢܫ ܕܒܓܢܣ̣ܗ ܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ̣ ܬ̇ܠܝ̣ܐ. ܘܟܕ ܡ̇ܠ̣ܝ ܐܝܟ ܬܠܬ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ܆ ܡܝ̣ܬ̣ ܒܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ̣ ܐ̇ܝܟܐ ܕܐܦ ܗ̣ܘܐ “now, those opposed to the synod of Chalcedon, after a long period of time, first ordained in Severus’s place Sergius, a person from Tella in regard to his family. When he had completed about three years, he died in the imperial city where he had also been”: After the death of Severus in 538, it lasted about twenty years until the anti-Chalcedonians in his tradition created a new (titular) patriarch of Antioch in c. 557/558. This was an important step in the gradual development of a parallel Severan episcopal hierarchy. In the mid-sixth century, many Severans, including John of Ephesus himself, still perceived pro- and anti-Chalcedonians as part of the same church and hoped that the ascendancy Chalcedon had enjoyed since the death of emperor Anastasius in 518 might again be reverted (see 3.1.3.j and 3.1.37.c). Consequently, there was no need for the establishment of parallel ecclesiastical structures; a small number of anti-Chalcedonian bishops (for instance Theodosius of Alexandria and in particular Jacob Burdʿoyo) sufficed for the ordination of priests and thus for the survival of the Severan communion. This mindset gradually changed in the second half of the 6th century when the alienation progressed and a new generation of ecclesiastical leaders increasingly came to see themselves as independent from the Chalcedonian imperial church. The start of the new Severan hierarchy in the 550s and 560s was very slow, with Jacob ordaining probably only about fifteen bishops until the late 560s. John of Ephesus was one of them, and Sergius another. The titles of these new bishops did not correspond to their places of residence; as far as we know, John never lived in Ephesus, nor did Sergius in Antioch.

We are almost completely dependent on John of Ephesus for any information about Sergius. He features repeatedly in LES 48–50 where his origin from Tella, located in the province of Osrhoene, is confirmed Brooks 1926, 156.10–157.2; 236.10–12; 241.9–12; 1924, 689.2–11; 691.1–3; 697.2. He had a monastic background; John describes him as pious and full of Scriptural knowledge. Originally, he lived in the same monastery as Jacob Burdʿoyo; he became one of his close collaborators and accompanied him to Constantinople in the early 540s. It seems that the capital became his main residence; presumably, he lived together with the many other anti-Chalcedonians who had assembled there since the late 530s (see 3.1.5.a and 3.1.10.b). Probably around 557/558, Jacob ordained him patriarch of Antioch, yet he never left Constantinople and died three years later in c. 561 see the overviews in Allen 2011, 30–31 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 199 Honigmann 1951, 192–195.

During his time in the capital, Sergius became well acquainted with the theologian and philosopher John Philoponus, who even dedicated some of his books to him. Sergius was influenced by Philoponus’s theological views (“tritheism”) and he appears to have become an adherent of that doctrine in the last years of his life. Because of his continuous stay in Constantinople after his ordination as patriarch, some later authors assumed that he had been a Severan counter-patriarch not of Antioch, but of Constantinople (Photius, Bibliotheke 240 (Henry 1959–1991, 5:166.4); Nicephorus Callistus, EH 18.47 (PG 147:424C (Migne 1857–1866))).

div.head: EH 3.1.41.m (SR) 50.29–51.2
div.p: ܒܬܪ ܕܝܢ ܙܒܢܐ ܬܘܒ܆ ܗ̣ܘܐ ܒܬܪܗ ܦܘܠܘܣ ܐܢܫ ܐܠ̣ܟܣܢܕܪܝܐ ܣܘ̣ܢܩܠܘܣ ܕܬܐܕܣܝܣ ܕܐܠܟ̣ܣܢܕܪܝܐ. ܗ̇ܘ ܕܒܦܘܩܕܢܗ ܘܒܝ̣ܕܥܬܗ̣ ܗ̣ܘܐ ܒܝܘܡ̈ܬܐ ܒܝ̣̈ܫܐ “then, after some time, there came in his place Paul, a certain Alexandrian, the synkellos of Theodosius of Alexandria, who at Theodosius’s order and with his knowledge became bishop in the wicked days”: Paul of Antioch, often called “the Black,” plays a major role in EH 3 (see 3.1.17.d). He hailed from Alexandria and rose to become an abbot of a monastery in Syria (Documenta Monophysitica 23 (Chabot 1907, 143.22–23)).
