At the same time, he was a close collaborator/advisor (synkellos) of Theodosius of Alexandria and often resided with him in Constantinople. It appears that Paul hoped that he might eventually succeed Theodosius as the Severan patriarch of Alexandria, and it is probably due to these ambitions that he was very reluctant to become patriarch of Antioch instead Blaudeau 2022, 63–70 Allen 2013, 190–196 Honigmann 1951, 195–205 Brooks 1930, 473–476. According to LES 48 (Brooks 1924, 689.11–12), his ordination took place in 564, about three years after the death of Sergius, and was the result of a ruse: Theodosius had not in fact informed Paul that he had written to Jacob Burdʿoyo (who was in Syria at that time) to make him patriarch (Documenta Monophysitica 9 (Chabot 1907, 89.23–29)). Paul traveled to Syria on a mission for Theodosius, as he thought, and was then taken by surprise and ordained (Documenta Monophysitica 10 (Chabot 1907, 91.27–92.16)). Afterwards, he heavily complained about the fact that he had been tricked in the synodal letter he sent to Theodosius (Documenta Monophysitica 13 (Chabot 1907, 98–114)).

The hidden and secret circumstances of his ordination were problematic for Paul’s position among the Severans in Syria and Mesopotamia from the beginning. Theodosius may have hoped that Paul’s legitimacy would be strengthened if Jacob, the Severan éminence grise in the eastern provinces, ordained him. Just as John of Ephesus and the other Severan bishops introduced in Book 1, Paul spent much of his time in Constantinople and he participated there in the religious discussions in the late 560s (see 3.1.17.d). Probably, John calls him “bishop in the wicked days” because of the start of the persecution in 571. As discussed above, Paul damaged his reputation irrevocably by first entering into communion with the Chalcedonians and then becoming involved in 575 in the ordination of Theodore, whom most Alexandrian Severans did not recognize (3.1.40.d–3.1.40.e). As a consequence, many no longer considered Paul as the legitimate patriarch of Antioch, yet John of Ephesus supported Paul in this conflict (as is evident from 3.4.11–3.4.12; 3.4.34; 3.4.45–3.4.46). For this reason, he highlighted here that the ordination of Paul had been correct and had happened at the order of the Severan leader Theodosius, who was held in honor by all Severans, including Paul’s opponents.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.n (SR) 51.3–6
div.p: ܗܢܐ ܕܒ̣ܣܕܩܐ ܕܗ̣ܘܐ ܒܝܬ ܠܗ ܠܛ̇ܘܒܢܐ ܝܥܩܘܒ܆ ܥܕܬܐ ܕܡܗ̈ܝܡܢܐ ܐܣܬ̣ܕܩܬ̣̇ ܘܠܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ ܦ̈ܠܓ̇ܘܢ ܗ̣ܘܬ. ܘܒܩܖ̈ܒܐ ܘܒܬ̈ܟܬ̇ܘܫܐ ܕܠܐ ܡܬ̈ܪܝܚܝܢ܆ ܬܪ̈ܝܗܘܢ ܓ̇ܒ̈ܐ̣ ܥܡ ܚ̈ܕܕܐ ܢܦܠ̣ܘ “a schism arose between him and the blessed Jacob, the church of the faithful was ruptured, and it was split into two divisions. With grueling conflicts and battles, the two sides fell upon each other”: Jacob Burdʿoyo first supported Paul in the conflict with the Alexandrian Severans and Peter IV that erupted in 575 (see 3.1.40.e). However, Jacob eventually changed sides and consented to the formal deposition of Paul. The Severans in Syria and Mesopotamia were therefore divided into two factions, the supporters of Jacob and those of Paul. John of Ephesus laments this schism in EH 3.4.19–3.4.31; he describes violent clashes between the two parties in the monasteries and how the Chalcedonians were delighted at this inner-Severan struggle. The division persisted after the death of Jacob in 578; there were various unsuccessful attempts at reconciliation, for instance by the Arab client king al-Mundhir (3.4.41; see also 3.1.40.e). In the end, Paul retreated to Constantinople, he hid and died there in the early 580s, unbeknownst to the other Severan bishops (3.4.57) – John of Ephesus clearly assumed that he was still alive at the time of writing of this chapter (see 3.1.41.p) Allen 2013, 190–196 Honigmann 1951, 195–205 Brooks 1930, 473–476.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.o (SR) 51.6–9
div.p: ܘܗܠܝܢ ܬܘܒ ܕܠܩ̣ܘܒܠܗ ܕܦܘܠܐ: ܒܬܪ ܡ̇ܘܬܗ ܕܝܥܩܘܒ܆ ܐܚܪܢܐ ܦܛܪܝܪܟܐ ܒܐܢܛܝܟܝܐ ܠܐܢܫ ܕܫܡܗ ܦܛܪܘܣ ܐܩܝ̣ܡܘ: ܕܠܐ ܢܡ̇ܘܣ ܕܡܢ ܩ̇ܠܝܢܝܩܘܣ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ “then, those opposed to Paul after the death of Jacob lawlessly appointed a certain person named Peter, who was from the city of Callinicum, as another patriarch in Antioch”: Peter hailed from the city of Callinicum on the river Euphrates (modern Raqqa in Syria). He had a monastic background and was close to Jacob Burdʿoyo. In EH 3.4.45, John of Ephesus narrates his accession to the Severan patriarchate of Antioch in more detail: Apparently, Jacob Burdʿoyo tried to ordain Peter as counter-patriarch to the disputed Paul of Antioch twice, yet he refused to become patriarch because Paul had not been condemned canonically at a synod. It was only some years after the death of Jacob in 578 that Peter finally gave in, even though Paul was still alive at that point. In 581, he traveled to Alexandria and was ordained there by patriarch Damian. As noted in 3.1.40.f, Damian’s triumph over his rival Paul by making Peter patriarch of Antioch reestablished Alexandrian dominance in the Severan movement that had been shaken since the death of Theodosius in 566. According to 3.4.47–3.4.48 and 3.4.54–3.4.57, Paul had come to realize by that time that opposition to him was too strong and that his position was untenable; consequently, he went into hiding and gave up his claims. Despite John of Ephesus’s laments about the creation of a counter-patriarch, Peter does thus not seem to have faced serious challenges to his authority as leader of the Syrian Severans. However, the new-found harmony between the Severan communities in Syria and Egypt did not last long: Peter and Damian clashed in the mid-580s over the doctrine of “tritheism” (on which see 3.1.31.b). Peter repeatedly tried to counter Damian’s claim that he professed tritheism, yet Damian refused to meet with him. At the time of Peter’s death in 591, the schism was still unresolved; it was only overcome in c. 616 Allen 2013, 196–197; 2011, 34 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 2002, 286–288 and 406–408 Blaudeau 1997, 355–357 Ebied, Roey, and Wickham 1981, 34–43.

div.head: EH 3.1.41.p (SR) 51.9–11
div.p: ܘܗܠܝܢ ܗܟܢܐ ܗ̣̈ܘܝ̣ ܩܠܝܠܐܝܬ ܥܕܡܐ ܠܗܫܐ ܕܗܠܝܢ ܡܬ̈ܟܬ̇ܒܢ܇ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܝܗ̇ ܫܢܬ ܬܡܢܡܐܐ ܘܬܫܥܝܢ ܘܬܪ̈ܬܝܢ “such were the things that gradually unfolded up to the present time in which they are being written which is the year 892”: In EH 3, John of Ephesus sometimes indicated the year when he was writing a certain passage; other examples are found in 3.2.15; 3.4.13; 3.4.19; 3.4.61; 3.6.25 (van Ginkel 1995, 198 n.86). He always used the Seleucid era, one of the most common systems of numbering years in Syria and Mesopotamia throughout late antiquity. The era takes as its beginning the traditional founding date of the Seleucid empire in 312 BCE. In the variant John used, a new year begins on 1 September (van Ginkel 1995, 198); consequently, the year 892 corresponds to September 580–August 581. It appears that John finished working on Book 1 at this point (van Ginkel 1995, 73); this is also evident from EH 3.1.42, where John does not know about the death of Eutychius and the accession of John Nesteutes in April 582.

div.head: Summary (SR)
div.p: After providing the names of the bishops of Alexandria and Antioch, Book 1 closes with the patriarchs of Constantinople from the start of the reign of Justinian to the early 580s. As noted in the summary to 3.1.40, episcopal lists such as these were regularly employed in late antique ecclesiastical historiography, yet less often in John of Ephesus’s EH, where they only occur in Book 1. In this instance, John briefly mentions Epiphanius (sed. 520–535), yet provides more information for Anthimus (sed. 535–536) and his successors. This suggests that he focused on those patriarchs with whom he was acquainted personally after his move to Constantinople.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.a (SR) 51.14
div.p: ܒܩܘܣܛܢܛܝܢܦܘܠܝܣ “in Constantinople”: This is the first and only occurrence of the name Constantinople in Book 1 (with the exception of the heading of this chapter, yet this is probably not from John of Ephesus (see 3.1.28.a)). Normally, John refers to Constantinople as “the imperial city” (ܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ), and he does so again in the remainder of this chapter. However, the use of the capital’s name makes sense at the start of a list of the Constantinopolitan bishops; moreover, John employs the name at a couple of instances in Books 2–6 (3.2.24; 3.2.34; 3.4.7; 3.4.41; 3.6.10; 3.6.11) and it is thus not completely extraordinary.

The occurrence of the name Constantinople, unusual for Book 1, together with the closing dating formula to 892 (corresponding to 580/581) in 3.1.41.p, might be read to suggest that Book 1 originally ended with 3.1.41 and that the last chapter was only added at a later point. Yet this is improbable; this chapter was very likely written at the same time as the previous ones: there is no awareness of the death of Eutychius in April 582 (see 3.1.42.i). The dating to 892 does therefore not seem to close Book 1, but rather John’s narration about the inner-Severan dispute ripping apart the patriarchate of Antioch.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.b (SR) 51.14–17
div.p: ܒܩܘܣܛܢܛܝܢܦܘܠܝܣ ܡܢ ܒܬܪ ܐ̣ܦܦܢܝܣ܆ ܐܬ̣ܕܒܪ ܠܐܦܝܣܩܦܘܬܐ ܐܢܬ̣ܝܡܘܣ. ܗܢܐ ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ ܡܢ ܩܕܝܡ܆ ܐܦܝܣܩܘܦܐ ܕܛܪܦܙܘܢ̣ܛܐ ܡܕܝܢܬܐ. ܘܐܬ̣ܩܪܝ ܠܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ “in Constantinople after Epiphanius, Anthimus was brought to the episcopate. He had previously been bishop of the city of Trapezus, and he was summoned to the imperial city”: According to Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6029 (de Boor 1883, 217.1–2), patriarch Epiphanius (sed. 520–535) died on 5 June 535. He had been a staunch supporter of the pro-Chalcedonian religious policy of Justin I and Justinian. His successor Anthimus (sed. 535–536) soon proved much more controversial for the Chalcedonian establishment of Constantinople. The fact that he already was bishop of Trapezus prior to his appointment to the see of the capital was, at least initially, not perceived as very problematic. Such a practice violated canon law, yet this stipulation was occasionally ignored in late antiquity Alberigo 2006, I:27–28 Hess 2003, 162–178. Justinian probably chose him because he was no Chalcedonian hardliner and familiar with the negotiations with the anti-Chalcedonians. He had participated in religious talks in 532, and the emperor might have hoped that Anthimus’s conciliatory stance might lead to a lasting rapprochement of the anti-Chalcedonians around Severus of Antioch (see 3.1.42.c).

div.head: EH 3.1.42.c (SR) 51.17–19
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܗ̣ܘܐ ܒܗ̇ ܙܒܢܐ ܕܫ̈ܢܝܐ܆ ܟܕ ܐܬܩ̣ܪܝ ܗܘܐ ܣܐܘܪܐ ܕܐܢܛܝܟܝܐ ܡܢ ܐܓܝ̣ܦܛܘܣ ܒܝܕ ܦܘܩܕܢܗ ܕܝܘܣܛܝܢܝܢܐ: ܕܢܡ̇ܠܠܘܢ ܡܛܠ ܚܕܝܘܬܐ “after he had been in the imperial city for a period of several years, when Severus of Antioch was summoned from Egypt by order of Justinian so that might they talk about the union”: John of Ephesus was apparently unaware that Anthimus was bishop of Constantinople for less than a year, from June 535 to March 536. It is clear that John wrote the episcopal lists at the end of Book 1 from memory since he indicated vaguely on more than one occasion that the length of some episcopates lasted “some years” (see 3.1.40.a). Yet at the same time, John shows himself well informed about the core events of Anthimus’s short patriarchate. Moreover, he has preserved the correct order of succession, unlike his contemporary Evagrius who wrongly assumed that Epiphanius (sed. 520–535) had been Anthimus’s successor (EH 4.11 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 161.18–19)).

Justinian hoped to undo the divide between the Chalcedonians and the opponents to the council around Severus of Antioch (see 3.1.5.a). Severus had not participated in the religious discussions that took place in Constantinople in 532, yet he eventually accepted Justinian’s invitation and traveled to Constantinople from his place of exile in Egypt in 535 (see 3.1.41.b). Anthimus met with the anti-Chalcedonian champion and entered into communion with him and with the new anti-Chalcedonian patriarch Theodosius of Alexandria (Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 9.21–26 Greatrex 2011, 374–396 Brooks 1921, 141–173; Evagrius, EH 4.11 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 160–162)). Yet contrary to Justinian’s aims, Severus never accepted the emperor’s moderate interpretation of Chalcedon and even persuaded Anthimus to turn away from Chalcedon and embrace his Christological stance. This triggered considerable resistance among pro-Chalcedonian circles in the capital and undermined Anthimus’s position Menze 2018, 853–854 Leppin 2011, 183–186 Price 2009, 12–13 Leppin 2006, 200–201 Uthemann 1999, 39–43 Frend 1972, 269–271.
On the contentious Tome of Leo, see 3.1.18.g.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.d (SR) 51.22–24
div.p: ܫܒ̣ܩܗ ܠܬܪܘܢܘܣ ܕܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ܆ ܘܢܦ̣ܩ ܘܐܬ̇ܚܝܕ ܥܡ ܣܐܘܪܣ ܘܬܐܕܣܝܣ ܕܐܠܟ̣ܣܢܕܪܝܐ “he left the throne of the imperial city. He was expelled and entered into a union with Severus and Theodosius of Alexandria”: The fate of Anthimus was sealed when the Roman bishop Agapetus (sed. 535–536) arrived in Constantinople in early 536. Officially, Agapetus acted as an ambassador sent by the Ostrogoths in order to prevent war in Italy, yet a crucial reason for the journey had been complaints about Anthimus’s turn against Chalcedon and his communion with Severus and Theodosius. The fact that Anthimus had previously been bishop of Trapezus rendered it easy to attack his legitimacy as patriarch without having to engage with the real bone of contention, the status of the council of Chalcedon. Accordingly, Agapetus did not accept Anthimus because of his uncanonical translation as bishop from Trapezus. Confronted both with Agapetus’s demands and the vociferous monastic resistance to Anthimus in Constantinople, Justinian withdrew his support, Anthimus abdicated and went into hiding (see Liberatus, Breviarium 21 (Schwartz 1962b, 135–136); Malalas, Chronicle 18.83 (Thurn 2000, 404); Pseudo-Zachariah, EH 9.19 Greatrex 2011, 369–371 Brooks 1921, 137.3–138.9; Evagrius, EH 4.11 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 160–162)).

Already on 13 March 536, Agapetus ordained Menas as the new patriarch of Constantinople. At a synod held in Constantinople in May/June 536, Anthimus, Severus, and other anti-Chalcedonian leaders were anathematized; Justinian confirmed this decree and expelled them from Constantinople in Novel 42 from 6 August 536 (Schöll and Kroll 1895, 263–269). However, Anthimus did not leave Constantinople; he joined the anti-Chalcedonian clerics and monastics who had assembled in the palace of Hormisdas (see 3.1.10.b), enjoyed the protection of empress Theodora, and still resided there after her death in 548 (LES 48 (Brooks 1924, 686–687)). It is thus likely that John of Ephesus met the ex-patriarch when he came to Constantinople in the early 540s (van Ginkel 1995, 30–31), and that he started the list of the bishops of Constantinople with Anthimus because he was the first patriarch he had known personally. The events around Anthimus and Agapetus are discussed in Hasse-Ungeheuer 2016, 203–210 Leppin 2011, 187–189 Price 2009, 13–14 Menze 2008b, 196–208 Leppin 2006, 201–206 Uthemann 1999, 46–47 Grillmeier and Hainthaler 1989, 365–371 Frend 1972, 272–273.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.e (SR) 51.24–27
div.p: ܒܬܪ ܕܝܢ ܗܢܐ ܝܬ̣ܒ ܥܠ ܬܪܘܢܘܣ ܕܡܕܝܢܬ ܡ̇ܠܟܘܬܐ܆ ܐܢܫ ܡܐ̣ܝܢܐ ܐܠ̣ܟܣܢܕܪܝܐ. ܕܐ̣ܝܬܘܗܝ ܗܘܐ̣ ܐܟ̣ܣܢܕܟܪܐ ܕܐܟ̣ܣܢܕܘܟܝܢ ܕܫܡܫܘܢ. ܘܟܕ ܗܢܐ ܡ̇ܠܝ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ܆ ܢܦ̣ܩ ܡܢ ܥܠܡܐ “then after this one, a certain Menas from Alexandria who had been in charge of the hospice of Samson sat on the throne of the imperial city. When he had completed some years, he left the world”: The ordination of Menas (sed. 536–552) on 13 March 536 was particular in that he was the only patriarch of Constantinople to be ordained by a Roman bishop. In the eyes of Agapetus, the domination of the church of Rome over Constantinople was thus reaffirmed. Menas’s background from Alexandria and his former position as xenodocharios of the hospice of Samson are confirmed in Liberatus, Breviarium 21 (Schwartz 1962b, 136.12–15); Malalas, Chronicle 18.83 (Thurn 2000, 404); Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 112.30).

On the hospices (ξενοδοχεῖα) of Constantinople, see 3.1.29.f; the hospice of Samson was located close to Hagia Sophia, on the northern side of the church. It was destroyed during the Nika riot in 532 and then splendidly rebuilt by Justinian (Procopius, Buildings 1.2.14–16 (Haury and Wirth 1962–1964, 4:19.5–15)); Menas might have been its first xenodocharios after the reconstruction (Janin 1969, 561–562).

Defining events of Menas’s patriarchate were the dedications of Hagia Sophia in 537 (Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6030 (de Boor 1883, 217)) and of the Church of the Apostles in 550 (Malalas, Chronicle 18.109 (Thurn 2000, 412); Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6042 (de Boor 1883, 227.10–15)); the patriarch presided over both ceremonies. Furthermore, Menas was engaged in the Three Chapters controversy; after initial resistance, he supported Justinian against the prevaricating Roman bishop Vigilius (sed. 537–555) and assisted in organizing the fifth ecumenical council of 553 on the Three Chapters controversy and the theological issues at stake, see Leppin 2011, 293–298 Price 2009, 16–28 and 76–98 Meier 2003, 281–289 Uthemann 1999, 60–70. However, Menas died before the start of the council; again, John of Ephesus does not know how long he was patriarch and therefore vaguely speaks of “some years” (see 3.1.42.c). Malalas, Chronicle 18.115 (Thurn 2000, 415) indicates that Menas died in August 552; the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 113.19–23) gives 23 August as the day of his death.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.f (SR) 51.28–29
div.p: ܘܩ̣ܡ ܒܬܪܗ ܐܢܫ ܥܠܝܡܐ ܕܝܪܝܐ ܐܦ̣ܩܪܝܣܪܐ ܕܐܡ̣ܣܝܐ ܕܫܡܗ̣ ܐ̣ܘܛܘܟ “and was succeeded by a certain young monk, the apokrisiarios of Amasia, whose name was Eutychius”: The main source for patriarch Eutychius of Constantinople (sed. 552–565; 577–582) is the hagiographic Life of Eutychius, written by the presbyter Eustratius probably soon after Eutychius’s death in the first half of the 580s (Averil Cameron 1988, 244–245). The Life confirms the information provided by John of Ephesus: Eutychius had a monastic background and was the head of a monastery close to Amasia in northern Asia Minor. When the bishops were asked to assemble in Constantinople for the fifth ecumenical council, the metropolitan of Amasia decided not to travel there himself because of his age, but sent Eutychius as his representative (Life of Eutychius 514–563 (Laga 1992, 19–21)). Eutychius arrived in Constantinople well before the death of Menas in August 552; the start of the council was delayed because of ultimately unsuccessful attempts to ensure the participation of the Roman bishop Vigilius. The Life 606–638 (Laga 1992, 22–23) claims that soon after his arrival, Eutychius caught the attention of both Menas and Justinian because of his profound scriptural knowledge. Evagrius, EH 4.38 (Bidez and Parmentier 1898, 187.17–29), preserves essentially the same account according to which Eutychius pointed out that a passage from the Old Testament justified the condemnation of deceased authors as heretical (Theodore of Mopsuestia in the context of the Three Chapters controversy) (Averil Cameron 1988, 228–229).

It seems that for this reason, Eutychius became one of Justinian’s favorites. He was ordained patriarch very soon after Menas’s death on 23 August 552. Malalas, Chronicle 18.115 (Thurn 2000, 415) and, dependent on him, Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6044 (de Boor 1883, 228.14–18) state that this happened when Menas was still laying in church and had not yet been buried; the Epitome historiarum ecclesiasticarum (Cramer 1839, 113.19–23) even asserts that his ordination took place on the very day of Menas’s death. It makes sense that the patriarchal throne was not left vacant for a long period during the run-up to an ecumenical council when many bishops were already present in Constantinople.

It is interesting that John of Ephesus qualifies Eutychius as “young” (ܥܠܝܡܐ). The Life of Eutychius 708–711 (Laga 1992, 25) explains that he had attained the “perfect” age of forty years when he was ordained. This may indeed be a rather young age for someone to become patriarch; moreover, Eutychius was likely the first patriarch younger than John of Ephesus (born in 507) himself.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.g (SR) 51.29–52.1
div.p: ܐ̇ܝܟܢܐ ܕܟܕ ܗܢܐ ܠܒ̣ܟ ܐܝܟ ܫ̈ܢܝܐ ܬܪ̈ܬܥܣܪܐ܆ ܐܫܬ̣ܕܝ ܘܢܦ̣ܩ ܡܢ ܟܘܪܣܝܗ “when he had held the see for about twelve years, he was banished and expelled from his see”: According to the Life of Eutychius 855–911 (Laga 1992, 30–31), Eutychius’s position at the head of the church was universally accepted after his chairing of the ecumenical council of 553. However, he seriously clashed with Justinian in early 565 when he rejected the emperor’s edict on aphthartism which he considered heretical (Roggo 2022, 31–33). A circle of people supportive of the imperial ambitions of Justin II made use of this conflict and plotted against the patriarch in order to bring Justin’s ally John Scholasticus to the patriarchal throne Roggo 2022, 72–78; 2019, 440–445 Averil Cameron 1988, 234–235; see 3.1.34.d and 3.1.36.e. They produced various pretexts against Eutychius (Roggo 2022, 47–50); eventually, he was arrested on 22 January 565 van den Ven 1965, 329 n.1 Stein 1949, 688 n.1. His opponents convoked a synod, yet Eutychius refused to follow the summonses and to appear in front of that synod which he considered as illegal. Therefore, the synod declared him deposed and after three weeks of detention in different monasteries close to Constantinople, he was sent into exile to his old monastery in Amasia (Life of Eutychius 1038–1175 (Laga 1992, 35–39)). He remained there until 577, throughout the patriarchate of John Scholasticus.

div.head: EH 3.1.42.h (SR) 52.2–4
div.p: ܘܗ̣ܘܐ ܒܬܪܗ̣ ܝܘܚܢܢ. ܐܢܫ ܣ̣ܘܪܝܝܐ ܡܢ ܣ̣ܪܡܝܢ ܩ̣ܪܝܬܐ ܕܒܒܝܬ ܐܢܛ̈ܝܘܟܝܐ. ܗܢܐ ܕܟܕ ܗ̣ܘܐ܆ ܥܒ̣ܕ ܩ̇ܬܪܣܝܣ ܕܐ̣ܘܛܘܟܐ̣ ܘܐ̣ܘܛܘܟܐ ܕܝܠܗ “after him came John, a certain Syrian from the village of Sirmin in the region of the Antiochians. When he was bishop, he deposed Eutychius and Eutychius did the same to him”: On John Scholasticus’s origins, his patriarchate up to the start of the suppression of the anti-Chalcedonian bishops in 571, and his image in John of Ephesus’s EH, see 3.1.5.e. John Scholasticus was a close ally of Justin II already before the latter’s accession as emperor in November 565. The Life of Symeon the Younger 202–203 (van den Ven 1962, 176–177) narrates that patriarch Domninus of Antioch sent John to Constantinople as his apokrisiarios in the 550s. Prior to his departure, John visited the holy man Symeon, who predicted to him that he would become patriarch and Justin emperor. Upon his arrival in Constantinople, John shared this piece of information with Justin, and accordingly, so the Life holds, the two men became allies (cf. van den Ven 1965, 320–323 and 350–352). The faction backing Justin’s claims was thus interested in ensuring John’s accession as patriarch so that he would be able to support Justin.

John of Ephesus’s short statement here confirms the information provided in the Life of Eutychius 1081–1117 (Laga 1992, 36–37): Already prior to his own deposition and the ordination of John Scholasticus on 1 February 565, Eutychius excommunicated the members of the synod that was acting against him, undoubtedly including John Scholasticus. Consequently, Eutychius never accepted John as patriarch, but considered him as a usurper. This caused considerable problems when Eutychius returned to Constantinople in 577 after John’s death since he insisted that all the actions John had carried out during his patriarchate 565–577 were invalid (EH 3.2.31–3.2.34; see Roggo (2022, 6–8 and 178–182)).

div.head: EH 3.1.42.i (SR) 52.4–6
div.p: ܘܟܕ ܡ̇ܠܝ ܝܘܚܢܢ ܬܪ̈ܬܥܣܪܐ ܫ̈ܢܝܢ ܘܦ̣ܠܓܗ̣̇ ܡܝ̣ܬ. ܘܬܘܒ ܐܬܩ̣ܪܝ ܐ̣ܘܛܘܟ̣ ܘܗܦ̣ܟ ܠܟܘܪܣܝܗ “when John had completed twelve and a half years, he died. Then Eutychius was summoned, and he returned to his see”: John Scholasticus died on 31 August 577 (Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6069 (de Boor 1883, 248.9–10); see 3.1.36.e and 3.1.37.h). The Caesar Tiberius had enjoyed a good relationship with Eutychius during his exile (Life of Eutychius 1885–1900 (Laga 1992, 61)); the former patriarch was therefore invited to return to Constantinople and to reclaim his throne. He arrived on 3 October 577; both John of Ephesus (EH 3.2.31) and the Life of Eutychius 2110–2207 (Laga 1992, 68–71) record that Eutychius’s entry was triumphal and that large crowds welcomed him. Yet this was to change soon when it turned out that he wanted to delete everything reminiscent of the patriarchate of John Scholasticus; moreover, his controversial theological stances alienated parts of the population (see 3.1.42.h and Roggo (2022, 121–126)).

Eutychius died on 6 April 582; his successor John IV Nesteutes (sed. 582–595) was ordained on 12 April (Theophanes, Chronicle AM 6074 (de Boor 1883, 251.22–24)). John of Ephesus recorded the accession of John Nesteutes in EH 3.3.39 and he also mentioned him repeatedly in Book 5. However, he clearly did not update the episcopal lists at the end of Book 1 after 581 (see 3.1.41.p).
